☸️ Kubernetes for Beginners

Complete Learning Guide — From Zero to Production

Master container orchestration step-by-step using real examples with the anihpj Django project and jobpost app

🟢 Beginner Friendly ☸️ Kubernetes v1.28+ 🐍 Django 5.2.5 🐳 Docker 📚 35 Chapters ⏱️ ~40 Hours

🚀 Your Kubernetes Learning Journey

🎯 Welcome! This guide takes you from complete beginner to deploying production-ready applications on Kubernetes. Every concept is explained with real examples using the anihpj/jobpost Django project — you'll learn by doing, not just reading.

📚 Complete Table of Contents

📚 🟢 Beginner

Part 1: Foundation

Understanding the Basics

Learn what Kubernetes is, why it exists, and how its architecture works. Covers Docker prerequisites and core objects.

Ch 1-4 ~6 hours
🛠️ 🟢 Beginner

Part 2: Hands-On

Setting Up and Deploying

Set up your local environment, master kubectl commands, write YAML configs, and deploy anihpj to Kubernetes.

Ch 5-8 ~8 hours
⚙️ 🟢 Beginner

Part 3: Configuration & Storage

Managing Config and Data

Master ConfigMaps, Secrets, and persistent storage. Learn to separate configuration from code.

Ch 9-11 ~5 hours
🌐 🟡 Intermediate

Part 4: Networking

Networking and Exposure

Understand Kubernetes networking, Services (ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer), and Ingress for HTTP routing.

Ch 12-14 ~5 hours
📈 🟡 Intermediate

Part 5: Scaling & Reliability

Auto-Scaling and Self-Healing

Implement horizontal/vertical autoscaling, configure health probes, and manage resource requests and limits.

Ch 15-17 ~5 hours
🔄 🟡 Intermediate

Part 6: Updates & Maintenance

Deployment Strategies

Master rolling updates, blue-green and canary deployments, rollbacks, and scheduled jobs with CronJobs.

Ch 18-20 ~5 hours
📦 🟡 Intermediate

Part 7: Workload Types

StatefulSets, DaemonSets & More

Learn when to use StatefulSets for databases, DaemonSets for node-level services, and ReplicaSets.

Ch 21-22b ~4 hours
🔐 🟡 Intermediate

Part 8: Security

RBAC, Pod Security & Secrets

Implement RBAC, configure Pod Security Standards, Network Policies, and secrets management best practices.

Ch 23-25 ~5 hours
👁️ 🟡 Intermediate

Part 9: Observability

Logging, Monitoring & Debugging

Set up centralized logging, Prometheus monitoring, Grafana dashboards, and master debugging techniques.

Ch 26-28 ~5 hours
🚀 🔴 Advanced

Part 10: Production

Production Considerations

Production checklist, managed K8s services (EKS, GKE, AKS), and CI/CD integration with GitOps.

Ch 29-31 ~6 hours
⚡ 🔴 Advanced

Part 11: Advanced Patterns

Expert-Level Concepts

Multi-container patterns (sidecar, ambassador), advanced scheduling, service mesh, and custom operators.

Ch 32-35 ~6 hours

📎 Appendices — Quick Reference

📋 Appendix A: kubectl Commands

Complete command reference organized by category

📝 Appendix B: YAML Templates

Copy-paste ready templates for all resources

🔧 Appendix C: Common Errors

Troubleshooting guide with solutions

📖 Appendix D: Glossary

A-Z Kubernetes terminology explained

📦 Appendix E: anihpj Manifests

Complete K8s manifests for the example project

🗺️ Appendix F: Learning Path

Next steps and certification guidance

📚 Part 1: Foundation Chapters 1-4

Chapter 1: What is Kubernetes? (The Big Picture)

Before diving into commands and config files, let's understand why Kubernetes exists, what problems it solves, and whether it's the right tool for your project. We'll use our anihpj/jobpost Django app to make every concept concrete.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~1.5 hours 📖 Conceptual 🎯 Big Picture

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why containers alone aren't enough for production
  • ✅ The evolution from bare metal → VMs → containers → orchestration
  • ✅ Kubernetes' 8 core benefits and when to use (or skip) K8s
  • ✅ How Kubernetes fits into the developer workflow
  • ✅ Real-world companies running Kubernetes at massive scale

1.1 The Problem Kubernetes Solves

Imagine you've built anihpj — a Django app with a jobpost module listing jobs filtered by Skills, Location, and Author. It works perfectly on your laptop. But how do you run it in production so thousands of users can access it reliably?

📖 Key Term: Container Orchestration

Container orchestration is the automated management of containerized applications across multiple machines — including deployment, scaling, networking, and availability. Kubernetes is the industry-standard container orchestrator.

The Evolution of Deployment

Software deployment has evolved through four major eras. Each solved problems of the previous one — but introduced new challenges:

🖥️
2000s
Bare Metal

One app per physical server. No isolation. Resource waste.

❌ Wasteful & rigid
💻
2010s
Virtual Machines

Multiple VMs per server. Better isolation. Still heavy — each VM runs a full OS.

⚠️ Heavy overhead
📦
2015+
Containers

Lightweight, fast, shared kernel. But managing hundreds manually? Nightmare.

🟡 Almost there...
☸️
2018+
Kubernetes

Orchestrated containers. Auto-healing, auto-scaling, zero-downtime deploys.

✅ Production-ready

Why Containers Alone Aren't Enough

Docker gives you a container. Kubernetes gives you an army of containers that manage themselves. Here's what you'd have to do manually without it:

😰 Managing anihpj WITHOUT Kubernetes
  • 🔴 SSH into the server manually at 3 AM when the process crashes
  • 🔴 Restart the crashed Django process by hand — python manage.py runserver again
  • 🔴 No load balancing — one server handles ALL traffic to /jobs/
  • 🔴 Deploying = copy files via SCP + pray nothing breaks
  • 🔴 Scaling = buy a new server, install everything from scratch, configure nginx
  • 🔴 Rolling back a bad deploy = restore from backup (if you remembered to make one)
🎉 Managing anihpj WITH Kubernetes
  • ✅ Pod crashes at 3 AM → K8s restarts it in seconds, you sleep
  • ✅ Traffic spikes → K8s spins up more pods automatically
  • ✅ Deploy new version → K8s does it with zero downtime
  • ✅ Bad deploy? → kubectl rollout undo — instant rollback
  • ✅ Config changes? → Update a ConfigMap, pods reload automatically

1.2 Kubernetes Defined

📖 Official Definition

"An open-source system for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications."

In plain English: Kubernetes is an operating system for your containers. You tell it what you want (3 copies of anihpj, always running) — and it figures out how to make that happen.

Quick Facts

☸️
Name Origin

K8s = K + 8 letters + s. Greek for "helmsman" — the person steering the ship.

🏛️
Born at Google

Evolved from Google's internal Borg system. Open-sourced in 2014 and donated to CNCF.

🔤
Written in Go

Built with Google's Go language for high concurrency and performance.

📜
Apache 2.0 License

Fully open-source. Free to use, modify, and distribute commercially.

Kubernetes Lineage
   Google Borg (2003)          Google Omega (2013)          Kubernetes (2014)
  ┌───────────────────┐       ┌───────────────────┐       ┌───────────────────┐
  │ Internal cluster  │──────▶│ Next-gen scheduler│──────▶│ Open-source for   │
  │ manager at Google │       │ research project  │       │ everyone! ☸️       │
  │ Ran Gmail, Search │       │ Flexible queuing  │       │ CNCF graduated    │
  └───────────────────┘       └───────────────────┘       └───────────────────┘
                                                                    │
                                                                    ▼
                                                          Largest open-source
                                                          project after Linux

Kubernetes by the Numbers

CNCF Survey — K8s Adoption 96%
Fortune 100 companies using K8s 77%
GitHub Stars (Top 10 OSS projects) 110K+
Active GitHub Contributors 3,500+

1.3 Key Benefits of Kubernetes

Kubernetes provides eight fundamental capabilities that make container orchestration production-ready:

⚠️ What Kubernetes is NOT
  • ❌ Not a PaaS — It doesn't build your code or manage databases for you (but you can run databases on it)
  • ❌ Not Docker — Docker builds containers; K8s orchestrates them. They complement each other
  • ❌ Not just for microservices — Monoliths like anihpj work great on K8s too
  • ❌ Not only for big companies — Managed K8s (EKS, GKE, AKS) makes it accessible to small teams
📈
Auto-Scaling

More users → more pods. Fewer users → fewer pods. Scale based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics.

💚
Self-Healing

Pod crashes? Kubernetes restarts it. Node dies? Pods are rescheduled to healthy nodes automatically.

⚖️
Load Balancing

Traffic is distributed evenly across all healthy pods. No single pod gets overwhelmed.

🔄
Rolling Updates

Deploy new versions with zero downtime. Automatic rollback if health checks fail.

🔐
Secret Management

Store passwords, API keys, and configs separately from your code. Inject at runtime.

🌐
Service Discovery

Pods find each other by name via built-in DNS. No hardcoded IPs or manual wiring.

💾
Storage Orchestration

Attach local disks, cloud storage, or NFS shares automatically to your pods.

⏰
Batch Execution

Run one-off tasks (migrations) and scheduled jobs (CronJobs) like python manage.py migrate.

💡 anihpj Example

With Kubernetes, if your jobpost app gets a spike in traffic from a job board going viral, the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) can scale from 3 pods to 15 pods in under a minute — then scale back down when traffic normalizes, saving you money.

1.4 Kubernetes vs. Other Options

Kubernetes isn't the only way to deploy containers. Here's how it compares to the alternatives:

Feature Manual Deploy Docker Compose Docker Swarm Kubernetes
Auto-scaling ❌ None ❌ None ⚠️ Basic ✅ Advanced (HPA, VPA)
Self-healing ❌ Manual restart ⚠️ restart: always ✅ Reschedule ✅ Full (pod + node)
Rolling updates ❌ Downtime ❌ Rebuild all ✅ Basic ✅ Zero-downtime
Service discovery ❌ Hardcoded IPs ✅ DNS (local) ✅ DNS overlay ✅ DNS + Ingress
Multi-node ❌ Single server ❌ Single host ✅ Cluster ✅ Large clusters
Complexity ✅ Very simple ✅ Simple ⚠️ Moderate ❌ Steep learning curve
Best for Dev/testing Local dev, small apps Small clusters Production at scale
📌 Key Takeaway

Docker Compose is perfect for local development of anihpj. But when you need reliable production hosting with auto-scaling, self-healing, and zero-downtime deploys — that's where Kubernetes shines.

1.5 Where Kubernetes Fits in Your Workflow

Kubernetes is the last mile of your development pipeline — the engine that runs your app in production:

👨‍💻
Developer Write code for anihpj
→
📦
Git Push Push to GitHub
→
🏗️
CI/CD Build Docker image
→
🗄️
Registry Store anihpj:v1
→
☸️
K8s Cluster Deploy 3 pods
→
👥
Users Access /jobs/
deployment-pipeline.yaml 
# Simplified CI/CD pipeline for anihpj
stages:
  - build:
      script: docker build -t registry.example.com/anihpj:${GIT_SHA} .
  - push:
      script: docker push registry.example.com/anihpj:${GIT_SHA}
  - deploy:
      script: kubectl set image deployment/anihpj anihpj=registry.example.com/anihpj:${GIT_SHA}
      # Kubernetes handles the rest — rolling update, health checks, traffic switching

1.6 When NOT to Use Kubernetes

Kubernetes is powerful — but it's not always the right choice. Here's a decision framework:

Should You Use Kubernetes?
❓ Do you have more than one service/microservice?
No — Single app
Consider a simple VPS or PaaS (Heroku, Railway)
Yes ↓
❓ Do you need auto-scaling or high availability?
No — Low traffic
Docker Compose on a single server is likely sufficient
Yes ↓
❓ Does your team have K8s operational expertise?
No — Small team
Use managed K8s (EKS, GKE, AKS) to reduce ops burden
Yes ↓
✅ Kubernetes is a great fit for your project!
⚠️ Common Mistake

Don't adopt Kubernetes just because "everyone uses it." For a personal blog or small side project like a single-instance anihpj, Docker Compose on a $5/month VPS is perfectly fine. Kubernetes adds complexity that only pays off at scale.

1.7 Real-World Companies Using Kubernetes

These companies bet their infrastructure on Kubernetes — here's what they achieved:

🎵
Spotify
150+ microservices migrated

Reduced deployment time by 80%. Engineers can deploy independently without coordinating with ops.

🏠
Airbnb
1,000+ pods across clusters

Dynamic scaling handles holiday travel spikes. Auto-scale from hundreds to thousands of pods in minutes.

📌
Pinterest
30,000+ pods — 99.99% uptime

Migrated from a custom infrastructure to Kubernetes. Reduced incident response time dramatically.

📰
The New York Times
GKE migration complete

Unified their print and digital publishing pipeline. Now deploys 30x faster than before.

💡 Think About It

If anihpj's jobpost feature went viral and you suddenly had 100,000 users browsing /jobs/, Kubernetes would auto-scale your pods, balance the load, and keep the site running — while you enjoy your coffee.

1.8 Kubernetes Ecosystem Overview

Kubernetes doesn't work alone. It sits at the center of a rich ecosystem of tools managed by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF):

📦
Helm

Package manager for K8s

📊
Prometheus

Metrics & monitoring

📈
Grafana

Visualization dashboards

🔀
Istio

Service mesh & traffic

☸️
Kubernetes

Core orchestrator

🔄
ArgoCD

GitOps continuous delivery

📋
Fluentd

Log collection

🔍
Jaeger

Distributed tracing

🛡️
Falco

Runtime security

🗃️
etcd

Distributed key-value store

🌐
Envoy

Edge & service proxy

📝
OPA/Gatekeeper

Policy enforcement

📌 Don't Worry!

You don't need to learn all these tools at once. This guide introduces them gradually — for now, just know that Kubernetes is the foundation and these tools extend its capabilities for monitoring, security, deployment, and more.

Chapter 2: Prerequisites — Docker Fundamentals

Before you can orchestrate containers with Kubernetes, you need to understand what containers are and how to build them. This chapter covers Docker — the tool you'll use to package anihpj into a portable, deployable unit.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~2 hours 🐳 Hands-On 📦 Docker

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Containers vs VMs — why containers won
  • ✅ Docker images, containers, registries, and how they relate
  • ✅ Writing a production-ready Dockerfile for a Django app
  • ✅ Essential Docker commands for building, running, and debugging
  • ✅ Docker Compose for multi-container local development
  • ✅ Container registries — where images live

2.1 What is Docker?

Think of a shipping container. Before containers, cargo was loaded loose — fragile items broke, liquids spilled, loading took days. The standardized shipping container changed everything: any cargo, any ship, any port.

Docker does the same for software. It packages your app with everything it needs (Python, Django, dependencies, settings) into a standardized unit that runs identically everywhere — your laptop, a test server, or a cloud datacenter.

Containers vs Virtual Machines

Both provide isolation, but they work at fundamentally different levels:

📦 Container Approach
┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐
│App│ │App│ │App│ │App│  ← Your apps
└─┬─┘ └─┬─┘ └─┬─┘ └─┬─┘
┌─┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─┐
│   Container Engine   │  ← Docker / containerd
├──────────────────────┤
│   Host OS (shared)   │  ← One Linux kernel
├──────────────────────┤
│  Hardware / Cloud    │
└──────────────────────┘

✅ Lightweight (~50 MB)   ✅ Seconds to start   ✅ Share OS kernel   ✅ 100s per host

💻 Virtual Machine Approach
┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐
│App│ │App│ │App│ │App│  ← Your apps
└─┬─┘ └─┬─┘ └─┬─┘ └─┬─┘
┌─┴─┐ ┌─┴─┐ ┌─┴─┐ ┌─┴─┐
│ OS│ │ OS│ │ OS│ │ OS│  ← Full OS each!
└─┬─┘ └─┬─┘ └─┬─┘ └─┬─┘
┌─┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─┐
│      Hypervisor      │  ← VMware / Hyper-V
├──────────────────────┤
│  Hardware / Cloud    │
└──────────────────────┘

❌ Heavy (~1-2 GB)   ❌ Minutes to start   ❌ Full OS per VM   ❌ ~10 per host

Performance Comparison

Metric Container Virtual Machine Winner
Memory ~50 MB ~1-2 GB 📦 Container (40x less)
Startup Time ~1-3 seconds ~30-90 seconds 📦 Container (30x faster)
Disk Size ~100 MB ~10-40 GB 📦 Container (100x smaller)
Density per Host 100s per host ~10 per host 📦 Container (10x more)
Isolation Process-level Hardware-level 💻 VM (stronger)

2.2 Docker Key Concepts

Docker has four core concepts that map neatly to things you already know from Python:

📝
Dockerfile Recipe / Blueprint
→ build →
📦
Image Template / Class
→ run →
🚀
Container Instance / Object
↕ push / pull
🗄️
Registry Like PyPI for images
📝
Dockerfile

A text file with step-by-step instructions to build an image. Like a recipe — "start with Python 3.11, install Django, copy my code, run gunicorn."

📦
Image

A read-only template built from the Dockerfile. Like a Python class — it defines what the container will be, but isn't running yet.

🚀
Container

A running instance of an image. Like a Python object — you can have many containers from one image, each isolated.

🗄️
Registry

A storage service for images. Docker Hub is the default (like PyPI). Push to share, pull to download.

Image Layers — The Cake Diagram

Docker images are built in layers. Each instruction in a Dockerfile creates a new layer. Unchanged layers are cached, making rebuilds fast:

Docker Image Layers for anihpj
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐  ← Layer 5: CMD (entrypoint)
│  CMD ["gunicorn", "anihpj.wsgi", "-b", "0.0.0.0:8000"]  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤  ← Layer 4: COPY application code
│  COPY . /app/  (models.py, views.py, urls.py)            │  ⚡ Changes often
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤  ← Layer 3: RUN pip install
│  RUN pip install -r requirements.txt                     │  📦 Cached if unchanged!
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤  ← Layer 2: COPY requirements
│  COPY requirements.txt /app/                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤  ← Layer 1: Base image
│  FROM python:3.11-slim  (Debian + Python runtime)        │  🏗️ Rarely changes
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Layer Caching Trick

Copy requirements.txt and run pip install before copying your app code. That way, Docker only re-installs dependencies when requirements.txt changes — not every time you edit views.py.

Tags — Versioning Your Images

Tags let you version your images. Without tags, Docker uses latest by default (which is confusing in production):

terminal 
# Good: explicit version tags
docker build -t anihpj:v1.0 .
docker build -t anihpj:v1.1 .
docker build -t anihpj:v2.0-beta .

# Also common: use git commit SHA
docker build -t anihpj:abc123f .

2.3 Docker Workflow Explained

The Docker workflow is a cycle: write, build, tag, push, pull, run. Here's the full loop:

①
Write Dockerfile
→
②
Build docker build .
→
③
Tag docker tag
→
④
Push docker push
→
⑤
Pull docker pull
→
⑥
Run docker run -p
📌 In Practice

During local development, you'll mostly use steps 1-2-6 (write → build → run). The push/pull steps come into play when deploying to a server or Kubernetes cluster, where CI/CD pipelines automate the full cycle.

2.4 Creating a Dockerfile for anihpj

Let's write a production-ready Dockerfile for our Django project. Every line is annotated so you understand exactly what it does:

Dockerfile 
# ── Stage 1: Base image ──────────────────────────────────────
# Use Python 3.11 slim (Debian-based, ~120 MB vs ~900 MB full)
FROM python:3.11-slim

# ── Stage 2: Set environment variables ───────────────────────
# Prevent Python from writing .pyc files and buffer stdout/stderr
ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1
ENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1

# ── Stage 3: Set working directory ───────────────────────────
# All subsequent commands run inside /app
WORKDIR /app

# ── Stage 4: Install system dependencies ─────────────────────
# gcc and libpq-dev are needed for psycopg2 (PostgreSQL adapter)
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    gcc libpq-dev \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# ── Stage 5: Install Python dependencies ─────────────────────
# Copy requirements FIRST for layer caching (only re-install
# when requirements.txt changes, not when views.py changes)
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# ── Stage 6: Copy application code ──────────────────────────
# Now copy the entire anihpj project
COPY . .

# ── Stage 7: Collect static files ───────────────────────────
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

# ── Stage 8: Expose port ────────────────────────────────────
# Document which port the app listens on (doesn't publish it)
EXPOSE 8000

# ── Stage 9: Run the application ────────────────────────────
# Use gunicorn for production (not manage.py runserver!)
CMD ["gunicorn", "anihpj.wsgi:application", \
     "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", \
     "--workers", "3"]
⚠️ Common Mistake

Never use python manage.py runserver in production! It's single-threaded and not designed for real traffic. Use gunicorn (or uvicorn for async) with multiple workers.

Multi-Stage Build (Advanced)

For even smaller images, use a multi-stage build. Build everything in a "builder" stage, then copy only the final artifacts to a clean slim image:

Dockerfile.multistage 
# ── Build stage: install dependencies with build tools ──
FROM python:3.11-slim AS builder
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y gcc libpq-dev
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --prefix=/install -r requirements.txt

# ── Production stage: clean, small image ────────────────
FROM python:3.11-slim
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends libpq5 \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
COPY --from=builder /install /usr/local
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

# 🔒 Run as non-root user (security best practice)
RUN adduser --disabled-password --no-create-home appuser
USER appuser

EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["gunicorn", "anihpj.wsgi:application", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", "--workers", "3"]
🔒 Docker Security Checklist
  • ✅ Run as non-root — Always add USER appuser (never run as root in production)
  • ✅ No secrets in images — Never put passwords or API keys in ENV or COPY. Use K8s Secrets instead
  • ✅ Pin base image versions — Use python:3.11.7-slim not python:latest
  • ✅ Scan for vulnerabilities — Run docker scout cves anihpj:v1 before deploying
  • ✅ Minimal base image — Use -slim or -alpine to reduce attack surface
💡 Why python:3.11-slim?
  • python:3.11 — Full Debian image (~900 MB) — everything included, but huge
  • python:3.11-slim — Stripped Debian (~120 MB) — great balance ✅
  • python:3.11-alpine — Tiny (~50 MB) — but musl libc can cause issues with some packages

2.5 The .dockerignore File

Just like .gitignore keeps junk out of your repo, .dockerignore keeps junk out of your image. Smaller images = faster builds, faster pulls, less attack surface.

.dockerignore 
# Version control
.git
.gitignore

# Python
__pycache__
*.pyc
*.pyo
*.egg-info
env/
venv/
.venv/

# Database (don't ship SQLite into the image!)
db.sqlite3

# IDE
.vscode/
.idea/
*.swp

# Docker
Dockerfile
docker-compose*.yml
.dockerignore

# OS
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db

# Documentation
*.md
docs/

Impact on Image Size

Without .dockerignore
~450 MB (includes .git, env/, __pycache__)
With .dockerignore
~160 MB (only what's needed)

2.6 Docker Commands You Need to Know

Here are the essential Docker commands organized by what you're doing:

🏗️ Build & Tag

CommandWhat It DoesExample
docker buildBuild image from Dockerfiledocker build -t anihpj:v1 .
docker tagAdd another tag to an imagedocker tag anihpj:v1 registry/anihpj:v1
docker imagesList local imagesdocker images

🚀 Run & Manage

CommandWhat It DoesExample
docker runCreate + start a containerdocker run -p 8000:8000 anihpj:v1
docker run -dRun in background (detached)docker run -d -p 8000:8000 anihpj:v1
docker psList running containersdocker ps
docker ps -aList ALL containers (incl. stopped)docker ps -a
docker stopGracefully stop containerdocker stop <id>
docker startRestart a stopped containerdocker start <id>

🔍 Inspect & Debug

CommandWhat It DoesExample
docker logsView container outputdocker logs <id>
docker logs -fFollow logs (live tail)docker logs -f <id>
docker exec -itOpen shell inside containerdocker exec -it <id> bash
docker inspectFull JSON detail of containerdocker inspect <id>

🧹 Cleanup

CommandWhat It DoesExample
docker rmRemove stopped containerdocker rm <id>
docker rmiRemove imagedocker rmi anihpj:v1
docker system pruneRemove all unused datadocker system prune -a

🗄️ Registry

CommandWhat It DoesExample
docker loginAuthenticate to registrydocker login docker.io
docker pushUpload image to registrydocker push registry/anihpj:v1
docker pullDownload image from registrydocker pull nginx:latest

2.7 Building and Running anihpj Container

Let's put it all together — here's the step-by-step to containerize and run anihpj:

📝
Step 1
Write Dockerfile

Create the Dockerfile in the anihpj/ project root

🏗️
Step 2
Build Image

docker build -t anihpj:v1 .

🚀
Step 3
Run Container

docker run -p 8000:8000 anihpj:v1

🌐
Step 4
Access App

Visit http://localhost:8000/jobs/

terminal — Build & Run anihpj 
# Step 1: Make sure you're in the project directory
cd anihpj/

# Step 2: Build the Docker image
docker build -t anihpj:v1 .
# ✅ Output: Successfully tagged anihpj:v1

# Step 3: Run the container
docker run -d -p 8000:8000 --name anihpj-app anihpj:v1
# ✅ Output: a1b2c3d4e5f6... (container ID)

# Step 4: Open your browser → http://localhost:8000/jobs/

# Step 5: Check the logs to see Django output
docker logs anihpj-app
# [INFO] Starting gunicorn 21.2.0
# [INFO] Listening at: http://0.0.0.0:8000 (1)
# [INFO] Using worker: sync
# [INFO] Booting worker with pid: 7, 8, 9

# Step 6: Peek inside the running container
docker exec -it anihpj-app bash
# root@a1b2c3d4:/app# ls
# anihpj/  jobpost/  manage.py  requirements.txt

# Step 7: Stop and remove when done
docker stop anihpj-app
docker rm anihpj-app
💡 Flags Explained
  • -d — Detached mode (runs in background)
  • -p 8000:8000 — Map host port 8000 to container port 8000
  • --name anihpj-app — Give the container a human-friendly name
  • -t anihpj:v1 — Tag the image with name and version
Port Mapping: -p HOST:CONTAINER
   YOUR MACHINE                          DOCKER CONTAINER
  ┌──────────────┐                      ┌──────────────────┐
  │              │                      │  anihpj (Django)  │
  │  Browser     │   -p 8000:8000      │                  │
  │  localhost ──────────────────────▶  │  gunicorn :8000  │
  │  :8000       │   host → container  │                  │
  │              │                      │  0.0.0.0:8000    │
  └──────────────┘                      └──────────────────┘

  You could also map to a different host port:
  -p 80:8000    →  access at localhost:80, container still on 8000
  -p 9090:8000  →  access at localhost:9090

2.8 Docker Compose — Running Multiple Containers

Real apps need more than one container. anihpj needs Django + PostgreSQL + Redis. Docker Compose lets you define and run multi-container setups with a single YAML file:

docker-compose.yml 
version: "3.9"

services:
  # ── Django Application ─────────────────────────
  web:
    build: .
    command: gunicorn anihpj.wsgi:application --bind 0.0.0.0:8000
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgres://anihpj:secret@db:5432/anihpj_db
      - REDIS_URL=redis://cache:6379/0
    depends_on:
      - db
      - cache

  # ── PostgreSQL Database ────────────────────────
  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: anihpj_db
      POSTGRES_USER: anihpj
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: secret
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data

  # ── Redis Cache ────────────────────────────────
  cache:
    image: redis:7-alpine

volumes:
  postgres_data:
terminal — Docker Compose Commands 
# Start all services in background
docker compose up -d

# Check status
docker compose ps

# View logs from all services
docker compose logs -f

# Run Django migrations inside the web container
docker compose exec web python manage.py migrate

# Stop everything
docker compose down

# Stop and remove volumes (database data!)
docker compose down -v
🐳 Docker Compose

✅ Simple YAML file
✅ Great for local dev
✅ One command: docker compose up
❌ Single host only
❌ No auto-scaling
❌ No self-healing across nodes

☸️ Kubernetes

✅ Multi-host clusters
✅ Auto-scaling (HPA)
✅ Self-healing across nodes
✅ Rolling updates & rollbacks
⚠️ More complex configuration
⚠️ Steeper learning curve

📌 The Natural Progression

Most projects start with Docker Compose for local development, then graduate to Kubernetes for production. You'll keep using Compose on your laptop — but deploy to K8s when it's time to go live.

2.9 Container Registries

Container registries are where Docker images live. When Kubernetes needs to run anihpj, it pulls the image from a registry. Here are your options:

Registry Provider Free Tier Best For
Docker Hub Docker Inc. 1 private repo Open-source, public images
GitHub GHCR GitHub 500 MB free (private) GitHub Actions CI/CD
Amazon ECR AWS 500 MB / 12 months EKS clusters on AWS
Google GCR / Artifact Registry Google Cloud 500 MB free GKE clusters on GCP
Azure ACR Microsoft Basic tier ($5/mo) AKS clusters on Azure
terminal — Push to Docker Hub 
# 1. Login to Docker Hub
docker login

# 2. Tag with your Docker Hub username
docker tag anihpj:v1 yourusername/anihpj:v1

# 3. Push to Docker Hub
docker push yourusername/anihpj:v1

# 4. Anyone (or Kubernetes) can now pull it:
docker pull yourusername/anihpj:v1
💡 For This Guide

In later chapters, when Kubernetes pulls the anihpj image, it will fetch it from whichever registry you push to. For local development with Minikube, you can even load images directly without a registry: minikube image load anihpj:v1

Chapter 3: Kubernetes Architecture (How It All Fits Together)

Now that you understand why Kubernetes exists and how Docker packages apps, let's look under the hood. This chapter dissects the architecture — control plane, worker nodes, and how every component collaborates to keep your anihpj pods running.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~1.5 hours 🏗️ Architecture 🧠 Conceptual

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ What a Kubernetes cluster is and its minimum requirements
  • ✅ The 5 control plane components and what each one does
  • ✅ The 3 worker node components that run your containers
  • ✅ The full 8-step journey of how a pod gets created
  • ✅ The declarative model — desired state vs actual state

3.1 The Cluster — The Big Picture

A Kubernetes cluster is a set of machines (physical or virtual) that work together to run containerized applications. Every cluster has two types of machines:

🧠
Control Plane (The Brain)

Makes all the decisions: what runs where, when to scale, how to recover. In production, you run 3+ control plane nodes for high availability.

💪
Worker Nodes (The Muscles)

Actually run your application containers. Each worker runs your anihpj pods, handles traffic, and reports status back to the control plane.

Complete Kubernetes Cluster Architecture
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        KUBERNETES CLUSTER                           │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                   🧠 CONTROL PLANE (The Brain)                │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  ┌─────────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌───────────┐ ┌──────────────┐  │  │
│  │  │ API Server  │ │  etcd  │ │ Scheduler │ │  Controller  │  │  │
│  │  │ 🛡️ Front    │ │ 💾 Data │ │ 📍 Assign │ │ 🔄 Manager   │  │  │
│  │  │    Door     │ │  Store │ │    Pods   │ │    Loops     │  │  │
│  │  └──────┬──────┘ └────────┘ └───────────┘ └──────────────┘  │  │
│  └─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│            │  kubectl / API calls                                   │
│  ┌─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │         ▼                                                     │  │
│  │  💪 WORKER NODE 1              💪 WORKER NODE 2               │  │
│  │  ┌──────────────────────┐   ┌──────────────────────┐         │  │
│  │  │ kubelet | kube-proxy │   │ kubelet | kube-proxy │         │  │
│  │  ├──────────────────────┤   ├──────────────────────┤         │  │
│  │  │ ┌────┐┌────┐┌────┐  │   │ ┌────┐┌────┐┌────┐  │         │  │
│  │  │ │📦  ││📦  ││📦  │  │   │ │📦  ││📦  ││📦  │  │         │  │
│  │  │ │web ││redis││nginx│  │   │ │web ││web ││ db │  │         │  │
│  │  │ └────┘└────┘└────┘  │   │ └────┘└────┘└────┘  │         │  │
│  │  └──────────────────────┘   └──────────────────────┘         │  │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📌 Minimum Cluster

For local development (Minikube, kind), you get a single node that acts as both control plane and worker. In production, you'd have at least 3 control plane nodes (for high availability) and 2+ worker nodes (for capacity).

3.2 Control Plane Components (The Brain)

The control plane makes all cluster-level decisions. It has five components, each with a specific job:

a) kube-apiserver — The Front Door

🛡️
What It Does

The only component that everything talks to. Every kubectl command, every internal communication, every dashboard click goes through the API Server. It validates requests, authenticates users, and routes commands.

🔑
Key Responsibilities

• REST API endpoint (HTTPS)
• Authentication & authorization
• Admission control (mutating & validating)
• Gateway to etcd (only component that reads/writes etcd)

API Server as the Central Hub
    kubectl ──────┐
    Dashboard ────┤
    CI/CD ────────┤──▶  🛡️ API Server  ──▶  💾 etcd
    kubelet ──────┤      (authenticates,
    Scheduler ────┤       validates,
    Controllers ──┘       stores)

b) etcd — The Cluster's Database

💾
What It Does

A distributed key-value store that holds all cluster state — every pod, service, config, secret. Uses the Raft consensus algorithm to ensure data consistency across replicas.

📁
What It Stores

• Which pods exist and their status
• Deployment configurations
• Secrets and ConfigMaps
• Node registrations
• Service endpoints

🚨 Critical Warning

If etcd data is lost and there's no backup, your entire cluster state is gone. Always back up etcd in production. Everything else in Kubernetes is stateless — etcd is the single source of truth.

c) kube-scheduler — The Pod Placement Engine

📍
What It Does

Watches for newly created pods with no assigned node. Evaluates each node's resources, constraints, and affinities, then picks the best fit. Example: "anihpj pod needs 256Mi RAM → Node 2 has 512Mi free → assign to Node 2."

📦
New Pod Unscheduled
→
🔍
Filter Which nodes can fit?
→
⚖️
Score Rank candidates
→
📍
Bind Assign to best node

d) kube-controller-manager — The Reconciliation Loops

🔄
What It Does

Runs multiple controller loops that continuously compare desired state to actual state, and take action to fix any drift. Each controller handles one type of resource.

🔁 Deployment Controller

Ensures the correct number of ReplicaSets exist for each Deployment.

📋 ReplicaSet Controller

Maintains the desired number of pod replicas. Creates or deletes pods as needed.

💻 Node Controller

Monitors node health. Marks unhealthy nodes and reschedules their pods.

⏰ Job Controller

Watches for Job objects and creates pods to run tasks to completion.

Controller Loop Pattern (Watch → Compare → Act)
     ┌─────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────┐
     │  Desired State   │         │  Actual State    │
     │  replicas: 3     │───?───▶│  2 pods running  │
     └─────────────────┘  match? └─────────────────┘
                                          │
                                     ❌ NO!
                                          │
                                          ▼
                                  🔄 Controller creates
                                     1 more pod → ✅ 3 pods

e) cloud-controller-manager

☁️
What It Does

Integrates Kubernetes with cloud provider APIs (AWS, GCP, Azure). Creates cloud load balancers when you create a LoadBalancer Service, manages cloud routes, and handles node lifecycle in cloud environments. Not present in local clusters like Minikube or kind.

3.3 Worker Node Components (The Muscles)

Worker nodes are where your application containers actually run. Each node has three essential components:

a) kubelet — The Node Agent

🤖
What It Does

An agent running on every node. Receives pod specifications from the API Server, ensures the containers described in the spec are running and healthy, and reports status back.

📋
Responsibilities

• Pull container images
• Start/stop containers
• Run health checks (liveness, readiness)
• Report pod & node status to API Server

b) kube-proxy — The Network Plumber

🔀
What It Does

A network proxy on every node that maintains network rules (iptables or IPVS). When a Service is created, kube-proxy ensures traffic to that Service's ClusterIP is forwarded to the correct backend pods. This is how anihpj-service routes to the right pod, even if pods restart with new IPs.

c) Container Runtime

⚙️
What It Does

The software that actually creates and runs containers. Kubernetes itself doesn't run containers — it delegates to a runtime via the Container Runtime Interface (CRI).

RuntimeStatusNotes
containerd✅ DefaultMost common. Used by Docker, EKS, GKE, AKS.
CRI-O✅ SupportedLightweight, OCI-compliant. Popular with OpenShift.
Docker Engine⚠️ DeprecatedRemoved as a runtime in K8s 1.24+. Docker images still work — only the runtime shim was removed.
💡 Docker Images Still Work!

When people say "Docker was removed from Kubernetes," they mean the Docker runtime shim (dockershim) was removed. Your Docker-built images are OCI-compliant and work perfectly with containerd or CRI-O.

How Worker Node Components Communicate

Worker Node Internal Flow
  🧠 Control Plane (API Server)
         │
         │ Pod spec: "run anihpj:v2 on port 8000"
         │
    ┌────▼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │  💪 WORKER NODE                                       │
    │                                                       │
    │  🤖 kubelet                                           │
    │    │  "I received a pod spec, let me handle it"       │
    │    │                                                  │
    │    ├──▶ ⚙️ Container Runtime (containerd)             │
    │    │      │  Pull anihpj:v2 from registry             │
    │    │      │  Create & start the container            │
    │    │      └──▶ 🐍 anihpj container running :8000     │
    │    │                                                  │
    │    └──▶ Reports status back to API Server             │
    │                                                       │
    │  🔀 kube-proxy                                        │
    │    │  "Service anihpj-svc created, update rules"      │
    │    └──▶ iptables/IPVS rules updated                  │
    │         Traffic to ClusterIP → forwarded to pod      │
    └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

3.4 How a Pod Gets Created — The Full Journey

When you run kubectl apply -f anihpj-deployment.yaml, here's the complete 8-step chain of events:

The Pod Creation Journey — 8 Steps
  👨‍💻 You                   🛡️ API Server            💾 etcd
   │                        │                       │
   │── ① kubectl apply ───▶│                       │
   │                        │── ② validate ────────▶│
   │                        │   + store             │── ③ saved!
   │                        │                       │
  🔄 Controller              │                       │
   │── ④ sees new deploy ─▶│                       │
   │   creates ReplicaSet   │                       │
   │── ⑤ creates Pod obj ─▶│                       │
   │                        │                       │
  📍 Scheduler               │                       │
   │── ⑥ picks best node ─▶│                       │
   │   "Node 2 has space"   │                       │
   │                        │                       │
  💪 Node 2 (kubelet)        │                       │
   │── ⑦ pulls anihpj:v1   │                       │
   │── ⑧ starts container  │                       │
   │                        │                       │
  ✅ Pod Running!             │                       │
①
kubectl apply

You send your YAML to the API Server

②③
Validate & Store

API Server authenticates, validates, stores in etcd

④⑤
Controllers Act

Deployment controller creates ReplicaSet, which creates Pod objects

⑥⑦⑧
Schedule & Run

Scheduler assigns node, kubelet pulls image, container starts

📌 Key Insight

No single component "runs" a pod. It's a relay race: API Server → etcd → Controller → Scheduler → kubelet → Container Runtime. Each component watches for changes and does its part. This decoupled design is what makes Kubernetes resilient.

Verify Each Step With kubectl

terminal — Tracing the Journey 
# After applying anihpj-deployment.yaml, trace each step:

# ① Check the Deployment was created
kubectl get deployment anihpj-deployment

# ④ Check the ReplicaSet was created by the controller
kubectl get replicaset

# ⑤ Check the Pods were created by the ReplicaSet
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj

# ⑥ See which NODE each pod was scheduled on
kubectl get pods -o wide

# ⑦⑧ See events — the full story: scheduling, pulling, starting
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>
# Events:
#   Scheduled → Pulling → Pulled → Created → Started

3.5 Control Plane vs Worker Node

Aspect 🧠 Control Plane 💪 Worker Node
Purpose Manage cluster state & make decisions Run application pods
Components API Server, etcd, Scheduler, Controller Manager kubelet, kube-proxy, Container Runtime
User workloads? Generally no (tainted by default) Yes — this is where anihpj pods run
Count (dev) 1 (Minikube combines both) 1 (same node)
Count (prod) 3+ (for high availability) 2+ (scale as needed)
If it fails? Cluster can't schedule or heal (critical!) Pods reschedule to other nodes

3.6 Kubernetes API and API Groups

Every resource in Kubernetes belongs to an API group. This organizes the hundreds of resource types and controls versioning:

API GroupVersionResourcesExample
Core (legacy) v1 Pod, Service, ConfigMap, Secret, Namespace, Node apiVersion: v1
apps apps/v1 Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, ReplicaSet apiVersion: apps/v1
batch batch/v1 Job, CronJob apiVersion: batch/v1
networking networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress, NetworkPolicy, IngressClass apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
rbac rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 Role, ClusterRole, RoleBinding apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
autoscaling autoscaling/v2 HorizontalPodAutoscaler apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
📌 API Versioning

APIs go through maturity stages: alpha (v1alpha1 — experimental) → beta (v1beta1 — mostly stable) → stable (v1 — production-ready). Always use stable versions in production YAML files.

3.7 Understanding Desired State vs Actual State

This is the most important mental model in Kubernetes. You don't tell Kubernetes how to do things — you tell it what you want, and it continuously works to make reality match your description.

📝 Declarative (Kubernetes Way)

"I want 3 replicas of anihpj:v2 running with 256Mi memory each."

You declare the end state. Kubernetes figures out the steps and continuously enforces it — even if a pod crashes at 3 AM.

⌨️ Imperative (Traditional Way)

"SSH to server, pull the latest code, restart gunicorn, check if it's running, configure nginx..."

You specify every step. If something breaks, you have to detect it and fix it manually.

The Reconciliation Loop in Action
┌──────────────────────┐            ┌──────────────────────┐
│    DESIRED STATE     │            │    ACTUAL STATE       │
│    (your YAML)       │            │    (what exists)      │
│                      │            │                       │
│  replicas: 3         │    ≠?      │  🟢 pod-1 Running     │
│  image: anihpj:v2    │ ────────▶  │  🟢 pod-2 Running     │
│  memory: 256Mi       │  compare   │  🔴 pod-3 Crashed!    │
└──────────────────────┘            └──────────┬────────────┘
                                               │
                                        🔄 Controller detects
                                           mismatch!
                                               │
                                               ▼
                                      Creates new pod-3
                                               │
                                               ▼
                                    🟢 pod-3 Running ✅
                                    All 3 replicas healthy!
🎉 What This Means for anihpj
  • ✅ You write a YAML file saying "I want 3 copies of anihpj running"
  • ✅ If pod crashes → Controller creates a replacement automatically
  • ✅ If node dies → Scheduler moves pods to healthy nodes
  • ✅ If you change the image tag → Deployment controller does a rolling update
  • ✅ You never SSH into servers — you just update the YAML and kubectl apply
💡 The Golden Rule

Never make manual changes to a cluster. Always edit your YAML files and re-apply. That way, your YAML is always the single source of truth, and you can reproduce your entire deployment from scratch.

Chapter 4: Core Kubernetes Objects (The Building Blocks)

Kubernetes manages your applications through objects — persistent entities that represent the desired state of your cluster. This chapter covers the essential building blocks: Pods, Deployments, Services, Namespaces, Labels, and how they all connect.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~2 hours 🧱 Core Objects 📝 YAML

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ The anatomy of every Kubernetes object (apiVersion, kind, metadata, spec)
  • ✅ Pods — what they are, their lifecycle, and why you never create them directly
  • ✅ The Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pod hierarchy
  • ✅ Service types and how pods are exposed to traffic
  • ✅ Namespaces, Labels, Selectors, and Annotations
  • ✅ How all objects connect in a real application

4.1 What are Kubernetes Objects?

Every resource in Kubernetes — a Pod, a Deployment, a Service — is an object. Each object is a "record of intent": you tell Kubernetes what you want, and the system continuously works to ensure that state exists.

Anatomy of a Kubernetes Object

Every object has four required fields:

📋
apiVersion

Which API group and version to use. Example: v1 for Pods, apps/v1 for Deployments.

🏷️
kind

The type of object. Example: Pod, Deployment, Service, ConfigMap.

📛
metadata

Identifying info: name, namespace, labels, annotations. Must be unique within a namespace.

⚙️
spec

The desired state — what you want. Varies by object type. This is where you define containers, replicas, ports, etc.

Every K8s object follows this structure 
apiVersion: v1              # ← Which API version
kind: Pod                   # ← What type of object
metadata:                   # ← Identity & organization
  name: anihpj-pod
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    tier: backend
spec:                       # ← Desired state (varies by kind)
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
# status: ...              # ← Set by Kubernetes (read-only)

4.2 Pods — The Smallest Deployable Unit

a) What is a Pod?

A Pod is a wrapper around one or more containers that share the same network and storage. Think of it as an apartment building:

🏠 Pod = Apartment

Shared address (IP), shared hallways (network namespace), shared utilities (volumes). Containers inside the pod communicate via localhost.

🚪 Container = Room

Each container has its own process, filesystem, and resources. But it shares the pod's IP address and mounted volumes with its neighbors.

Single-Container vs Multi-Container Pod
  SINGLE-CONTAINER POD (most common)     MULTI-CONTAINER POD (sidecar pattern)
  ┌─────────────────────┐                ┌────────────────────────────────┐
  │  📦 Pod              │                │  📦 Pod                        │
  │  ┌────────────────┐ │                │  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  │
  │  │  🐍 anihpj      │ │                │  │ 🐍 anihpj │  │ 📊 log   │  │
  │  │  Django app    │ │                │  │  Django   │  │ collector│  │
  │  │  port 8000     │ │                │  │  :8000    │  │  sidecar │  │
  │  └────────────────┘ │                │  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  │
  │  IP: 10.244.1.5     │                │  IP: 10.244.1.6               │
  │  Volume: /app/data  │                │  Shared volume: /var/log      │
  └─────────────────────┘                └────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Rule of Thumb

If two containers must be deployed, scaled, and stopped together — put them in one pod. Otherwise, use separate pods. For anihpj, the Django app gets its own pod. PostgreSQL gets a separate pod.

b) Pod Lifecycle and Phases

Every pod goes through a lifecycle with distinct phases:

Pod Lifecycle State Machine
                    ┌─────────────┐
                    │  ⏳ Pending   │ ← Image pulling, scheduling
                    └──────┬──────┘
                           │
              ┌────────────┼────────────┐
              ▼                         ▼
       ┌─────────────┐          ┌─────────────┐
       │  🟢 Running   │          │  ❌ Failed   │ ← Image not found,
       └──┬────────┬──┘          └─────────────┘   node down, etc.
          │        │
          ▼        ▼
   ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────────┐
   │✅ Succeed │ │🔄 CrashLoop  │ → restartPolicy
   │ ed       │ │  BackOff     │   decides next
   └──────────┘ └──────────────┘   action
PhaseMeaningCommon Cause
PendingPod accepted, waiting to be scheduled or image pulledImage downloading, no node has capacity
RunningAt least one container is runningNormal operation
SucceededAll containers exited successfully (exit code 0)Jobs, batch tasks
FailedAt least one container exited with an errorApp crash, misconfiguration
CrashLoopBackOffContainer keeps crashing; K8s retries with increasing delayBug in app, missing env vars, wrong command
📌 Restart Policies
  • Always (default) — Always restart, even on success. Used for web servers like anihpj.
  • OnFailure — Only restart if exit code ≠ 0. Used for Jobs.
  • Never — Never restart. Used for debugging.

c) Pod YAML for anihpj

anihpj-pod.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    tier: backend
    version: v1
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
          protocol: TCP
      env:
        - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
          value: "anihpj.settings"
        - name: PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE
          value: "1"
      resources:
        requests:
          memory: "128Mi"
          cpu: "100m"
        limits:
          memory: "256Mi"
          cpu: "500m"
  restartPolicy: Always
⚠️ Don't Create Bare Pods in Production!

This YAML creates a single pod. If it crashes and restartPolicy can't fix it (e.g., the node dies), it's gone forever. In production, always use a Deployment — which manages pods through a ReplicaSet and can recreate them on other nodes.

d) When Pods Die

💀
Pods are Ephemeral

Pods are mortal. They are not resurrected after deletion. They get new IPs when restarted. Their local filesystem is wiped. Treat pods like cattle, not pets.

🛡️
Deployments are Durable

Deployments ensure the right number of pods are always running. Pod dies? Deployment creates a replacement. Node dies? Pods are rescheduled elsewhere.

4.3 ReplicaSets — Ensuring Availability

A ReplicaSet ensures a specified number of pod replicas are running at all times. If a pod dies, the ReplicaSet creates a new one to maintain the desired count.

ReplicaSet Maintaining 3 anihpj Pods
   ReplicaSet: "maintain 3 replicas of app=anihpj"

   🟢 pod-1 (anihpj)     🟢 pod-2 (anihpj)     🟢 pod-3 (anihpj)
   Running               Running               Running
                                                     │
                                                  💥 Crashes!
                                                     │
   🟢 pod-1 (anihpj)     🟢 pod-2 (anihpj)     🔴 pod-3 (dead)
                                                     │
                                              🔄 ReplicaSet detects!
                                                     │
   🟢 pod-1 (anihpj)     🟢 pod-2 (anihpj)     🟢 pod-4 (anihpj) ← NEW!
   Running               Running               Running ✅
📌 You Rarely Create ReplicaSets Directly

In practice, you create Deployments, which automatically create and manage ReplicaSets for you. The Deployment adds rolling updates and rollback capabilities on top of what ReplicaSets provide.

4.4 Deployments — Managing Application Updates

The Deployment is the most commonly used workload resource. It manages the full lifecycle: creating pods, scaling, rolling updates, and rollbacks.

The Object Hierarchy: Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pods
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     🏢 Deployment                              │
│          "I want 3 replicas of anihpj:v2"                     │
│                      manages ▼                                 │
│                ┌────────────────────┐                          │
│                │   📋 ReplicaSet    │                          │
│                │  "Maintain 3 pods" │                          │
│                └──┬──────┬──────┬──┘                          │
│                   │      │      │                              │
│              manages  manages  manages                        │
│                   ▼      ▼      ▼                              │
│                ┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐                           │
│                │📦 1│ │📦 2│ │📦 3│  ← anihpj Pods            │
│                └────┘ └────┘ └────┘                           │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  🏢 Deployment  = CEO ("We need 3 workers running anihpj:v2")
  📋 ReplicaSet  = Manager ("I'll maintain exactly 3 pods")
  📦 Pod         = Worker (actually runs the container)
anihpj-deployment.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-deployment
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3                    # ← Run 3 copies of our app
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj               # ← Find pods with this label
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate          # ← Zero-downtime updates
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1               # ← Create 1 extra pod during update
      maxUnavailable: 0          # ← Never have fewer than 3 running
  template:                      # ← Pod template (what each pod looks like)
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj             # ← Must match selector above!
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v2
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "128Mi"
              cpu: "100m"
            limits:
              memory: "256Mi"
              cpu: "500m"

Update Strategies

🔄 RollingUpdate (Default)

Gradually replaces old pods with new ones. At no point are all pods down. Users experience zero downtime.

v1 → mix of v1+v2 → v2

💥 Recreate

Kill ALL old pods first, then create new ones. Causes downtime but guarantees no two versions run simultaneously.

v1 → all down → v2

Rolling Update In Action (3 replicas, v1 → v2)
  Step 1:  🟢v1  🟢v1  🟢v1              ← All running v1
  Step 2:  🟢v1  🟢v1  🟢v1  🔵v2        ← New v2 pod created (maxSurge: 1)
  Step 3:  🟢v1  🟢v1  🔵v2  ✅v2 ready   ← v2 passes health check
  Step 4:  🟢v1  🔵v2  🔵v2              ← Old v1 pod terminated
  Step 5:  🔵v2  🔵v2  🔵v2              ← All running v2 ✅

Essential Deployment Commands

terminal — Managing Deployments 
# Create or update a deployment
kubectl apply -f anihpj-deployment.yaml

# Check rollout status (watch it happen live)
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj-deployment

# View rollout history
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj-deployment

# Undo last deployment (instant rollback!)
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj-deployment

# Scale to 5 replicas
kubectl scale deployment/anihpj-deployment --replicas=5

# Update image (triggers rolling update)
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj-deployment anihpj=anihpj:v3

4.5 Services — Exposing Your Application

a) Why Services Exist

😰
The Problem

Pods get random IP addresses that change every time they restart. You can't hardcode 10.244.1.5 because that pod might be replaced by one at 10.244.2.9 tomorrow.

✅
The Solution: Services

A Service provides a stable IP and DNS name that never changes. It load-balances traffic across all pods matching its selector, regardless of their actual IPs.

b) Service Types

🔒
ClusterIP (Default)

Internal-only access. Other pods can reach it, but external users cannot. Perfect for internal services like databases.

IP: 10.96.x.x (cluster-internal)
Use: Inter-service communication

🔓
NodePort

Exposes the service on a static port (30000-32767) on every node. Good for development and testing.

Access: <NodeIP>:30080
Use: Dev/testing environments

🌐
LoadBalancer

Cloud provider creates an external load balancer with a public IP. The standard for production traffic.

Access: 52.x.x.x (public IP)
Use: Production deployments

📎
ExternalName

Maps a Service to an external DNS name (CNAME). No proxying — just DNS aliasing.

Maps to: mydb.rds.amazonaws.com
Use: External services like RDS

Service Type Traffic Flow Comparison
  ClusterIP:      Internal pod ──▶ ClusterIP:80 ──▶ anihpj pod:8000
                  (inside cluster only)

  NodePort:       Browser ──▶ NodeIP:30080 ──▶ ClusterIP:80 ──▶ pod:8000
                  (accessible from outside via node IP)

  LoadBalancer:   Browser ──▶ PublicIP:80 ──▶ NodePort ──▶ ClusterIP ──▶ pod:8000
                  (cloud LB in front, production-ready)

  ExternalName:   pod ──▶ my-db-svc ──DNS──▶ mydb.rds.amazonaws.com
                  (just a DNS alias, no proxying)
anihpj-service.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-service
spec:
  type: ClusterIP              # ← Internal access (default)
  selector:
    app: anihpj                # ← Route to pods with label app=anihpj
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 80                 # ← Service listens on port 80
      targetPort: 8000         # ← Forward to container port 8000

c) Service Discovery

Kubernetes provides built-in DNS. Every Service gets a DNS name following this pattern:

Service DNS Format
  <service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local

  Example: anihpj-service.default.svc.cluster.local

  Short forms (within same namespace):
    • anihpj-service                          ← Most common
    • anihpj-service.default                  ← Explicit namespace
    • anihpj-service.default.svc.cluster.local ← Fully qualified
💡 Practical Example

In your Django settings.py, instead of hardcoding a PostgreSQL IP, you'd use the Service name:
DATABASES = {'HOST': 'postgres-service'}
Kubernetes DNS resolves this to the PostgreSQL pod's IP, no matter where it runs.

4.6 Namespaces — Organizing Resources

Namespaces are virtual clusters within your physical cluster. They let you divide resources by team, environment, or project:

Cluster Divided into Namespaces
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     KUBERNETES CLUSTER                          │
│                                                                │
│  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐                   │
│  │  📁 default       │  │  📁 kube-system   │                   │
│  │  (your apps)     │  │  (K8s internals) │                   │
│  │  • anihpj pods   │  │  • CoreDNS       │                   │
│  │  • anihpj-svc    │  │  • kube-proxy    │                   │
│  │  • postgres pods │  │  • metrics-server│                   │
│  └──────────────────┘  └──────────────────┘                   │
│                                                                │
│  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐                   │
│  │  📁 staging       │  │  📁 production    │                   │
│  │  (test env)      │  │  (live env)      │                   │
│  │  • anihpj:v2-rc  │  │  • anihpj:v1     │                   │
│  │  • test database │  │  • prod database │                   │
│  └──────────────────┘  └──────────────────┘                   │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
NamespacePurposeCreated By
defaultWhere your objects go if you don't specify a namespaceKubernetes
kube-systemKubernetes internal components (DNS, proxy, metrics)Kubernetes
kube-publicPublicly readable data (cluster info)Kubernetes
kube-node-leaseNode heartbeat leases for health monitoringKubernetes
terminal — Working with Namespaces 
# List all namespaces
kubectl get namespaces

# Create a namespace
kubectl create namespace staging

# Deploy anihpj to the staging namespace
kubectl apply -f anihpj-deployment.yaml -n staging

# List pods in a specific namespace
kubectl get pods -n staging

# List pods in ALL namespaces
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces

# Set default namespace (avoid typing -n every time)
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=staging

# Delete a namespace (⚠️ deletes EVERYTHING inside it!)
kubectl delete namespace staging
⚠️ Namespace Scope

Not all objects are namespaced. Namespaced: Pods, Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, Secrets. Cluster-scoped: Nodes, PersistentVolumes, ClusterRoles, Namespaces themselves. Run kubectl api-resources --namespaced=true to see the full list.

4.7 Labels and Selectors — The Glue

Labels are key-value pairs attached to objects. Selectors are how you query objects by their labels. This is how Services find their pods, and how Deployments manage their ReplicaSets.

Labels in action 
# Pod labels
metadata:
  labels:
    app: anihpj          # Which application
    tier: backend        # Which tier (frontend/backend/database)
    version: v2          # Which version
    env: production      # Which environment

# Service selector — "route traffic to pods with app=anihpj"
spec:
  selector:
    app: anihpj

# Deployment selector — "manage pods with app=anihpj"
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
Labels Connect Everything
 🏢 Deployment                  📡 Service
 selector:                     selector:
   app: anihpj  ──────────┐      app: anihpj  ──────────┐
                          │                             │
                          ▼                             ▼
                    ┌──────────┐                  ┌──────────┐
                    │ 📦 Pod    │                  │ 📦 Pod    │
                    │ labels:  │  ← SAME POD! →   │ labels:  │
                    │  app:    │                  │  app:    │
                    │  anihpj  │                  │  anihpj  │
                    └──────────┘                  └──────────┘

   Deployment manages it ──── AND ──── Service routes traffic to it
📌 Two Types of Selectors
  • Equality-based: app=anihpj, tier!=frontend
  • Set-based: tier in (frontend, backend), env notin (test)
terminal — Querying with Labels 
# Show all pods with their labels
kubectl get pods --show-labels

# Filter pods by label
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj
kubectl get pods -l "tier in (backend,frontend)"
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,version=v2

# Add a label to an existing pod
kubectl label pod anihpj-pod env=production

# Remove a label (note the dash at the end)
kubectl label pod anihpj-pod env-

# Filter ALL resource types by label
kubectl get all -l app=anihpj
💡 Label Best Practices

Use the recommended labels from the Kubernetes docs for consistency across tools:
app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
app.kubernetes.io/version: "2.0"
app.kubernetes.io/component: backend
app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: kubectl

4.8 Annotations — Metadata for Machines

Unlike labels (used for selection), annotations store non-identifying metadata that tools and libraries read. They're not used for querying.

Annotation examples 
metadata:
  annotations:
    # Deployment tracking
    kubernetes.io/change-cause: "Updated jobpost views for pagination"
    
    # Build info
    app.kubernetes.io/version: "2.1.0"
    git.commit/sha: "abc123f"
    
    # Ingress configuration
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
    
    # Contact info
    team: "backend-team"
    oncall: "dev@example.com"
🏷️ Labels

• Used for selection and grouping
• Must be short (63 chars max value)
• Used by Services, Deployments, kubectl
• Example: app: anihpj

📝 Annotations

• Used for metadata only
• Can be large (256 KB total)
• Used by tools, controllers, humans
• Example: git.commit/sha: "abc123"

4.9 Object Relationships — The Big Picture

Here's how all the core objects connect in a real application like anihpj:

Complete Object Relationship Map for anihpj
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     📁 Namespace: default                             │
│                                                                      │
│   🌐 Ingress ──── routes ────▶ 📡 Service ──── selects ────▶ 📦 Pods  │
│   (anihpj.com)               (anihpj-svc)               (app=anihpj)│
│   /jobs/ → anihpj-svc:80     ClusterIP                      │       │
│                                                     ┌────────┘       │
│                                                     │                │
│   📝 ConfigMap ──── env ────▶  Pod reads config     │ managed by     │
│   (django-config)             DJANGO_DEBUG=False     │                │
│                                                     │                │
│   🔐 Secret ────── env ────▶  Pod reads secrets     │                │
│   (db-credentials)            DB_PASSWORD=***       │                │
│                                                     │                │
│   💾 PVC ───────── mount ──▶  Pod mounts volume     │                │
│   (media-storage)             /app/media/           │                │
│                                                ┌────┴─────┐         │
│                                                │ReplicaSet│         │
│                                                ├──────────┤         │
│                                                │Deployment│         │
│                                                └──────────┘         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Summary of Chapter 4

Think of it as a chain: Deployment manages a ReplicaSet, which manages Pods. A Service gives pods a stable address. Namespaces organize everything. Labels are the glue that connects objects. ConfigMaps and Secrets inject configuration. This is the foundation for everything else in this guide.

Quick Reference — All Core Objects at a Glance

ObjectapiVersionPurposeCreated By
Podv1Run one or more containersReplicaSet (usually)
ReplicaSetapps/v1Maintain desired pod countDeployment
Deploymentapps/v1Manage rolling updates & rollbacksYou (kubectl apply)
Servicev1Stable networking & load balancingYou (kubectl apply)
Namespacev1Virtual cluster isolationYou or Kubernetes
ConfigMapv1Non-sensitive configurationYou (kubectl apply)
Secretv1Sensitive data (passwords, keys)You (kubectl apply)
Ingressnetworking.k8s.io/v1HTTP/HTTPS routing & TLSYou (kubectl apply)

🛠️ Part 2: Hands-On Chapters 5-8

Chapter 5: Setting Up Your Kubernetes Environment

Time to get your hands dirty. In this chapter you'll install a local Kubernetes cluster, learn to talk to it with kubectl, and run your very first pod — all on your own machine before touching any cloud infrastructure.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~1 hour 🛠️ Hands-On 💻 Local Setup

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Compare 5 local Kubernetes development tools and pick the right one
  • ✅ Install Minikube on Windows, macOS, or Linux
  • ✅ Install and configure kubectl — the K8s command-line tool
  • ✅ Start your first cluster and verify it's running
  • ✅ Deploy and access your first pod (nginx hello-world)
  • ✅ Manage cluster lifecycle (start, stop, pause, delete)
  • ✅ Work with multiple clusters using contexts

5.1 Local Development Options

You don't need a cloud account to learn Kubernetes. Several tools let you run a full cluster right on your laptop. Here's how they compare:

⭐ Minikube

Recommended for beginners

🟢 Beginner
💾 RAM: 2-4 GB
🖥️ Win / Mac / Linux

Full K8s in a VM/container. Addons, dashboard, and multi-node support.

🐳 Docker Desktop

1-Click Enable

🟢 Easiest
💾 RAM: 4 GB
🖥️ Win / Mac

Toggle in Docker settings. Limited config compared to Minikube.

📦 kind

K8s in Docker

🟡 Lightweight
💾 RAM: 1 GB
🖥️ Win / Mac / Linux

Uses containers as nodes. Popular for CI/CD testing.

🔧 k3s

Rancher Lightweight

🟡 Edge / IoT
💾 RAM: 512 MB
🖥️ Linux only

Single binary, production-ready. Great for resource-constrained environments.

🔮 MicroK8s

Canonical Snap

🟡 Linux Focus
💾 RAM: 1 GB
🖥️ Linux (snap)

Snap install, built-in add-ons. Good for Ubuntu users.

Feature ⭐ Minikube Docker Desktop kind k3s MicroK8s
Difficulty🟢 Easy🟢 Easiest🟡 Medium🟡 Medium🟡 Medium
Multi-node✅ Yes❌ No✅ Yes✅ Yes✅ Yes
Dashboard✅ Built-in❌ No❌ No❌ No✅ Add-on
Addons✅ 30+❌ Limited❌ None✅ Helm✅ 20+
CI/CD Use🟡 OK❌ No✅ Best🟡 OK🟡 OK
Production❌ Dev only❌ Dev only❌ Testing✅ Yes✅ Yes
💡 Our Recommendation

For this guide, we use Minikube. It gives you a complete Kubernetes cluster with built-in dashboard, add-ons, and multi-node support — the closest experience to a real production cluster without any cloud spending.

5.2 Installing Minikube

Minikube creates a single-node (or multi-node) Kubernetes cluster on your machine. It can use Docker, VirtualBox, Hyper-V, or other drivers as its backend.

5.2a Prerequisites

🔧 Hardware Requirements
  • 2+ CPUs
  • 2 GB+ free RAM (4 GB recommended)
  • 20 GB+ free disk space
  • Internet connection (for image pulls)
📦 Software Requirements
  • A container/VM runtime (pick one):
  • 🐳 Docker (recommended)
  • 📦 VirtualBox
  • 🖥️ Hyper-V (Windows Pro/Enterprise)

5.2b Windows Installation

PowerShell (Administrator) 
# Option 1: Using Chocolatey
choco install minikube

# Option 2: Using Scoop
scoop install minikube

# Option 3: Direct download from GitHub
# Download the latest .exe from:
# https://github.com/kubernetes/minikube/releases/latest
# Move minikube.exe to a folder in your PATH

# Verify installation
minikube version
# output: minikube version: v1.34.0

5.2c macOS Installation

Terminal 
# Option 1: Using Homebrew (recommended)
brew install minikube

# Option 2: Direct download (Apple Silicon / M1+)
curl -LO https://storage.googleapis.com/minikube/releases/latest/minikube-darwin-arm64
sudo install minikube-darwin-arm64 /usr/local/bin/minikube

# Option 2b: Direct download (Intel)
curl -LO https://storage.googleapis.com/minikube/releases/latest/minikube-darwin-amd64
sudo install minikube-darwin-amd64 /usr/local/bin/minikube

# Verify
minikube version

5.2d Linux Installation

Terminal 
# Option 1: Direct download (x86_64)
curl -LO https://storage.googleapis.com/minikube/releases/latest/minikube-linux-amd64
sudo install minikube-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/minikube

# Option 2: Using apt (Debian/Ubuntu)
curl -LO https://storage.googleapis.com/minikube/releases/latest/minikube_latest_amd64.deb
sudo dpkg -i minikube_latest_amd64.deb

# Option 3: Using rpm (Fedora/RHEL)
curl -LO https://storage.googleapis.com/minikube/releases/latest/minikube-latest.x86_64.rpm
sudo rpm -Uvh minikube-latest.x86_64.rpm

# Verify
minikube version

5.2e Verifying Installation

Terminal — Expected Output 
$ minikube version
minikube version: v1.34.0
commit: 210b148df93a80eb872ecbeb7e35281b3c582c61

$ which minikube        # macOS/Linux
$ where minikube        # Windows
C:\ProgramData\chocolatey\bin\minikube.exe
📝 Verification Checklist

If minikube version prints a version number, you're good! If you get "command not found", make sure the install directory is in your system PATH environment variable.

5.3 Installing kubectl

kubectl (pronounced "kube-control" or "kube-C-T-L") is the command-line tool you use to communicate with any Kubernetes cluster — local or cloud.

How kubectl Connects to a Cluster

  ┌──────────────┐         ┌───────────────┐         ┌──────────────────┐
  │  👨‍💻 You       │  ──▶   │  kubectl CLI  │  ──▶   │  API Server      │
  │  (terminal)  │  HTTP   │  reads        │  REST   │  (control plane) │
  │              │         │  ~/.kube/     │  API    │                  │
  └──────────────┘         │  config       │         │  ┌────────────┐  │
                            └───────────────┘         │  │ etcd       │  │
                                                      │  │ scheduler  │  │
                                                      │  │ controller │  │
                                                      │  └────────────┘  │
                                                      └──────────────────┘
Installing kubectl — All Platforms 
# ── Windows ──
choco install kubernetes-cli          # Chocolatey
scoop install kubectl                 # Scoop

# ── macOS ──
brew install kubectl                  # Homebrew

# ── Linux (Debian/Ubuntu) ──
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y kubectl

# ── Verify (all platforms) ──
kubectl version --client
# Client Version: v1.31.0
💡 kubectl Is Separate From Your Cluster

kubectl doesn't run Kubernetes — it's just the CLI tool that talks to a cluster. You can use the same kubectl binary to connect to Minikube, EKS, GKE, AKS, or any cluster. It reads ~/.kube/config to know which cluster to target.

Shell Completion (optional but very useful) 
# Bash
echo 'source <(kubectl completion bash)' >> ~/.bashrc

# Zsh
echo 'source <(kubectl completion zsh)' >> ~/.zshrc

# PowerShell
kubectl completion powershell | Out-String | Invoke-Expression

5.4 Starting Your First Cluster

With Minikube and kubectl installed, let's spin up your first Kubernetes cluster:

OptionDefaultDescriptionExample
--driverdockerVM/container runtimeminikube start --driver=hyperv
--cpus2Number of CPUsminikube start --cpus=4
--memory2200mbRAM allocationminikube start --memory=4096
--disk-size20000mbDisk spaceminikube start --disk-size=40g
--kubernetes-versionlatest stableK8s version to installminikube start --kubernetes-version=v1.30.0
--nodes1Multi-node clusterminikube start --nodes=3
-pminikubeProfile/cluster nameminikube start -p dev-cluster
Starting your cluster 
# Start a cluster (uses Docker driver by default)
$ minikube start
😄  minikube v1.34.0 on Darwin 14.0 (arm64)
✨  Using the docker driver based on existing profile
👍  Starting "minikube" primary control-plane node
🐳  Preparing Kubernetes v1.31.0 on Docker 27.2.0 ...
🔎  Verifying Kubernetes components...
🌟  Enabled addons: storage-provisioner, default-storageclass
🏄  Done! kubectl is now configured to use "minikube" cluster

# Check cluster status
$ minikube status
minikube
type: Control Plane
host: Running
kubelet: Running
apiserver: Running
kubeconfig: Configured

# Cluster details
$ kubectl cluster-info
Kubernetes control plane is running at https://127.0.0.1:55000
CoreDNS is running at https://127.0.0.1:55000/api/v1/namespaces/kube-system/services/kube-dns:dns/proxy

# See your node
$ kubectl get nodes
NAME       STATUS   ROLES           AGE   VERSION
minikube   Ready    control-plane   45s   v1.31.0

What Just Happened

  minikube start
       │
       ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  1. Creates a VM or Docker container         │
  │  2. Installs Kubernetes inside it            │
  │  3. Starts the control plane components:     │
  │     • API Server    • etcd                   │
  │     • Scheduler     • Controller Manager     │
  │  4. Configures kubectl (~/.kube/config)       │
  │  5. Enables default add-ons                  │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │
       ▼
  ✅ You now have a running Kubernetes cluster!
Open the Dashboard (Web UI) 
# Open Kubernetes Dashboard in your browser
$ minikube dashboard
🔌  Enabling dashboard ...
🤔  Verifying dashboard health ...
🚀  Launching proxy ...
🤔  Verifying proxy health ...
🎉  Opening http://127.0.0.1:57683/api/v1/namespaces/kubernetes-dashboard/...
📝 The Dashboard

The Kubernetes Dashboard is a web-based UI that shows all running workloads, services, and cluster health. It's a great way to visually verify what's happening while you learn the CLI commands.

5.5 Your First Pod — Hello World

Before we deploy anihpj, let's run a quick hello-world to prove your cluster works:

Running your first pod 
# Create a single pod running nginx
$ kubectl run my-first-pod --image=nginx
pod/my-first-pod created

# Watch it come to life
$ kubectl get pods -w
NAME           READY   STATUS              RESTARTS   AGE
my-first-pod   0/1     ContainerCreating   0          3s
my-first-pod   1/1     Running             0          8s

# Forward port so you can access it from your browser
$ kubectl port-forward my-first-pod 8080:80
Forwarding from 127.0.0.1:8080 -> 80

Now open http://localhost:8080 in your browser — you'll see the nginx welcome page!

🎉 Congratulations!

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                               │
  │   You just ran your first Kubernetes Pod!     │
  │                                               │
  │   kubectl run  ──▶  Pod Created  ──▶  🌐      │
  │                                               │
  │   nginx is running inside a container,        │
  │   inside a pod, on your Minikube cluster.     │
  │                                               │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Exploring your pod 
# Detailed info about the pod
$ kubectl describe pod my-first-pod

# View pod logs
$ kubectl logs my-first-pod

# Shell into the running container
$ kubectl exec -it my-first-pod -- /bin/bash
root@my-first-pod:/# curl localhost
# outputs the nginx HTML

# Clean up when done
$ kubectl delete pod my-first-pod
pod "my-first-pod" deleted

5.6 Cluster Lifecycle Commands

Minikube gives you full control over your local cluster. Here are the commands you'll use daily:

Command What It Does When to Use
minikube startCreates or starts the clusterBeginning of your work session
minikube stopStops the cluster (preserves state)End of your work session
minikube pausePauses K8s processes (saves CPU)Taking a break but keeping state
minikube unpauseResumes paused clusterReturning from a break
minikube deleteDestroys the cluster entirelyStarting fresh or cleaning up
minikube statusShows cluster healthChecking if cluster is running
minikube dashboardOpens web dashboardVisual cluster inspection
minikube addons listShows available add-onsEnabling extras (metrics, ingress)
💡 Daily Workflow

Start your day with minikube start, do your work, then minikube stop when done. Your deployments, services, and config are preserved between stop/start cycles. Only minikube delete destroys everything.

AddonWhat It DoesEnable Command
dashboardWeb-based cluster management UIminikube addons enable dashboard
metrics-serverCPU/memory metrics for kubectl topminikube addons enable metrics-server
ingressNGINX Ingress Controller for HTTP routingminikube addons enable ingress
storage-provisionerDynamic PersistentVolume provisioningminikube addons enable storage-provisioner
registryPrivate container image registryminikube addons enable registry

5.7 Multiple Clusters and Contexts

As you advance, you'll work with multiple clusters (dev, staging, production). Kubernetes uses a kubeconfig file to manage connections.

The kubeconfig File (~/.kube/config)

  ~/.kube/config
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
