  │  clusters:           ◄── Where to connect            │
  │    - minikube  →  https://127.0.0.1:55000            │
  │    - prod-eks  →  https://eks.amazonaws.com/...      │
  │    - dev-gke   →  https://gke.googleapis.com/...     │
  │                                                      │
  │  users:              ◄── How to authenticate          │
  │    - minikube-user   (client certificate)            │
  │    - aws-admin       (token)                         │
  │    - gke-dev         (oauth)                         │
  │                                                      │
  │  contexts:           ◄── cluster + user combos        │
  │    - minikube  =  minikube cluster + minikube-user   │
  │    - prod      =  prod-eks cluster + aws-admin       │
  │    - dev       =  dev-gke cluster  + gke-dev         │
  │                                                      │
  │  current-context: minikube  ◄── active right now     │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Managing Contexts 
# List all contexts
$ kubectl config get-contexts
CURRENT   NAME       CLUSTER    AUTHINFO        NAMESPACE
*         minikube   minikube   minikube-user   default

# See current context
$ kubectl config current-context
minikube

# Switch to a different context
$ kubectl config use-context prod
Switched to context "prod".

# Set default namespace for a context
$ kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=anihpj-app
⚠️ Always Check Your Context Before Running Commands

The most common Kubernetes mistake is running commands against the wrong cluster. Before running any destructive command (delete, scale to 0, etc.), always verify: kubectl config current-context. Some teams protect production by requiring explicit --context=prod flags.

Chapter 6: kubectl — Your Command Center

kubectl is the single most important tool in your Kubernetes toolkit. Every operation — creating pods, scaling deployments, debugging failures — goes through this CLI. Master it, and you master Kubernetes.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~2 hours 🛠️ Hands-On ⌨️ CLI Mastery

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ The kubectl command syntax and how every command is structured
  • ✅ Imperative vs Declarative — two approaches and when to use each
  • ✅ 30+ essential commands organized by category
  • ✅ Output formats (-o wide, -o yaml, -o json, jsonpath)
  • ✅ Resource abbreviations to speed up your workflow
  • ✅ Filtering with labels, fields, and namespaces
  • ✅ Practice exercises using the anihpj project

6.1 kubectl Syntax Structure

Every kubectl command follows the same pattern. Once you learn this structure, you can construct any command from memory:

Command Anatomy

  kubectl       get        pods      anihpj-pod     -n prod
  ──┬──        ─┬─        ─┬──      ───┬─────     ──┬───
    │           │          │          │             │
  🔵 CLI     🟢 VERB    🟡 TYPE    🟠 NAME      🔴 FLAGS
  (tool)    (action)   (resource)  (which one)   (options)
🟢 Common Verbs (Actions)
  • get — list resources
  • describe — detailed info
  • create — create new
  • apply — create or update
  • delete — remove
  • edit — edit live
  • logs — container logs
  • exec — run command in pod
🟡 Common Types (Resources)
  • pods (po) — running containers
  • services (svc) — networking
  • deployments (deploy) — workloads
  • namespaces (ns) — isolation
  • configmaps (cm) — config
  • secrets — sensitive data
  • nodes (no) — cluster machines
  • ingress (ing) — HTTP routing
Pattern in Action — Examples 
# kubectl  VERB     TYPE         NAME            FLAGS
kubectl    get      pods                                      # all pods
kubectl    get      pod          anihpj-pod                   # specific pod
kubectl    get      pods                         -n production # in namespace
kubectl    get      pods                         -o wide       # extra columns
kubectl    describe deployment   anihpj-deploy                # full details
kubectl    delete   service      anihpj-svc                   # remove service
kubectl    logs                  anihpj-pod-abc                # view logs
kubectl    exec     -it          anihpj-pod-abc  -- /bin/bash  # shell in

6.2 Imperative vs Declarative

There are two fundamental ways to work with Kubernetes. Understanding the difference is crucial:

🗣️ Imperative — "Do This Now"

You tell Kubernetes exactly what action to take, step by step.

# Create a pod directly
kubectl run anihpj --image=anihpj:v1

# Scale a deployment
kubectl scale deploy anihpj --replicas=5

# Expose a service
kubectl expose deploy anihpj --port=8000

✅ Pros: Quick, great for learning & debugging

❌ Cons: No version control, not reproducible, hard to track

📄 Declarative — "Make It Like This" ⭐

You describe the desired state in a YAML file, and K8s figures out how to get there.

# Apply from a file
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

# Apply an entire directory
kubectl apply -f k8s/

# Update (just change YAML and re-apply)
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

✅ Pros: Version controlled, reproducible, team-friendly

❌ Cons: More setup, need to learn YAML

💡 Rule of Thumb

Use imperative commands for quick tests and debugging. Use declarative YAML files for anything that goes to production. In this guide we'll learn both, but all our anihpj deployment files will be declarative.

6.3 Essential kubectl Commands — Categorized

Here are the commands you'll use 90% of the time, organized by what you're trying to do:

6.3a 🔍 Viewing / Inspecting

CommandWhat It DoesExample
kubectl get <resource>List resourceskubectl get pods
kubectl get allList pods, services, deployments, etc.kubectl get all -n anihpj-app
kubectl describe <resource> <name>Detailed info with eventskubectl describe pod anihpj-abc
kubectl logs <pod>View container outputkubectl logs anihpj-abc -f
kubectl logs <pod> --previousLogs from crashed containerkubectl logs anihpj-abc --previous
kubectl top podsCPU/memory usagekubectl top pods -n anihpj-app
kubectl get eventsCluster event logkubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp

6.3b ➕ Creating / Applying

CommandWhat It DoesExample
kubectl create -f <file>Create from YAML (fails if exists)kubectl create -f pod.yaml
kubectl apply -f <file>Create or update from YAMLkubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
kubectl apply -f <dir>/Apply all files in directorykubectl apply -f k8s/
kubectl run <name> --image=<img>Quick pod (imperative)kubectl run nginx --image=nginx
kubectl create deploy <name> --image=<img>Quick deployment (imperative)kubectl create deploy anihpj --image=anihpj:v1
📝 create vs apply

create fails if the resource already exists. apply creates if missing, updates if it exists. For declarative workflows, always use apply.

kubectl apply Lifecycle

  kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
           │
           ▼
  ┌──────────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐
  │ Resource exists? │────▶│ YES → Update  │
  └──────────────────┘     │ (patch diff)  │
           │               └───────────────┘
           ▼
  ┌──────────────────┐
  │ NO → Create      │
  │ (new resource)   │
  └──────────────────┘

6.3c ✏️ Modifying

CommandWhat It DoesExample
kubectl edit <resource> <name>Edit resource live in your editorkubectl edit deploy anihpj
kubectl scale deploy <name> --replicas=NChange replica countkubectl scale deploy anihpj --replicas=5
kubectl set image deploy/<n> <c>=<img>Update container imagekubectl set image deploy/anihpj anihpj=anihpj:v2
kubectl label <resource> <name> key=valAdd or update a labelkubectl label pod anihpj-abc env=prod
kubectl annotate <resource> <name> k=vAdd an annotationkubectl annotate pod anihpj-abc note="testing"
kubectl patch <resource> <name> -p '...'Partial update via JSON patchkubectl patch deploy anihpj -p '{"spec":{"replicas":3}}'

6.3d ❌ Deleting

CommandWhat It DoesExample
kubectl delete <resource> <name>Delete specific resourcekubectl delete pod anihpj-abc
kubectl delete -f <file>Delete everything defined in filekubectl delete -f deployment.yaml
kubectl delete pods --allDelete all pods in namespacekubectl delete pods --all -n anihpj-app
kubectl delete ns <namespace>Delete namespace (and everything in it!)kubectl delete ns anihpj-app
🚨 Danger Zone

kubectl delete ns <name> deletes the namespace and every resource inside it — pods, services, deployments, secrets, everything. There is no undo. Always double-check with kubectl config current-context first.

6.3e 🔧 Debugging

CommandWhat It DoesExample
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/bashShell into a running containerkubectl exec -it anihpj-abc -- /bin/bash
kubectl exec <pod> -- <cmd>Run a single commandkubectl exec anihpj-abc -- python manage.py check
kubectl port-forward <pod> local:containerForward port to localhostkubectl port-forward anihpj-abc 8000:8000
kubectl cp <pod>:/path ./localCopy files from podkubectl cp anihpj-abc:/app/db.sqlite3 ./backup.db
kubectl attach <pod>Attach to a running processkubectl attach anihpj-abc
kubectl debug <pod>Create debug containerkubectl debug anihpj-abc -it --image=busybox

Debugging Decision Tree

  Pod not working?
       │
       ├── Not starting? ──▶ kubectl describe pod <name>
       │                      Look at "Events" section
       │
       ├── Crashing? ──────▶ kubectl logs <pod> --previous
       │                      See crash output
       │
       ├── Running but       kubectl logs <pod> -f
       │   wrong output? ──▶ Stream live logs
       │
       ├── Need to inspect   kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/bash
       │   inside? ────────▶ Explore filesystem, test commands
       │
       └── Network issue? ──▶ kubectl port-forward <pod> 8000:8000
                              Test directly, bypass Service

6.3f 📊 Cluster Admin

CommandWhat It DoesExample
kubectl cluster-infoAPI server & CoreDNS endpointskubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodesList all cluster nodeskubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl get eventsRecent cluster eventskubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp
kubectl api-resourcesAll available resource typeskubectl api-resources --namespaced=true
kubectl api-versionsAll API versions availablekubectl api-versions
kubectl explain <resource>Built-in docs for any resourcekubectl explain pod.spec.containers
💡 kubectl explain Is Your Best Friend

kubectl explain is a built-in reference. Use it to discover every field of any resource without leaving the terminal:
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources

6.4 kubectl Output Formats

By default, kubectl shows a simple table. But you can extract much more detail:

FlagOutputWhen to Use
(default)Simple tableQuick overview
-o wideTable + extra columns (node, IP)See which node a pod runs on
-o yamlFull YAML definitionExport or inspect full resource
-o jsonFull JSON definitionPipe to jq for parsing
-o nameJust resource namesScripting & piping
-o jsonpath='{...}'Custom field extractionExtract specific values
-o custom-columns=...Custom table columnsBuild your own table view
Same Pod — Different Formats 
# Default — simple table
$ kubectl get pods
NAME           READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
anihpj-abc     1/1     Running   0          5m

# Wide — extra columns
$ kubectl get pods -o wide
NAME          READY  STATUS   RESTARTS  AGE  IP          NODE      
anihpj-abc    1/1    Running  0         5m   172.17.0.4  minikube  

# YAML — full definition (great for exporting)
$ kubectl get pod anihpj-abc -o yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-abc
  namespace: default
...

# JSONPath — extract specific fields
$ kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'
anihpj-abc anihpj-def anihpj-ghi

# Custom columns
$ kubectl get pods -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,STATUS:.status.phase,IP:.status.podIP
NAME          STATUS    IP
anihpj-abc    Running   172.17.0.4
JSONPath PatternWhat It ExtractsUse Case
{.metadata.name}Resource nameScripting
{.items[*].metadata.name}All resource names in a listBatch operations
{.status.podIP}Pod's internal IPDebugging networking
{.spec.containers[0].image}First container's imageVersion checks
{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\n"}{end}One name per lineClean output for piping

6.5 Shortcuts and Resource Abbreviations

Kubernetes lets you use short names for all resource types. These will save you thousands of keystrokes:

Full NameShortExample
podspokubectl get po
servicessvckubectl get svc
deploymentsdeploykubectl get deploy
replicasetsrskubectl get rs
namespacesnskubectl get ns
configmapscmkubectl get cm
persistentvolumeclaimspvckubectl get pvc
nodesnokubectl get no
statefulsetsstskubectl get sts
daemonsetsdskubectl get ds
ingressesingkubectl get ing
serviceaccountssakubectl get sa
persistentvolumespvkubectl get pv
cronjobscjkubectl get cj
💡 Pro Tip: Aliases

Power users go further with shell aliases. Add these to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:

alias k='kubectl'
alias kgp='kubectl get pods'
alias kgs='kubectl get svc'
alias kgd='kubectl get deploy'
alias kaf='kubectl apply -f'
alias kdp='kubectl describe pod'
🏷️ Labels

Used to identify and select resources.

  • Queryable with -l selectors
  • Used by Services, Deployments, ReplicaSets
  • Max 63 characters
  • Example: app: anihpj, env: prod
📝 Annotations

Used to attach metadata for tools/humans.

  • NOT queryable via selectors
  • Used by Ingress controllers, monitoring, CI/CD
  • Can store large values (256 KB)
  • Example: description: "Job posting API"

6.6 Filtering and Querying

In a cluster with hundreds of resources, filtering is essential:

Filtering Techniques 
# ── By Label ──
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj                   # exact match
kubectl get pods -l 'app in (anihpj,frontend)'   # set-based
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,env=prod          # multiple labels (AND)
kubectl get pods -l '!canary'                    # doesn't have label

# ── By Field ──
kubectl get pods --field-selector status.phase=Running
kubectl get pods --field-selector spec.nodeName=minikube

# ── By Namespace ──
kubectl get pods -n kube-system                  # specific namespace
kubectl get pods -A                              # ALL namespaces
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces                # same as -A

# ── Watch Mode (live updates) ──
kubectl get pods -w                              # streams changes live

# ── Sorting ──
kubectl get pods --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
kubectl get pods --sort-by=.status.phase

# ── Combining ──
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj -n production -o wide --sort-by=.status.startTime

Filtering Flow

  All Resources in Cluster
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  pod/a  pod/b  pod/c  pod/d  pod/e   │
  │  svc/x  svc/y  deploy/z  cm/config   │
  └────────────────┬──────────────────────┘
                   │
     ┌─────────────┼─────────────┐
     ▼             ▼             ▼
  -n namespace  -l label    --field-selector
  (which room)  (which tag)  (which state)
     │             │             │
     └─────────────┼─────────────┘
                   ▼
          Filtered Results ──▶ -o format ──▶ Display

6.7 Practice Exercises with anihpj

Try these exercises to solidify your kubectl skills. Each one uses a real command you'll need when deploying anihpj:

Exercise 1

List all running pods and sort by age

Show Solution kubectl get pods --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
Exercise 2

Get the complete YAML of a running pod

Show Solution kubectl get pod anihpj-abc -o yaml
Exercise 3

Execute a Django shell inside the anihpj pod

Show Solution kubectl exec -it anihpj-abc -- python manage.py shell
Exercise 4

View the last 50 lines of logs from anihpj

Show Solution kubectl logs anihpj-abc --tail=50
Exercise 5

Forward port 8000 and access the jobpost API

Show Solution kubectl port-forward anihpj-abc 8000:8000
Then visit: http://localhost:8000/jobs/
Exercise 6

Find all pods with label app=anihpj across all namespaces

Show Solution kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj -A
Exercise 7

List all available resource types in your cluster

Show Solution kubectl api-resources
Exercise 8

Get only the names of all pods in JSON path

Show Solution kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'
Exercise 9

Scale the anihpj deployment to 5 replicas

Show Solution kubectl scale deploy anihpj --replicas=5
Exercise 10

Copy the SQLite database from a pod to your local machine

Show Solution kubectl cp anihpj-abc:/app/db.sqlite3 ./backup.db

Chapter 7: YAML Configuration Files — The Language of Kubernetes

Every Kubernetes resource is defined in YAML. This chapter teaches you the syntax, the four required fields, and how to write complete manifests for every component of the anihpj project — pods, deployments, services, configmaps, and secrets.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~2 hours 📄 Configuration 📝 Declarative

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why Kubernetes chose YAML over JSON and XML
  • ✅ YAML syntax fundamentals — keys, lists, indentation, multi-line strings
  • ✅ The 4 required fields in every Kubernetes manifest
  • ✅ Common apiVersion values for each resource type
  • ✅ Write complete YAML files for anihpj (Pod, Deployment, Service, ConfigMap, Secret)
  • ✅ Multi-document YAML and validation best practices

7.1 Why YAML? (And Not JSON or XML?)

Kubernetes accepts both YAML and JSON, but the community overwhelmingly uses YAML. Here's why:

✅ YAML (5 lines)
name: anihpj
replicas: 3
image: anihpj:v1
port: 8000
debug: false

Clean & readable

🟡 JSON (10 lines)
{
  "name": "anihpj",
  "replicas": 3,
  "image": "anihpj:v1",
  "port": 8000,
  "debug": false
}

More brackets & quotes

❌ XML (12 lines)
<config>
  <name>anihpj</name>
  <replicas>3</replicas>
  <image>anihpj:v1</image>
  <port>8000</port>
  <debug>false</debug>
</config>

Very verbose

⭐ YAML Wins — 60% Fewer Characters, 100% More Readable

📄
Human-writable
💬
Supports comments
📏
Less syntax noise
🔀
Git-diff friendly

7.2 YAML Syntax Crash Course

Everything you need to know about YAML syntax in one section. Master these rules and you'll never have a YAML error again:

YAML Fundamentals 
# ── 1. Key-Value Pairs ──
name: anihpj
replicas: 3
debug: false

# ── 2. Nested Objects (indent 2 spaces) ──
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    env: production

# ── 3. Lists / Arrays (- prefix) ──
ports:
  - containerPort: 8000
  - containerPort: 8080
  
containers:
  - name: web
    image: anihpj:v1
  - name: sidecar
    image: nginx

# ── 4. Multi-line Strings ──
description: |          # literal block — preserves newlines
  This is line one.
  This is line two.
  Each line stays separate.

summary: >             # folded block — joins lines
  This is a long sentence
  that spans multiple lines
  but becomes a single paragraph.

# ── 5. Comments ──
# This is a comment — ignored by K8s

# ── 6. Data Types ──
enabled: true           # boolean
emptyValue: null        # null
replicas: 3             # integer
ratio: 0.75             # float
name: "anihpj"          # string (quotes optional unless ambiguous)
port: "8080"            # string (forced with quotes)

📋 YAML Quick Reference Card

  ┌─────────────────┬──────────────────┬────────────────────┐
  │  Key: Value      │  Lists            │  Indentation        │
  │  name: bob      │  - apple         │  2 spaces ONLY     │
  │  age: 30        │  - banana        │  (never tabs!)     │
  │                 │  - cherry        │                    │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────┤
  │  Boolean         │  Null             │  Comments           │
  │  enabled: true  │  value: null     │  # this is ignored │
  │  debug: false   │  value: ~        │                    │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────┤
  │  Multi-line      │  Multi-doc        │  AVOID              │
  │  text: |        │  --- (separator) │  ❌ tabs           │
  │    preserves    │  ... (end)       │  ❌ : in values    │
  │    newlines     │                  │  ❌ unquoted yes   │
  └─────────────────┴──────────────────┴────────────────────┘
🚨 The #1 YAML Mistake

Using tabs instead of spaces. YAML strictly requires spaces for indentation. Configure your editor to use 2 spaces for YAML files. In VS Code: add "[yaml]": { "editor.tabSize": 2, "editor.insertSpaces": true } to your settings.

❌ Common YAML Errors
# Wrong: colon missing space
name:anihpj

# Wrong: inconsistent indentation
metadata:
  name: pod
   labels:         # 3 spaces instead of 2!
    app: anihpj

# Wrong: tab character (invisible!)
spec:
→ containers:      # tab instead of spaces
✅ Correct YAML
# Right: space after colon
name: anihpj

# Right: consistent 2-space indent
metadata:
  name: pod
  labels:          # 2 spaces consistently
    app: anihpj

# Right: spaces only
spec:
  containers:      # 2 spaces, always

7.3 The Four Required Fields

Every Kubernetes YAML manifest has exactly four top-level fields. Know these, and you can write any K8s resource:

The 4 Required Fields — Annotated

  apiVersion: v1              ◄── ① Which API group & version?
                                    (v1, apps/v1, batch/v1, etc.)

  kind: Pod                   ◄── ② What type of object?
                                    (Pod, Deployment, Service, etc.)

  metadata:                   ◄── ③ Who is it? (identity)
    name: anihpj-pod              name, namespace, labels, annotations
    labels:
      app: anihpj

  spec:                       ◄── ④ What do you want? (desired state)
    containers:                   The actual configuration — differs
      - name: anihpj               per resource type
        image: anihpj:v1
        ports:
          - containerPort: 8000
① apiVersion

Tells K8s which API endpoint to use. Different resources live in different API groups.

② kind

The type of object you're creating. Must match the apiVersion.

③ metadata

Identity: name, namespace, labels, annotations. How K8s finds this resource.

④ spec

Your desired state. This is where the real config goes — differs for each kind.

7.4 Common apiVersion Values

Each resource type belongs to a specific API group. Here's the mapping you'll use most often:

Kind apiVersion API Group Notes
Podv1coreCore API — no group prefix
Servicev1coreCore API
ConfigMapv1coreCore API
Secretv1coreCore API
Namespacev1coreCore API
PersistentVolumev1coreCore API
PersistentVolumeClaimv1coreCore API
Deploymentapps/v1appsMost common workload
ReplicaSetapps/v1appsUsually managed by Deployment
StatefulSetapps/v1appsFor stateful apps (databases)
DaemonSetapps/v1appsOne per node
Jobbatch/v1batchRun-to-completion
CronJobbatch/v1batchScheduled jobs
Ingressnetworking.k8s.io/v1networkingHTTP/HTTPS routing
NetworkPolicynetworking.k8s.io/v1networkingFirewall rules
💡 How to Find the Right apiVersion

Can't remember? Use: kubectl api-resources | grep Deployment — it shows the API group. Or use kubectl explain deployment — the first line shows the version.

7.5 Creating YAML for anihpj — Complete Examples

Let's write real, production-ready YAML files for every component of our Django application:

7.5a Pod YAML — anihpj-pod.yaml

anihpj-pod.yaml 
apiVersion: v1                    # Core API
kind: Pod                          # Resource type
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod                 # Pod name (must be unique in namespace)
  labels:
    app: anihpj                    # Label for selection
    component: web                 # What this pod does
    version: v1                    # Track version
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj                 # Container name
      image: anihpj:v1             # Docker image
      ports:
        - containerPort: 8000      # Django default port
          protocol: TCP
      env:
        - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
          value: "anihpj.settings"
        - name: DEBUG
          value: "False"
      resources:                   # Resource limits
        requests:
          memory: "128Mi"
          cpu: "250m"              # 0.25 CPU core
        limits:
          memory: "256Mi"
          cpu: "500m"              # 0.5 CPU core
      readinessProbe:              # Is the app ready for traffic?
        httpGet:
          path: /jobs/
          port: 8000
        initialDelaySeconds: 10
        periodSeconds: 5
⚠️ Pods Alone Are Not Enough

A bare Pod has no self-healing — if it crashes, it stays dead. That's why we wrap pods in a Deployment which ensures the desired number of replicas are always running. Use bare pods only for quick tests.

Pod vs Deployment vs ReplicaSet Hierarchy

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Deployment  (you write this)                        │
  │  ├── manages rolling updates                        │
  │  ├── stores revision history                        │
  │  └── creates ↓                                      │
  │     ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │     │  ReplicaSet  (auto-created — don't edit!)    │   │
  │     │  ├── ensures N pods are always running     │   │
  │     │  └── creates ↓                             │   │
  │     │     ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐│   │
  │     │     │ Pod #1   │ │ Pod #2   │ │ Pod #3   ││   │
  │     │     │ anihpj:v1│ │ anihpj:v1│ │ anihpj:v1││   │
  │     │     └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘│   │
  │     └───────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

7.5b Deployment YAML — anihpj-deployment.yaml

anihpj-deployment.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1               # apps API group
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-deployment
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3                      # Run 3 instances
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj                  # Must match template labels
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 1            # At most 1 pod down during update
      maxSurge: 1                  # At most 1 extra pod during update
  template:                        # Pod template (creates the pods)
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj                # Must match selector above
        component: web
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v1
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: anihpj-config   # Load all ConfigMap keys as env vars
            - secretRef:
                name: anihpj-secret   # Load all Secret keys as env vars
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "128Mi"
              cpu: "250m"
            limits:
              memory: "256Mi"
              cpu: "500m"
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /jobs/
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 15
            periodSeconds: 10
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /jobs/
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 15

7.5c Service YAML — anihpj-service.yaml

anihpj-service.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  type: NodePort                   # Accessible from outside the cluster
  selector:
    app: anihpj                    # Targets pods with this label
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 80                     # Service port (what clients use)
      targetPort: 8000             # Container port (Django)
      nodePort: 30080              # External port on node (30000-32767)

Traffic Flow Through This Service

  Browser → :30080 (nodePort) → :80 (service port) → :8000 (container)

  ┌──────────┐     ┌────────────────┐     ┌──────────────────┐
  │  User    │────▶│  Service       │────▶│  Pod (anihpj)    │
  │  :30080  │     │  anihpj-svc    │     │  container :8000 │
  └──────────┘     │  selector:     │     └──────────────────┘
                   │    app=anihpj  │────▶│  Pod (anihpj)    │
                   └────────────────┘     │  container :8000 │
                                          └──────────────────┘
                                    ────▶│  Pod (anihpj)    │
                                          │  container :8000 │
                                          └──────────────────┘

7.5d ConfigMap YAML — anihpj-config.yaml

anihpj-config.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config
  labels:
    app: anihpj
data:
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: "anihpj.settings"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*"
  DEBUG: "False"
  DATABASE_ENGINE: "django.db.backends.sqlite3"
  STATIC_URL: "/static/"
📝 ConfigMap Values Are Always Strings

Even though Django expects DEBUG=False as a boolean, in a ConfigMap all values are strings. Your Django code should handle the conversion: DEBUG = os.environ.get('DEBUG', 'False') == 'True'

7.5e Secret YAML — anihpj-secret.yaml

anihpj-secret.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-secret
  labels:
    app: anihpj
type: Opaque                       # Generic secret type
stringData:                        # Use stringData for plain text (K8s auto-encodes)
  SECRET_KEY: "django-insecure-change-this-in-production-abc123"
  DATABASE_URL: "sqlite:///db.sqlite3"

# Alternative: use 'data' with base64-encoded values
# data:
#   SECRET_KEY: ZGphbmdvLWluc2VjdXJlLWNoYW5nZS10aGlzLWluLXByb2R1Y3Rpb24=
🚨 Secrets Are Not Encrypted by Default

Kubernetes Secrets are only base64-encoded, not encrypted. Anyone with API access can decode them. For production, use a proper secrets manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault) or enable K8s encryption at rest. Never commit Secret YAML files to Git.

7.6 Multi-Document YAML Files

You can combine multiple Kubernetes resources in a single file using the --- separator:

anihpj-all-in-one.yaml — Combined Deployment + Service 
# ── Deployment ──
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-deployment
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v1
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
---
# ── Service ──
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-service
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8000
      nodePort: 30080
✅ One File Per Resource
  • Easier to manage individually
  • Cleaner Git diffs
  • Can apply selectively
  • Best for: production
📄 All-in-One File
  • Quick to share
  • Single kubectl apply
  • Harder to find things
  • Best for: demos / learning

7.7 Validating YAML Before Applying

Always validate your YAML before sending it to the cluster. Catch mistakes early:

Validation Commands 
# Dry-run — simulates without creating anything
$ kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml --dry-run=client
deployment.apps/anihpj-deployment created (dry run)

# Diff — shows what would change vs. current state
$ kubectl diff -f deployment.yaml
# Shows a unified diff of new vs. existing resource

# Server-side dry-run — validates against the API server
$ kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml --dry-run=server

# Explain — check valid fields for a resource
$ kubectl explain deployment.spec.strategy
🔧 VS Code Setup for YAML
  • Install the YAML extension (Red Hat)
  • Install the Kubernetes extension (Microsoft)
  • Auto-complete for all K8s fields
  • Inline validation & error highlighting
  • Hover docs for every field
🌐 Online Validators
  • kubeval — CLI schema validator
  • kubeconform — faster alternative
  • yamllint — pure YAML syntax check
  • kube-score — best practice checks
  • Datree — policy enforcement

7.8 YAML Best Practices

Follow these rules to write clean, maintainable Kubernetes YAML:

✅ DO
  • ✅ Use 2-space indentation consistently
  • ✅ Always specify resource requests and limits
  • ✅ Use labels on every resource
  • ✅ Add comments for non-obvious config
  • ✅ Quote strings that look like numbers/booleans
  • ✅ Use stringData for secrets (auto-encodes)
  • ✅ Include readinessProbe and livenessProbe
  • ✅ Validate with --dry-run=client before applying
❌ DON'T
  • ❌ Never use tabs (spaces only!)
  • ❌ Don't hardcode secrets in YAML
  • ❌ Don't use :latest tag in production
  • ❌ Don't skip resource limits
  • ❌ Don't commit Secret files to Git
  • ❌ Don't copy-paste without understanding
  • ❌ Don't mix tabs and spaces
  • ❌ Don't forget the selector field in Deployments

Chapter 8: Deploying anihpj to Kubernetes — Full Walkthrough

This is the big one. We'll take our anihpj Django project from a local codebase to a fully running Kubernetes deployment — step by step. By the end, you'll have a real app served by Kubernetes pods.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~2.5 hours 🚀 Full Deployment 🛠️ Hands-On

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Pre-deployment checklist — everything you need ready
  • ✅ Structure your project for Kubernetes
  • ✅ Build and make your Docker image available to Minikube
  • ✅ Create namespace, ConfigMap, Secret, Deployment, and Service
  • ✅ Verify everything works and access your app
  • ✅ Run Django migrations inside a pod
  • ✅ Troubleshoot common deployment issues

8.1 Preparation Checklist

Before deploying, make sure you have everything in place:

Pre-Flight Checklist

  ✅  Docker installed and running            docker --version
  ✅  Minikube installed                       minikube version
  ✅  kubectl installed                        kubectl version --client
  ✅  anihpj Dockerfile created and tested     docker build -t anihpj:v1 .
  ✅  Kubernetes cluster running               minikube status
  ✅  YAML manifests prepared                  ls k8s/
  ✅  requirements.txt up to date              pip freeze

8.2 Project Structure for K8s Deployment

Here's how our anihpj project looks when ready for Kubernetes:

📁 Directory Layout

  anihpj/
  ├── manage.py                   ← Django entry point
  ├── anihpj/
  │   ├── settings.py              ← Django settings
  │   ├── urls.py                  ← Root URL config
  │   └── wsgi.py                  ← WSGI app
  ├── jobpost/
  │   ├── models.py                ← Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost
  │   ├── views.py                 ← jobpost_list, jobpost_detail, skills_list
  │   └── urls.py                  ← App URLs
  ├── Dockerfile                   ← How to containerize
  ├── .dockerignore                ← Files to exclude from image
  ├── requirements.txt             ← Python dependencies
  └── k8s/                        ← Kubernetes manifests
      ├── namespace.yaml           ← Isolated namespace
      ├── configmap.yaml           ← Non-sensitive config
      ├── secret.yaml              ← Sensitive config (not in Git!)
      ├── deployment.yaml          ← Pod template + replicas
      ├── service.yaml             ← Networking / routing
      └── ingress.yaml             ← HTTP routing (optional)
💡 The k8s/ Directory Convention

Keeping all Kubernetes manifests in a k8s/ folder lets you deploy everything at once with kubectl apply -f k8s/. This is a common convention used across the industry.

Dockerfile — The Blueprint for Our Image 
# Use official Python 3.11 slim image for smaller size
FROM python:3.11-slim

# Set environment variables
ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1

# Set the working directory
WORKDIR /app

# Install dependencies first (Docker layer caching)
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy the entire project
COPY . .

# Expose Django's port
EXPOSE 8000

# Run the Django development server
CMD ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
.dockerignore — Keep the Image Clean 
__pycache__
*.pyc
*.pyo
db.sqlite3
.git
.gitignore
env/
venv/
k8s/
*.md

8.3 Step 1: Build and Push the Docker Image

Minikube runs its own Docker daemon. To avoid pushing to a remote registry, we build directly inside Minikube's Docker environment:

Building for Minikube 
# Option A: Use Minikube's Docker daemon (recommended for local dev)
# This makes images built here visible to the Minikube cluster
$ eval $(minikube docker-env)        # macOS/Linux
$ minikube docker-env | Invoke-Expression  # Windows PowerShell

# Build the image
$ docker build -t anihpj:v1 ./anihpj/
Sending build context to Docker daemon  46.08kB
Step 1/7 : FROM python:3.11-slim
Step 2/7 : WORKDIR /app
Step 3/7 : COPY requirements.txt .
Step 4/7 : RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
Step 5/7 : COPY . .
Step 6/7 : EXPOSE 8000
Step 7/7 : CMD ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
Successfully built abc123def
Successfully tagged anihpj:v1

# Verify the image exists in Minikube's Docker
$ docker images | grep anihpj
anihpj   v1   abc123def   2 minutes ago   198MB
Alternative: Push to a Registry (for cloud deployments) 
# Option B: Push to Docker Hub (for remote/cloud clusters)
$ docker build -t anihpj:v1 ./anihpj/
$ docker tag anihpj:v1 yourusername/anihpj:v1
$ docker login
$ docker push yourusername/anihpj:v1
📝 imagePullPolicy: Never

When using Minikube's Docker daemon, add imagePullPolicy: Never to your deployment YAML. This tells Kubernetes to use the local image instead of trying to pull from a registry.

🔄 How minikube docker-env Works

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                     YOUR HOST MACHINE                          │
  │                                                                │
  │  ┌──────────────┐    eval $(minikube     ┌─────────────────┐   │
  │  │  Terminal /   │    docker-env)         │   Minikube VM   │   │
  │  │  Shell        │ ─────────────────────▶ │                 │   │
  │  │              │    Points DOCKER_HOST   │  ┌───────────┐  │   │
  │  │  $ docker    │    to Minikube's        │  │  Docker   │  │   │
  │  │    build ... │    Docker daemon        │  │  Daemon   │  │   │
  │  └──────┬───────┘                         │  └─────┬─────┘  │   │
  │         │                                 │        │        │   │
  │         │    docker build -t app:v1 .     │        ▼        │   │
  │         └─────────────────────────────────┼▶ ┌──────────┐   │   │
  │                                           │  │  Images  │   │   │
  │    WITHOUT docker-env:                    │  │ app:v1   │   │   │
  │    Image built on HOST → not in Minikube  │  │ nginx:.. │   │   │
  │                                           │  └──────────┘   │   │
  │    WITH docker-env:                       │        │        │   │
  │    Image built INSIDE Minikube → visible  │        ▼        │   │
  │    to kubelet → no registry needed! ✅     │  ┌──────────┐   │   │
  │                                           │  │  Pods    │   │   │
  │                                           │  │ can pull │   │   │
  │                                           │  │ locally  │   │   │
  │                                           │  └──────────┘   │   │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────┘

8.4 Step 2: Create the Namespace

Isolate our app in its own namespace to keep things organized:

k8s/namespace.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-app
  labels:
    project: anihpj
    environment: development
Apply it 
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/namespace.yaml
namespace/anihpj-app created

$ kubectl get ns
NAME              STATUS   AGE
default           Active   30m
kube-system       Active   30m
kube-public       Active   30m
anihpj-app        Active   5s    ← our new namespace!

8.5 Step 3: Create ConfigMap and Secrets

Separate your configuration from your code. ConfigMaps for non-sensitive settings, Secrets for passwords and keys:

k8s/configmap.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config
  namespace: anihpj-app
data:
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: "anihpj.settings"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*"
  DEBUG: "False"
k8s/secret.yaml (DO NOT commit to Git!) 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-secret
  namespace: anihpj-app
type: Opaque
stringData:
  SECRET_KEY: "your-super-secret-django-key-here"
  DATABASE_URL: "sqlite:///db.sqlite3"
Apply both 
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/configmap.yaml
configmap/anihpj-config created

$ kubectl apply -f k8s/secret.yaml
secret/anihpj-secret created

# Verify
$ kubectl get cm,secret -n anihpj-app
NAME                         DATA   AGE
configmap/anihpj-config      3      10s

NAME                         TYPE     DATA   AGE
secret/anihpj-secret         Opaque   2      5s

8.6 Step 4: Create the Deployment

The Deployment controls how many pod instances run and how updates are rolled out:

k8s/deployment.yaml — Fully Annotated 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-deployment
  namespace: anihpj-app              # Deploy into our namespace
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3                        # Run 3 pod instances
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj                    # How Deployment finds its pods
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 1              # Keep at least 2 running during updates
      maxSurge: 1                    # Allow 1 extra pod during rollout
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        version: v1
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v1
          imagePullPolicy: Never     # Use local image (Minikube)
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          envFrom:                   # Load ALL keys from ConfigMap
            - configMapRef:
                name: anihpj-config
            - secretRef:             # Load ALL keys from Secret
                name: anihpj-secret
          resources:
            requests:                # Minimum guaranteed resources
              memory: "128Mi"
              cpu: "250m"
            limits:                  # Maximum allowed resources
              memory: "256Mi"
              cpu: "500m"
          readinessProbe:            # Is the app ready for traffic?
            httpGet:
              path: /jobs/
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 15
            periodSeconds: 10
          livenessProbe:             # Is the app still alive?
            httpGet:
              path: /jobs/
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 15
Apply and watch 
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yaml
deployment.apps/anihpj-deployment created

# Watch pods come up
$ kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -w
NAME                                 READY   STATUS              RESTARTS   AGE
anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-abc12    0/1     ContainerCreating   0          2s
anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-def34    0/1     ContainerCreating   0          2s
anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-ghi56    0/1     ContainerCreating   0          2s
anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-abc12    1/1     Running             0          8s
anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-def34    1/1     Running             0          9s
anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-ghi56    1/1     Running             0          10s

🔄 Rolling Update — How Pods Transition

With maxUnavailable: 1 and maxSurge: 1, Kubernetes ensures minimal downtime during deployments:

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ROLLING UPDATE: v1 → v2  (replicas: 3, maxSurge: 1)      │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                                                             │
  │  Step 1: Initial State (3 pods running v1)                  │
  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐                     │
  │  │ Pod v1 ✅│  │ Pod v1 ✅│  │ Pod v1 ✅│                     │
  │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘                     │
  │                                                             │
  │  Step 2: Create 1 new pod (surge), terminate 1 old          │
  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐       │
  │  │ Pod v1 ✅│  │ Pod v1 ✅│  │ Pod v1 ❌│  │ Pod v2 🔄│       │
  │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘       │
  │                                                             │
  │  Step 3: v2 pod ready, continue replacing                   │
  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐                     │
  │  │ Pod v1 ✅│  │ Pod v2 ✅│  │ Pod v2 🔄│                     │
  │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘                     │
  │                                                             │
  │  Step 4: Complete — all pods running v2                      │
  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐                     │
  │  │ Pod v2 ✅│  │ Pod v2 ✅│  │ Pod v2 ✅│                     │
  │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘                     │
  │                                                             │
  │  ✅ = Running    🔄 = Starting    ❌ = Terminating           │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Trigger a Rolling Update 
# Option 1: Update image version
$ kubectl set image deployment/anihpj-deployment anihpj=anihpj:v2 -n anihpj-app

# Option 2: Edit the YAML and re-apply
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yaml

# Watch the rollout progress
$ kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj-deployment -n anihpj-app
Waiting for deployment "anihpj-deployment" rollout to finish: 1 out of 3 new replicas updated...
Waiting for deployment "anihpj-deployment" rollout to finish: 2 out of 3 new replicas updated...
deployment "anihpj-deployment" successfully rolled out

# Rollback if something goes wrong!
$ kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj-deployment -n anihpj-app
deployment.apps/anihpj-deployment rolled back

8.7 Step 5: Create the Service

Expose the deployment so traffic can reach our Django app:

k8s/service.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-service
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: anihpj                    # Matches pods with label app=anihpj
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 80                     # Service port
      targetPort: 8000             # Django port inside container
      nodePort: 30080              # External port (30000-32767)
Apply 
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/service.yaml
service/anihpj-service created

$ kubectl get svc -n anihpj-app
NAME             TYPE       CLUSTER-IP      EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)        AGE
anihpj-service   NodePort   10.96.142.178   <none>        80:30080/TCP   5s

📊 Kubernetes Service Types Comparison

Type Access Scope Use Case External IP?
ClusterIP Internal only (within cluster) Microservice-to-microservice communication ❌ No
NodePort External via node IP:port Development, testing, simple exposure ⚠️ Via node IP + port (30000-32767)
LoadBalancer External via cloud LB Production on cloud providers (AWS, GCP, Azure) ✅ Yes (cloud-provisioned)
ExternalName DNS alias to external service Map cluster DNS to external domains N/A (CNAME record)

🌐 Service Routing — How Traffic Flows

    External Traffic
         │
         ▼
  ┌──────────────┐     NodePort opens port 30080
  │    Node IP   │     on EVERY node in the cluster
  │  :30080      │
  └──────┬───────┘
         │
         ▼
  ┌──────────────┐     Service uses selector: app=anihpj
  │   Service    │     to find matching pods
  │  ClusterIP   │
  │ 10.96.142.x  │
  └──────┬───────┘
         │ load balances (round-robin)
    ┌────┼────┐
    ▼    ▼    ▼
  ┌────┐┌────┐┌────┐  
  │Pod1││Pod2││Pod3│  Each pod receives
  │:8K ││:8K ││:8K │  traffic on port 8000
  └────┘└────┘└────┘

8.8 Step 6: Verify Everything

Time to check that all our resources are healthy:

Verification Commands 
# See ALL resources in our namespace
$ kubectl get all -n anihpj-app
NAME                                     READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
pod/anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-abc12    1/1     Running   0          2m
pod/anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-def34    1/1     Running   0          2m
pod/anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-ghi56    1/1     Running   0          2m

NAME                     TYPE       CLUSTER-IP      EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)        AGE
service/anihpj-service   NodePort   10.96.142.178   <none>        80:30080/TCP   1m

NAME                                READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
deployment.apps/anihpj-deployment   3/3     3            3           2m

NAME                                           DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   AGE
replicaset.apps/anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4    3         3         3       2m

# View detailed deployment info
$ kubectl describe deployment anihpj-deployment -n anihpj-app

# Stream application logs
$ kubectl logs -f deployment/anihpj-deployment -n anihpj-app

# Check ConfigMap values
$ kubectl get cm anihpj-config -n anihpj-app -o yaml

What You Should See

  ┌─ anihpj-app namespace ──────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                         │
  │  ConfigMap: anihpj-config ✅                             │
  │  Secret: anihpj-secret ✅                                │
  │                                                         │
  │  Deployment: anihpj-deployment (3/3 ready) ✅            │
  │  ├── Pod: anihpj-...-abc12  (Running) ✅                │
  │  ├── Pod: anihpj-...-def34  (Running) ✅                │
  │  └── Pod: anihpj-...-ghi56  (Running) ✅                │
  │                                                         │
  │  Service: anihpj-service (NodePort :30080) ✅            │
  │                                                         │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

✅ Deployment Health Checklist

🟢 Healthy Indicators
  • All pods → STATUS: Running
  • READY → Shows 1/1 (all containers ready)
  • RESTARTS → 0 (no crash loops)
  • Deployment → AVAILABLE matches replicas
  • Service endpoints → Lists pod IPs (not empty)
  • Logs → No errors, HTTP 200 responses
🔴 Trouble Signs
  • STATUS → Pending, CrashLoopBackOff, Error
  • READY → Shows 0/1 (container not ready)
  • RESTARTS → High number (app keeps crashing)
  • Events → FailedScheduling, FailedMount
  • Endpoints → Empty (selector doesn't match)
  • Describe → Warning events at bottom

8.9 Step 7: Access Your Application

The moment of truth — let's access our Django app through Kubernetes:

Accessing the app 
# Option 1: minikube service (opens browser automatically)
$ minikube service anihpj-service -n anihpj-app
|-----------|----------------|-------------|---------------------------|
| NAMESPACE |      NAME      | TARGET PORT |            URL            |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|---------------------------|
| anihpj-app| anihpj-service |          80 | http://192.168.49.2:30080 |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|---------------------------|
🎉 Opening service anihpj-app/anihpj-service in default browser...

# Option 2: Get the URL manually
$ minikube service anihpj-service -n anihpj-app --url
http://192.168.49.2:30080

# Option 3: Port-forward directly to a pod
$ kubectl port-forward svc/anihpj-service 8000:80 -n anihpj-app
# Visit http://localhost:8000/jobs/

🎉 Your App is Live on Kubernetes!

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                              │
  │   anihpj is running on Kubernetes!           │
  │                                              │
  │   📍 http://192.168.49.2:30080/jobs/         │
  │   📍 http://192.168.49.2:30080/jobs/1/       │
  │   📍 http://192.168.49.2:30080/skills/       │
  │                                              │
  │   3 replicas  •  auto-healing  •  scalable   │
  │                                              │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

8.10 Step 8: Run Django Migrations

Django needs database migrations to set up tables. Run them inside one of the pods:

Running migrations & creating superuser 
# Get one of the pod names
$ kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -o name | head -1
pod/anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-abc12

# Run migrations
$ kubectl exec -it anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-abc12 -n anihpj-app \
    -- python manage.py migrate
Operations to perform:
  Apply all migrations: admin, auth, contenttypes, jobpost, sessions
Running migrations:
  Applying contenttypes.0001_initial... OK
  Applying auth.0001_initial... OK
  Applying jobpost.0001_initial... OK
  ...

# Create superuser
$ kubectl exec -it anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-abc12 -n anihpj-app \
    -- python manage.py createsuperuser
Username: admin
Email: admin@anihpj.com
Password: ********

# Load sample data (if you have a fixture)
$ kubectl exec -it anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-abc12 -n anihpj-app \
    -- python manage.py loaddata sample_data.json
⚠️ SQLite in Kubernetes

With SQLite, data is stored inside the container. If the pod restarts, the data is lost. This is fine for learning, but for production you'd use PostgreSQL or MySQL with a PersistentVolume (covered in Part 3). Migrations also only run on one pod — each pod has its own SQLite file.

8.11 Troubleshooting First Deployment

Things don't always go right the first time. Here are the most common issues and how to fix them:

Symptom Possible Cause Diagnosis Command Fix
ImagePullBackOff Image not found kubectl describe pod Check image name, add imagePullPolicy: Never for local
CrashLoopBackOff App crashes on start kubectl logs <pod> Fix code error, check env vars and dependencies
Pending Not enough resources kubectl describe pod Lower resource requests or add nodes
CreateContainerConfigError Missing ConfigMap/Secret kubectl describe pod Create the ConfigMap/Secret first
Connection refused Service misconfigured kubectl get svc + kubectl get endpoints Verify selector matches pod labels and ports match
ErrImageNeverPull Image not in local Docker docker images Run eval $(minikube docker-env) then rebuild
Debugging Workflow 
# 1. Check pod status
$ kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app
# Look for non-Running status

# 2. Get detailed events
$ kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n anihpj-app
# Scroll to "Events" section at the bottom

# 3. Check logs
$ kubectl logs <pod-name> -n anihpj-app
# Add --previous to see logs from a crashed container

# 4. Shell in and inspect
$ kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -n anihpj-app -- /bin/bash
# Check files, env vars, network connectivity

# 5. Check endpoints (Service → Pod binding)
$ kubectl get endpoints anihpj-service -n anihpj-app
# Should list pod IPs — empty means selector mismatch

🔍 Debugging Decision Flowchart

  Pod not working? Start here:
  ─────────────────────────────
                │
                ▼
      ┌───────────────────┐
      │ kubectl get pods  │
      │ What is STATUS?   │
      └────────┬──────────┘
               │
     ┌─────────┼──────────┬───────────────┐
     ▼         ▼          ▼               ▼
  Pending   ImagePull   CrashLoop     Running but
            BackOff     BackOff       not working
     │         │          │               │
     ▼         ▼          ▼               ▼
  describe   describe   kubectl        kubectl
  pod →      pod →      logs →         get svc →
  Events     Events     App error?     Endpoints
     │         │          │            empty?
     ▼         ▼          ▼               │
  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐    ┌──┴──┐
  │Resource││ │Image   ││ │Fix the││    │YES  │ NO
  │limits? ││ │name    ││ │code or││    │     │ │
  │Lower   ││ │wrong?  ││ │env var││    ▼     ▼
  │requests││ │Policy? ││ │error  ││  Fix    Port
  │or add  ││ │docker- ││ │       ││  label  mismatch?
  │nodes   ││ │env?    ││ │       ││  selector Check
  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘  └─────────┘

8.12 The Complete Deployment Workflow

Here's the entire end-to-end flow you just completed, in one visual summary:

End-to-End Deployment Pipeline

  ① PREPARE              ② BUILD              ③ DEPLOY              ④ VERIFY
  ┌────────────┐       ┌────────────┐       ┌────────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐
  │ Dockerfile │──────▶│ docker     │──────▶│ kubectl apply  │───▶│ kubectl get │
  │ YAML files │       │ build -t   │       │ -f k8s/        │    │ all -n app  │
  │ .env       │       │ anihpj:v1  │       │   namespace    │    │ logs -f     │
  │ requirements│      └────────────┘       │   configmap    │    │ describe    │
  └────────────┘                            │   secret       │    └─────────────┘
                                            │   deployment   │          │
                                            │   service      │          ▼
                                            └────────────────┘    ⑤ ACCESS
                                                                  ┌─────────────┐
                                                                  │ minikube    │
                                                                  │ service     │
                                                                  │ anihpj-svc  │
                                                                  │ -n app      │
                                                                  │             │
                                                                  │ 🎉 LIVE!    │
                                                                  │ /jobs/      │
                                                                  │ /skills/    │
                                                                  └─────────────┘
Quick Reference — All Commands in Order 
# The complete deployment in 8 commands:

# 1. Point Docker to Minikube
eval $(minikube docker-env)

# 2. Build the image
docker build -t anihpj:v1 ./anihpj/

# 3. Deploy everything
kubectl apply -f k8s/namespace.yaml
kubectl apply -f k8s/configmap.yaml
kubectl apply -f k8s/secret.yaml
kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yaml
kubectl apply -f k8s/service.yaml

# 4. Or deploy all at once:
kubectl apply -f k8s/

# 5. Check status
kubectl get all -n anihpj-app

# 6. Run migrations
kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -o name | head -1) \
    -n anihpj-app -- python manage.py migrate

# 7. Access the app
minikube service anihpj-service -n anihpj-app
💡 Part 2 Complete!

You've gone from zero to a fully deployed Django application on Kubernetes. You can now set up clusters, use kubectl fluently, write YAML manifests, and deploy real applications. In Part 3, we'll learn about ConfigMaps in depth, Secrets management, and Persistent Storage — the key to stateful applications.

⚙️ Part 3: Configuration & Storage Chapters 9-11

Chapter 9: ConfigMaps — Managing Configuration

Learn how to externalize application configuration from your container images. ConfigMaps let you change settings without rebuilding images — the Kubernetes way of managing environment-specific configuration.

⚙️ Configuration 🔑 Key-Value Pairs 📦 Environment Injection 🟢 Beginner

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ What ConfigMaps are and why they exist
  • ✅ 4 ways to create ConfigMaps (literal, file, env-file, YAML)
  • ✅ 3 ways to consume ConfigMaps in Pods (envFrom, selective, volume)
  • ✅ Complete ConfigMap setup for the anihpj Django project
  • ✅ Updating strategies and immutable ConfigMaps

9.1 What are ConfigMaps?

A ConfigMap is a Kubernetes API object that stores non-sensitive configuration data as key-value pairs. It separates your configuration from your container image, so you can change settings without rebuilding or redeploying your application.

🧠 The Core Idea: Separate Config from Code

  ❌ WITHOUT ConfigMaps (hardcoded):
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Docker Image                                │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  settings.py                           │  │
  │  │  DEBUG = False          ← hardcoded!   │  │
  │  │  ALLOWED_HOSTS = '*'    ← hardcoded!   │  │
  │  │  DB_HOST = 'postgres'   ← hardcoded!   │  │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │  Change any setting? → Rebuild entire image! │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  ✅ WITH ConfigMaps (externalized):
  ┌──────────────┐      ┌─────────────────────┐
  │ Docker Image │      │  ConfigMap (K8s)     │
  │ ┌──────────┐ │      │                     │
  │ │ settings │◀├──────┤  DEBUG: "False"      │
  │ │ reads    │ │ env  │  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*"  │
  │ │ from env │ │ vars │  DB_HOST: "postgres" │
  │ └──────────┘ │      └─────────────────────┘
  │ Same image   │      Change config? Just update
  │ everywhere!  │      the ConfigMap! No rebuild!
  └──────────────┘
🔄 Analogy: Django's settings.py

Think of a ConfigMap like externalizing your settings.py. Instead of hardcoding DEBUG = False inside the image, you store it in a ConfigMap and inject it as an environment variable. Same image runs in dev (DEBUG=True) and prod (DEBUG=False).

📏 ConfigMap Quick Facts
  • Max size: 1 MB per ConfigMap
  • Data type: Non-sensitive only
  • Namespace-scoped: Lives in a specific namespace
  • NOT encrypted: Use Secrets for sensitive data
  • Can store: Simple values, entire config files, JSON/YAML
⚠️ ConfigMaps Are NOT for Sensitive Data

Never store passwords, API keys, tokens, or certificates in ConfigMaps. They are stored in plain text and visible to anyone with read access. Use Secrets (Chapter 10) for sensitive data.

9.2 Creating ConfigMaps — 4 Methods

Kubernetes gives you four ways to create ConfigMaps. Each method fits different workflows:

📋 4 Methods at a Glance

  Method           Source               Best For
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  --from-literal   Command-line args    Quick one-off values
  --from-file      Any file             Config files (nginx.conf, etc.)
  --from-env-file  .env formatted       Migrating from Docker .env
  YAML manifest    Full YAML file       GitOps, version control ⭐

9.2a From Literal Values (--from-literal)

The quickest way — pass key=value pairs directly on the command line:

Creating from literal values 
# Create a ConfigMap with multiple key-value pairs
$ kubectl create configmap anihpj-config \
    --from-literal=DEBUG=False \
    --from-literal=ALLOWED_HOSTS=* \
    --from-literal=DATABASE_ENGINE=django.db.backends.postgresql

configmap/anihpj-config created

# Verify what was created
$ kubectl get configmap anihpj-config -o yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config
  namespace: default
data:
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*"
  DATABASE_ENGINE: django.db.backends.postgresql
  DEBUG: "False"

9.2b From a File (--from-file)

Store an entire file as a single key in the ConfigMap. The filename becomes the key, the file content becomes the value:

Creating from a file 
# Create a config file first
$ cat > nginx.conf << 'EOF'
server {
    listen 80;
    server_name anihpj.example.com;
    location / {
        proxy_pass http://localhost:8000;
    }
}
EOF

# Create ConfigMap from the file
$ kubectl create configmap nginx-config --from-file=nginx.conf
configmap/nginx-config created

# The key = filename, value = file contents
$ kubectl describe configmap nginx-config
Name:         nginx-config
Data
====
nginx.conf:     ← key is the filename
----
server {        ← value is the full file content
    listen 80;
    ...
}

9.2c From an Env File (--from-env-file)

If you already have a .env file from Docker Compose, you can import it directly:

Creating from .env file 
# Your existing .env file
$ cat .env
DEBUG=False
ALLOWED_HOSTS=*
DATABASE_ENGINE=django.db.backends.postgresql
DATABASE_NAME=anihpj_db

# Import directly — each line becomes a separate key
$ kubectl create configmap anihpj-config --from-env-file=.env
configmap/anihpj-config created

# Each line is now a separate key-value pair
$ kubectl get configmap anihpj-config -o jsonpath='{.data}' | python -m json.tool
{
    "ALLOWED_HOSTS": "*",
    "DATABASE_ENGINE": "django.db.backends.postgresql",
    "DATABASE_NAME": "anihpj_db",
    "DEBUG": "False"
}
💡 Migrating from Docker Compose?

If you currently use env_file: .env in your docker-compose.yml, the --from-env-file method is a direct migration path. Same file format, same key=value syntax.

9.2d From YAML Manifest (Recommended ⭐)

The best method for production — version-controlled, declarative, and reproducible:

k8s/configmap.yaml — The GitOps Way 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: config
data:
  # Simple key-value pairs
  DEBUG: "False"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*"
  DATABASE_ENGINE: "django.db.backends.postgresql"
  DATABASE_NAME: "anihpj_db"
  DATABASE_HOST: "postgres-service"
  DATABASE_PORT: "5432"
  STATIC_URL: "/static/"

  # You can also embed entire config files!
  settings_override.py: |
    # This file can be volume-mounted into the container
    CACHES = {
        'default': {
            'BACKEND': 'django.core.cache.backends.redis.RedisCache',
            'LOCATION': 'redis://redis-service:6379',
        }
    }
Apply it 
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/configmap.yaml
configmap/anihpj-config created

$ kubectl get cm -n anihpj-app
NAME             DATA   AGE
anihpj-config    8      5s

9.3 Using ConfigMaps in Pods — 3 Ways

Once a ConfigMap exists, you need to inject its data into your pods. Kubernetes offers three methods, each suited for different scenarios:

📊 3 Methods — Visual Data Flow

  Method A: envFrom             Method B: Selective Key        Method C: Volume Mount
  (ALL keys → env vars)         (Pick specific keys)           (Keys → files)

  ┌──────────────┐            ┌──────────────┐             ┌──────────────┐
  │  ConfigMap   │            │  ConfigMap   │             │  ConfigMap   │
  │  DEBUG=False │            │  DEBUG=False │             │  DEBUG=False │
  │  HOST=*      │            │  HOST=*      │             │  HOST=*      │
  │  DB=postgres │            │  DB=postgres │             │  DB=postgres │
  └──────┬───────┘            └──────┬───────┘             └──────┬───────┘
         │ ALL keys                  │ pick one                   │ as files
         ▼                           ▼                            ▼
  ┌──────────────┐            ┌──────────────┐             ┌──────────────┐
  │  Pod env:    │            │  Pod env:    │             │  Pod files:  │
  │  $DEBUG=False│            │  $HOST=*     │             │/etc/config/  │
  │  $HOST=*     │            │  (only this  │             │  DEBUG → 0   │
  │  $DB=postgres│            │   one key)   │             │  HOST  → *   │
  └──────────────┘            └──────────────┘             │  DB → pg     │
                                                           └──────────────┘
  Best for:                   Best for:                    Best for:
  All config in pod           Cherry-picking from          Config files,
                              multiple ConfigMaps          hot-reload ⭐

9.3a As Environment Variables — envFrom (All Keys)

The simplest approach — inject every key from the ConfigMap as an environment variable:

Pod with envFrom — all ConfigMap keys become env vars 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      envFrom:                        # ← inject ALL keys
        - configMapRef:
            name: anihpj-config       # ← from this ConfigMap
Verify inside the pod 
# Check that env vars are set
$ kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- env | grep -E 'DEBUG|ALLOWED|DATABASE'
DEBUG=False
ALLOWED_HOSTS=*
DATABASE_ENGINE=django.db.backends.postgresql
DATABASE_NAME=anihpj_db
DATABASE_HOST=postgres-service

9.3b As Individual Env Vars — Selective Keys

When you need specific keys from one or multiple ConfigMaps, or want to rename the environment variable:

Pod with selective keys — cherry-pick what you need 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      env:
        # Pick specific keys from a ConfigMap
        - name: DJANGO_DEBUG              # ← env var name in the pod
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-config         # ← which ConfigMap
              key: DEBUG                  # ← which key

        # You can pick from DIFFERENT ConfigMaps
        - name: DB_ENGINE
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-config
              key: DATABASE_ENGINE

        # Mix with direct values too
        - name: APP_VERSION
          value: "2.1.0"                  # ← hardcoded value

9.3c As Volume Mounts — Files in a Directory

Mount the ConfigMap as files inside the container. Each key becomes a filename, each value becomes the file content. This is the only method that supports hot-reload — Kubernetes updates the files automatically when the ConfigMap changes.

Pod with ConfigMap volume mount 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      volumeMounts:
        - name: config-volume
          mountPath: /etc/config          # ← mount point inside container
          readOnly: true
  volumes:
    - name: config-volume
      configMap:
        name: anihpj-config              # ← which ConfigMap to use
What it looks like inside the pod 
# Each key becomes a file
$ kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- ls /etc/config/
ALLOWED_HOSTS
DATABASE_ENGINE
DATABASE_HOST
DATABASE_NAME
DATABASE_PORT
DEBUG
STATIC_URL

# File content = key value
$ kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- cat /etc/config/DEBUG
False

$ kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- cat /etc/config/DATABASE_HOST
postgres-service
💡 Volume Mounts Support Hot-Reload!

When a ConfigMap is updated, volume-mounted files are automatically refreshed (within ~60 seconds). This means your app can detect and apply configuration changes without restarting. Environment variables do NOT auto-update — pods must be restarted.

📊 Which Method Should You Use?

Feature envFrom (All) Selective Key Volume Mount
Ease of use ⭐⭐⭐ Simplest ⭐⭐ Moderate ⭐ Most setup
Granularity All or nothing Pick specific keys All or specific
Hot-reload ❌ Needs restart ❌ Needs restart ✅ Auto-updates files
Key renaming ❌ Uses original keys ✅ Custom env var names ✅ Custom filenames
Best for Simple apps, all config Multi-source, renaming Config files, dynamic

9.4 ConfigMap for Django Settings in anihpj

Here's a complete, production-ready ConfigMap for our anihpj Django project. This is what your deployment YAML references:

k8s/configmap.yaml — Complete anihpj Config 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: config
    environment: production
data:
  # Django core settings
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: "anihpj.settings"
  DEBUG: "False"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "anihpj.example.com,www.anihpj.example.com"

  # Database settings (non-sensitive parts only!)
  DATABASE_ENGINE: "django.db.backends.postgresql"
  DATABASE_NAME: "anihpj_db"
  DATABASE_HOST: "postgres-service"         # K8s Service name
  DATABASE_PORT: "5432"

  # Static and media files
  STATIC_URL: "/static/"
  MEDIA_URL: "/media/"
  STATIC_ROOT: "/app/staticfiles/"
  MEDIA_ROOT: "/app/media/"

  # Application settings
  LANGUAGE_CODE: "en-us"
  TIME_ZONE: "UTC"
  PAGINATION_SIZE: "20"

🔗 How ConfigMap Connects to the Deployment

  ┌──────────────────┐     envFrom:           ┌──────────────────────┐
  │   ConfigMap      │     configMapRef:      │   Deployment         │
  │   anihpj-config  │────────────────────▶  │   anihpj-deployment  │
  │                  │                        │                      │
  │  DEBUG=False     │     Pod reads these    │   Container env:     │
  │  DB_HOST=pg-svc  │     as env vars        │   $DEBUG → False     │
  │  DB_PORT=5432    │     at startup         │   $DB_HOST → pg-svc  │
  │  STATIC_URL=/s/  │                        │   $DB_PORT → 5432    │
  └──────────────────┘                        └──────────────────────┘
                                                       │
  ┌──────────────────┐     secretRef:                  │
  │   Secret         │────────────────────────────────│
  │   anihpj-secret  │     (passwords, keys)          │
  │  SECRET_KEY=xxx  │                                ▼
  │  DB_PASS=yyy     │                          ┌──────────┐
  └──────────────────┘                          │ Django    │
                                                │ settings  │
                                                │ reads env │
                                                │ os.environ│
                                                └──────────┘
settings.py — Reading from environment 
import os

# These values come from ConfigMap (env vars)
DEBUG = os.environ.get('DEBUG', 'True') == 'True'
ALLOWED_HOSTS = os.environ.get('ALLOWED_HOSTS', '*').split(',')

# Database — ConfigMap for host/port, Secret for password
DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': os.environ.get('DATABASE_ENGINE', 'django.db.backends.sqlite3'),
        'NAME': os.environ.get('DATABASE_NAME', BASE_DIR / 'db.sqlite3'),
        'HOST': os.environ.get('DATABASE_HOST', 'localhost'),
        'PORT': os.environ.get('DATABASE_PORT', '5432'),
        'USER': os.environ.get('DATABASE_USER', 'anihpj'),
        'PASSWORD': os.environ.get('DATABASE_PASSWORD', ''),  # From Secret!
    }
}

STATIC_URL = os.environ.get('STATIC_URL', '/static/')
MEDIA_URL = os.environ.get('MEDIA_URL', '/media/')

9.5 Updating ConfigMaps

What happens when you need to change a ConfigMap that's already in use? The behavior depends on how the ConfigMap is consumed:

📂 Volume Mounts

✅ Auto-updates (~60 seconds)

Files inside the mounted directory are automatically refreshed by the kubelet. Your app can detect and reload the new values — no restart needed.

🔤 Environment Variables

❌ NOT auto-updated

Environment variables are set at pod startup and never change. You must restart (delete) the pod for new values to take effect.

Updating and restarting 
# Method 1: Edit directly
$ kubectl edit configmap anihpj-config -n anihpj-app
# Opens in editor → change values → save → done

# Method 2: Replace with updated YAML
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/configmap.yaml

# For env-var based pods — you MUST restart:
$ kubectl rollout restart deployment/anihpj-deployment -n anihpj-app
deployment.apps/anihpj-deployment restarted

# Watch the rolling restart
$ kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj-deployment -n anihpj-app
⚠️ ConfigMap Updates ≠ Automatic Pod Restarts

Kubernetes will never automatically restart pods when a ConfigMap changes. If your app consumes config via environment variables, you must trigger a kubectl rollout restart manually. Only volume-mounted ConfigMaps auto-refresh the files (but your app still needs to re-read them).

9.6 Immutable ConfigMaps

For configuration that should never change once deployed, Kubernetes supports immutable ConfigMaps:

Immutable ConfigMap 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config-v2
  namespace: anihpj-app
immutable: true                    # ← Cannot be changed after creation!
data:
  DEBUG: "False"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "anihpj.example.com"
  DATABASE_HOST: "postgres-service"
🔒 Protection

Prevents accidental changes. Any kubectl edit or apply with modified data will be rejected.

⚡ Performance

The kubelet skips periodic re-checks on immutable ConfigMaps, reducing API server load at scale.

📋 Versioning

To update, create a new ConfigMap (e.g., config-v3) and update your Deployment to reference it.

🛠️ ConfigMap kubectl Quick Reference

Task Command
List all ConfigMaps kubectl get configmaps / kubectl get cm
View ConfigMap data kubectl describe cm anihpj-config
View as YAML kubectl get cm anihpj-config -o yaml
Get a specific key kubectl get cm anihpj-config -o jsonpath='{.data.DEBUG}'
Edit interactively kubectl edit cm anihpj-config
Delete ConfigMap kubectl delete cm anihpj-config
Create from YAML kubectl apply -f k8s/configmap.yaml
Dry-run + export kubectl create cm my-cm --from-literal=k=v --dry-run=client -o yaml

Chapter 10: Secrets — Managing Sensitive Data

Secrets are like ConfigMaps, but designed for sensitive data — passwords, API keys, TLS certificates. Learn how to create, consume, and secure them properly. Spoiler: base64 is NOT encryption.

🔐 Sensitive Data 🔑 Passwords & Keys 🛡️ RBAC Protected 🟢 Beginner

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ What Secrets are and how they differ from ConfigMaps
  • ✅ The 6 types of Kubernetes Secrets
  • ✅ 4 ways to create Secrets (literal, file, YAML, stringData)
  • ✅ 3 ways to consume Secrets in Pods
  • ✅ Complete Secret setup for the anihpj Django project
  • ✅ Security best practices for production

10.1 What are Secrets?

A Secret is a Kubernetes object that stores sensitive data such as passwords, OAuth tokens, SSH keys, and TLS certificates. Like ConfigMaps, they decouple configuration from containers — but with extra access controls.

🚨 CRITICAL: Base64 Is NOT Encryption!

Secrets are base64 encoded, not encrypted. Anyone who can read the Secret can decode the values instantly:

This is NOT security 
# "Encoded" secret value
$ echo 'c3VwZXJzZWNyZXRwYXNzd29yZA==' | base64 -d
supersecretpassword    ← 😱 Anyone can decode this!

# Base64 is just packaging, like putting a letter in an envelope
# vs actual encryption, which is like putting it in a locked safe

🔐 Base64 Encoding vs Actual Encryption

  BASE64 ENCODING (what Secrets do by default):
  ┌──────────────┐   encode    ┌──────────────────────────┐   decode
  │ "mypassword" │───────────▶│ "bXlwYXNzd29yZA=="       │──────────▶ "mypassword"
  └──────────────┘             └──────────────────────────┘
  Anyone can reverse this! It's just a different representation.

  ACTUAL ENCRYPTION (what you should enable):
  ┌──────────────┐   encrypt   ┌──────────────────────────┐   need key
  │ "mypassword" │───────────▶│ "a8#$kL!m@9xZ..."        │──────────▶ 🔑 required!
  └──────────────┘    🔑 key   └──────────────────────────┘
  Only someone with the encryption key can reverse this.

📊 Secret vs ConfigMap Comparison

Feature ConfigMap Secret
Data type Non-sensitive configuration Passwords, keys, certificates
Encoding Plain text Base64 encoded
Size limit 1 MB 1 MB
Mount as Env vars / Volumes Env vars / Volumes
RBAC Recommended ⚠️ Required
Memory storage Normal storage tmpfs (RAM) when mounted
Git-safe? ✅ Yes ❌ Never commit to Git!

10.2 Types of Secrets

Kubernetes has 6 built-in Secret types, each designed for a specific use case:

Type Use Case Required Keys
Opaque (default) General-purpose key-value pairs Any
kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson Docker registry credentials .dockerconfigjson
kubernetes.io/tls TLS certificate + private key tls.crt, tls.key
kubernetes.io/basic-auth Username + password username, password
kubernetes.io/ssh-auth SSH private key ssh-privatekey
kubernetes.io/service-account-token Auto-created for ServiceAccounts Auto-managed
📝 Which Type Should You Use?

For most applications like anihpj, Opaque is what you need — it stores any key-value data (Django SECRET_KEY, database passwords, API tokens). The specialized types add validation and are used by specific Kubernetes components.

10.3 Creating Secrets — Methods

Similar to ConfigMaps, Secrets can be created in multiple ways:

📋 4 Secret Creation Methods at a Glance

  Method               Source                Best For
  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  --from-literal       Command-line args     Quick one-off secrets
  --from-file          TLS certs, SSH keys   File-based credentials
  YAML (data:)         Base64-encoded YAML   When you manually encode
  YAML (stringData:)   Plain text YAML       GitOps workflow ⭐ (don't commit!)

10.3a From Literal Values

Creating from the command line 
# Create secret with key-value pairs
$ kubectl create secret generic anihpj-secret \
    --from-literal=SECRET_KEY='django-insecure-x7k!m#abc123' \
    --from-literal=DATABASE_PASSWORD='supersecretpassword' \
    --from-literal=EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD='smtp-pass-456'

secret/anihpj-secret created

# View the secret (values are base64 encoded)
$ kubectl get secret anihpj-secret -o yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-secret
type: Opaque
data:
  DATABASE_PASSWORD: c3VwZXJzZWNyZXRwYXNzd29yZA==
  EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD: c210cC1wYXNzLTQ1Ng==
  SECRET_KEY: ZGphbmdvLWluc2VjdXJlLXg3ayFtI2FiYzEyMw==
