10.3b From a File

Creating from files (TLS certs, SSH keys, etc.) 
# TLS certificates
$ kubectl create secret tls anihpj-tls \
    --cert=tls.crt \
    --key=tls.key

# Generic secret from file
$ kubectl create secret generic ssh-key \
    --from-file=ssh-privatekey=~/.ssh/id_rsa

10.3c From YAML — Base64 Encoded

When using the data field, values must be base64-encoded:

Step 1: Encode your values 
# Encode values to base64 (use -n to avoid trailing newline!)
$ echo -n 'supersecretpassword' | base64
c3VwZXJzZWNyZXRwYXNzd29yZA==

$ echo -n 'django-insecure-x7k!m#abc123' | base64
ZGphbmdvLWluc2VjdXJlLXg3ayFtI2FiYzEyMw==
Step 2: Use in YAML 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-secret
  namespace: anihpj-app
type: Opaque
data:                                           # ← base64-encoded values
  SECRET_KEY: ZGphbmdvLWluc2VjdXJlLXg3ayFtI2FiYzEyMw==
  DATABASE_PASSWORD: c3VwZXJzZWNyZXRwYXNzd29yZA==

10.3d Using stringData — Auto-Encoded (Recommended ⭐)

The stringData field lets you write plain text — Kubernetes encodes it for you:

k8s/secret.yaml — Much easier to read! 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-secret
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
type: Opaque
stringData:                                    # ← plain text! Auto-encoded on apply
  SECRET_KEY: "django-insecure-x7k!m#abc123"
  DATABASE_PASSWORD: "supersecretpassword"
  EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD: "smtp-pass-456"
💡 stringData vs data

Use stringData when writing YAML — no manual base64 encoding needed. Kubernetes automatically converts it to base64 data when the Secret is stored. However, never commit this YAML to Git — the plain-text values are visible in the file!

10.4 Using Secrets in Pods

Secrets are consumed in pods the same 3 ways as ConfigMaps — but with secretRef instead of configMapRef:

Method YAML Key App Reads Via Auto-Update? Best For
Env Vars secretRef / secretKeyRef os.environ["KEY"] ❌ Pod restart needed DB passwords, API keys
Volume Mounts volumes.secret Read file from path ✅ ~60s kubelet sync TLS certs, config files
imagePullSecrets imagePullSecrets Kubelet uses internally N/A Private registries only

10.4a As Environment Variables

Pod with Secret as env vars 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: anihpj-config          # Non-sensitive config
        - secretRef:
            name: anihpj-secret          # Sensitive data
      # Or pick individual keys:
      env:
        - name: DJANGO_SECRET_KEY
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-secret
              key: SECRET_KEY

10.4b As Volume Mounts

Pod with Secret as mounted files 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      volumeMounts:
        - name: secret-volume
          mountPath: /etc/secrets         # ← files appear here
          readOnly: true                  # ← always set readOnly!
  volumes:
    - name: secret-volume
      secret:
        secretName: anihpj-secret
        defaultMode: 0400                 # ← restrictive permissions
📝 Secrets Are Stored in RAM

When mounted as volumes, Secrets are stored in tmpfs (RAM-based filesystem), not on disk. This means they're never written to the node's persistent storage — more secure against disk-based attacks.

10.4c As imagePullSecrets

If your container images are in a private registry (Docker Hub private, AWS ECR, GCR), you need credentials to pull them:

Private registry authentication 
# Create Docker registry secret
$ kubectl create secret docker-registry my-registry-cred \
    --docker-server=registry.example.com \
    --docker-username=myuser \
    --docker-password=mypassword \
    --docker-email=dev@example.com
Reference in Pod/Deployment 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod
spec:
  imagePullSecrets:                           # ← at pod level
    - name: my-registry-cred
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: registry.example.com/anihpj:v1   # private image

10.5 Secrets for anihpj — Complete Setup

Here's a complete, production-ready Secret setup for our Django project:

k8s/secret.yaml — Complete anihpj Secrets 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-secret
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: secrets
type: Opaque
stringData:
  # Django core
  SECRET_KEY: "django-insecure-x7k!m#abc123def456ghi789"

  # Database credentials
  DATABASE_USER: "anihpj_admin"
  DATABASE_PASSWORD: "P@ssw0rd!Str0ng2024"

  # Email SMTP
  EMAIL_HOST_USER: "noreply@anihpj.com"
  EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD: "smtp-app-password-here"

  # Third-party API keys
  STRIPE_SECRET_KEY: "sk_live_abc123..."
  SENTRY_DSN: "https://key@sentry.io/12345"

🔐 Complete Config + Secrets Architecture for anihpj

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    anihpj Deployment                           │
  │                                                                │
  │  ┌──────────────────┐    envFrom:         ┌────────────────┐   │
  │  │   ConfigMap      │    configMapRef  ──▶│   Pod          │   │
  │  │   anihpj-config  │                     │                │   │
  │  │                  │                     │  DEBUG=False   │   │
  │  │  DEBUG           │    envFrom:         │  DB_HOST=pg    │   │
  │  │  DB_HOST         │    secretRef    ──▶│  SECRET_KEY=.. │   │
  │  │  DB_PORT         │                     │  DB_PASS=..    │   │
  │  │  ALLOWED_HOSTS   │                     │                │   │
  │  └──────────────────┘                     │  settings.py   │   │
  │                                           │  reads via     │   │
  │  ┌──────────────────┐                     │  os.environ    │   │
  │  │   Secret         │                     └────────────────┘   │
  │  │   anihpj-secret  │                                         │
  │  │                  │    ⚠️ Never in Git!                      │
  │  │  SECRET_KEY      │    ⚠️ RBAC protected                    │
  │  │  DB_PASSWORD     │    ⚠️ Base64 in etcd                    │
  │  │  STRIPE_KEY      │                                         │
  │  └──────────────────┘                                         │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

10.6 Secret Security Best Practices

Kubernetes Secrets provide a basic level of security. For production, follow these practices:

✅ DO
  • Enable encryption at rest for etcd
  • Use RBAC to limit who can read Secrets
  • Keep Secret YAML out of Git (.gitignore)
  • Rotate secrets regularly
  • Use stringData over manual base64
  • Set readOnly: true on volume mounts
  • Use external secret managers (Vault, AWS SM)
  • Set restrictive file permissions (0400)
❌ DON'T
  • Never commit Secret YAML to Git
  • Never log secret values in apps
  • Never echo secrets in Dockerfiles
  • Never share Secrets across namespaces
  • Never assume base64 = encrypted
  • Never grant get secrets to all users
  • Never hardcode secrets in container images
  • Never use kubectl get secret -o yaml in logs

🛡️ Secret Security Layers

  Layer 1: RBAC                    Who can read Secrets?
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  │ ClusterRole: only admins get "get secrets"         │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
           │
  Layer 2: Encryption at Rest      How are they stored?
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  │ etcd stores encrypted values (AES-CBC, AES-GCM)   │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
           │
  Layer 3: Network Policies        Who can access pods?
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  │ Only authorized pods can reach secret-consuming pods│
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
           │
  Layer 4: External Managers       Best-in-class
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  │ HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure KV     │
  │ Automatic rotation, audit logs, fine-grained ACL   │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🛠️ Secret kubectl Quick Reference

Task Command
List all Secrets kubectl get secrets
View Secret (keys only) kubectl describe secret anihpj-secret
Decode a value kubectl get secret anihpj-secret -o jsonpath='{.data.SECRET_KEY}' | base64 -d
Create from literal kubectl create secret generic my-secret --from-literal=key=value
Create TLS Secret kubectl create secret tls my-tls --cert=tls.crt --key=tls.key
Create registry cred kubectl create secret docker-registry my-reg --docker-server=... --docker-username=...
Delete Secret kubectl delete secret anihpj-secret
Dry-run + export kubectl create secret generic my-s --from-literal=k=v --dry-run=client -o yaml

Chapter 11: Volumes and Persistent Storage

Containers are ephemeral — when a pod dies, so does its data. Volumes solve this by providing storage that outlives individual containers and even pods. Master PVs, PVCs, and StorageClasses to keep your data safe.

💾 Persistent Data 📁 Volume Mounts 🏗️ StorageClasses 🟢 Beginner

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why container data is ephemeral and how volumes solve it
  • ✅ Volume types: emptyDir, hostPath, PV, PVC, and more
  • ✅ PersistentVolumes — the cluster-wide storage pool
  • ✅ PersistentVolumeClaims — requesting storage from pods
  • ✅ StorageClasses — dynamic provisioning magic
  • ✅ Adding persistent storage to anihpj (DB, media, static)
  • ✅ Volume lifecycle and reclaim policies

11.1 The Problem: Container Data is Ephemeral

Every container gets a writable filesystem layer. But this layer is ephemeral — it's destroyed when the container stops. This is a huge problem for stateful applications.

💥 Data Loss on Pod Restart

  BEFORE RESTART:                        AFTER RESTART:
  ┌──────────────────────────┐          ┌──────────────────────────┐
  │  anihpj Pod              │          │  anihpj Pod (NEW)        │
  │                          │          │                          │
  │  💾 db.sqlite3           │   🔄→   │  💾 db.sqlite3           │
  │     5 JobPosts ✅         │          │     0 JobPosts ❌         │
  │                          │          │                          │
  │  📷 media/uploads/       │          │  📷 media/uploads/       │
  │     12 resumes ✅         │          │     0 resumes ❌          │
  │                          │          │                          │
  │  📄 logs/django.log      │          │  📄 logs/django.log      │
  │     500 entries ✅        │          │     0 entries ❌          │
  └──────────────────────────┘          └──────────────────────────┘
  😀 Everything is here!                😱 Everything is GONE!
❌ Without Volumes
  • Database resets on every restart
  • Uploaded files disappear
  • Logs gone — can't debug
  • Session data lost — users logged out
✅ With Volumes
  • Database persists across restarts
  • Uploaded files survive pod recreation
  • Logs preserved for monitoring
  • Data on external storage, not container

11.2 Volume Types Overview

Kubernetes supports many volume types, organized into four categories:

Category Type Description Survives Pod?
Temporary emptyDir Scratch space, shared between containers ❌ No
hostPath Mounts from node filesystem (dev only!) ⚠️ Node-tied
Network/Cloud nfs Network File System mount ✅ Yes
awsElasticBlockStore AWS EBS disk ✅ Yes
csi Container Storage Interface (universal) ✅ Yes
Abstracted PersistentVolume Cluster-wide storage resource ✅ Yes
PersistentVolumeClaim Request for storage by a pod ✅ Yes
Config configMap Mounts ConfigMap as files ✅ (data in CM)
secret Mounts Secret as files (tmpfs) ✅ (data in Secret)
projected Combines multiple sources ✅ (varies)

🗂️ Volume Decision Tree

  Need storage?
  │
  ├── Temporary scratch space between containers?
  │   └── ✅ emptyDir
  │
  ├── Config files or secrets?
  │   └── ✅ configMap / secret volume
  │
  ├── Persistent data (survives pod restart)?
  │   │
  │   ├── Dynamic provisioning (cloud)?
  │   │   └── ✅ PVC + StorageClass (recommended)
  │   │
  │   └── Pre-provisioned by admin?
  │       └── ✅ PVC + manually created PV
  │
  └── Development on Minikube only?
      └── ⚠️ hostPath (never in production!)

11.3 emptyDir — Temporary Shared Storage

An emptyDir volume is created when a pod is assigned to a node, and exists as long as that pod runs. When the pod is removed, the data is permanently deleted.

📝 When to Use emptyDir

Use emptyDir for temporary data that containers in the same pod need to share — like a sidecar processing temp files, or caching data between an init container and the main app.

Pod with emptyDir — shared between 2 containers 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-with-cache
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      volumeMounts:
        - name: shared-cache
          mountPath: /app/cache              # Django writes temp files here

    - name: cache-cleaner                     # Sidecar: cleans old cache
      image: busybox
      command: ["sh", "-c", "while true; do find /cache -mmin +60 -delete; sleep 300; done"]
      volumeMounts:
        - name: shared-cache
          mountPath: /cache                   # Same volume, different path

  volumes:
    - name: shared-cache
      emptyDir:
        sizeLimit: 500Mi                     # Optional: limit size

📦 emptyDir Lifecycle

  Pod Created    Pod Running              Pod Deleted
  ────┬──────────────┬───────────────────────┬────
      │              │                       │
      ▼              ▼                       ▼
  ┌────────┐   ┌──────────────────┐   ┌────────────┐
  │emptyDir│   │  Container A     │   │  emptyDir  │
  │created │   │  writes /cache   │   │  DELETED   │
  │(empty) │   │  Container B     │   │  💀 gone!  │
  └────────┘   │  reads  /cache   │   └────────────┘
               └──────────────────┘
               Both share the same data
⚡ RAM-Backed emptyDir with medium: Memory

By default, emptyDir uses the node's disk. Set medium: "Memory" to use tmpfs (RAM-based) for blazing-fast I/O — ideal for caches, temp computation, or session data. Be aware: it counts against your container's memory limit!

RAM-backed emptyDir — ultra-fast I/O 
volumes:
  - name: fast-cache
    emptyDir:
      medium: Memory           # ← stored in RAM (tmpfs), not disk
      sizeLimit: 128Mi         # ← counts against container memory limit!
Feature emptyDir: {} (Disk) emptyDir: {medium: Memory}
Backing store Node disk (ext4/xfs) RAM (tmpfs)
Speed Standard disk I/O ⚡ Near-instant
Counts against Node ephemeral storage Container memory limit
Use case Temp files, build artifacts Caches, session stores, IPC

11.4 hostPath — Node Filesystem (Development Only!)

A hostPath volume mounts a file or directory from the host node's filesystem into a pod. Data persists even when the pod is deleted — but only on that specific node.

🚨 WARNING: Never Use hostPath in Production!
  • Data is tied to ONE node — if pod moves nodes, data is gone
  • Security risk — pod can access the host filesystem
  • No portability — breaks in multi-node clusters
  • Only use in Minikube/kind for local development
hostPath — Minikube development only 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-dev
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      volumeMounts:
        - name: dev-data
          mountPath: /app/data
  volumes:
    - name: dev-data
      hostPath:
        path: /tmp/anihpj-data              # Path on the NODE
        type: DirectoryOrCreate              # Create if not exists

📋 hostPath type Values

Type Behavior
"" (empty) No validation — whatever path you give, it uses (default)
DirectoryOrCreate Creates directory if missing (permissions 0755, same owner as kubelet)
Directory Directory must already exist — fails otherwise
FileOrCreate Creates an empty file if missing (permissions 0644)
File File must already exist — fails otherwise
Socket UNIX socket must already exist at given path
CharDevice Character device must already exist
BlockDevice Block device must already exist

11.5 PersistentVolumes (PV) — The Storage Pool

A PersistentVolume (PV) is a piece of storage provisioned by an administrator (or dynamically via StorageClass). It exists independently of any pod — like a "parking spot" for data in the cluster.

🏗️ PV as a Storage Pool

  ┌────────────────── Kubernetes Cluster ──────────────────────┐
  │                                                            │
  │   PV Pool (provisioned by admin):                          │
  │   ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐    │
  │   │ PV-1    │  │ PV-2    │  │ PV-3    │  │ PV-4    │    │
  │   │ 10Gi    │  │ 50Gi    │  │ 5Gi     │  │ 100Gi   │    │
  │   │ RWO     │  │ RWX     │  │ RWO     │  │ RWO     │    │
  │   │ SSD     │  │ NFS     │  │ HDD     │  │ SSD     │    │
  │   │ 🟢 Free │  │ 🔵 Bound│  │ 🟢 Free │  │ 🟢 Free │    │
  │   └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘    │
  │                     │                                      │
  │                     ▼ Bound to                             │
  │                ┌──────────┐                                │
  │                │ PVC:     │                                │
  │                │ postgres │                                │
  │                │ 50Gi RWX │                                │
  │                └──────────┘                                │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📊 Access Modes

Mode Abbreviation Description Use Case
ReadWriteOnce RWO Read-write by a single node Databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL)
ReadOnlyMany ROX Read-only by many nodes Shared config, static assets
ReadWriteMany RWX Read-write by many nodes Shared uploads (NFS)
k8s/pv-postgres.yaml — PersistentVolume 
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: postgres-pv
  labels:
    type: local
    app: anihpj
spec:
  capacity:
    storage: 10Gi                            # Storage size
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce                          # Single node read-write
  persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: Retain      # Keep data on PVC delete
  storageClassName: manual                   # Must match PVC
  hostPath:                                  # For Minikube dev
    path: /mnt/data/postgres

11.6 PersistentVolumeClaims (PVC) — Requesting Storage

A PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) is a pod's request for storage. Kubernetes looks through the available PVs and binds the PVC to the best matching one — based on size, access mode, and StorageClass.

🔗 PVC → PV Binding Process

  Developer creates PVC:              Kubernetes finds matching PV:
  ┌──────────────────────┐            ┌──────────────────────┐
  │  PVC: postgres-pvc   │            │  PV: postgres-pv     │
  │  Request: 10Gi       │───BIND───▶│  Capacity: 10Gi      │
  │  Access: RWO         │            │  Access: RWO         │
  │  Class: manual       │            │  Class: manual       │
  └──────────────────────┘            │  Status: 🔵 Bound    │
                                      └──────────────────────┘

  Pod uses the PVC:
  ┌──────────────────────┐
  │  Pod: anihpj-db      │
  │  volumes:            │
  │    - persistentVolume │
  │      Claim:          │
  │        claimName:    │──── references ──── PVC ── bound to ── PV
  │        postgres-pvc  │
  └──────────────────────┘
k8s/pvc-postgres.yaml — PersistentVolumeClaim 
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: postgres-pvc
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce                          # Must match PV
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi                          # Must be ≤ PV capacity
  storageClassName: manual                   # Must match PV
Using PVC in a Deployment 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: postgres
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: postgres
          image: postgres:15
          volumeMounts:
            - name: postgres-storage
              mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data   # PostgreSQL data dir
      volumes:
        - name: postgres-storage
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: postgres-pvc                 # Reference the PVC

11.7 StorageClasses — Dynamic Provisioning

Instead of manually creating PVs, StorageClasses enable dynamic provisioning — Kubernetes automatically creates a PV when a PVC is submitted. No admin intervention needed!

⚡ Dynamic Provisioning Flow

  ① Developer              ② StorageClass          ③ Cloud Provider     ④ Pod
  ┌──────────────┐     ┌────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐  ┌──────────┐
  │ Creates PVC: │     │ Provisioner    │     │ Creates real    │  │ Mounts   │
  │ "I need 10Gi │────▶│ triggers auto- │────▶│ disk (EBS, GCE  │─▶│ volume   │
  │  SSD storage"│     │ create PV      │     │ PD, Azure Disk) │  │ ✅ data!  │
  └──────────────┘     └────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘  └──────────┘
                                                     │
                        No admin setup needed!       ▼
                        StorageClass handles     10Gi SSD disk
                        everything!              auto-created
k8s/storageclass.yaml — Dynamic Provisioning 
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: fast-ssd
provisioner: kubernetes.io/aws-ebs         # Cloud provider plugin
parameters:
  type: gp3                                # SSD disk type
  iopsPerGB: "10"
  fsType: ext4
reclaimPolicy: Retain                      # Keep disk on PVC delete
allowVolumeExpansion: true                 # Allow resizing later
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer    # Bind when pod is scheduled
PVC using StorageClass (no PV needed!) 
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: postgres-pvc
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi
  storageClassName: fast-ssd               # ← References the StorageClass
                                            # PV will be auto-created!
🔧 Manual (PV + PVC)

Admin pre-creates PVs. PVCs bind to existing PVs. Good for on-prem or special storage.

⚡ Dynamic (SC + PVC)

StorageClass auto-creates PVs. No admin needed. Standard for cloud environments.

🌟 Default StorageClass

Most clusters have a default SC. PVCs without storageClassName use it automatically.

11.8 Adding Persistent Storage to anihpj

Our Django job posting app needs persistent storage for three things: the database, user-uploaded media files, and static files. Here's the complete setup:

11.8a PostgreSQL Database Volume

The database is the most critical piece — losing it means losing all JobPost records, user accounts, and application state.

k8s/postgres-storage.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: postgres-pvc
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: database
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce                  # Only one postgres pod writes
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi                  # Enough for job postings data
  storageClassName: standard         # Use cluster default
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: postgres
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  replicas: 1                        # Single DB instance
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: postgres
          image: postgres:15
          ports:
            - containerPort: 5432
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: anihpj-config
            - secretRef:
                name: anihpj-secret
          volumeMounts:
            - name: postgres-data
              mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
              subPath: pgdata              # Avoids "lost+found" issue
      volumes:
        - name: postgres-data
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: postgres-pvc
💡 The subPath Trick

PostgreSQL fails if the data directory contains anything other than its own files. Using subPath: pgdata mounts into a subdirectory, avoiding the lost+found directory that cloud volumes create at the root.

11.8b Django Media Files Volume

User-uploaded files (resumes, profile photos) need to persist and be accessible by all Django pods if you scale horizontally.

k8s/media-storage.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: media-pvc
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: media
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteMany                   # Multiple pods can write uploads
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 5Gi
  storageClassName: standard

11.8c Putting It All Together — anihpj Deployment

Here's the Django Deployment referencing both ConfigMap, Secret, and persistent volumes:

k8s/anihpj-deployment.yaml — Complete with storage 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v1
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: anihpj-config         # Non-sensitive settings
            - secretRef:
                name: anihpj-secret         # Passwords, keys
          volumeMounts:
            - name: media-files
              mountPath: /app/media          # User uploads
            - name: static-files
              mountPath: /app/staticfiles    # Collected static files
      volumes:
        - name: media-files
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: media-pvc
        - name: static-files
          emptyDir: {}                       # Rebuilt on collectstatic

🏗️ Complete anihpj Storage Architecture

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                        anihpj Namespace                           │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ┌────────────────────┐       ┌────────────────────┐              │
  │  │  ConfigMap          │       │  Secret             │              │
  │  │  (anihpj-config)   │       │  (anihpj-secret)   │              │
  │  │  DEBUG, DB_HOST...  │       │  SECRET_KEY, PASS.. │              │
  │  └─────────┬──────────┘       └──────────┬─────────┘              │
  │            │ envFrom                      │ envFrom                │
  │            ▼                              ▼                        │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐          │
  │  │                  anihpj Deployment                   │          │
  │  │     ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐     │          │
  │  │     │  Pod: anihpj                             │     │          │
  │  │     │                                          │     │          │
  │  │     │  /app/media ──── PVC: media-pvc ──── 5Gi │     │          │
  │  │     │  /app/static ── emptyDir (temp)          │     │          │
  │  │     └─────────────────────────────────────────┘     │          │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘          │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐          │
  │  │                postgres Deployment                   │          │
  │  │     ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐     │          │
  │  │     │  Pod: postgres                           │     │          │
  │  │     │                                          │     │          │
  │  │     │  /var/lib/postgresql/data                │     │          │
  │  │     │    └── PVC: postgres-pvc ──── 10Gi       │     │          │
  │  │     └─────────────────────────────────────────┘     │          │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘          │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

11.9 Volume Lifecycle and Reclaim Policies

PersistentVolumes have a lifecycle independent of pods. Understanding the states and reclaim policies prevents accidental data loss.

🔄 PV State Machine

  ┌───────────────┐   PVC binds    ┌───────────────┐   PVC deleted   ┌───────────────┐
  │   Available   │───────────────▶│    Bound      │───────────────▶│   Released    │
  │   🟢 Free     │                │   🔵 In use   │                │   🟡 Waiting  │
  └───────────────┘                └───────────────┘                └───────┬───────┘
         ▲                                                                  │
         │                                                       ┌──────────┴──────────┐
         │                                                       ▼                      ▼
         │ Admin reclaims                               ┌───────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐
         └──────────────────────────────────────────────│  Retain       │     │   Delete     │
                                                        │  📋 Manual    │     │   🔴 Gone    │
                                                        │  cleanup      │     │   PV removed │
                                                        └───────────────┘     └──────────────┘

📊 Reclaim Policies

Policy What Happens Use Case
Retain PV stays, data preserved. Admin manually reclaims. ⭐ Databases, critical data
Delete PV and underlying storage (cloud disk) deleted automatically Temporary workloads, CI/CD
Recycle (deprecated) PV data wiped (rm -rf), PV made available again Legacy — use dynamic provisioning instead
💡 Best Practice

Always use Retain for databases and important data. Use Delete only for ephemeral workloads. And always take regular snapshots/backups — reclaim policies are not a backup strategy!

🛠️ PV / PVC kubectl Quick Reference

Task Command
List PersistentVolumes kubectl get pv
List PersistentVolumeClaims kubectl get pvc -n anihpj-app
PV details (capacity, status) kubectl describe pv postgres-pv
PVC binding status kubectl describe pvc postgres-pvc -n anihpj-app
List StorageClasses kubectl get storageclass
Watch PV/PVC status live kubectl get pv,pvc -n anihpj-app -w
Delete PVC (careful!) kubectl delete pvc media-pvc -n anihpj-app
Patch PV reclaim policy kubectl patch pv my-pv -p '{"spec":{"persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy":"Retain"}}'

🌐 Part 4: Networking & Exposure Chapters 12-14

Chapter 12: Kubernetes Networking Fundamentals

Understand how pods, services, and the outside world communicate. Kubernetes networking follows a flat network model where every pod gets its own IP — no NAT, no port mapping. Master these fundamentals before diving into Services and Ingress.

🌐 Networking 🔗 Pod-to-Pod 📡 DNS & Routing 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ The 4 fundamental rules of the Kubernetes network model
  • ✅ How containers, pods, and services communicate
  • ✅ How DNS resolution works inside a cluster (CoreDNS)
  • ✅ Network Policies — firewall rules for pods
  • ✅ CNI plugins and how to choose one
  • ✅ Troubleshooting common network issues

12.1 The Kubernetes Network Model — 4 Rules

Every Kubernetes cluster must implement these 4 networking rules. They are non-negotiable — every CNI plugin, cloud provider, and cluster setup must guarantee them:

🔢 Rule 1: Unique Pod IPs

Every pod gets its own unique IP address. No two pods share the same IP within the cluster.

🔗 Rule 2: Same-Node Communication

Pods on the same node can communicate directly using their pod IPs — no extra configuration needed.

🌉 Rule 3: Cross-Node, No NAT

Pods on different nodes can communicate without NAT (Network Address Translation). The source IP is preserved.

🤖 Rule 4: Agent-to-Pod

System agents on a node (kubelet, kube-proxy) can communicate with all pods on that node.

🗺️ Kubernetes Network Model — Visualized

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                        KUBERNETES CLUSTER                              │
  │                                                                         │
  │   NODE 1  (10.0.1.x)                    NODE 2  (10.0.2.x)            │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────┐          ┌──────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │                          │          │                          │    │
  │  │  📦 Pod A (anihpj)       │  Rule 3  │  📦 Pod C (redis)        │    │
  │  │  IP: 10.0.1.5           │◄────────►│  IP: 10.0.2.8           │    │
  │  │         │                │ No NAT!  │                          │    │
  │  │  Rule 2 │ same node     │          │  📦 Pod D (postgres)     │    │
  │  │         ▼                │          │  IP: 10.0.2.9           │    │
  │  │  📦 Pod B (nginx)        │          │                          │    │
  │  │  IP: 10.0.1.6           │          │                          │    │
  │  │                          │          │                          │    │
  │  │  🤖 kubelet (Rule 4)    │          │  🤖 kubelet (Rule 4)    │    │
  │  │     can reach all pods  │          │     can reach all pods  │    │
  │  └──────────────────────────┘          └──────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                                                         │
  │  Rule 1: ✅ Every pod has a unique IP (10.0.1.5, 10.0.1.6, etc.)       │
  │  Rule 2: ✅ Pod A ↔ Pod B communicate directly (same node)             │
  │  Rule 3: ✅ Pod A ↔ Pod C communicate without NAT (cross-node)         │
  │  Rule 4: ✅ kubelet on each node can reach every pod on its node        │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📝 Why This Matters for anihpj

Because of these rules, your anihpj Django pod at 10.0.1.5 can directly reach the PostgreSQL pod at 10.0.2.9 on a different node — no special configuration. Kubernetes networking is flat by default: every pod can talk to every other pod. (We'll restrict this with Network Policies in section 12.6.)

💡 Real-World Analogy: The Office Building

Think of the Kubernetes network like an office building with an intercom system:

  • Rule 1 — Every office has its own unique extension number (pod IP)
  • Rule 2 — Offices on the same floor can shout down the hall (same-node bridge)
  • Rule 3 — Offices on different floors use the intercom — no operator needed (cross-node, no NAT)
  • Rule 4 — Building security can reach any office on each floor (agents/kubelet)

12.2 Container-to-Container Communication (Same Pod)

Containers within the same pod share the same network namespace. This means they share the same IP address and can communicate via localhost — just like two processes on the same machine.

📦 Shared Network Namespace Inside a Pod

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    POD  (IP: 10.0.1.5)                   │
  │                 Shared Network Namespace                  │
  │                                                          │
  │   ┌─────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────┐       │
  │   │  Container 1    │         │  Container 2    │       │
  │   │  🐍 Django       │  local  │  🌐 Nginx        │       │
  │   │  (anihpj)       │◄─────►│  (reverse proxy) │       │
  │   │  :8000          │  host  │  :80             │       │
  │   └─────────────────┘         └─────────────────┘       │
  │            │                          │                   │
  │            └──────────┬───────────────┘                   │
  │                       │                                   │
  │              localhost (127.0.0.1)                        │
  │              Shared port space                            │
  │              Same IP for both                             │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  
  Nginx config:  proxy_pass http://localhost:8000;
  Django:        ALLOWED_HOSTS = ["localhost", "10.0.1.5"]
Pod with Django + Nginx sidecar sharing localhost 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-with-nginx
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      ports:
        - containerPort: 8000             # Django listens here

    - name: nginx-sidecar
      image: nginx:alpine
      ports:
        - containerPort: 80               # Nginx listens here
      volumeMounts:
        - name: nginx-conf
          mountPath: /etc/nginx/conf.d
  volumes:
    - name: nginx-conf
      configMap:
        name: nginx-config                # proxy_pass http://localhost:8000
⚠️ Port Conflicts!

Since containers in the same pod share the port space, two containers cannot listen on the same port. If both try to use port 8000, one will fail to start. Always assign unique ports to each container.

12.3 Pod-to-Pod Communication

Pods communicate directly using their IP addresses. The mechanism differs depending on whether the pods are on the same node or different nodes.

🔀 Same Node vs Cross-Node Communication

  SAME NODE (via virtual bridge):          CROSS-NODE (via CNI overlay):
  ─────────────────────────────            ───────────────────────────────

  ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐                ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐
  │ Pod A    │ │ Pod B    │                │ Pod A    │    │ Pod C    │
  │ 10.0.1.5 │ │ 10.0.1.6 │                │ 10.0.1.5 │    │ 10.0.2.8 │
  └────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘                └────┬─────┘    └────┬─────┘
       │             │                          │               │
  ┌────┴─────────────┴─────┐          ┌────────┴───┐    ┌─────┴────────┐
  │   veth pairs           │          │  Node 1    │    │  Node 2      │
  │      │                 │          │  eth0      │    │  eth0        │
  │   cbr0 (bridge)        │          └────┬───────┘    └─────┬────────┘
  │   Virtual L2 bridge    │               │                  │
  └────────────────────────┘               │   CNI overlay    │
                                           │   (VXLAN/BGP)    │
  ⚡ Fast: direct bridge                   └────────┬─────────┘
     No routing needed                              │
                                           Physical network / cloud VPC
Aspect Same Node Cross-Node
Mechanism Linux virtual bridge (cbr0) CNI plugin overlay network
Speed ⚡ Very fast (no network hop) 🔄 Slight overhead (encapsulation)
NAT required? No No (Rule 3)
Example tech veth pairs + bridge Calico (BGP), Flannel (VXLAN), Cilium (eBPF)

12.4 Pod-to-Service Communication

Direct pod-to-pod communication has a problem: pod IPs change when pods restart. Services solve this by providing a stable virtual IP (ClusterIP) that routes traffic to healthy pods. We'll deep-dive into Services in Chapter 13.

🔄 How kube-proxy Routes Service Traffic

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                       SERVICE ROUTING FLOW                         │
  │                                                                    │
  │  1. Pod sends request to Service ClusterIP                        │
  │                                                                    │
  │     📦 anihpj pod                                                  │
  │     curl http://postgres-svc:5432                                 │
  │         │                                                          │
  │         ▼                                                          │
  │  2. CoreDNS resolves "postgres-svc" → 10.96.45.120 (ClusterIP)   │
  │         │                                                          │
  │         ▼                                                          │
  │  3. kube-proxy intercepts traffic to 10.96.45.120                 │
  │     (via iptables rules or IPVS)                                  │
  │         │                                                          │
  │         ▼                                                          │
  │  4. Routes to a healthy backend pod                                │
  │                                                                    │
  │     ┌────────────────┐                                             │
  │     │  postgres-svc  │ ClusterIP: 10.96.45.120                    │
  │     │  (Service)     │                                             │
  │     └───┬─────┬──────┘                                             │
  │         │     │      load-balanced                                 │
  │         ▼     ▼                                                    │
  │     ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐                                              │
  │     │Pod 1 │ │Pod 2 │ ← Endpoints (healthy pods matching labels)  │
  │     │pg-abc│ │pg-xyz│                                              │
  │     └──────┘ └──────┘                                              │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🔧 iptables Mode (Default)

kube-proxy writes iptables rules on each node. Simple and reliable but O(n) — performance degrades with thousands of Services. Random backend selection.

⚡ IPVS Mode (Performance)

Uses Linux IPVS (IP Virtual Server) kernel module. O(1) lookups — handles 10,000+ Services efficiently. Supports round-robin, least-connections, and weighted algorithms.

💡 Why Not Just Use Pod IPs?

When a Deployment rolls out an update, old pods are terminated and new ones are created with new IPs. If your anihpj app is hardcoded to 10.0.2.9 for the database, it breaks instantly. Services give you a stable DNS name and IP that always routes to the current healthy pods.

🔢 Service Port vs TargetPort vs ContainerPort

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  These three port fields confuse everyone. Here's how they chain:  │
  │                                                                     │
  │   Client (another pod)                                             │
  │   curl http://anihpj-svc:80                                       │
  │                    │                                                │
  │                    │  ① Service "port" = 80                        │
  │                    │     (what clients connect to)                  │
  │                    ▼                                                │
  │            ┌──────────────┐                                        │
  │            │  Service     │                                        │
  │            │  port: 80    │──────────────┐                         │
  │            │             ─┤              │                         │
  │            │  targetPort: │  ② "targetPort" = 8000                │
  │            │    8000      │     (forwards to pod port)             │
  │            └──────────────┘              │                         │
  │                                          ▼                         │
  │                               ┌────────────────┐                   │
  │                               │  Pod           │                   │
  │                               │  containerPort:│  ③ Container     │
  │                               │    8000        │     listens here  │
  │                               │  (Django)      │                   │
  │                               └────────────────┘                   │
  │                                                                     │
  │  Summary:  client:80 ──▶ Service ──▶ pod:8000 ──▶ container:8000  │
  │                 ①              ②              ③                    │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

12.5 DNS in Kubernetes

Every Kubernetes cluster runs CoreDNS — a built-in DNS server that automatically creates DNS records for every Service and Pod. This is why you can use names like postgres-svc instead of IP addresses.

🌐 CoreDNS Resolution Flow

  📦 anihpj pod
  │
  │  import psycopg2
  │  conn = psycopg2.connect(host="postgres-svc", port=5432)
  │                                  │
  │                                  ▼
  │                          ┌──────────────┐
  │                          │   CoreDNS    │
  │                          │  (kube-dns)  │
  │                          └──────┬───────┘
  │                                 │ resolves
  │                                 ▼
  │         "postgres-svc"                           Full FQDN:
  │         ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  │         Same namespace:  postgres-svc              (short)
  │         Cross namespace: postgres-svc.database     (.namespace)
  │         Full FQDN:       postgres-svc.database.svc.cluster.local
  │                                 │
  │                                 ▼
  │                          ClusterIP: 10.96.45.120
  │                                 │
  │                                 ▼
  │                          ┌──────────────┐
  │                          │ postgres pod │
  │                          │ 10.0.2.9     │
  │                          └──────────────┘

📋 DNS Name Formats

Resource DNS Format Example (anihpj)
Service (same ns) <service-name> anihpj-svc
Service (cross ns) <svc>.<namespace> postgres-svc.database
Service (full FQDN) <svc>.<ns>.svc.cluster.local anihpj-svc.anihpj-app.svc.cluster.local
Pod <pod-ip-dashed>.<ns>.pod.cluster.local 10-0-1-5.anihpj-app.pod.cluster.local
Headless Service <pod-name>.<svc>.<ns>.svc.cluster.local postgres-0.postgres-headless.database.svc.cluster.local
Testing DNS from inside a pod 
# Verify DNS is working
$ kubectl exec -it anihpj-pod -- nslookup kubernetes
Server:    10.96.0.10
Address:   10.96.0.10#53
Name:      kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local
Address:   10.96.0.1

# Resolve a service name
$ kubectl exec -it anihpj-pod -- nslookup postgres-svc.anihpj-app
Name:      postgres-svc.anihpj-app.svc.cluster.local
Address:   10.96.45.120

# Check the pod's DNS config (/etc/resolv.conf)
$ kubectl exec -it anihpj-pod -- cat /etc/resolv.conf
nameserver 10.96.0.10
search anihpj-app.svc.cluster.local svc.cluster.local cluster.local
ndots:5
💡 Why Short Names Work

The search line in /etc/resolv.conf tells the pod to automatically append .anihpj-app.svc.cluster.local when resolving short names. That's why postgres-svc works — it becomes postgres-svc.anihpj-app.svc.cluster.local automatically.

🔍 Understanding ndots:5 — Why External DNS Is Slow

  ndots:5 means: if the name has fewer than 5 dots, try all search
  domains FIRST before querying it as-is.

  Looking up "google.com" (1 dot, < 5):
  ──────────────────────────────────────
  Try 1: google.com.anihpj-app.svc.cluster.local  → NXDOMAIN ❌
  Try 2: google.com.svc.cluster.local              → NXDOMAIN ❌
  Try 3: google.com.cluster.local                  → NXDOMAIN ❌
  Try 4: google.com                                → 142.250.x.x ✅

  😱 That's 3 wasted DNS queries for every external name!

  ✅ Fix: Use FQDN with trailing dot to bypass search:
     "google.com."  → resolves immediately (0 wasted queries)
  
  ✅ Or set ndots:1 in pod's dnsConfig for external-heavy apps:
     spec:
       dnsConfig:
         options:
           - name: ndots
             value: "1"

12.6 Network Policies — Firewall Rules for Pods

By default, Kubernetes has no network isolation — every pod can talk to every other pod. Network Policies let you restrict traffic flow, acting as firewall rules at the pod level.

🔥 Before vs After Network Policy

  ❌ WITHOUT NetworkPolicy              ✅ WITH NetworkPolicy
  (default — wide open):               (locked down):

  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐        ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐
  │ Pod A │←→│ Pod B │←→│ Pod C │        │ Pod A │  │ Pod B │  │ Pod C │
  │ front │  │ back  │  │  db   │        │ front │──│ back  │──│  db   │
  └──┬────┘  └──┬────┘  └──┬────┘        └──────┘  └──────┘  └──────┘
     │          │          │                 │         │          │
     ├──────────┼──────────┤              ┌──┘    ┌────┘     ┌───┘
     │          │          │              │       │          │
  ALL talk to ALL! 😱                  front→back only  back→db only ✅
                                       front✗→db  BLOCKED! 🛡️
k8s/network-policy.yaml — Only allow frontend → backend 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-frontend-to-backend
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj                        # Apply to anihpj (backend) pods
      role: backend
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress                             # Control incoming traffic
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: anihpj
              role: frontend              # Only from pods with this label
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8000                      # Only on Django's port
k8s/db-network-policy.yaml — Database only accepts backend 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: database-restrict
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres                       # Apply to postgres pods
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: anihpj
              role: backend               # Only backend can reach DB
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432
  egress:
    - to: []                              # DB doesn't need outbound access
    - ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53                        # Except DNS resolution
⚠️ CNI Must Support NetworkPolicy

Not all CNI plugins enforce Network Policies! Flannel does NOT support them. You need Calico, Cilium, or Weave Net. In Minikube, enable the Calico addon: minikube start --cni=calico.

Default Deny All — The Zero Trust Starting Point 
# Block ALL traffic to all pods in a namespace.
# Then whitelist only what's needed (like the policies above).
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  podSelector: {}           # {} = all pods in the namespace
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress                # Blocks both incoming AND outgoing
💡 Best Practice: Default Deny + Whitelist

In production, apply a Default Deny All policy first, then add specific allow policies. This follows the principle of least privilege — pods can only communicate with what you explicitly permit.

12.7 CNI Plugins — The Network Plumbing

The Container Network Interface (CNI) is the standard that defines how networking is configured for containers. Your CNI plugin is responsible for assigning IPs to pods, creating virtual networks, and routing traffic between nodes.

🔌 Where CNI Fits in the Stack

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Your Application (anihpj)                       │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  Kubernetes API (Services, Pods, NetworkPolicy)  │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  CNI Plugin (Calico / Flannel / Cilium)          │  ← You choose this!
  │  • Assigns pod IPs from CIDR range               │
  │  • Creates virtual network interfaces (veth)     │
  │  • Routes traffic between nodes                  │
  │  • Enforces NetworkPolicy (if supported)         │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  Linux Kernel (iptables, IPVS, eBPF, bridges)   │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  Physical / Cloud Network                        │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📊 CNI Plugin Comparison

Plugin Overlay NetworkPolicy Performance Best For
Calico BGP (native) or VXLAN ✅ Full support ⚡ Excellent Production, security-focused
Flannel VXLAN ❌ None 🔄 Good Simple clusters, learning
Cilium eBPF (kernel-level) ✅ Advanced (L7) ⚡⚡ Best High-performance, observability
Weave Net VXLAN mesh ✅ Basic 🔄 Good Multi-cloud, ease of use
Canal Flannel networking ✅ Calico policies 🔄 Good Flannel users who need policies
💡 Recommendation for anihpj

For learning, Flannel is simplest (Minikube default). For production, use Calico (battle-tested, NetworkPolicy support) or Cilium (next-gen eBPF performance). Most managed Kubernetes services (EKS, GKE, AKS) come with a pre-configured CNI.

12.8 Troubleshooting Network Issues

Network problems are the most common issues in Kubernetes. Use this systematic checklist to diagnose and resolve them:

🔍 Network Debugging Flowchart

  Pod can't reach a Service?
  │
  ├── 1. Can the pod resolve DNS?
  │   $ kubectl exec pod -- nslookup kubernetes
  │   ├── ❌ DNS fails → check CoreDNS pods (kube-system namespace)
  │   └── ✅ DNS works → continue
  │
  ├── 2. Can the pod reach the Service IP?
  │   $ kubectl exec pod -- curl -s http://svc-name:port
  │   ├── ❌ Connection refused → Service port YAML wrong?
  │   └── ✅ Response OK → not a network issue
  │
  ├── 3. Does the Service have endpoints?
  │   $ kubectl get endpoints svc-name
  │   ├── ❌ No endpoints → labels don't match! Check:
  │   │   $ kubectl describe svc svc-name     (Selector)
  │   │   $ kubectl get pods --show-labels     (Pod labels)
  │   └── ✅ Endpoints listed → continue
  │
  ├── 4. Are the backend pods running?
  │   $ kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj
  │   ├── ❌ CrashLoopBackOff → app issue, check logs
  │   └── ✅ Running → continue
  │
  └── 5. Is a NetworkPolicy blocking traffic?
      $ kubectl get networkpolicy -n namespace
      ├── Policy exists → check ingress/egress rules
      └── No policies → check CNI plugin health

🛠️ Network Debugging Commands

Task Command
Test DNS resolution kubectl exec -it pod -- nslookup svc-name
Curl a service from a pod kubectl exec -it pod -- curl -s http://svc:port
Check Service endpoints kubectl get endpoints svc-name
View pod's IP and node kubectl get pod -o wide
Check CoreDNS status kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
List network policies kubectl get networkpolicy -A
Debug with a temp pod kubectl run debug --rm -it --image=busybox -- sh
Check kube-proxy logs kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy
📝 The #1 Mistake: Selector Mismatch

Over 50% of "Service not working" issues come from label selector mismatch. The Service's spec.selector labels must exactly match the pod's metadata.labels. Use kubectl describe svc and kubectl get pods --show-labels to compare.

Chapter 13: Services Deep Dive

Services are the glue of Kubernetes networking. They provide stable endpoints for mortal pods, load-balance traffic, and expose your application internally or to the outside world. This chapter covers all 4 Service types, when to use each, and complete examples for anihpj.

🔗 Services ⚖️ Load Balancing 🌐 Exposure 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why Services exist — the pod IP instability problem
  • ✅ 4 Service types: ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, ExternalName
  • ✅ Headless Services for StatefulSets
  • ✅ Multi-port Services and session affinity
  • ✅ Decision guide for choosing the right Service type

13.1 Why Services Exist — The Problem

Pods are mortal. They get created, crash, are replaced by Deployments, and each time they get a new IP address. You can't point your frontend to a pod IP — it will break on the next restart.

💀 The Problem: Pod IPs Are Unstable

  ❌ WITHOUT Services:                       ✅ WITH Services:

  ┌───────┐  hardcoded IP   ┌───────┐       ┌───────┐  stable name   ┌──────────┐
  │ front │──10.0.2.9──────▶│ db    │       │ front │──"db-svc"────▶│ Service  │
  │  end  │                 │Pod v1 │       │  end  │               │ db-svc   │
  └───────┘                 └───┬───┘       └───────┘               └───┬──┬───┘
                                │ 💥 crash!                              │  │
                                ▼                               routes to│  │
  ┌───────┐  10.0.2.9?     ┌───────┐        any healthy pod     │  │
  │ front │──❌ BROKEN!────▶│ db    │                      ┌─────┘  └─────┐
  │  end  │  OLD IP!       │Pod v2 │                      ▼              ▼
  └───────┘                │10.0.3.2│                 ┌───────┐     ┌───────┐
                           └───────┘                  │ db    │     │ db    │
                                                      │Pod v1 │     │Pod v2 │
                                                      └───────┘     └───────┘
❌ Without Services
  • Pod IPs change on every restart
  • No load balancing between replicas
  • No health checking of backends
  • Apps hardcode IPs → instant breakage
✅ With Services
  • Stable DNS name + virtual IP
  • Automatic load balancing across pods
  • Only routes to healthy (ready) pods
  • Apps use names → survive any restart
📝 How Services Find Pods

Services use label selectors to discover pods. Any pod whose labels match the Service's spec.selector becomes a backend endpoint. When pods are created or destroyed, the endpoint list updates automatically.

🔄 Service Label Selector — How Matching Works

  Service YAML:                         Pod YAML:
  ───────────                           ────────
  spec:                                 metadata:
    selector:                             labels:
      app: anihpj  ◄──── MUST MATCH ────► app: anihpj   ✅ Endpoint!
      role: backend ◄─── MUST MATCH ────► role: backend  ✅

  If ALL selector labels match → pod becomes an endpoint
  If ANY label is missing/different → pod is EXCLUDED

  ❌ Common mistake:
     Service selector:  app: anihpj         (lowercase)
     Pod label:         app: Anihpj         (capital A)
     Result: 0 endpoints → Service returns nothing!

13.2 ClusterIP Service (Default)

A ClusterIP Service gets a stable virtual IP accessible only from inside the cluster. This is the default type and the most common — use it for internal communication between microservices.

🔒 ClusterIP — Internal Only

  🔒 INTERNAL CLUSTER ONLY — not accessible from outside

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                   Kubernetes Cluster                   │
  │                                                       │
  │    📦 frontend pod                                    │
  │    curl http://anihpj-svc:8000/api/jobs/             │
  │         │                                             │
  │         ▼                                             │
  │    ┌──────────────────────┐                           │
  │    │  anihpj-svc          │  ClusterIP: 10.96.78.50  │
  │    │  (ClusterIP Service) │  Port: 8000              │
  │    └────┬────────────┬────┘                           │
  │         │            │     label selector:            │
  │         ▼            ▼     app: anihpj               │
  │    ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐                           │
  │    │ anihpj  │  │ anihpj  │  ← endpoint pods         │
  │    │ pod-abc │  │ pod-xyz │                           │
  │    │ :8000   │  │ :8000   │                           │
  │    └─────────┘  └─────────┘                           │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    ❌ Cannot reach 10.96.78.50 from your laptop browser
k8s/clusterip-service.yaml — anihpj internal Service 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-svc
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  type: ClusterIP                         # Default — can omit this line
  selector:
    app: anihpj                           # Must match pod labels!
  ports:
    - name: http
      protocol: TCP
      port: 8000                          # Service listens on this port
      targetPort: 8000                    # Forward to container port
Verify the Service is working 
# Create and check the Service
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/clusterip-service.yaml
$ kubectl get svc -n anihpj-app
NAME          TYPE        CLUSTER-IP     EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)    AGE
anihpj-svc    ClusterIP   10.96.78.50    <none>         8000/TCP   5s

# Verify endpoints (must show pod IPs!)
$ kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc -n anihpj-app
NAME          ENDPOINTS                       AGE
anihpj-svc    10.0.1.5:8000,10.0.1.6:8000    5s

# Test from inside the cluster
$ kubectl exec -it debug-pod -- curl http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/api/jobs/
{"jobs": [...]}
💡 Using Different port and targetPort

The Service port and targetPort don't have to match. A common pattern is to expose port 80 on the Service while the container listens on 8000:

  ports:
    - port: 80          # Clients use: curl anihpj-svc:80
      targetPort: 8000  # Forwards to container port 8000

This lets internal consumers use the standard port 80 while Django runs on its default 8000.

13.3 NodePort Service

A NodePort Service opens a specific port (30000-32767) on every node in the cluster. External traffic hitting any node's IP on that port gets routed to the Service, then to a healthy pod.

🚪 NodePort — External Access via Node IP

  YOUR BROWSER (external)
  http://192.168.49.2:30080/jobs/
         │
         ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                   Kubernetes Cluster                     │
  │                                                         │
  │   Node 1 (192.168.49.2)        Node 2 (192.168.49.3)  │
  │   Port 30080 OPEN ✅            Port 30080 OPEN ✅      │
  │         │                              │                │
  │         └──────────┬───────────────────┘                │
  │                    ▼                                     │
  │           ┌──────────────────┐                          │
  │           │  anihpj-svc      │  ClusterIP: 10.96.78.50 │
  │           │  NodePort: 30080 │                          │
  │           └───┬─────────┬────┘                          │
  │               │         │                               │
  │               ▼         ▼                               │
  │          ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐                          │
  │          │anihpj-1│ │anihpj-2│                          │
  │          │ :8000  │ │ :8000  │                          │
  │          └────────┘ └────────┘                          │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
k8s/nodeport-service.yaml — Access anihpj from outside 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-nodeport
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
    - name: http
      protocol: TCP
      port: 8000                          # Service port (internal)
      targetPort: 8000                    # Container port
      nodePort: 30080                     # External port (30000-32767)
Access anihpj from Minikube 
# Get the Minikube node IP
$ minikube ip
192.168.49.2

# Access anihpj in your browser
$ curl http://192.168.49.2:30080/api/jobs/

# Or use Minikube's built-in shortcut
$ minikube service anihpj-nodeport -n anihpj-app --url
http://192.168.49.2:30080
⚠️ NodePort Limitations

NodePort is great for development but not ideal for production: limited port range (30000-32767), no HTTPS, no domain names, and users must know the node IP. Use LoadBalancer or Ingress for production.

🔢 NodePort Three-Port Mapping

  External user → NodeIP:nodePort → Service:port → Pod:targetPort

  curl http://192.168.49.2:30080
                      │
          ┌───────────┴──────────────────────────────────────┐
          │                                                  │
          │   nodePort: 30080    port: 8000    targetPort: 8000
          │   ─────────────    ───────────    ───────────────
          │   Opens on EVERY   Service's     Container's
          │   cluster node     internal port  listening port
          │                                                  │
          │   Accessible from  Used by pods   Where app code │
          │   OUTSIDE cluster  inside cluster actually runs  │
          └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  💡 These don't all have to be the same number!
     nodePort: 30080 → port: 80 → targetPort: 8000

13.4 LoadBalancer Service

A LoadBalancer Service provisions an external load balancer from your cloud provider (AWS ELB, GCP LB, Azure LB). It gets a public IP that routes traffic to your pods — the production way to expose services.

☁️ LoadBalancer — Cloud-Provisioned Public IP

  🌍 INTERNET
  Users browse https://anihpj.example.com
         │
         ▼
  ┌────────────────────┐
  │  Cloud Load        │ Public IP: 52.14.200.130
  │  Balancer          │ (AWS ELB / GCP LB / Azure LB)
  │  (auto-provisioned)│
  └────────┬───────────┘
           │
  ┌────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                   Kubernetes Cluster                  │
  │                                                      │
  │    ┌───────────────────────┐                         │
  │    │  anihpj-lb            │  ClusterIP: 10.96.78.50│
  │    │  (LoadBalancer)       │  External: 52.14.200.130│
  │    └───┬──────────────┬────┘                         │
  │        │              │                              │
  │        ▼              ▼                              │
  │   ┌────────┐    ┌────────┐    ┌────────┐            │
  │   │anihpj-1│    │anihpj-2│    │anihpj-3│            │
  │   │ :8000  │    │ :8000  │    │ :8000  │            │
  │   └────────┘    └────────┘    └────────┘            │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
k8s/loadbalancer-service.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-lb
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
    - name: http
      protocol: TCP
      port: 80                            # Public-facing port
      targetPort: 8000                    # Container port
Check external IP assignment 
# Wait for external IP (may take 1-2 minutes on cloud)
$ kubectl get svc anihpj-lb -n anihpj-app -w
NAME        TYPE           CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP      PORT(S)        AGE
anihpj-lb   LoadBalancer   10.96.78.50   <pending>          80:31234/TCP   10s
anihpj-lb   LoadBalancer   10.96.78.50   52.14.200.130    80:31234/TCP   90s

# On Minikube (no cloud LB), use tunnel:
$ minikube tunnel
# Now EXTERNAL-IP will be assigned
📝 Cost Warning

Each LoadBalancer Service provisions a separate cloud load balancer (~$18-25/month each on AWS/GCP/Azure). If you have 5 services, that's 5 LBs = $100+/month. Use Ingress (Chapter 14) to share one LB across multiple services.

13.5 ExternalName Service

An ExternalName Service creates a DNS CNAME alias for an external service. No proxying, no port mapping — it simply resolves the service name to an external DNS name. Use it to abstract external dependencies.

🔗 ExternalName — DNS Alias to External Service

  📦 anihpj pod
  │
  │  conn = psycopg2.connect(host="database")  # Uses K8s service name
  │         │
  │         ▼
  │  ┌───────────────────────┐
  │  │  Service: database    │   type: ExternalName
  │  │  (ExternalName)       │   externalName: mydb.rds.amazonaws.com
  │  └───────────┬───────────┘
  │              │ CNAME redirect (DNS level)
  │              ▼
  │  ┌───────────────────────────────────┐
  │  │  mydb.rds.amazonaws.com          │  ← External managed database
  │  │  (AWS RDS / Azure DB / Cloud SQL) │     outside the cluster
  │  └───────────────────────────────────┘
  
  ✅ If you migrate to in-cluster DB later, just change the Service type
     to ClusterIP — your app code doesn't change at all!
k8s/externalname-service.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: database
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  type: ExternalName
  externalName: mydb.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com   # No selector needed!

♻️ ExternalName Migration Scenario

  Phase 1: Using managed cloud DB
  ════════════════════════════════
  Service "database" (ExternalName) → mydb.rds.amazonaws.com
  App code: conn = psycopg2.connect(host="database")  ← doesn't change!

  Phase 2: Migrate to in-cluster PostgreSQL
  ═══════════════════════════════════════════
  Change Service "database" from ExternalName → ClusterIP
  Add selector: app: postgres
  App code: conn = psycopg2.connect(host="database")  ← STILL the same!

  ✅ Zero code changes during database migration!

13.6 Headless Services (clusterIP: None)

A Headless Service has no ClusterIP. Instead of load-balancing, DNS returns the individual pod IPs directly. Essential for StatefulSets where each pod needs to be addressed individually (e.g., database replicas).

🎯 Regular vs Headless DNS Resolution

  REGULAR Service (ClusterIP):           HEADLESS Service (clusterIP: None):

  nslookup postgres-svc                   nslookup postgres-headless
  → 10.96.45.120  (1 ClusterIP)          → 10.0.1.10  (pod-0 IP)
                                          → 10.0.2.11  (pod-1 IP)
                                          → 10.0.2.12  (pod-2 IP)

  ┌────────────────┐                      ┌────────────────┐
  │  postgres-svc  │ 10.96.45.120        │ postgres-      │ No ClusterIP!
  │  (ClusterIP)   │                      │ headless       │
  └───┬──────┬─────┘                      └───┬──────┬─────┘
      │      │  random                        │      │  direct to each
      ▼      ▼                                ▼      ▼
  ┌──────┐┌──────┐                       ┌──────┐┌──────┐
  │pg-0  ││pg-1  │                       │pg-0  ││pg-1  │
  └──────┘└──────┘                       └──────┘└──────┘
                                         postgres-0.postgres-headless
                                         postgres-1.postgres-headless
                                         ↑ Each pod addressable by name!
k8s/headless-service.yaml — For StatefulSet pods 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres-headless
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  clusterIP: None                         # ← This makes it headless!
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
    - name: postgres
      port: 5432
      targetPort: 5432
💡 When to Use Headless

Use Headless Services with StatefulSets — where each pod has a unique identity (postgres-0, postgres-1). This enables direct pod-to-pod communication for replication: a replica can find the primary at postgres-0.postgres-headless.

13.7 Multi-Port Services

A single Service can expose multiple ports. Useful when your application listens on more than one port — for example, HTTP + metrics, or HTTP + gRPC.

k8s/multi-port-service.yaml — HTTP + Metrics 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-svc
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
    - name: http                          # Name required for multi-port!
      protocol: TCP
      port: 8000
      targetPort: 8000
    - name: metrics                       # Prometheus scrape endpoint
      protocol: TCP
      port: 9090
      targetPort: 9090
⚠️ Port Names Are Required

When a Service has multiple ports, each port must have a name field. This is required so Kubernetes (and Ingress) can distinguish between them. Single-port Services don't need names but it's good practice to always add one.

13.8 Session Affinity

By default, Services use random/round-robin load balancing. With session affinity, requests from the same client IP always go to the same pod — useful for sticky sessions.

Setting Behavior Use Case
None (default) Random pod per request (round-robin) Stateless apps (REST APIs, microservices)
ClientIP Same client IP → same pod (10,800s timeout) Legacy apps with server-side sessions
Service with session affinity 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-sticky
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  sessionAffinity: ClientIP               # Sticky sessions
  sessionAffinityConfig:
    clientIP:
      timeoutSeconds: 3600                # 1 hour sticky window
  ports:
    - port: 8000
      targetPort: 8000
💡 Django Best Practice

For anihpj, prefer stateless design (session affinity: None). Store sessions in Redis or the database instead of in-memory. This way any pod can handle any request and you can scale freely without sticky sessions.

13.9 Service Type Decision Guide

Choosing the right Service type is critical. Use this decision tree and comparison table to make the right choice for each workload:

🗺️ Service Type Decision Tree

  ┌───────────────────────────────┐
  │  Where does traffic           │
  │  come from?                   │
  └──────────────┬────────────────┘
                 │
       ┌─────────┴──────────┐
       ▼                    ▼
   INTERNAL              EXTERNAL
   (cluster only)        (outside world)
       │                    │
  ┌────┴────────┐    ┌──────┴───────────────┐
  │ Need direct │    │                      │
  │ pod IPs?    │    │ Is it for            │
  │             │    │ development or       │
  │ Yes → HEADLESS   │ production?          │
  │ No  → ClusterIP  │                      │
  └─────────────┘    ┌──────┴────────┐
                     ▼               ▼
                Development      Production
                     │               │
               ┌─────┴─────┐   ┌────┴──────────┐
               │  NodePort  │   │ Need HTTP     │
               │  :30000+   │   │ routing?      │
               └────────────┘   │               │
                           ┌───┴───┐     ┌─────┴──────┐
                           │  No   │     │   Yes      │
                           │       │     │            │
                       LoadBalancer │     │  Ingress   │
                       (1 per svc) │     │  (Ch 14)   │
                       └───────────┘     └────────────┘

  Aliasing an external DNS?  →  ExternalName

📊 Complete Service Type Comparison

Feature ClusterIP NodePort LoadBalancer ExternalName Headless
Accessible from Inside cluster Node IP:Port Public IP DNS CNAME Inside cluster
Load balancing ✅ Yes ✅ Yes ✅ Cloud LB ❌ No ❌ No (direct)
Stable IP ✅ ClusterIP ✅ ClusterIP + NodePort ✅ External IP ❌ No IP ❌ No IP
Cloud cost Free Free 💰 ~$18/mo each Free Free
Use case Internal comms Dev/testing Production External alias StatefulSets

🛠️ Service kubectl Quick Reference

Task Command
List all Services kubectl get svc -n anihpj-app
Service details kubectl describe svc anihpj-svc -n anihpj-app
Check endpoints kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc -n anihpj-app
Expose a Deployment kubectl expose deploy anihpj --port=8000 --type=ClusterIP
Port-forward to local kubectl port-forward svc/anihpj-svc 8000:8000
Delete a Service kubectl delete svc anihpj-svc -n anihpj-app

Chapter 14: Ingress — HTTP/HTTPS Smart Routing

Ingress is a Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) load balancer that provides path-based routing, host-based routing, and TLS termination through a single entry point. Instead of provisioning one LoadBalancer per service, Ingress lets you share one load balancer across all your services.

🚀 Ingress 🔀 Routing 🔒 TLS/SSL 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ What Ingress is and why it saves money over LoadBalancers
  • ✅ Ingress Controllers — the required first step
  • ✅ Path-based, host-based, and combined routing rules
  • ✅ TLS/SSL termination and automatic certificates
  • ✅ Common Ingress annotations for fine-tuning
  • ✅ Complete Ingress setup for the anihpj project

14.1 What is Ingress?

An Ingress is a Kubernetes API object that manages external HTTP/HTTPS access to services. It acts as a smart reverse proxy — routing requests based on URL paths and hostnames to the correct backend service.

💰 Without Ingress vs With Ingress

  ❌ WITHOUT Ingress — 1 LoadBalancer per Service ($$$):

  ┌───────┐   ┌───────┐   ┌───────┐
  │ LB 1  │   │ LB 2  │   │ LB 3  │    3 × $18/mo = $54/mo 😱
  │$18/mo │   │$18/mo │   │$18/mo │
  └───┬───┘   └───┬───┘   └───┬───┘
      │           │           │
  ┌───┴──┐   ┌───┴──┐   ┌───┴──┐
  │ API  │   │Admin │   │ Docs │
  └──────┘   └──────┘   └──────┘


  ✅ WITH Ingress — 1 shared entry point ($):

  ┌────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         1 Ingress Controller       │   1 × $18/mo = $18/mo 🎉
  │                                    │
  │  /api/*    → anihpj-svc:8000      │
  │  /admin/*  → admin-svc:8000       │
  │  /docs/*   → docs-svc:3000        │
  └──┬──────────────┬─────────────┬────┘
     │              │             │
  ┌──┴──┐      ┌───┴──┐     ┌───┴──┐
  │ API │      │Admin │     │ Docs │
  └─────┘      └──────┘     └──────┘

  SAVINGS: $36/month (66% cheaper!) 💪
🔧 What Ingress Provides
  • Path-based routing (/api, /admin)
  • Host-based routing (virtual hosts)
  • TLS/SSL termination (HTTPS)
  • Load balancing across pods
  • URL rewriting and redirects
⚡ Two Parts Required
  • Ingress Resource — YAML rules (routing config)
  • Ingress Controller — the actual proxy (NGINX, Traefik)
  • Without a controller, Ingress resources do nothing!
  • Controller runs as pods in the cluster

14.2 Ingress vs LoadBalancer Service

Both expose services externally, but they operate at different layers and serve different purposes:

Feature LoadBalancer Service Ingress
OSI Layer L4 (TCP/UDP) L7 (HTTP/HTTPS)
Cost 💰 1 cloud LB per service 💵 1 LB shared across all services
Path routing ❌ No (/api, /admin) ✅ Yes
Host routing ❌ No (virtual hosts) ✅ Yes
TLS termination ❌ No (pass-through) ✅ Yes (HTTPS at the edge)
Non-HTTP protocols ✅ TCP, UDP, gRPC ⚠️ HTTP/HTTPS only (usually)
URL rewriting ❌ No ✅ Yes (annotations)
Best for Single service, non-HTTP Multiple HTTP services, web apps
💡 Rule of Thumb

For web applications like anihpj, always use Ingress. Use LoadBalancer only for non-HTTP protocols (databases, message queues, gRPC services) that Ingress can't handle.

14.3 Ingress Controllers — Required First Step

An Ingress resource alone does nothing — you need an Ingress Controller running in the cluster to process the rules. Think of Ingress resources as config files and the controller as the web server that reads them.

⚙️ How the Ingress Controller Works

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                   INGRESS CONTROLLER FLOW                     │
  │                                                               │
  │  1. You create Ingress YAML                                  │
  │     (rules: /api → svc-a, /admin → svc-b)                   │
  │                    │                                          │
  │                    ▼                                          │
  │  2. K8s API stores Ingress resource                          │
  │                    │                                          │
  │                    ▼                                          │
  │  3. Ingress Controller watches for changes                   │
  │     (nginx-ingress-controller pod)                           │
  │                    │                                          │
  │                    ▼                                          │
  │  4. Controller generates NGINX config from rules             │
  │     /api  → proxy_pass http://svc-a-upstream;               │
  │     /admin → proxy_pass http://svc-b-upstream;              │
  │                    │                                          │
  │                    ▼                                          │
  │  5. NGINX reloads config, starts routing traffic             │
  │                                                               │
  │  External request → Controller pod → matched Service → Pod   │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

14.3a NGINX Ingress Controller

The most popular Ingress controller. Battle-tested, widely documented, and works everywhere.

Install NGINX Ingress on Minikube 
# Option 1: Minikube addon (easiest)
$ minikube addons enable ingress

# Verify the controller is running
$ kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx
NAME                                        READY   STATUS
