ingress-nginx-controller-5d88495688-abc12   1/1     Running

# Option 2: Helm install (any cluster)
$ helm repo add ingress-nginx https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx
$ helm install ingress-nginx ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx

14.3b Traefik

Auto-discovery, built-in Let's Encrypt support, and a web dashboard. Default in k3s. Great for automatic HTTPS.

Install Traefik via Helm 
$ helm repo add traefik https://traefik.github.io/charts
$ helm install traefik traefik/traefik

# Traefik auto-discovers Ingress resources
# and can obtain Let's Encrypt certs automatically

14.3c HAProxy Ingress

High-performance option used by enterprises needing advanced TCP/HTTP features, rate limiting, and WAF capabilities.

Install HAProxy Ingress via Helm 
$ helm repo add haproxy-ingress https://haproxy-ingress.github.io/charts
$ helm install haproxy haproxy-ingress/haproxy-ingress

# Handles high concurrency better than NGINX
# Supports TCP passthrough for databases

14.3d AWS ALB Ingress Controller

Provisions AWS Application Load Balancers natively. Best choice for EKS clusters — integrates with AWS WAF, Shield, and ACM certificates.

Install AWS Load Balancer Controller on EKS 
# Requires IAM OIDC provider + IAM policy
$ helm repo add eks https://aws.github.io/eks-charts
$ helm install aws-load-balancer-controller eks/aws-load-balancer-controller \
    -n kube-system \
    --set clusterName=my-cluster

# Uses annotation: alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/scheme: internet-facing

14.3e Istio Ingress Gateway

Part of the Istio service mesh. Provides advanced traffic management, mTLS, and observability. Overkill for simple apps but powerful for microservice architectures.

Install Istio + Gateway 
# Install Istio
$ istioctl install --set profile=demo
$ kubectl label namespace anihpj-app istio-injection=enabled

# Uses Gateway + VirtualService CRDs instead of Ingress
# Provides automatic mTLS between all services

📊 Ingress Controller Comparison

Controller Auto-HTTPS Complexity Best For
NGINX Via cert-manager 🟢 Low General purpose, learning
Traefik ✅ Built-in Let's Encrypt 🟢 Low Auto HTTPS, k3s, small teams
HAProxy Via cert-manager 🟡 Medium Enterprise, high performance
AWS ALB ✅ Via ACM 🟡 Medium AWS EKS only
Istio ✅ mTLS built-in 🔴 High Service mesh, microservices

14.4 Ingress Rules — Routing

Ingress rules define how external HTTP requests are routed to internal Services. You can route by URL path, hostname, or both.

14.4a Path-Based Routing

Route different URL paths to different backend services. All paths share the same domain name.

🔀 Path-Based Routing Flow

  anihpj.local
       │
       ├── /api/jobs/     ──▶  anihpj-svc:8000    (Django API)
       │
       ├── /admin/        ──▶  anihpj-svc:8000    (Django Admin)
       │
       ├── /static/       ──▶  static-svc:80      (Nginx static files)
       │
       └── /              ──▶  frontend-svc:3000   (React/Vue SPA)
k8s/ingress-path.yaml — Path-based routing 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-app
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
    - host: anihpj.local
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /api
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000
          - path: /admin
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: frontend-svc
                port:
                  number: 3000
pathType Behavior Example: path: /api
Prefix Matches URL prefix (with path segments) Matches /api, /api/jobs, /api/jobs/1
Exact Matches exact URL only Matches /api only, NOT /api/jobs
ImplementationSpecific Up to the Ingress controller Controller-dependent behavior

14.4b Host-Based Routing

Route different domain names to different services — like virtual hosting. Each domain can point to a different backend.

🌐 Host-Based Routing Flow

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │              Ingress Controller                    │
  │                                                    │
  │  api.anihpj.com    ──▶  anihpj-svc:8000           │
  │  admin.anihpj.com  ──▶  admin-svc:8000            │
  │  docs.anihpj.com   ──▶  docs-svc:3000             │
  │  *.anihpj.com      ──▶  default-svc:80 (wildcard) │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
k8s/ingress-host.yaml — Host-based routing 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-host-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
    - host: api.anihpj.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000
    - host: admin.anihpj.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: admin-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000

14.4c Combined (Path + Host)

For complex setups, combine host-based and path-based routing in a single Ingress resource:

k8s/ingress-combined.yaml — Host + Path routing 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-combined-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
    - host: api.anihpj.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /v1
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-v1-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000
          - path: /v2
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-v2-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000
    - host: www.anihpj.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: frontend-svc
                port:
                  number: 3000

14.5 TLS/SSL with Ingress

Ingress handles HTTPS termination at the edge — clients connect via HTTPS, and the Ingress controller forwards plain HTTP to your pods internally. This means your application doesn't need to handle TLS certificates.

🔒 TLS Termination at the Ingress

  🌍 Browser (HTTPS)
  https://anihpj.com/api/jobs/
         │
         │  TLS encrypted 🔒
         ▼
  ┌──────────────────────────┐
  │  Ingress Controller      │
  │  (has TLS cert + key)    │  ← HTTPS terminates here
  │                          │
  │  Decrypts → plain HTTP   │
  └──────────┬───────────────┘
             │
             │  plain HTTP (inside cluster — safe)
             ▼
  ┌──────────────────┐
  │  anihpj-svc:8000 │
  │  (ClusterIP)     │
  └────────┬─────────┘
           │
           ▼
  ┌──────────────┐
  │ anihpj pod   │  ← App serves plain HTTP
  │ 🐍 Django     │     No TLS config needed!
  └──────────────┘
Step 1: Create TLS Secret from certificate files 
# Create a TLS secret from your certificate + private key
$ kubectl create secret tls anihpj-tls \
    --cert=tls.crt \
    --key=tls.key \
    -n anihpj-app

# Verify
$ kubectl get secret anihpj-tls -n anihpj-app
NAME          TYPE                DATA   AGE
anihpj-tls    kubernetes.io/tls   2      5s
Step 2: Ingress with TLS configuration 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-tls-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-app
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true"    # Force HTTPS
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  tls:                                     # ← TLS section
    - hosts:
        - anihpj.com
        - www.anihpj.com
      secretName: anihpj-tls              # TLS Secret created above
  rules:
    - host: anihpj.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000
💡 Automatic Certificates with cert-manager

For production, install cert-manager to automatically obtain and renew free TLS certificates from Let's Encrypt. Just add an annotation and cert-manager handles everything:

Install cert-manager + Create a ClusterIssuer 
# Step 1: Install cert-manager with CRDs
$ kubectl apply -f https://github.com/cert-manager/cert-manager/releases/download/v1.14.4/cert-manager.yaml

# Step 2: Verify it's running
$ kubectl get pods -n cert-manager
NAME                                       READY   STATUS
cert-manager-7dd5f5b4d7-abc12             1/1     Running
cert-manager-cainjector-6c9f8b5d8-xyz34   1/1     Running
cert-manager-webhook-5c4f9b6d7-def56      1/1     Running
k8s/cluster-issuer.yaml — Let's Encrypt production issuer 
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: ClusterIssuer
metadata:
  name: letsencrypt-prod
spec:
  acme:
    server: https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory
    email: admin@anihpj.com
    privateKeySecretRef:
      name: letsencrypt-prod-key
    solvers:
      - http01:
          ingress:
            class: nginx
Automatic HTTPS with cert-manager + Let's Encrypt 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-auto-tls
  annotations:
    cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: "letsencrypt-prod"  # ← auto-cert!
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true"
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  tls:
    - hosts:
        - anihpj.com
      secretName: anihpj-tls-auto         # cert-manager creates this!
  rules:
    - host: anihpj.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000

14.6 Ingress Annotations — Controller-Specific Config

Annotations let you fine-tune controller behavior — rewrites, redirects, rate limiting, CORS headers, and more. These are controller-specific (NGINX annotations won't work on Traefik).

📋 Common NGINX Ingress Annotations

Annotation Purpose Example Value
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target Rewrite URL path before forwarding /
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect Force HTTP → HTTPS redirect "true"
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-body-size Max upload size (Django file uploads) "10m"
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-read-timeout Backend read timeout (seconds) "60"
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/enable-cors Enable CORS headers "true"
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/limit-rps Rate limit (requests per second) "10"
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/use-regex Enable regex in path matching "true"
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/affinity Session affinity (sticky sessions) "cookie"
Ingress with Django-specific annotations 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-app
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-body-size: "10m"      # Resume uploads
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-read-timeout: "60"    # Long requests
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/enable-cors: "true"         # API CORS
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/limit-rps: "10"             # Rate limiting
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  tls:
    - hosts: ["anihpj.com"]
      secretName: anihpj-tls
  rules:
    - host: anihpj.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000

🔀 Understanding rewrite-target — The Tricky One

  WITHOUT rewrite-target:
  ═══════════════════════
  Request: /api/jobs/
  Backend receives: /api/jobs/        ← Django sees the full path ✅
  (Usually what you want for Django!)

  WITH rewrite-target: /
  ═══════════════════════
  Request: /api/jobs/
  Backend receives: /jobs/            ← /api stripped! Django gets /jobs/
  (Useful when backend doesn't expect the path prefix)

  WITH rewrite-target using regex capture:
  ═══════════════════════════════════════════
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /$2
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/use-regex: "true"
  path: /api(/|$)(.*)

  Request: /api/jobs/1    → Backend receives: /jobs/1
  Request: /api/admin/    → Backend receives: /admin/
  Request: /api           → Backend receives: /

  ⚠️  For Django apps: usually DON'T use rewrite-target.
      Django's URL routing expects the full path as configured.

14.7 Setting Up Ingress for anihpj — Complete Walkthrough

Let's set up a working Ingress for anihpj on Minikube — from enabling the addon to browsing anihpj.local in your browser.

🏗️ Complete anihpj Ingress Architecture

  🌍 http://anihpj.local
         │
         │  (browser / curl)
         ▼
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                Minikube Ingress (NGINX)                    │
  │                                                            │
  │   anihpj.local/api/*    ──▶  anihpj-svc:8000 (Django)    │
  │   anihpj.local/admin/*  ──▶  anihpj-svc:8000 (Django)    │
  │   anihpj.local/static/* ──▶  anihpj-svc:8000 (Django)    │
  │   anihpj.local/jobs/*   ──▶  anihpj-svc:8000 (Django)    │
  └──────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
                         │
                         ▼
                ┌──────────────────┐
                │  anihpj-svc      │ ClusterIP: 10.96.78.50
                │  (ClusterIP)     │ Port: 8000
                └────┬────────┬────┘
                     │        │
                     ▼        ▼
               ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐
               │anihpj-1│ │anihpj-2│
               │ :8000  │ │ :8000  │
               └────────┘ └────────┘
Step 1: Enable Ingress addon in Minikube 
# Enable the NGINX Ingress Controller
$ minikube addons enable ingress

# Wait for the controller pod to be ready
$ kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx -w
NAME                                        READY   STATUS    AGE
ingress-nginx-controller-5d88495688-abc12   1/1     Running   30s
Step 2: Create the anihpj Ingress resource 
# k8s/ingress.yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-app
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-body-size: "10m"
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
    - host: anihpj.local
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000
Step 3: Apply and get the Ingress IP 
# Apply the Ingress
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/ingress.yaml

# Check the Ingress and get the ADDRESS
$ kubectl get ingress -n anihpj-app
NAME             CLASS   HOSTS          ADDRESS         PORTS   AGE
anihpj-ingress   nginx   anihpj.local   192.168.49.2    80      10s

# The ADDRESS is the Minikube IP
Step 4: Update hosts file (map domain to IP) 
# Windows — Run as Administrator:
# Edit C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts
# Add this line:
192.168.49.2    anihpj.local

# macOS / Linux:
$ sudo sh -c 'echo "192.168.49.2 anihpj.local" >> /etc/hosts'
Step 5: Access anihpj in your browser! 
# Test with curl
$ curl http://anihpj.local/api/jobs/
{"jobs": [{"id": 1, "title": "Django Developer"}, ...]}

# Or open in browser:
# → http://anihpj.local/admin/
# → http://anihpj.local/api/jobs/

# Verify traffic is hitting the right pods
$ kubectl logs -f -l app=anihpj -n anihpj-app
[INFO] GET /api/jobs/ 200 OK
📝 Ingress Debugging Tips
  • kubectl describe ingress anihpj-ingress -n anihpj-app — check rules and backend status
  • kubectl logs -n ingress-nginx -l app.kubernetes.io/name=ingress-nginx — controller logs
  • If ADDRESS stays empty → run minikube tunnel or check the controller pod
  • 404 errors → path/host mismatch or backend Service not found
  • 503 errors → backend pods not ready or endpoints empty

🛠️ Ingress kubectl Quick Reference

Task Command
List all Ingresses kubectl get ingress -n anihpj-app
Ingress details & rules kubectl describe ingress anihpj-ingress -n anihpj-app
Controller logs kubectl logs -n ingress-nginx -l app.kubernetes.io/name=ingress-nginx
Check controller pods kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx
Delete Ingress kubectl delete ingress anihpj-ingress -n anihpj-app
Enable Minikube Ingress minikube addons enable ingress

📈 Part 5: Scaling & Reliability Chapters 15-17

Chapter 15: Scaling Applications

Learn how Kubernetes automatically adjusts your application's capacity to match demand. From manual replica scaling to fully automated HPA, VPA, and Cluster Autoscaler — this chapter gives you the tools to handle traffic spikes without breaking a sweat.

📈 Scaling 🤖 Autoscaling ⚖️ HPA / VPA 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why scaling matters and what happens when traffic spikes
  • ✅ Horizontal vs vertical scaling — and why K8s prefers horizontal
  • ✅ Manual scaling with kubectl scale
  • ✅ Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) — CPU, memory, and custom metrics
  • ✅ Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) — right-sizing containers
  • ✅ Cluster Autoscaler — scaling the nodes themselves
  • ✅ Pod Disruption Budgets (PDB) — safe voluntary disruptions

15.1 Why Scaling Matters

Imagine your anihpj job board gets featured on Hacker News. Traffic spikes 10× in minutes. Without scaling, your users see 503 errors, slow responses, and crashes. With Kubernetes scaling, pods are added automatically, traffic is absorbed, and pods scale back down when the rush is over.

📊 Traffic Spike Without vs With Scaling

  ❌ WITHOUT SCALING                        ✅ WITH KUBERNETES SCALING
  ─────────────────────                     ─────────────────────────
  Traffic: ████████████████ 10x             Traffic: ████████████████ 10x
  Pods:    📦📦📦 (fixed 3)                 Pods:    📦📦📦📦📦📦📦📦📦📦 (auto 10)
  Result:  🔴 503 errors, crashes           Result:  🟢 All requests served
                                            
  Response                                  Response
  Time (ms)                                 Time (ms)
  3000│     ╱╲                              200│ ──────────────────
  2000│   ╱    ╲                            150│
  1000│ ╱        ╲           (crash!)       100│
   200│╱           ╲──── dead                50│
      └──────────────────▶ time                └──────────────────▶ time
       normal  spike  down                      normal  spike  normal

  💡 Scaling = automatically matching capacity to demand
📝 Real-World Impact

Companies like Spotify and Airbnb handle millions of requests by auto-scaling pods from 10 to 1000+ during peak hours, then scaling back to save costs. Kubernetes makes this native — no custom scripts needed.

15.2 Horizontal vs Vertical Scaling

There are two fundamental approaches to scaling. Horizontal means adding more pod replicas — Kubernetes' preferred method. Vertical means giving individual pods more CPU/memory. Each has trade-offs.

↔️ Horizontal vs ↕️ Vertical — Visual Comparison

  HORIZONTAL SCALING (Scale Out)             VERTICAL SCALING (Scale Up)
  Add more small pods                        Make one pod bigger

  Before:  ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐                   Before:  ┌─────┐
           │📦│ │📦│ │📦│                            │ 📦  │
           └──┘ └──┘ └──┘                            └─────┘
            3 pods × 256Mi each                       1 pod × 256Mi

  After:   ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐         After:   ┌──────────┐
           │📦│ │📦│ │📦│ │📦│ │📦│                  │  📦📦📦  │
           └──┘ └──┘ └──┘ └──┘ └──┘                  └──────────┘
            5 pods × 256Mi each (+2)                   1 pod × 1Gi (+3×)

  ✅ No single point of failure              ⚠️ Single point of failure
  ✅ No downtime to add pods                 ⚠️ May need pod restart
  ✅ Practically unlimited                   ❌ Capped by node resources
Aspect Horizontal ✅ Vertical ⚠️
Mechanism Add more pod replicas Give pod more CPU/RAM
Downtime None — add pods live May need pod restart
Max Limit Practically unlimited Node resource ceiling
Failure Impact Lose 1 of 5 pods (20%) Lose 1 of 1 pod (100%)
K8s Native HPA (built-in) VPA (add-on)
Best For Stateless apps (web servers) Stateful apps (databases)
💡 Rule of Thumb

Always prefer horizontal scaling for stateless workloads like your anihpj Django app. Reserve vertical scaling for stateful workloads (databases) where you can't simply add replicas. Kubernetes was designed ground-up for horizontal scaling.

15.3 Manual Scaling

The simplest way to scale — manually set the number of replicas. Useful for predictable workloads or one-time adjustments before a known traffic event (like a product launch).

Manual Scaling Commands 
# Scale anihpj from current replicas to 5
kubectl scale deployment anihpj --replicas=5 -n anihpj-app

# Watch pods spin up in real-time
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -w

# Output:
# NAME                      READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-abc12   1/1     Running   0          2d
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-def34   1/1     Running   0          2d
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-ghi56   1/1     Running   0          2d
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-jkl78   0/1     Pending   0          3s   ◀── new
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-mno90   0/1     Pending   0          3s   ◀── new

# Scale back down
kubectl scale deployment anihpj --replicas=3 -n anihpj-app

# Or edit the YAML directly
kubectl edit deployment anihpj -n anihpj-app
# Change spec.replicas: 5
k8s/deployment-scaled.yaml — Declarative Scaling 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  replicas: 5                    # ← change this number to scale
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:latest
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 250m
              memory: 256Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 512Mi
📝 Imperative vs Declarative

kubectl scale is imperative (quick but not tracked in Git). For production, always update spec.replicas in your YAML and apply — this way your scaling decisions are version-controlled and reproducible.

15.4 Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)

The HPA automatically adjusts the number of pod replicas based on observed metrics (CPU, memory, or custom). It's the workhorse of Kubernetes autoscaling — check metrics every 15 seconds, calculate the desired replica count, and scale.

How HPA Works

The HPA runs a continuous control loop every 15 seconds. It fetches metrics from the Metrics Server, compares them against your target, and calculates the ideal replica count using a simple formula.

🔄 HPA Control Loop — Continuous Feedback Cycle

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                     HPA CONTROL LOOP                            │
  │                                                                  │
  │      ┌──────────────┐  every 15s   ┌───────────────────┐        │
  │ ┌───▶│ Metrics      │─────────────▶│ HPA Controller    │        │
  │ │    │ Server       │              │                   │        │
  │ │    │              │              │ target: 50% CPU   │        │
  │ │    │ CPU: 85%     │              │ current: 85% CPU  │        │
  │ │    │ Mem: 60%     │              │ replicas: 3       │        │
  │ │    └──────────────┘              │ → SCALE UP!       │        │
  │ │                                   └────────┬──────────┘        │
  │ │                                            │                   │
  │ │    ┌──────────────────────┐                │                   │
  │ │    │   anihpj pods        │◀───────────────┘                   │
  │ │    │                      │   adjust replicas                  │
  │ │    │ 📦📦📦  →  📦📦📦📦📦  │   3 → 5 pods                      │
  │ │    └──────────────────────┘                                    │
  │ │              │                                                  │
  │ └──────────────┘  metrics feed back into the loop                │
  │                                                                  │
  │  📐 Formula:                                                     │
  │  desiredReplicas = ⌈currentReplicas × (currentMetric / target)⌉ │
  │  Example: ⌈3 × (85% / 50%)⌉ = ⌈5.1⌉ = 6 pods                  │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📝 Prerequisite: Metrics Server

HPA requires the Metrics Server to be installed in your cluster. On Minikube: minikube addons enable metrics-server. On cloud providers it's usually pre-installed. Without it, HPA cannot read CPU/memory usage.

⬆️ Scale-Up Behavior

Happens immediately when metrics exceed target. New pods are created within seconds. Stabilization window: 0 seconds (default) — fast reaction to traffic spikes.

⬇️ Scale-Down Behavior

Delayed by a 5-minute stabilization window (default). Prevents flapping — won't scale down immediately after a brief dip. Configurable via behavior.scaleDown.

CPU-Based Autoscaling

The most common autoscaling metric. Target a specific average CPU utilization across all pods — HPA adds pods when average CPU goes above the target and removes pods when it drops below.

Quick: Create HPA via kubectl 
# Create HPA: target 50% CPU, min 2 pods, max 10 pods
kubectl autoscale deployment anihpj \
  --cpu-percent=50 \
  --min=2 \
  --max=10 \
  -n anihpj-app

# Check HPA status
kubectl get hpa -n anihpj-app
# NAME     REFERENCE           TARGETS   MINPODS   MAXPODS   REPLICAS   AGE
# anihpj  Deployment/anihpj   23%/50%   2         10        3          5m
k8s/hpa-cpu.yaml — HPA (CPU-Based) 
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-hpa
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj                       # Target deployment
  minReplicas: 2                       # Never go below 2 pods
  maxReplicas: 10                      # Never exceed 10 pods
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 50       # Target: 50% average CPU
  behavior:
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300  # Wait 5 min before scaling down
      policies:
        - type: Percent
          value: 50                    # Scale down max 50% at a time
          periodSeconds: 60
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0    # Scale up immediately
      policies:
        - type: Percent
          value: 100                   # Can double pods instantly
          periodSeconds: 60
💡 Why 50% CPU Target?

Setting the target to 50% gives you headroom for bursts. If you set it to 90%, pods would already be near full capacity when a spike hits — scaling wouldn't happen fast enough. 50% means pods typically run at half capacity, so they can absorb a sudden 2× spike while HPA provisions new pods.

Memory-Based Autoscaling

Scale based on memory utilization instead of (or in addition to) CPU. Useful for applications with memory-heavy workloads like caching, data processing, or Django apps with large querysets.

k8s/hpa-memory.yaml — HPA (Memory-Based) 
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-hpa-memory
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: memory
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 70       # Scale when avg memory > 70%
⚠️ Memory Scaling Caveat

Memory-based scaling doesn't work as cleanly as CPU. Memory often doesn't decrease after load drops — applications hold onto allocated memory. This means HPA may scale up but never scale back down. For this reason, CPU-based autoscaling is generally preferred. Use memory scaling as a secondary metric.

Custom Metrics Autoscaling

Scale on business-relevant metrics like HTTP requests per second, queue depth, or active connections. This requires a metrics adapter (typically the Prometheus Adapter) to expose custom metrics to the HPA.

k8s/hpa-custom.yaml — HPA (Custom Metrics) 
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-hpa-custom
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
    # Scale on HTTP requests per second per pod
    - type: Pods
      pods:
        metric:
          name: http_requests_per_second   # From Prometheus adapter
        target:
          type: AverageValue
          averageValue: 100                # Target: 100 req/s per pod
    # Also keep CPU in check
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 60
📡 HTTP Requests/sec

Scale when each pod handles more than N requests per second. Best for web apps like anihpj.

📬 Queue Depth

Scale when a message queue (Redis, RabbitMQ) exceeds N pending jobs. Best for worker pods.

🔗 Active Connections

Scale on WebSocket or DB connection count. Best for real-time or connection-heavy apps.

Testing HPA

The best way to validate your HPA is to generate artificial load and watch it react in real time. Here's a full walkthrough using anihpj:

Load Test: HPA in Action 
# ① Terminal 1: Watch HPA status
kubectl get hpa anihpj-hpa -n anihpj-app -w
# NAME          REFERENCE           TARGETS   MINPODS   MAXPODS   REPLICAS
# anihpj-hpa   Deployment/anihpj   12%/50%   2         10        2

# ② Terminal 2: Generate load — hammer the /jobs/ endpoint
kubectl run load-generator --image=busybox --restart=Never -- \
  /bin/sh -c "while true; do wget -q -O- http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/jobs/; done"

# ③ Watch HPA react (Terminal 1 output over time):
# anihpj-hpa   Deployment/anihpj   12%/50%   2   10   2     ← idle
# anihpj-hpa   Deployment/anihpj   67%/50%   2   10   2     ← load detected!
# anihpj-hpa   Deployment/anihpj   67%/50%   2   10   4     ← scaling up!
# anihpj-hpa   Deployment/anihpj   52%/50%   2   10   5     ← still adjusting
# anihpj-hpa   Deployment/anihpj   38%/50%   2   10   5     ← stabilized ✅

# ④ Stop the load
kubectl delete pod load-generator

# ⑤ After ~5 min stabilization, HPA scales back down
# anihpj-hpa   Deployment/anihpj   8%/50%    2   10   2     ← scaled down ✅

📈 HPA Reaction Timeline

  Replicas
  10│
   8│
   6│                    ┌────────┐
   5│               ┌────┘        └──────────┐
   4│          ┌────┘                         │
   3│     ┌────┘                              │
   2│─────┘                                   └────────────── 2
   1│
    └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────▶ time
     idle    load     scaling    peak     cooldown   idle
             starts   up                  (5 min)

  ■ Scale-up: immediate (0s stabilization)
  ■ Scale-down: delayed (300s stabilization window)

15.5 Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA)

VPA adjusts CPU and memory requests and limits of individual containers, rather than changing the number of replicas. It watches actual usage, learns patterns, and recommends (or applies) optimal resource values.

👁️ Off Mode

VPA calculates recommendations but doesn't apply them. Use kubectl describe vpa to read suggestions. Safest mode to start with.

🚀 Initial Mode

VPA sets resources only at pod creation time. Existing pods are not restarted. Good for new deployments.

⚡ Auto Mode

VPA actively adjusts resources and restarts pods to apply new values. Most aggressive — use carefully in production.

k8s/vpa-anihpj.yaml — Vertical Pod Autoscaler 
apiVersion: autoscaling.k8s.io/v1
kind: VerticalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-vpa
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  targetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj
  updatePolicy:
    updateMode: "Off"                   # Start with recommendations only
  resourcePolicy:
    containerPolicies:
      - containerName: anihpj
        minAllowed:
          cpu: 100m
          memory: 128Mi
        maxAllowed:
          cpu: 2
          memory: 2Gi
Reading VPA Recommendations 
# Check what VPA recommends
kubectl describe vpa anihpj-vpa -n anihpj-app

# Output:
# Recommendation:
#   Container Recommendations:
#     Container Name:  anihpj
#     Lower Bound:     Cpu: 100m, Memory: 192Mi
#     Target:          Cpu: 250m, Memory: 384Mi     ◀── use these!
#     Upper Bound:     Cpu: 1,    Memory: 1Gi
#     Uncapped Target: Cpu: 250m, Memory: 384Mi
⚠️ VPA + HPA Conflict

Do NOT use VPA and HPA on the same metric (CPU or memory) simultaneously. They'll fight each other — VPA changes resource requests, which changes HPA's percentage calculations. Use them together only when HPA scales on custom metrics and VPA handles CPU/memory.

15.6 Cluster Autoscaler

While HPA and VPA scale pods, the Cluster Autoscaler scales nodes. When pods can't be scheduled because no node has enough resources, the Cluster Autoscaler asks your cloud provider to add more machines. When nodes are underutilized, it removes them to save cost.

🔗 The Three-Level Autoscaling Pyramid

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │            🏔️ KUBERNETES AUTOSCALING PYRAMID              │
  │                                                            │
  │  Level 3:   ┌──────────────────────────┐                   │
  │  CLUSTER    │   Cluster Autoscaler     │  Nodes ±          │
  │             │   "Need more machines"   │                   │
  │             └────────────┬─────────────┘                   │
  │                          │                                  │
  │  Level 2:   ┌────────────▼─────────────┐                   │
  │  POD (H)    │   HPA                    │  Pods ±           │
  │             │   "Need more replicas"   │                   │
  │             └────────────┬─────────────┘                   │
  │                          │                                  │
  │  Level 1:   ┌────────────▼─────────────┐                   │
  │  POD (V)    │   VPA                    │  CPU/Mem ±        │
  │             │   "Need bigger pods"     │                   │
  │             └──────────────────────────┘                   │
  │                                                            │
  │  🔄 Trigger chain:                                         │
  │  Traffic ↑ → Pods need more CPU → HPA adds pods            │
  │  → Node full → Cluster Autoscaler adds node                │
  │  → Traffic ↓ → HPA removes pods → Node empty → Node removed│
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🔄 Cluster Autoscaler Flow

  SCALE UP:
  ┌──────────┐     ┌───────────┐     ┌────────────────┐     ┌──────────┐
  │ HPA adds │────▶│ Pod stuck │────▶│ Cluster        │────▶│ Cloud    │
  │ pods     │     │ Pending   │     │ Autoscaler     │     │ Provider │
  └──────────┘     │ (no room) │     │ detects gap    │     │ adds node│
                   └───────────┘     └────────────────┘     └──────────┘

  SCALE DOWN:
  ┌──────────┐     ┌───────────┐     ┌────────────────┐     ┌──────────┐
  │ HPA      │────▶│ Pods      │────▶│ Cluster        │────▶│ Cloud    │
  │ removes  │     │ moved to  │     │ Autoscaler     │     │ Provider │
  │ pods     │     │ other     │     │ detects empty  │     │ removes  │
  └──────────┘     │ nodes     │     │ node (10 min)  │     │ node     │
                   └───────────┘     └────────────────┘     └──────────┘
Cloud Provider Cluster Autoscaler Setup Node Group Config
EKS (AWS) Cluster Autoscaler Helm chart + IAM role for service account Managed Node Groups with min/max sizes
GKE (Google) Built-in — enable via --enable-autoscaling Node pool autoscaling (min/max nodes)
AKS (Azure) Built-in — enable via --enable-cluster-autoscaler Node pools with --min-count / --max-count
Minikube Not applicable (single-node development cluster) Use HPA/VPA only for local testing

15.7 Pod Disruption Budgets (PDB)

A PDB limits how many pods can be voluntarily disrupted at the same time during operations like node drains, cluster upgrades, or autoscaler scale-downs. It ensures your application stays available during planned maintenance.

🔢 minAvailable

Always keep at least N pods (or N%) running. Example: minAvailable: 2 — even during a node drain, at least 2 anihpj pods must stay up.

🚫 maxUnavailable

At most N pods (or N%) can be down simultaneously. Example: maxUnavailable: 1 — only 1 anihpj pod can be disrupted at a time.

k8s/pdb-anihpj.yaml — Pod Disruption Budget 
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pdb
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  minAvailable: 2                        # Always keep ≥2 pods running
  # OR use: maxUnavailable: 1            # At most 1 pod down at a time
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj                        # Targets anihpj pods

🛡️ PDB in Action: Node Drain Scenario

  PDB: minAvailable: 2 (always keep ≥ 2 anihpj pods running)
  anihpj has 3 replicas spread across 2 nodes

  Node 1 (being drained)        Node 2
  ┌────────────────────┐        ┌────────────────────┐
  │ 📦 anihpj-abc      │        │ 📦 anihpj-def      │
  │ 📦 anihpj-ghi      │        │                    │
  └────────────────────┘        └────────────────────┘

  Admin runs: kubectl drain node-1

  Step 1: K8s checks PDB — need ≥ 2 pods running
  Step 2: Evict anihpj-abc (still 2 running: ghi + def) ✅ 
  Step 3: Wait for anihpj-abc to be rescheduled on Node 2
  Step 4: New pod ready on Node 2 (now 3 running)
  Step 5: Evict anihpj-ghi (still 2 running: def + new) ✅

  Result: At no point were fewer than 2 pods running!

  WITHOUT PDB: both pods on Node 1 evicted at once → only 1 pod left → outage risk!
💡 PDB Best Practices
  • Always create PDBs for production deployments with ≥ 2 replicas
  • Use minAvailable when you know the minimum safe count
  • Use maxUnavailable when the replica count varies (with HPA)
  • PDBs only protect against voluntary disruptions (drains, upgrades). They do NOT protect against crashes or node failures
  • Don't set minAvailable equal to your total replicas — it would block all drains!
📝 Chapter 15 Quick Reference
Command / Resource Purpose
kubectl scale deployment anihpj --replicas=5 Manual scaling
kubectl autoscale deployment anihpj --cpu-percent=50 --min=2 --max=10 Create HPA
kubectl get hpa -w Watch HPA status
kubectl describe vpa anihpj-vpa Read VPA recommendations
kubectl get pdb List Pod Disruption Budgets
kubectl top pods Check resource usage (needs metrics-server)

Chapter 16: Health Checks and Self-Healing

Kubernetes can automatically detect unhealthy containers and take corrective action — restarting crashed processes, removing broken pods from load balancers, and waiting for slow apps to start. All powered by three types of probes you configure in your pod spec.

❤️ Health Checks 🔄 Self-Healing 🔍 Probes 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why health checks prevent silent failures in production
  • ✅ The three probe types: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup
  • ✅ HTTP GET, TCP Socket, and Command Exec probe mechanisms
  • ✅ Readiness probes for zero-downtime deployments
  • ✅ Startup probes for slow-starting applications
  • ✅ Probe configuration parameters and timing
  • ✅ Complete probe implementation for anihpj
  • ✅ Common probe mistakes to avoid

16.1 Why Health Checks Matter

Without health checks, Kubernetes only knows if your container process is running — not if it's actually working correctly. A Python process can be alive but stuck in a deadlock, connected to the wrong database, or returning 500 errors on every request.

❌ Without Probes

Django app connects to wrong DB hostname → app runs but returns 500 errors on every request → Kubernetes thinks pod is healthy (process is running) → traffic keeps routing → all users see errors for hours

✅ With Probes

Readiness probe checks /readyz → DB connection test fails → K8s removes pod from Service endpoints → no traffic routed to broken pod → liveness probe triggers restart → new pod connects correctly → zero user impact

🔄 Self-Healing Flow — How Probes Protect Users

  User Request → Service Load Balancer → Pod Endpoint
                                          │
                                    ┌─────▼──────┐
                                    │ Pod anihpj  │
                                    │             │
                                    │  Readiness  │──── FAIL ──▶ Remove from
                                    │  Probe ❌   │              Service endpoints
                                    │             │              (no traffic)
                                    │  Liveness   │
                                    │  Probe ❌   │──── FAIL ──▶ Restart container
                                    │             │              (kill → recreate)
                                    └─────────────┘
                                          │
                                    After restart:
                                    ┌─────────────┐
                                    │ Pod anihpj  │
                                    │             │
                                    │  Readiness ✅│──── PASS ──▶ Add back to
                                    │  Liveness ✅ │              Service endpoints
                                    │             │              (traffic resumes)
                                    └─────────────┘

16.2 The Three Probe Types

Kubernetes offers three distinct probes, each answering a different question about your container's health. They work together to cover the full application lifecycle — starting up, staying alive, and being ready for traffic.

Probe Question It Answers On Failure When It Runs
❤️ Liveness "Is the process alive and not deadlocked?" Container is restarted Continuously, after startup completes
🟢 Readiness "Can it handle user traffic right now?" Removed from Service endpoints Continuously, after startup completes
🚀 Startup "Has it finished starting up?" Liveness & Readiness wait Only during initial startup

⏱️ Probe Timeline — When Each Probe Runs

  Time →  0s         10s        20s        30s        40s  ...
          │           │           │           │           │
  Startup ████████████████████████ ✅ done!
          │  (checking if app started)       │           │
          │                                  │           │
  Liveness                                   ████████████████ ... (forever)
          │                          (starts after startup succeeds)
          │                                  │           │
  Readiness                                  ████████████████ ... (forever)
          │                          (starts after startup succeeds)
          │                                  │           │

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  🔑 Key Insight:                                             │
  │  Startup probe BLOCKS liveness and readiness until it passes│
  │  This prevents liveness from killing slow-starting apps!    │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
❤️ Liveness

"Still alive?"
Fail → 🔄 RESTART pod
Use: GET /healthz
Check: internal process health

🟢 Readiness

"Ready for traffic?"
Fail → 🚫 REMOVE from Service
Use: GET /readyz
Check: DB, cache, dependencies

🚀 Startup

"Done booting?"
Fail → ⏳ WAIT (block probes)
Use: GET /startupz
Check: migrations, warmup

16.3 Liveness Probes — "Are You Still Alive?"

Liveness probes detect when a container is running but broken — deadlocks, infinite loops, corrupted state. Kubernetes restarts the container to recover. There are three probe mechanisms:

HTTP GET Probe

The most common type. Kubernetes sends an HTTP GET request to a specified endpoint. A response code between 200-399 means healthy, anything else means unhealthy → restart.

anihpj/jobpost/views.py — Health Endpoint 
# Simple liveness endpoint — only checks if Django process is alive
from django.http import JsonResponse

def healthz(request):
    """Liveness probe: is the process alive and responding?"""
    return JsonResponse({"status": "alive"}, status=200)
k8s/liveness-http.yaml — HTTP GET Liveness Probe 
containers:
  - name: anihpj
    image: anihpj:latest
    ports:
      - containerPort: 8000
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz              # Endpoint to check
        port: 8000                  # Container port
        httpHeaders:                # Optional custom headers
          - name: Host
            value: anihpj.local
      initialDelaySeconds: 10       # Wait 10s after start
      periodSeconds: 15             # Check every 15 seconds
      timeoutSeconds: 3             # Timeout per check
      failureThreshold: 3           # 3 failures → restart
      successThreshold: 1           # 1 success → healthy

TCP Socket Probe

Kubernetes attempts a TCP connection to the specified port. If the connection succeeds, the container is alive. Useful for non-HTTP services like databases, Redis, or custom TCP servers.

k8s/liveness-tcp.yaml — TCP Socket Liveness 
containers:
  - name: anihpj
    image: anihpj:latest
    ports:
      - containerPort: 8000
    livenessProbe:
      tcpSocket:
        port: 8000                  # Try to connect to this port
      initialDelaySeconds: 10
      periodSeconds: 15
      failureThreshold: 3
📝 When to Use TCP vs HTTP

Use TCP probes for services that don't speak HTTP (PostgreSQL on port 5432, Redis on port 6379). For web apps like anihpj that serve HTTP, prefer HTTP GET probes because they validate the application layer, not just that the port is open.

Command Execution Probe

Kubernetes runs a command inside the container. Exit code 0 = healthy, any other exit code = unhealthy. Useful for complex checks that can't be expressed as HTTP or TCP.

k8s/liveness-exec.yaml — Command Execution Liveness 
containers:
  - name: anihpj
    image: anihpj:latest
    livenessProbe:
      exec:
        command:
          - python
          - manage.py
          - check                   # Django system check
          - --deploy                # Include deployment checks
      initialDelaySeconds: 15
      periodSeconds: 30             # Less frequent — exec is heavier
      timeoutSeconds: 5
      failureThreshold: 3
Probe Mechanism Check Method Best For Overhead
HTTP GET HTTP request → check status code Web apps (Django, Flask, Express) Low
TCP Socket TCP connection → check port open Databases, Redis, gRPC Very Low
Exec Command Run command → check exit code Complex custom checks High (spawns process)

16.4 Readiness Probes — "Can You Handle Requests?"

Readiness probes determine whether a pod should receive traffic. Unlike liveness, a failed readiness probe doesn't restart the pod — it removes the pod from Service endpoints. Once the probe passes again, the pod is re-added. This is crucial for zero-downtime deployments and dependency failures.

🚦 Readiness Probe — Traffic Flow Impact

  READINESS = PASS ✅                    READINESS = FAIL ❌
  ─────────────────                      ─────────────────
  Service Endpoints:                     Service Endpoints:
  ┌──────────────────────┐               ┌──────────────────────┐
  │ • anihpj-abc (10.0.1.5) │            │ • anihpj-abc (10.0.1.5) │
  │ • anihpj-def (10.0.1.6) │            │ (anihpj-def removed!) │
  │ • anihpj-ghi (10.0.1.7) │            │ • anihpj-ghi (10.0.1.7) │
  └──────────────────────┘               └──────────────────────┘
  Traffic → all 3 pods                   Traffic → only 2 pods
                                         anihpj-def still runs but
                                         gets NO traffic until ready
anihpj/jobpost/views.py — Readiness Endpoint 
from django.http import JsonResponse
from django.db import connection

def readyz(request):
    """Readiness probe: can we serve user requests?"""
    try:
        # Check database connectivity
        with connection.cursor() as cursor:
            cursor.execute("SELECT 1")
        
        return JsonResponse({
            "status": "ready",
            "database": "connected"
        }, status=200)
    except Exception as e:
        return JsonResponse({
            "status": "not_ready",
            "error": str(e)
        }, status=503)                       # 503 → not ready
k8s/readiness-probe.yaml 
containers:
  - name: anihpj
    image: anihpj:latest
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /readyz
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 5          # Start checking after 5s
      periodSeconds: 10               # Check every 10 seconds
      timeoutSeconds: 3
      failureThreshold: 3             # 3 failures → remove from LB
      successThreshold: 2             # Need 2 consecutive passes to
                                      # be added back (prevents flapping)
💡 Use Cases for Readiness Probes
  • During deployment: New pods aren't added to Service until they're fully initialized
  • Database failover: If DB goes down, pods are removed from traffic until DB reconnects
  • Cache warmup: Pod needs to pre-load data before serving requests
  • Dependency checks: Verify Redis, external APIs, or message queues are reachable

16.5 Startup Probes — "Have You Finished Starting?"

Startup probes solve a critical problem: slow-starting applications. If your Django app runs collectstatic and database migrations on boot (taking 30+ seconds), the liveness probe might kill it before it even finishes starting. The startup probe blocks all other probes until it succeeds.

k8s/startup-probe.yaml 
containers:
  - name: anihpj
    image: anihpj:latest
    startupProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 5
      periodSeconds: 5                # Check every 5 seconds
      failureThreshold: 30            # 30 failures × 5s = 150s max
                                      # App has up to 2.5 min to start
      successThreshold: 1             # 1 success → startup complete
    # These probes don't run until startup succeeds:
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz
        port: 8000
      periodSeconds: 15
      failureThreshold: 3
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /readyz
        port: 8000
      periodSeconds: 10
      failureThreshold: 3

🚀 Startup Probe — Protection Against Premature Kills

  WITHOUT startup probe:                 WITH startup probe:
  ─────────────────────                  ────────────────────
  0s  Container starts                   0s  Container starts
  3s  Liveness check → FAIL (starting)  5s  Startup check → FAIL (starting)
  6s  Liveness check → FAIL             10s Startup check → FAIL
  9s  Liveness check → FAIL             15s Startup check → FAIL
  ❌  3 failures → RESTART!              20s Startup check → FAIL
                                         25s Startup check → PASS ✅
  Then restarts in a loop forever...         └▶ Start liveness + readiness
  (CrashLoopBackOff)                         App works normally! 🎉

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Max startup time = failureThreshold × periodSeconds        │
  │  Example: 30 × 5s = 150 seconds (2.5 minutes)              │
  │  If app doesn't start in that time → container is killed    │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

16.6 Probe Configuration Parameters

All three probe types share the same timing parameters. Getting these right is critical — too aggressive and you'll get unnecessary restarts, too lenient and failures go undetected.

Parameter Default Description Recommendation
initialDelaySeconds 0 Wait N seconds before first probe check Set to your app's typical startup time
periodSeconds 10 How often to check (seconds between probes) 10-30s for liveness, 5-10s for readiness
timeoutSeconds 1 Max time to wait for a response 3-5s (higher for DB-dependent checks)
successThreshold 1 Consecutive successes needed to pass 1 for liveness, 1-2 for readiness
failureThreshold 3 Consecutive failures before action taken 3 for liveness, 3 for readiness

⏱️ Timing Parameters — Visual Example

  initialDelay=10s    periodSeconds=15s           failureThreshold=3
  │                   │                           │
  │    ┌──────────────▼───┬───────────┬───────────┬───────────┐
  ▼    │                  │           │           │           │
  0s   10s               25s         40s         55s         70s
  ─────┤──────────────────┤───────────┤───────────┤───────────┤──▶
       │                  │           │           │           │
  wait │   1st check      2nd check   3rd check   action!
       │   (timeout: 3s)  ✅ pass     ❌ fail     ❌ fail     ❌ fail
       │                                                      │
       │                                               ┌──────▼──────┐
       │                                               │  RESTART    │
       │                                               │  (liveness) │
       │                                               │  or REMOVE  │
       │                                               │  (readiness)│
       │                                               └─────────────┘

16.7 Implementing All Probes for anihpj

Here's the complete implementation — Django health check views, URL routes, and a Deployment with all three probes configured:

anihpj/jobpost/views.py — Health Check Views 
from django.http import JsonResponse
from django.db import connection

def healthz(request):
    """
    LIVENESS probe endpoint.
    Only checks if the Django process is alive and responding.
    Do NOT check external dependencies here!
    """
    return JsonResponse({"status": "alive"}, status=200)


def readyz(request):
    """
    READINESS probe endpoint.
    Checks if the app can serve user requests:
    - Database connection is working
    - (Add more checks as needed: Redis, external APIs)
    """
    checks = {}
    
    # Check database
    try:
        with connection.cursor() as cursor:
            cursor.execute("SELECT 1")
        checks["database"] = "connected"
    except Exception as e:
        checks["database"] = f"failed: {e}"
        return JsonResponse({
            "status": "not_ready",
            "checks": checks
        }, status=503)
    
    return JsonResponse({
        "status": "ready",
        "checks": checks
    }, status=200)
anihpj/jobpost/urls.py — Health Routes 
from django.urls import path
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    # ... existing job routes ...
    path('healthz', views.healthz, name='liveness'),
    path('readyz', views.readyz, name='readiness'),
]
k8s/deployment-with-probes.yaml — Complete Probes 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:latest
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 250m
              memory: 256Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 512Mi
              
          # ── STARTUP PROBE ──
          # Gives app up to 150s to start (30 × 5s)
          startupProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 8000
            periodSeconds: 5
            failureThreshold: 30
            
          # ── LIVENESS PROBE ──
          # Checks every 15s if process is alive
          # 3 failures → restart container
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 8000
            periodSeconds: 15
            timeoutSeconds: 3
            failureThreshold: 3
            
          # ── READINESS PROBE ──
          # Checks every 10s if app can serve traffic
          # 3 failures → remove from Service endpoints
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /readyz
              port: 8000
            periodSeconds: 10
            timeoutSeconds: 5
            failureThreshold: 3
            successThreshold: 2

16.8 Common Probe Mistakes

Probes are powerful but misconfiguring them can cause more problems than having no probes at all. Here are the most common mistakes and how to avoid them:

❌ Liveness Depends on External Service

Liveness probe checks database connection → DB goes down briefly → ALL pods restart → app is completely down → DB comes back but now all pods are restarting → cascading failure!

✅ Fix: Liveness should ONLY check internal process health. Put DB checks in readiness probe.

❌ Timeout Too Low for Readiness

timeoutSeconds: 1 but DB query in readiness check takes 2s under load → probe "fails" even though app is working → pod removed from traffic → fewer pods → more load → more timeouts → cascade!

✅ Fix: Set timeout to at least 2-3× your normal response time. For DB checks, use 5s.

❌ Same Endpoint for Both Probes

Using /healthz for both liveness AND readiness → if the endpoint is designed to check dependencies, liveness will restart pods when dependencies fail (wrong!) → or if it's too simple, readiness won't catch real issues.

✅ Fix: Use /healthz (simple) for liveness, /readyz (thorough) for readiness.

❌ No Startup Probe for Slow Apps

App takes 45s to run migrations on start → liveness probe starts checking at 10s → 3 failures by 40s → pod killed before it finishes starting → CrashLoopBackOff → app never starts!

✅ Fix: Add startup probe with high failureThreshold to give slow apps time to start.

💡 The Golden Rules of Probes
  • Liveness = internal only — is the process alive? (never check external dependencies)
  • Readiness = external dependencies — can I serve traffic? (check DB, cache, APIs)
  • Startup = slow starters — block other probes until the app is initialized
  • Keep probes lightweight — they run every few seconds, don't add expensive logic
  • Different endpoints — /healthz for liveness, /readyz for readiness
📝 Chapter 16 Quick Reference
Command Purpose
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> See probe status and recent events
kubectl get events --field-selector reason=Unhealthy Find failed probe events
kubectl logs <pod-name> --previous Logs from the previous container (before restart)
kubectl get endpoints <service-name> Check which pods are in the Service (readiness)

Chapter 17: Resource Management

Control how much CPU and memory each container gets. Without resource management, a single runaway pod can starve every other workload on the node. Kubernetes provides requests, limits, QoS classes, quotas, and limit ranges to prevent this.

⚙️ Resources 📊 CPU & Memory 🛡️ QoS Classes 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why resource management prevents pod starvation and node crashes
  • ✅ Resource requests vs limits — guaranteed vs burst zones
  • ✅ CPU units (millicores) and memory units (Mi, Gi)
  • ✅ What happens when limits are exceeded (throttle vs OOMKill)
  • ✅ QoS classes — Guaranteed, Burstable, BestEffort
  • ✅ Resource Quotas for namespace-level caps
  • ✅ Limit Ranges for default pod limits
  • ✅ Right-sizing and monitoring anihpj resources

17.1 Why Resource Management Matters

Without resource limits, any pod can consume all available CPU and memory on a node. A memory leak in one container can cause every pod on the node to crash. Without resource requests, the scheduler can't make intelligent placement decisions.

💥 The "Noisy Neighbor" Problem

  WITHOUT resource limits:              WITH resource limits:
  ────────────────────────              ──────────────────────
  Node: 4Gi RAM total                   Node: 4Gi RAM total

  ┌────────────────────────┐            ┌────────────────────────┐
  │ 📦 anihpj (leak!)      │            │ 📦 anihpj              │
  │ Using: 3.5Gi ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │            │ Using: 450Mi ▓▓░░     │
  │ (no limit = takes all) │            │ Limit: 512Mi (capped!) │
  │                        │            │                        │
  │ 📦 postgres            │            │ 📦 postgres            │
  │ Using: 0.2Gi ▓░░░░░░░ │            │ Using: 800Mi ▓▓▓▓░░   │
  │ (starving!)            │            │ Limit: 1Gi (protected) │
  │                        │            │                        │
  │ 📦 redis               │            │ 📦 redis               │
  │ Using: 0.1Gi ░░░░░░░░ │            │ Using: 200Mi ▓▓░░░░   │
  │ 💀 OOMKilled!          │            │ Limit: 256Mi (safe)    │
  └────────────────────────┘            └────────────────────────┘
     One pod = all others die              Each pod gets fair share
⚠️ anihpj Real-World Scenario

Imagine your anihpj Django app has a view that loads all job posts into memory for export. Without memory limits, this single request could allocate 2Gi+ of RAM — pushing PostgreSQL and Redis off the node. With a 512Mi limit, the leaky container gets OOMKilled and restarted instead, leaving other pods untouched.

17.2 Resource Requests and Limits

Every container can specify two resource values for each resource type (CPU and memory): a request (minimum guaranteed) and a limit (maximum allowed). These two values create three zones of operation.

📊 Resource Zones — Request vs Limit

  Memory for anihpj container:

  0Mi      128Mi        256Mi              512Mi          ∞
   │────────│────────────│──────────────────│──────────────│
   │        │            │                  │              │
   │        │  🟢 GUARANTEED ZONE    │  🟡 BURST ZONE     │ 🔴 OOMKill!
   │        │  (request = 256Mi)   │  (up to limit)    │
   │        │  Scheduler reserves  │  Can use if node  │ Exceeds limit
   │        │  this for the pod    │  has spare capacity│ → container killed
   │        │                      │                    │
   │        ↑ request = 256Mi      ↑ limit = 512Mi
   │
   │  What each value controls:
   │  ┌──────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┐
   │  │ request (256Mi)      │ Scheduler uses this for pod     │
   │  │                      │ placement — "does node have     │
   │  │                      │ 256Mi FREE?" If no → Pending    │
   │  ├──────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
   │  │ limit (512Mi)        │ Kubelet enforces — container    │
   │  │                      │ CANNOT use more than 512Mi.     │
   │  │                      │ If it tries → OOMKilled!        │
   │  └──────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┘

Requests: Minimum Guaranteed Resources

The request is the minimum amount of CPU/memory the scheduler guarantees to a pod. When scheduling, Kubernetes checks: "Does this node have enough unrequested resources to fit this pod?" If the answer is no, the pod stays Pending.

✅ Scheduling Decision

Node has 2Gi allocatable, 1.5Gi already requested. Pod requests 256Mi → 1.5Gi + 256Mi = 1.756Gi < 2Gi → Scheduled!

❌ Scheduling Failure

Node has 2Gi allocatable, 1.9Gi already requested. Pod requests 256Mi → 1.9Gi + 256Mi = 2.156Gi > 2Gi → Pending!

Limits: Maximum Allowed Resources

The limit is the ceiling — the kubelet on each node enforces this. If a container tries to exceed its limit, the behavior depends on the resource type:

Exceed... CPU (250m / 500m) Memory (256Mi / 512Mi)
What happens ⏳ Throttled — process is slowed down. It can't use more than 500m CPU even if the node has spare cores. 💥 OOMKilled — container is killed and restarted. Memory can't be "throttled" like CPU.
Data loss? No — just slower Yes — container restarts, in-memory state is lost
Recovery Automatic (unthrottle when load drops) Pod restarts (may CrashLoopBackOff if persistent)

CPU Units (Millicores)

Kubernetes measures CPU in millicores (m). 1 CPU = 1000m = 1 full core. You can request fractions of a CPU core.

Value Meaning Equivalent Use Case
100m 100 millicores 0.1 CPU = 10% of a core Small sidecar containers
250m 250 millicores 0.25 CPU = 25% of a core Django app (typical)
500m 500 millicores 0.5 CPU = half a core Moderate workloads
1 or 1000m 1000 millicores 1 full CPU core CPU-intensive apps
2 2000 millicores 2 full CPU cores Multi-threaded processing

Memory Units

Memory is measured in bytes, but you'll typically use the binary suffixes (Mi, Gi) or decimal suffixes (M, G). Use the binary forms (Mi, Gi) — they're the standard in Kubernetes.

Suffix Name Bytes Typical Use
Ki Kibibyte 1,024 bytes Tiny containers
Mi Mebibyte 1,048,576 bytes (~1 MB) Most containers (128Mi-512Mi)
Gi Gibibyte 1,073,741,824 bytes (~1 GB) Databases, ML workloads
⚠️ Mi vs M — They're Different!

128Mi = 128 × 1024² = 134,217,728 bytes (mebibytes — binary)
128M = 128 × 1000² = 128,000,000 bytes (megabytes — decimal)
The difference is ~4.86%. Always use Mi and Gi in Kubernetes to avoid confusion.

k8s/resources.yaml — Resource Requests & Limits 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:latest
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          resources:
            requests:              # Minimum guaranteed
              cpu: 250m            # 25% of a core
              memory: 256Mi        # 256 mebibytes
            limits:                # Maximum allowed
              cpu: 500m            # 50% of a core
              memory: 512Mi        # 512 mebibytes (OOMKill if exceeded)

17.3 Quality of Service (QoS) Classes

Kubernetes automatically assigns a QoS class to every pod based on its resource configuration. This class determines the pod's eviction priority when the node runs out of resources — BestEffort pods are evicted first, Guaranteed pods last.

QoS Class Conditions Eviction Priority Use For
✅ Guaranteed Every container has requests = limits (for both CPU and memory) 🟢 Last to be evicted Production critical apps
⚠️ Burstable At least one container has requests ≠ limits 🟡 Middle priority Most development workloads
❌ BestEffort No requests or limits set on any container 🔴 First to be evicted Batch jobs, dev experiments

🏗️ QoS Eviction Priority — Who Gets Evicted First?

  Node under memory pressure — who gets evicted?

  FIRST evicted                                    LAST evicted
  ◄──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────►

  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐
  │ ❌ BEST EFFORT  │  │ ⚠️ BURSTABLE   │  │ ✅ GUARANTEED   │
  │                │  │                │  │                │
  │ No limits      │  │ requests set   │  │ requests =     │
  │ No requests    │  │ limits differ  │  │ limits         │
  │                │  │                │  │                │
  │ resources: {}  │  │ cpu: 250m/500m │  │ cpu: 500m      │
  │                │  │ mem: 256/512Mi │  │ mem: 512Mi     │
  │                │  │                │  │ (req = lim)    │
  │ "I don't care" │  │ "I need some   │  │ "I need        │
  │                │  │  but might     │  │  exactly this  │
  │ Evict: 1st 🔴  │  │  use more"     │  │  much"         │
  │                │  │ Evict: 2nd 🟡  │  │ Evict: LAST 🟢 │
  └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘

  💡 For production anihpj: aim for Guaranteed QoS (set requests = limits)
QoS Class Examples 
# ✅ GUARANTEED — requests = limits
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi
  limits:
    cpu: 500m          # Same as request
    memory: 512Mi      # Same as request

# ⚠️ BURSTABLE — requests < limits
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 250m
    memory: 256Mi
  limits:
    cpu: 500m          # Different from request
    memory: 512Mi      # Different from request

# ❌ BEST EFFORT — no resources specified
# (just don't include the resources section)
containers:
  - name: dev-tool
    image: busybox
    # No resources: section → BestEffort
Check a Pod's QoS Class 
# Check what QoS class a pod got
kubectl get pod anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-abc12 -n anihpj-app -o jsonpath='{.status.qosClass}'
# Output: Burstable

# Or describe it
kubectl describe pod anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-abc12 -n anihpj-app | grep "QoS Class"
# QoS Class: Burstable

17.4 Resource Quotas — Namespace-Level Limits

Resource Quotas set aggregate limits across an entire namespace. They prevent one team or application from consuming all cluster resources. Think of it as a "budget" for each namespace.

k8s/resource-quota.yaml — Namespace Quota for anihpj 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: anihpj-quota
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  hard:
    # Compute resources
    requests.cpu: "4"                   # Total CPU requests ≤ 4 cores
    requests.memory: 4Gi               # Total memory requests ≤ 4Gi
    limits.cpu: "8"                     # Total CPU limits ≤ 8 cores
    limits.memory: 8Gi                 # Total memory limits ≤ 8Gi
    
    # Object counts
    pods: "20"                          # Max 20 pods
    services: "10"                      # Max 10 Services
    persistentvolumeclaims: "5"         # Max 5 PVCs
    configmaps: "20"                    # Max 20 ConfigMaps
    secrets: "20"                       # Max 20 Secrets
Check Quota Usage 
# Apply the quota
kubectl apply -f k8s/resource-quota.yaml

# Check current usage vs limits
kubectl describe resourcequota anihpj-quota -n anihpj-app
# Name:                   anihpj-quota
# Namespace:              anihpj-app
# Resource                Used    Hard
# --------                ----    ----
# limits.cpu              1500m   8
# limits.memory           1536Mi  8Gi
# pods                    3       20
# requests.cpu            750m    4
# requests.memory         768Mi   4Gi
# services                2       10
💡 Quota Enforcement

When a ResourceQuota is active, every new pod must specify resource requests and limits. If a pod doesn't, the creation is rejected. This is by design — it forces teams to think about resource usage. Combine with LimitRanges (next section) to set defaults.

17.5 Limit Ranges — Default Pod Limits

A LimitRange sets default, minimum, and maximum resource values for containers in a namespace. Pods that don't specify resources get the defaults automatically — solving the "quota requires resources" problem.

k8s/limit-range.yaml — Default Limits for anihpj Namespace 
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: anihpj-limits
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  limits:
    - type: Container
      default:                          # Default limits (if not specified)
        cpu: 500m
        memory: 512Mi
      defaultRequest:                   # Default requests (if not specified)
        cpu: 250m
        memory: 256Mi
      min:                              # Minimum allowed
        cpu: 50m
        memory: 64Mi
      max:                              # Maximum allowed
        cpu: 2
        memory: 2Gi
    - type: Pod
      max:                              # Max per-pod totals
        cpu: 4
        memory: 4Gi

🔧 How LimitRange + ResourceQuota Work Together

  Developer creates a pod WITHOUT resources specified:

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Pod Spec → No resources set                              │
  │       │                                                    │
  │       ▼                                                    │
  │  LimitRange checks:                                        │
  │  ├── No requests? → Apply default: cpu=250m, mem=256Mi    │
  │  ├── No limits?   → Apply default: cpu=500m, mem=512Mi    │
  │  ├── Below min?   → Reject! (must be ≥ 50m CPU, 64Mi)    │
  │  └── Above max?   → Reject! (must be ≤ 2 CPU, 2Gi)      │
  │       │                                                    │
  │       ▼                                                    │
  │  ResourceQuota checks:                                     │
  │  ├── Total namespace CPU < 4 cores?   → Allow ✅           │
  │  └── Total namespace memory < 4Gi?    → Allow ✅           │
  │       │                                                    │
  │       ▼                                                    │
  │  Pod created with defaults applied! 🎉                     │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

17.6 Right-Sizing anihpj Resources

Setting resources too high wastes cluster capacity (and money). Setting them too low causes throttling and OOMKills. Here are recommended starting values for each component of the anihpj stack:

Component CPU Request CPU Limit Memory Request Memory Limit
Django (gunicorn) 250m 500m 256Mi 512Mi
PostgreSQL 500m 1000m 512Mi 1Gi
Redis 100m 250m 128Mi 256Mi
Nginx 50m 100m 64Mi 128Mi
📐 Total Cluster Footprint

Requests: 900m CPU + 960Mi memory
Limits: 1850m CPU + ~1.9Gi memory
With 3 Django replicas: ~1.4 CPU / 1.7Gi requested

🔄 Iteration Process

1. Start with these values
2. Monitor with kubectl top pods
3. Check VPA recommendations
4. Adjust up/down based on real usage

💡 Right-Sizing Tips
  • Start generous, then tighten. It's safer to over-allocate initially and reduce based on monitoring data than to under-allocate and suffer OOMKills.
  • Requests ≈ P50 usage. Set requests to match your typical (median) resource consumption.
  • Limits ≈ P99 usage + buffer. Set limits to handle your peaks with some headroom. 1.5-2× the request is common.
  • Use VPA in "Off" mode (Chapter 15.5) to get data-driven recommendations without auto-applying them.

17.7 Monitoring Resource Usage

You can't optimize what you can't measure. Kubernetes provides built-in commands and integrations to monitor resource consumption across pods and nodes.

Resource Monitoring Commands 
# ① Check pod resource usage (requires metrics-server)
kubectl top pods -n anihpj-app
# NAME                      CPU(cores)   MEMORY(bytes)
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-abc12   45m          187Mi
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-def34   52m          192Mi
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-ghi56   38m          175Mi
# postgres-0                120m         423Mi
# redis-0                   12m          84Mi

# ② Check node resource usage
kubectl top nodes
# NAME       CPU(cores)   CPU%   MEMORY(bytes)   MEMORY%
# minikube   287m         14%    1823Mi          46%

# ③ See resource requests vs actual usage for a pod
kubectl describe pod anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-abc12 -n anihpj-app | grep -A5 "Requests\|Limits"
# Limits:
#   cpu:     500m
#   memory:  512Mi
# Requests:
#   cpu:     250m
#   memory:  256Mi

# ④ Check for OOMKilled containers
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.containerStatuses[*].lastState.terminated.reason}{"\n"}{end}'

# ⑤ View VPA recommendations (if VPA installed)
kubectl describe vpa anihpj-vpa -n anihpj-app

📊 Resource Monitoring Checklist

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │           RESOURCE MONITORING WORKFLOW                        │
  │                                                               │
  │  1. OBSERVE        kubectl top pods / nodes                   │
  │     │              → What's actual usage?                     │
  │     ▼                                                         │
  │  2. COMPARE        usage vs requests vs limits                │
  │     │              → Are you over/under-allocating?           │
  │     ▼                                                         │
  │  3. DETECT         OOMKills, throttling, Pending pods         │
  │     │              → Are there resource-related problems?     │
  │     ▼                                                         │
  │  4. RECOMMEND      VPA suggestions, manual analysis           │
  │     │              → What should the values be?               │
  │     ▼                                                         │
  │  5. ADJUST         Update YAML → apply → monitor again        │
  │                    → Iterate until stable                     │
  │                                                               │
  │  🔧 Tools:                                                    │
  │  ├── Built-in:    kubectl top, describe, events               │
  │  ├── Metrics:     metrics-server (required for kubectl top)   │
  │  ├── VPA:         Vertical Pod Autoscaler (recommendations)   │
  │  └── Advanced:    Prometheus + Grafana (dashboards)           │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📝 Chapter 17 Quick Reference
Command / Resource Purpose
kubectl top pods -n anihpj-app Check CPU/memory usage per pod
kubectl top nodes Check CPU/memory usage per node
kubectl describe pod <name> See requests, limits, QoS class
kubectl describe resourcequota See namespace quota usage
kubectl describe limitrange See default/min/max limits
minikube addons enable metrics-server Enable metrics for kubectl top

🔄 Part 6: Updates & Maintenance Chapters 18-20

Chapter 18: Deployment Strategies

Learn how to safely update your applications in Kubernetes. From the default rolling update to advanced blue-green and canary deployments — this chapter covers all the strategies you need to deploy with confidence and zero downtime.

🔄 Rolling Updates 🔵🟢 Blue-Green 🐦 Canary 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Rolling updates — zero-downtime default strategy
  • ✅ Recreate strategy — when downtime is acceptable
  • ✅ Blue-Green deployments — instant rollback capability
  • ✅ Canary deployments — gradual traffic shifting
  • ✅ A/B testing — user-based routing
  • ✅ Complete walkthrough: updating anihpj safely
  • ✅ Strategy selection guide — picking the right approach

18.1 Rolling Updates (Default Strategy)

The rolling update is Kubernetes' default deployment strategy. It gradually replaces old pods with new ones, ensuring your application remains available throughout the update. No downtime, no user impact — when configured correctly.

🔄 Rolling Update — Step-by-Step Animation

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ROLLING UPDATE: anihpj v1 → v2                                      │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Step 1: Initial State (3 v1 pods running)                           │
  │  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐                                   │
  │  │ v1  🔵 │ │ v1  🔵 │ │ v1  🔵 │   ◀── all serving traffic        │
  │  │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ ready  │                                   │
  │  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘                                   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Step 2: Create 1 new v2 pod (maxSurge: 1)                           │
  │  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐                        │
  │  │ v1  🔵 │ │ v1  🔵 │ │ v1  🔵 │ │ v2  🟢 │  ◀── v2 starting      │
  │  │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ pending│                        │
  │  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘                        │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Step 3: v2 ready → terminate 1 v1 pod                               │
  │  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐                                   │
  │  │ v1  🔵 │ │ v1  🔵 │ │ v2  🟢 │   ◀── 1 replaced                 │
  │  │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ ready  │                                   │
  │  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘                                   │
  │                                                                       │
