  │  Step 4: Continue...                                                  │
  │  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐                                   │
  │  │ v1  🔵 │ │ v2  🟢 │ │ v2  🟢 │   ◀── 2 replaced                 │
  │  │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ ready  │                                   │
  │  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘                                   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Step 5: Complete! All v2 pods running                               │
  │  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐                                   │
  │  │ v2  🟢 │ │ v2  🟢 │ │ v2  🟢 │   ◀── all v2 ✅                   │
  │  │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ ready  │                                   │
  │  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘                                   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ⏱️ Zero downtime! Always ≥2 pods serving throughout                  │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📈 maxSurge

How many EXTRA pods can exist during the update. Default: 25%. If replicas=4, surge=1 means max 5 pods during update.

Higher = faster updates (more parallelism)

📉 maxUnavailable

How many pods can be DOWN during the update. Default: 25%. If replicas=4, unavailable=1 means at least 3 pods always ready.

Higher = faster updates (less waiting)

k8s/deployment-rolling.yaml — Rolling Update Configuration 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  replicas: 4
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate           # ← default strategy
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1                 # max 5 pods during update (4+1)
      maxUnavailable: 1           # min 3 pods always ready (4-1)
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v2        # update this to deploy
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          readinessProbe:         # ← CRITICAL for rolling updates!
            httpGet:
              path: /readyz
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 5
💡 Golden Rule: Readiness Probes

Rolling updates require readiness probes to work correctly. Without them, Kubernetes assumes pods are ready immediately — it will terminate old pods before new ones can actually handle traffic. Always define readiness probes!

Watch a Rolling Update in Real-Time 
# Trigger an update
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj anihpj=anihpj:v2 -n anihpj-app

# Watch pods transition
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -w
# NAME                      READY   STATUS              AGE
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-abc12   1/1     Running             5m    ← v1
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-def34   1/1     Running             5m    ← v1
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-ghi56   1/1     Running             5m    ← v1
# anihpj-6c9a2e8d3-jkl78   0/1     ContainerCreating   0s    ← v2 starting!
# anihpj-6c9a2e8d3-jkl78   1/1     Running             3s    ← v2 ready
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-abc12   1/1     Terminating         5m    ← v1 terminating

# Check rollout status
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# deployment "anihpj" successfully rolled out
🚨 Common Rolling Update Pitfalls
No Readiness Probe K8s terminates old pods before new ones can handle traffic → brief outage
Using :latest Same tag means K8s might not pull new image → deployment appears stuck
Too Low Resources New pods fail scheduling or OOMKill → rollout hangs at 50%
Slow Startup Apps Readiness probe times out before app is ready → CrashLoopBackOff
Database Locks Old pods hold DB connections → new pods can't connect

18.2 Recreate Strategy

The Recreate strategy terminates ALL old pods before creating ANY new pods. This results in downtime, but it's necessary when two versions of your application cannot coexist (e.g., incompatible database schemas).

🛑 Recreate Strategy — Complete Replacement

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  RECREATE STRATEGY: anihpj v1 → v2                                   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Step 1: All v1 pods running                                         │
  │  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐                                   │
  │  │ v1  🔵 │ │ v1  🔵 │ │ v1  🔵 │   ◀── serving traffic            │
  │  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘                                   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Step 2: ALL v1 pods terminated                                      │
  │  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐                                   │
  │  │   ❌   │ │   ❌   │ │   ❌   │   ◀── 🔴 DOWNTIME STARTS          │
  │  │ gone   │ │ gone   │ │ gone   │                                   │
  │  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘                                   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Step 3: v2 pods created                                             │
  │  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐                                   │
  │  │ v2  🟢 │ │ v2  🟢 │ │ v2  🟢 │   ◀── starting up                 │
  │  │pending │ │pending │ │pending │                                   │
  │  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘                                   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Step 4: v2 pods ready                                               │
  │  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐                                   │
  │  │ v2  🟢 │ │ v2  🟢 │ │ v2  🟢 │   ◀── 🟢 DOWNTIME ENDS           │
  │  │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ ready  │                                   │
  │  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘                                   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ⚠️ Downtime = time to terminate v1 + time to start v2               │
  │     Typically 10-60 seconds depending on app startup time            │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
k8s/deployment-recreate.yaml — Recreate Strategy 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  strategy:
    type: Recreate          # ← kills all, then creates all
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v2
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
⚠️ When to Use

Database schema migrations that break v1. Resource constraints (can't run 2 versions). Single-instance apps.

⏱️ Downtime

Yes — from the moment old pods terminate until new pods pass readiness. Plan maintenance windows!

✅ Simplicity

No version coexistence issues. Clean slate deployment. Simple rollback (deploy old version).

⚠️ Schedule Recreate Deployments

For production anihpj, never use Recreate during peak hours. Schedule updates for low-traffic periods (e.g., 2 AM). Use a maintenance page or redirect traffic elsewhere during the downtime window.

18.3 Blue-Green Deployments

Blue-Green deployment runs two complete environments simultaneously. Blue is the current production version. Green is the new version. Once Green is validated, you switch traffic instantly by updating the Service selector. Rollback is instant too — switch back to Blue.

🔵🟢 Blue-Green — Traffic Switching

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  BLUE-GREEN DEPLOYMENT                                            │
  │                                                                    │
  │  BEFORE SWITCH:                   AFTER SWITCH:                    │
  │                                                                    │
  │      Users                            Users                        │
  │        │                                │                          │
  │        ▼                                ▼                          │
  │  ┌───────────────┐              ┌───────────────┐                 │
  │  │   Service     │              │   Service     │                 │
  │  │ selector:     │              │ selector:     │                 │
  │  │ version: blue │              │ version: green│  ◀── 1 edit!   │
  │  └───────┬───────┘              └───────┬───────┘                 │
  │          │                              │                          │
  │     ┌────▼────┐                    ┌────▼────┐                    │
  │     │ 🔵 BLUE │ ◀── traffic        │ 🟢 GREEN│ ◀── traffic       │
  │     │ (v1)    │                    │ (v2)    │                    │
  │     │ 3 pods  │                    │ 3 pods  │                    │
  │     └─────────┘                    └─────────┘                    │
  │     ┌─────────┐                    ┌─────────┐                    │
  │     │ 🟢 GREEN│ idle               │ 🔵 BLUE │ idle/rollback     │
  │     │ (v2)    │                    │ (v1)    │                    │
  │     │ 3 pods  │                    │ 3 pods  │                    │
  │     └─────────┘                    └─────────┘                    │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ⚡ Switch: kubectl patch svc anihpj -p '{"spec":{"selector":     │
  │             {"app":"anihpj","version":"green"}}}'                  │
  │                                                                    │
  │  🔙 Rollback: kubectl patch svc anihpj -p '{"spec":{"selector":   │
  │               {"app":"anihpj","version":"blue"}}}'                 │
  │                                                                    │
  │  💰 Cost: 2x resources during deployment (both versions running)  │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
k8s/blue-green/deployment-blue.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-blue
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      version: blue               # ← Blue version label
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        version: blue
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v1        # ← v1 image
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
k8s/blue-green/deployment-green.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-green
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      version: green              # ← Green version label
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        version: green
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v2        # ← v2 image
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
k8s/blue-green/service.yaml — Selector Switch 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-svc
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  selector:
    app: anihpj
    version: blue         # ← Change to "green" to switch traffic
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8000
  type: LoadBalancer
Blue-Green Switch Commands 
# Step 1: Deploy green environment
kubectl apply -f deployment-green.yaml

# Step 2: Test green internally before switching
kubectl run curl-test --image=curlimages/curl --rm -it -- \
  curl http://anihpj-green.anihpj-app:8000/jobs/

# Step 3: Switch traffic to green (THE BIG SWITCH!)
kubectl patch svc anihpj-svc -n anihpj-app \
  -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"version":"green"}}}'

# Step 4: Verify
kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc -n anihpj-app

# ROLLBACK (if needed) — instant!
kubectl patch svc anihpj-svc -n anihpj-app \
  -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"version":"blue"}}}'
Aspect Blue-Green ✅ Considerations
Downtime Zero Traffic switches instantly
Rollback Speed Instant (<1 sec) Just change the selector
Testing Full environment test Green gets real production data
Cost 2× resources Both environments run simultaneously
Complexity Medium Two deployments to manage

18.4 Canary Deployments

Canary deployment routes a small percentage of traffic (e.g., 10%) to the new version while the majority continues using the old version. If problems are detected, only 10% of users are affected. Gradually increase traffic to the new version until it reaches 100%.

🐦 Canary — Gradual Traffic Shift

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  CANARY DEPLOYMENT — GRADUAL ROLLOUT                               │
  │                                                                    │
  │      Users ──▶ Service (selector: app=anihpj)                     │
  │                       │                                            │
  │               Load Balancer (round-robin by pod count)             │
  │                 ┌─────┴──────────────────────┐                     │
  │                 │                            │                     │
  │        ┌────────▼───────────┐      ┌─────────▼────────┐           │
  │        │ Deployment v1      │      │ Deployment v2    │           │
  │        │ replicas: 9        │      │ replicas: 1      │           │
  │        │                    │      │                  │           │
  │        │ 📦📦📦📦📦📦📦📦📦     │      │ 📦 (canary)      │           │
  │        │                    │      │                  │           │
  │        │    90% traffic     │      │   10% traffic    │           │
  │        └────────────────────┘      └──────────────────┘           │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ROLLOUT PHASES:                                                   │
  │  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
  │  Phase  │ v1 pods │ v2 pods │ v2 traffic │ Action                 │
  │  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
  │    1    │    9    │    1    │    10%     │ 🔍 Monitor errors      │
  │    2    │    7    │    3    │    30%     │ 📊 Check metrics       │
  │    3    │    5    │    5    │    50%     │ ⏳ Wait for issues     │
  │    4    │    2    │    8    │    80%     │ 🎯 Almost there        │
  │    5    │    0    │   10    │   100%     │ 🎉 Complete!           │
  │  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
  │                                                                    │
  │  🛑 ABORT: If errors spike at any phase, scale v2 to 0!           │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
k8s/canary/deployment-stable.yaml — Stable (v1) 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-stable
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  replicas: 9                     # 90% of total pods
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj                 # ← SAME label as canary
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        version: stable
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v1
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
k8s/canary/deployment-canary.yaml — Canary (v2) 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-canary
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  replicas: 1                     # 10% of total pods (canary)
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj                 # ← SAME label (shares traffic!)
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        version: canary
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v2        # ← NEW version
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
k8s/canary/service.yaml — Shared Service 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-svc
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  selector:
    app: anihpj               # ← Selects BOTH stable and canary pods!
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8000
Canary Rollout Commands 
# Phase 1: Deploy canary (10%)
kubectl apply -f deployment-canary.yaml
# Now: 9 stable + 1 canary = 10% canary traffic

# Monitor for errors
kubectl logs -l version=canary -n anihpj-app --tail=100

# Phase 2: Increase to 30%
kubectl scale deployment anihpj-stable --replicas=7 -n anihpj-app
kubectl scale deployment anihpj-canary --replicas=3 -n anihpj-app

# Phase 3: 50%
kubectl scale deployment anihpj-stable --replicas=5 -n anihpj-app
kubectl scale deployment anihpj-canary --replicas=5 -n anihpj-app

# Phase 4: 100% (promote canary)
kubectl scale deployment anihpj-stable --replicas=0 -n anihpj-app
kubectl scale deployment anihpj-canary --replicas=10 -n anihpj-app

# ABORT (if errors detected)
kubectl scale deployment anihpj-canary --replicas=0 -n anihpj-app
kubectl scale deployment anihpj-stable --replicas=10 -n anihpj-app
📝 Why "Canary"?

Coal miners used canary birds to detect dangerous gases — if the canary died, miners knew to evacuate. Similarly, canary deployments "test the waters" with a small subset of traffic. If the canary version fails, you know to abort before affecting all users.

18.5 A/B Testing Deployments

A/B testing routes specific users to specific versions based on headers, cookies, geographic location, or user segments. Unlike canary (random distribution), A/B testing is deterministic — the same user always sees the same version.

🎯 A/B Testing — User-Based Routing

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  A/B TESTING — ROUTE BY USER SEGMENT                               │
  │                                                                    │
  │  Users arrive with different characteristics:                     │
  │                                                                    │
  │  User request                                                     │
  │       │                                                            │
  │       ▼                                                            │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐                        │
  │  │  Ingress Controller (NGINX/Istio)      │                        │
  │  │  ─────────────────────────────────────  │                        │
  │  │  Rules:                                 │                        │
  │  │  • Header "X-Beta-User: true" → v2     │                        │
  │  │  • Cookie "ab_test=experiment" → v2    │                        │
  │  │  • Location: EU → v2 (GDPR version)    │                        │
  │  │  • User-Agent: mobile → v2             │                        │
  │  │  • Default → v1                        │                        │
  │  └─────────┬──────────────────┬───────────┘                        │
  │            │                  │                                    │
  │       ┌────▼─────┐       ┌────▼─────┐                             │
  │       │ 🔵 v1    │       │ 🟢 v2    │                             │
  │       │ Control  │       │ Experiment│                             │
  │       │ Group A  │       │ Group B   │                             │
  │       └──────────┘       └──────────┘                             │
  │                                                                    │
  │  📊 Compare metrics between groups:                                │
  │     • Conversion rate                                              │
  │     • Time on page                                                 │
  │     • Error rate                                                   │
  │     • Click-through rate                                           │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
k8s/ab-test/ingress.yaml — A/B with NGINX Ingress 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ab-test
  namespace: anihpj-app
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary: "true"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-by-header: "X-Beta-User"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-by-header-value: "true"
spec:
  rules:
    - host: anihpj.example.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-v2       # ← Beta users go here
                port:
                  number: 80
🎫 Header-Based

X-Beta-User: true
Best for: Internal testers, beta program users

🍪 Cookie-Based

ab_test=variant_b
Best for: Persistent user assignment across sessions

🌍 Geo-Based

Route by country/region
Best for: GDPR compliance, localized features

📝 A/B vs Canary

Canary: Random X% of traffic to new version. Goal: test stability.
A/B Testing: Specific users to specific version. Goal: compare user behavior. Both can coexist — use canary for deployment safety, A/B for feature experiments.

18.6 Updating anihpj Safely — Complete Walkthrough

Let's walk through a real update scenario: adding pagination to the /jobs/ endpoint in anihpj, from code change to production deployment.

🚀 Update Pipeline — Code to Production

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  SAFE UPDATE PIPELINE                                              │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ① CODE         ② BUILD         ③ TEST          ④ DEPLOY         │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────     │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐        │
  │  │ views.py │   │ docker   │   │ kubectl  │   │ kubectl  │        │
  │  │ edit     │──▶│ build    │──▶│ test     │──▶│ rollout  │        │
  │  │          │   │ -t :v2   │   │          │   │          │        │
  │  └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘        │
  │       │              │              │              │               │
  │       ▼              ▼              ▼              ▼               │
  │  Add pagination   Tag image     Run locally    Watch pods         │
  │  to jobpost_list  anihpj:v2     or staging     transition         │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ⑤ VERIFY        ⑥ MONITOR                                        │
  │  ─────────────────────────────                                     │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐                                      │
  │  │ curl     │   │ kubectl  │                                      │
  │  │ test     │──▶│ logs     │   🎉 Done!                           │
  │  │ endpoint │   │ -f       │                                      │
  │  └──────────┘   └──────────┘                                      │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Step 1: Make Code Change — jobpost/views.py 
# jobpost/views.py — add pagination
from django.core.paginator import Paginator
from django.http import JsonResponse
from .models import JobPost

def jobpost_list(request):
    """API endpoint for job listings with pagination"""
    jobs = JobPost.objects.all().order_by('-created_at')
    
    # NEW: Add pagination (10 jobs per page)
    paginator = Paginator(jobs, 10)
    page = request.GET.get('page', 1)
    jobs_page = paginator.get_page(page)
    
    return JsonResponse({
        'jobs': [
            {
                'id': job.id,
                'title': job.title,
                'location': str(job.location),
                'author': job.author.name,
            }
            for job in jobs_page
        ],
        'pagination': {
            'current_page': jobs_page.number,
            'total_pages': paginator.num_pages,
            'has_next': jobs_page.has_next(),
            'has_previous': jobs_page.has_previous(),
        }
    })
Steps 2-6: Build, Deploy, Verify 
# ② Build new image with version tag
docker build -t anihpj:v2 .

# ③ (Optional) Test locally first
docker run -p 8000:8000 anihpj:v2
curl http://localhost:8000/jobs/?page=1

# ④ Deploy to Kubernetes
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj \
  anihpj=anihpj:v2 \
  -n anihpj-app

# Record why this deployment was made
kubectl annotate deployment/anihpj \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="v2: Added pagination to /jobs/ endpoint" \
  -n anihpj-app

# Watch the rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# Waiting for deployment "anihpj" rollout to finish:
#   2 out of 3 new replicas have been updated...
#   3 out of 3 new replicas have been updated...
# deployment "anihpj" successfully rolled out

# ⑤ Verify the new feature
kubectl run curl-test --image=curlimages/curl --rm -it -- \
  curl "http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/jobs/?page=1"

# ⑥ Monitor logs for errors
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj -n anihpj-app --tail=50 -f

# Check deployment history
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         Initial deployment
# 2         v2: Added pagination to /jobs/ endpoint
💡 Pro Tip: Always Annotate Changes

Use kubernetes.io/change-cause annotation to document what each deployment revision contains. When you need to rollback weeks later, you'll know exactly what each revision does — invaluable for debugging production issues.

18.7 Deployment Strategy Selection Guide

Choosing the right deployment strategy depends on your requirements: downtime tolerance, rollback speed, risk level, and resource budget.

Strategy Downtime Risk Cost Rollback Best For
🔄 Rolling None Low Minimal Slow Most deployments
🛑 Recreate Yes Medium Same N/A Schema changes
🔵🟢 Blue-Green None Very Low 2× Instant Critical apps
🐦 Canary None Lowest +10-20% Fast High-risk updates
🎯 A/B Test None Low Variable Fast Feature experiments

🧭 Strategy Decision Flowchart

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  WHICH DEPLOYMENT STRATEGY SHOULD YOU USE?                          │
  │                                                                      │
  │                  ┌────────────────────────┐                         │
  │                  │ Can old + new versions │                         │
  │                  │ coexist?                │                         │
  │                  └───────────┬────────────┘                         │
  │                         NO   │   YES                                 │
  │                        ┌─────┴─────┐                                 │
  │                        │           │                                 │
  │                        ▼           ▼                                 │
  │               ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────────────┐                │
  │               │ 🛑 RECREATE │  │ Is this a high-risk│                │
  │               │ (downtime) │  │ change?            │                │
  │               └────────────┘  └─────────┬──────────┘                │
  │                                    NO   │   YES                      │
  │                                   ┌─────┴─────┐                      │
  │                                   │           │                      │
  │                                   ▼           ▼                      │
  │                          ┌────────────┐  ┌───────────────┐          │
  │                          │ 🔄 ROLLING │  │ Need instant  │          │
  │                          │ (default)  │  │ rollback?     │          │
  │                          └────────────┘  └──────┬────────┘          │
  │                                            NO   │   YES              │
  │                                           ┌─────┴─────┐              │
  │                                           │           │              │
  │                                           ▼           ▼              │
  │                                   ┌────────────┐  ┌───────────┐     │
  │                                   │ 🐦 CANARY  │  │ 🔵🟢 BLUE │     │
  │                                   │ (10% test) │  │    GREEN  │     │
  │                                   └────────────┘  └───────────┘     │
  │                                                                      │
  │  💡 Default recommendation: Rolling Update (simple, zero-downtime)  │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
✅ For anihpj Job Board

Use Rolling Update for most updates. It's simple, zero-downtime, and sufficient for a typical web app. Add readiness probes for safety.

🔄 For anihpj DB Migrations

Use a Job for migrations before the Deployment update. Separate the migration from the app update to avoid coexistence issues.

Chapter 19: Rollbacks and Version Management

Deployments don't always go as planned. Learn how Kubernetes tracks deployment history, how to roll back to previous versions instantly, and best practices for image tagging and change tracking.

🔙 Rollbacks 📜 Revision History 🏷️ Image Tagging 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ How Kubernetes tracks deployment revision history
  • ✅ Rolling back to previous and specific versions
  • ✅ Pausing and resuming rollouts for inspection
  • ✅ Image tagging strategies for production
  • ✅ Annotating deployments with change causes
  • ✅ Hands-on: Update and rollback anihpj

19.1 Deployment Revision History

Every time you update a Deployment, Kubernetes creates a new revision and stores the previous one. This history allows you to roll back to any previous state. By default, Kubernetes keeps the last 10 revisions.

📜 Revision History Timeline

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  DEPLOYMENT REVISION HISTORY (anihpj)                                │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Time ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────▶   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Rev 1         Rev 2            Rev 3          Rev 4 (current)       │
  │    ●─────────────●────────────────●───────────────●                  │
  │    │             │                │               │                   │
  │  v1.0.0        v1.1.0          v1.2.0          v1.2.1                │
  │  Initial       Added           Added           Bug fix               │
  │  deploy        pagination      filtering       for /jobs/            │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │  $ kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj                 │    │
  │  │                                                               │    │
  │  │  REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE                                       │    │
  │  │  1         Initial deployment                                 │    │
  │  │  2         v1.1.0: Added pagination to /jobs/ endpoint        │    │
  │  │  3         v1.2.0: Added filtering by skills                  │    │
  │  │  4         v1.2.1: Fixed null location bug                    │    │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ⚠️ Rollback creates a NEW revision (history is append-only)         │
  │     Rolling back to Rev 2 would create Rev 5 (copy of Rev 2)         │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Viewing Deployment History 
# View all revisions
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app

# View details of a specific revision
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj --revision=2 -n anihpj-app
# Output shows the pod template for that revision:
# Pod Template:
#   Labels:       app=anihpj
#   Containers:
#    anihpj:
#     Image:      anihpj:v1.1.0
#     Port:       8000/TCP
#     ...

# Check current revision number
kubectl describe deployment anihpj -n anihpj-app | grep "revision"
# Annotations:  deployment.kubernetes.io/revision: 4
Configure Revision History Limit 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  revisionHistoryLimit: 10        # ← Keep last 10 revisions (default)
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    # ...
💡 Storage Consideration

Each revision stores a complete ReplicaSet object. For clusters with many deployments, consider lowering revisionHistoryLimit to 3-5 to reduce etcd storage usage. For critical applications, keep it at 10+ for more rollback options.

19.2 Rolling Back

When a deployment goes wrong — bugs, crashes, performance issues — you can instantly roll back to a known-good state. Kubernetes makes this as simple as one command.

🔙 Rollback Flow — Revert to Previous State

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ROLLBACK SCENARIO                                                    │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Current: Rev 4 (v1.2.1) — 🐞 BROKEN! 500 errors everywhere          │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ┌──────────┐     kubectl rollout undo     ┌──────────┐              │
  │  │ Rev 4    │ ─────────────────────────▶  │ Rev 5    │              │
  │  │ v1.2.1   │       (creates new rev)     │ (=Rev 3) │              │
  │  │ 🐞 broken │                             │ v1.2.0   │              │
  │  └──────────┘                              │ ✅ works  │              │
  │                                            └──────────┘              │
  │                                                                       │
  │  What happens internally:                                            │
  │  1. K8s looks up Rev 3's ReplicaSet template                         │
  │  2. Creates new pods using that template                             │
  │  3. Terminates Rev 4 pods (rolling update style)                     │
  │  4. Records this as Rev 5 (history is append-only)                   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ⚠️ Rev 5 is a COPY of Rev 3's config, not a pointer                 │
  │     If you rolled back again, you'd get Rev 6 (copy of Rev 4)        │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

To Previous Version (One Step Back)

The most common rollback — go back to the immediately previous revision.

Rollback to Previous Revision 
# Emergency! Current version is broken. Roll back NOW!
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app

# Output:
# deployment.apps/anihpj rolled back

# Watch the rollback in action
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -w
# anihpj-6c9a2e8d3-xxx   0/1   Terminating    ← broken v4 pods dying
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-yyy   1/1   Running        ← good v3 pods starting

To a Specific Revision

Sometimes you need to go back multiple versions — maybe the last 2 versions were both broken.

Rollback to Specific Revision 
# First, check what revisions are available
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         Initial deployment  
# 2         v1.1.0: Added pagination ◀── this one was stable!
# 3         v1.2.0: Added filtering (broke search)
# 4         v1.2.1: Fix attempt (still broken)

# Roll back to revision 2 (the last known-good state)
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj --to-revision=2 -n anihpj-app

# Verify which image is now running
kubectl get deployment anihpj -n anihpj-app -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
# anihpj:v1.1.0 ✅

Verifying the Rollback

Always verify that the rollback was successful and the application is healthy.

Verify Rollback Success 
# 1. Check rollout status
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# deployment "anihpj" successfully rolled out

# 2. Verify all pods are running the correct version
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -o wide
kubectl describe deployment anihpj -n anihpj-app | grep Image
# Image: anihpj:v1.1.0

# 3. Test the application
kubectl run curl-test --image=curlimages/curl --rm -it -- \
  curl http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/jobs/

# 4. Check logs for errors
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj -n anihpj-app --tail=20

# 5. Verify revision history (rollback created new revision)
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         Initial deployment
# 3         v1.2.0: Added filtering
# 4         v1.2.1: Fix attempt
# 5         Rollback to revision 2   ◀── NEW
⚠️ Rollback Doesn't Fix the Code

Rollback is an emergency measure, not a solution. After stabilizing with a rollback, you still need to fix the actual bug, test properly, and deploy a corrected version. The broken code is still in your repository!

🚨 Emergency Rollback Checklist

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  SHOULD I ROLL BACK?                                                  │
  │                                                                       │
  │  🚨 IMMEDIATE ROLLBACK (don't wait):                                  │
  │  ├── 500 errors spiking on monitoring                                │
  │  ├── Users reporting unable to load pages                            │
  │  ├── Error rate > 1% (or your SLO threshold)                         │
  │  ├── Pods in CrashLoopBackOff                                        │
  │  └── Memory/CPU 100% on new pods                                      │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ⏳ INVESTIGATE FIRST (can wait):                                      │
  │  ├── Slightly slower response times (< 2x)                           │
  │  ├── Warning logs (but no errors)                                    │
  │  ├── One pod restarted once                                          │
  │  └── User reports but you can't reproduce                            │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ✅ DON'T ROLL BACK (expected behavior):                              │
  │  ├── Deployment in progress (some pods terminating)                  │
  │  ├── Brief spike during transition                                   │
  │  └── Old revision pods gracefully shutting down                      │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
  │  │  ROLLBACK COMMAND:                                          │     │
  │  │  kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app       │     │
  │  │                                                              │     │
  │  │  TO SPECIFIC VERSION:                                        │     │
  │  │  kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj --to-revision=N     │     │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

19.3 Pausing and Resuming Rollouts

You can pause a deployment mid-rollout to inspect its state before continuing. This is useful for canary-like testing within a rolling update, or to batch multiple changes together.

⏸️ Pause/Resume — Mid-Rollout Control

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  PAUSED ROLLOUT — Partial Deployment                                 │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Before pause (mid-rollout):                                         │
  │  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐                        │
  │  │ v1  🔵 │ │ v1  🔵 │ │ v2  🟢 │ │ v2  🟢 │   2 old, 2 new        │
  │  │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ pending│                        │
  │  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘                        │
  │                                                                       │
  │  $ kubectl rollout pause deployment/anihpj                           │
  │  ⏸️ Rollout paused!                                                   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  State is FROZEN:                                                    │
  │  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐                        │
  │  │ v1  🔵 │ │ v1  🔵 │ │ v2  🟢 │ │ v2  🟢 │   ← stays like this   │
  │  │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ ready  │                        │
  │  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘                        │
  │                                                                       │
  │  You can now:                                                        │
  │  • Test v2 pods: Are they working correctly?                         │
  │  • Check logs: Any errors in the new version?                        │
  │  • Monitor metrics: CPU/memory normal?                               │
  │  • Make MORE changes (they batch up until resume)                    │
  │                                                                       │
  │  $ kubectl rollout resume deployment/anihpj                          │
  │  ▶️ Rollout continues...                                              │
  │                                                                       │
  │  After resume (complete):                                            │
  │  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐                        │
  │  │ v2  🟢 │ │ v2  🟢 │ │ v2  🟢 │ │ v2  🟢 │   all new ✅          │
  │  │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ ready  │                        │
  │  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘                        │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Pause and Resume Commands 
# Start an update
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj anihpj=anihpj:v2 -n anihpj-app

# Pause mid-rollout
kubectl rollout pause deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# deployment.apps/anihpj paused

# Check status (shows "paused")
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# Waiting for deployment "anihpj" rollout to finish: 
#   2 out of 4 new replicas have been updated (paused)

# While paused, you can make additional changes (they batch up):
kubectl set env deployment/anihpj DEBUG=false -n anihpj-app
kubectl set resources deployment/anihpj -c anihpj \
  --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi -n anihpj-app

# Resume the rollout (all changes apply together)
kubectl rollout resume deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# deployment.apps/anihpj resumed
✅ Good Use Cases
  • Inspect partial rollout before continuing
  • Batch multiple changes into one rollout
  • Wait for approval during deployment
❌ Not Recommended
  • Leaving deployments paused for long periods
  • Using pause as a "canary" replacement
  • Pausing with no plan to resume

19.4 Image Tagging and Version Strategy

How you tag your container images directly impacts your ability to track, reproduce, and roll back deployments. Never use :latest in production — it's impossible to know which actual build is running.

🏷️ Image Tagging Strategies

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  IMAGE TAGGING STRATEGIES                                           │
  │                                                                      │
  │  ┌───────────────────┐  ┌───────────────────┐  ┌───────────────────┐│
  │  │ ❌ NEVER USE      │  │ ✅ SEMANTIC       │  │ ✅ GIT SHA        ││
  │  │                   │  │    VERSIONING     │  │                   ││
  │  │  anihpj:latest    │  │  anihpj:1.2.3     │  │  anihpj:abc123f   ││
  │  │                   │  │                   │  │                   ││
  │  │ • Changes without │  │ • Major.Minor.    │  │ • Exact git commit││
  │  │   warning         │  │   Patch           │  │ • Perfect for CI  ││
  │  │ • No traceability │  │ • Clear intent    │  │ • Full traceability│
  │  │ • Impossible to   │  │ • Easy to compare │  │ • Not human-      ││
  │  │   reproduce       │  │ • Team standard   │  │   readable        ││
  │  │                   │  │                   │  │                   ││
  │  │ What's running?   │  │ What's running?   │  │ What's running?   ││
  │  │ 🤷 No idea        │  │ v1.2.3 ✅         │  │ commit abc123f ✅ ││
  │  └───────────────────┘  └───────────────────┘  └───────────────────┘│
  │                                                                      │
  │  ┌───────────────────┐  ┌───────────────────┐                       │
  │  │ ✅ DATE-BASED     │  │ ✅ COMBINED       │                       │
  │  │                   │  │                   │                       │
  │  │  anihpj:2026-03-28│  │  anihpj:1.2.3-    │                       │
  │  │                   │  │  abc123f          │                       │
  │  │ • Daily builds    │  │                   │                       │
  │  │ • Easy to age out │  │ • Best of both!   │                       │
  │  │ • No version info │  │ • SemVer + SHA    │                       │
  │  │                   │  │ • Recommended     │                       │
  │  └───────────────────┘  └───────────────────┘                       │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Strategy Example Pros Cons Best For
❌ latest anihpj:latest Simple No traceability Never in prod
SemVer anihpj:1.2.3 Clear versioning Manual tagging Release versions
Git SHA anihpj:abc123f Exact commit Not human-readable CI/CD pipelines
Date anihpj:2026-03-28 Easy to age out No version info Daily builds
Combined anihpj:1.2.3-abc123f Best of both Longer tags Production ✅
Build with Proper Tags 
# Get version from git tag or package.json
VERSION=$(git describe --tags --always)
# e.g., v1.2.3 or abc123f (if no tag)

# Get short git SHA
GIT_SHA=$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)

# Build with combined tag (recommended)
docker build -t anihpj:${VERSION}-${GIT_SHA} .
# Result: anihpj:v1.2.3-abc123f

# Also tag as "latest" for local dev (but never deploy this!)
docker tag anihpj:${VERSION}-${GIT_SHA} anihpj:latest

# Push to registry
docker push myregistry.com/anihpj:${VERSION}-${GIT_SHA}
🚨 Why :latest is Dangerous

:latest is a moving target. When you deploy anihpj:latest, Kubernetes might pull a different image on each node depending on cache. After a few days, you won't know what version is running. Rollback becomes impossible. Always use explicit version tags!

19.5 Change Cause Annotations

The kubernetes.io/change-cause annotation records why a deployment was updated. This annotation appears in rollout history, making it invaluable when you need to roll back weeks later.

Recording Change Causes 
# Method 1: Add annotation after deployment update
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj anihpj=anihpj:v1.2.0 -n anihpj-app
kubectl annotate deployment/anihpj \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="v1.2.0: Added job filtering by skills" \
  -n anihpj-app

# Method 2: Use --record flag (deprecated but still works)
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj anihpj=anihpj:v1.2.0 \
  --record -n anihpj-app
# Records: kubectl set image deployment/anihpj anihpj=anihpj:v1.2.0

# View history with change causes
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         Initial deployment
# 2         v1.1.0: Added pagination to /jobs/ endpoint
# 3         v1.2.0: Added job filtering by skills  ◀── descriptive!
YAML with Change Cause Annotation 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: anihpj-app
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/change-cause: "v1.2.0: Added job filtering by skills"
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v1.2.0
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
✅ Good Change Causes
  • v1.2.0: Added job filtering by skills
  • Hotfix: Fixed null location crash
  • Security: Updated Django to 4.2.1
  • JIRA-1234: Pagination for /jobs/
❌ Bad Change Causes
  • update (too vague)
  • kubectl set image... (default record)
  • fixed stuff (not descriptive)
  • No annotation at all

19.6 Practice: Update and Rollback anihpj

Let's walk through a complete update-and-rollback scenario. We'll deploy a new version, discover a bug, roll back, fix the bug, and deploy again properly.

🎯 Practice Scenario Timeline

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  SCENARIO: The Pagination Bug                                        │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Time ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────▶   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Step 1      Step 2        Step 3        Step 4       Step 5        │
  │    ●───────────●─────────────●─────────────●─────────────●          │
  │    │           │             │             │             │           │
  │  Deploy v1   Deploy v2     BUG!         Rollback      Deploy v3   │
  │  (original)  (pagination)  500 errors   to v1         (fixed)      │
  │              added         on page 2!   ✅ stable     ✅ verified   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │  The Bug: pagination.get_page(page) fails when page > total │    │
  │  │  Fix: Add bounds checking before get_page()                  │    │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Step 1: Verify Current Deployment (v1) 
# Check current state
kubectl get deployment anihpj -n anihpj-app -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
# anihpj:v1

# Test the endpoint
kubectl run curl-test --image=curlimages/curl --rm -it -- \
  curl http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/jobs/
# Returns: {"jobs": [...], "count": 50}  ← Works! But no pagination
Step 2: Deploy v2 (with pagination) 
# Deploy the new version
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj anihpj=anihpj:v2 -n anihpj-app

# Record what changed
kubectl annotate deployment/anihpj \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="v2: Added pagination to /jobs/ endpoint" \
  -n anihpj-app

# Wait for rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# deployment "anihpj" successfully rolled out ✅
Step 3: Discover the Bug! 🐞 
# Test page 1 - works!
kubectl run curl-test --image=curlimages/curl --rm -it -- \
  curl "http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/jobs/?page=1"
# Returns: {"jobs": [...], "pagination": {"current_page": 1, ...}}

# Test page 2 - BUG!
kubectl run curl-test --image=curlimages/curl --rm -it -- \
  curl "http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/jobs/?page=999"
# Returns: 500 Internal Server Error! 💥

# Check logs
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj -n anihpj-app --tail=10
# InvalidPage: That page contains no results
Step 4: Emergency Rollback! 🚨 
# ROLLBACK NOW! Users are seeing 500 errors
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# deployment.apps/anihpj rolled back

# Verify rollback completed
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# deployment "anihpj" successfully rolled out

# Verify we're back on v1
kubectl get deployment anihpj -n anihpj-app -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
# anihpj:v1 ✅

# Test - should work now (no pagination, but no crash)
kubectl run curl-test --image=curlimages/curl --rm -it -- \
  curl "http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/jobs/"
# Returns: {"jobs": [...], "count": 50}  ← Stable again!
Step 5: Fix the Bug and Deploy v3 
# jobpost/views.py — FIXED version
from django.core.paginator import Paginator, EmptyPage
from django.http import JsonResponse
from .models import JobPost

def jobpost_list(request):
    jobs = JobPost.objects.all().order_by('-created_at')
    paginator = Paginator(jobs, 10)
    
    try:
        page = int(request.GET.get('page', 1))
        # FIX: Bounds checking!
        page = max(1, min(page, paginator.num_pages))
        jobs_page = paginator.get_page(page)
    except (ValueError, EmptyPage):
        jobs_page = paginator.get_page(1)
    
    return JsonResponse({
        'jobs': [{'id': j.id, 'title': j.title} for j in jobs_page],
        'pagination': {
            'current_page': jobs_page.number,
            'total_pages': paginator.num_pages,
        }
    })
Deploy the Fix (v3) 
# Build fixed version
docker build -t anihpj:v3 .

# Deploy
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj anihpj=anihpj:v3 -n anihpj-app

kubectl annotate deployment/anihpj \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="v3: Fixed pagination crash on invalid page" \
  -n anihpj-app

# Verify
kubectl run curl-test --image=curlimages/curl --rm -it -- \
  curl "http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/jobs/?page=999"
# Returns: {"jobs": [...of last page...], ...}  ← Fixed! ✅

# Final revision history
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         Initial deployment
# 3         v2: Added pagination to /jobs/ endpoint
# 4         Rollback to revision 1
# 5         v3: Fixed pagination crash on invalid page  ← Latest
💡 Lessons Learned
  • Always test edge cases before deploying (page 0, page 999, negative pages)
  • Rollback is your emergency brake — use it quickly when things break
  • Change causes help you understand revision history weeks later
  • Version tags (v1, v2, v3) let you track and rollback precisely

Chapter 20: Jobs and CronJobs

Not all workloads run forever. Jobs handle one-time tasks like database migrations or batch processing. CronJobs schedule recurring tasks like nightly backups or weekly reports. Master these for complete workflow automation.

⚡ Jobs 🕐 CronJobs 🔄 Batch Processing 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Jobs for one-time tasks (migrations, data seeding)
  • ✅ Job configuration: parallelism, retries, timeouts
  • ✅ Three job patterns: single, parallel, work queue
  • ✅ CronJobs for scheduled tasks (backups, reports)
  • ✅ Cron syntax and concurrency policies
  • ✅ Real-world CronJobs for anihpj
  • ✅ Monitoring and debugging Jobs

20.1 Jobs — One-Time Tasks

A Job creates pods that run to completion and then stop. Unlike Deployments (which keep pods running forever), Jobs are for tasks that should execute once and exit — like database migrations, data processing, or sending batch emails.

⚡ Job vs Deployment — Different Lifecycles

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  DEPLOYMENT (runs forever)           JOB (runs to completion)        │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────┐       ┌──────────────────────────┐     │
  │  │ Pod: anihpj-web          │       │ Pod: anihpj-migrate      │     │
  │  │                          │       │                          │     │
  │  │ Start ●─────────────────▶│       │ Start ●────────● Done    │     │
  │  │       (runs indefinitely)│       │         ▲      │         │     │
  │  │                          │       │         │ runs │         │     │
  │  │ If pod dies → restart    │       │         │      ▼         │     │
  │  │ Desired state: Running   │       │      migrate tables      │     │
  │  │                          │       │                          │     │
  │  │ ┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐    │       │      Status: Completed   │     │
  │  │ │ 📦 │ │ 📦 │ │ 📦 │    │       │      Pod: Succeeded ✅   │     │
  │  │ └────┘ └────┘ └────┘    │       └──────────────────────────┘     │
  │  │  Always 3 replicas       │                                       │
  │  └──────────────────────────┘                                       │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Use for:                           Use for:                         │
  │  • Web servers                      • Database migrations            │
  │  • API servers                      • Data imports/exports           │
  │  • Background workers               • Batch email sending            │
  │  • Any "always-on" service          • Report generation              │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
k8s/job-migrate.yaml — Django Database Migration 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: anihpj-migrate
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: migrate
          image: anihpj:v1.2.0
          command: ["python", "manage.py", "migrate", "--no-input"]
          envFrom:
            - secretRef:
                name: anihpj-db-secrets     # DATABASE_URL, etc.
      restartPolicy: Never                  # ← Required for Jobs!
  backoffLimit: 3                           # Retry up to 3 times on failure
k8s/job-seed-data.yaml — Seed Demo Data 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: anihpj-seed-data
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: seed
          image: anihpj:v1.2.0
          command: 
            - python
            - manage.py
            - shell
            - -c
            - |
              from jobpost.models import Skill, Author, Location, JobPost
              # Create sample skills
              skills = ['Python', 'Django', 'Kubernetes', 'Docker', 'AWS']
              for name in skills:
                  Skill.objects.get_or_create(name=name)
              # Create sample author
              author, _ = Author.objects.get_or_create(
                  name='Demo Company',
                  defaults={'email': 'demo@example.com'}
              )
              print(f'Created {len(skills)} skills and 1 author')
          envFrom:
            - secretRef:
                name: anihpj-db-secrets
      restartPolicy: Never
  ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 300              # Auto-delete after 5 minutes
Running and Monitoring Jobs 
# Run the migration job
kubectl apply -f job-migrate.yaml

# Watch job status
kubectl get jobs -n anihpj-app -w
# NAME              COMPLETIONS   DURATION   AGE
# anihpj-migrate   0/1           3s         3s
# anihpj-migrate   1/1           8s         8s   ◀── Completed!

# View job's pod
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -l job-name=anihpj-migrate
# NAME                     READY   STATUS      RESTARTS   AGE
# anihpj-migrate-abc12    0/1     Completed   0          15s

# Check migration output
kubectl logs job/anihpj-migrate -n anihpj-app
# Operations to perform:
#   Apply all migrations: admin, auth, contenttypes, jobpost, sessions
# Running migrations:
#   Applying jobpost.0001_initial... OK
#   Applying jobpost.0002_jobpost_skills... OK

# Delete completed job
kubectl delete job anihpj-migrate -n anihpj-app
⚠️ restartPolicy Must Be Never or OnFailure

Jobs cannot use restartPolicy: Always (the default for Deployments). Use Never (preferred) or OnFailure. With Never, a new pod is created on failure. With OnFailure, the same pod is restarted.

20.2 Job Configuration Parameters

Jobs have several parameters that control how they execute, retry on failure, and clean up after completion.

Parameter Default Description
completions 1 Number of times the job must complete successfully
parallelism 1 Number of pods running simultaneously
backoffLimit 6 How many retries before marking job as failed
activeDeadlineSeconds none Max time the job can run before being terminated
ttlSecondsAfterFinished none Auto-delete job N seconds after completion
restartPolicy — Must be Never or OnFailure
k8s/job-with-all-params.yaml — Full Configuration 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: anihpj-batch-process
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  completions: 5                    # Run 5 times total
  parallelism: 2                    # 2 pods at a time
  backoffLimit: 3                   # Give up after 3 failures
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 600        # Timeout: 10 minutes max
  ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 3600     # Delete job 1 hour after completion
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: processor
          image: anihpj:v1.2.0
          command: ["python", "manage.py", "process_batch"]
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 100m
              memory: 128Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 512Mi
      restartPolicy: Never
🔄 backoffLimit

Exponential backoff between retries: 10s, 20s, 40s... up to 6 minutes. After backoffLimit failures, job status becomes Failed.

🧹 ttlSecondsAfterFinished

Auto-cleanup! Without this, completed jobs stick around forever, cluttering your cluster. Set to 3600 (1 hour) or 86400 (1 day).

20.3 Job Patterns

There are three common patterns for running Jobs, depending on your workload: single execution, parallel with fixed completions, or work queue processing.

📊 Three Job Patterns

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  JOB PATTERNS                                                          │
  │                                                                        │
  │  A) SINGLE JOB              B) FIXED COMPLETIONS    C) WORK QUEUE      │
  │  completions: 1             completions: 5          completions: unset │
  │  parallelism: 1             parallelism: 2          parallelism: 3     │
  │                                                                        │
  │  ┌─────────────┐            Round 1:                ┌───────────────┐  │
  │  │ Pod 1       │            ┌────┐ ┌────┐           │   Queue       │  │
  │  │             │            │ 📦 │ │ 📦 │           │ [A][B][C][D] │  │
  │  │ migrate     │            └────┘ └────┘           │ [E][F][G]    │  │
  │  │             │            (2 at a time)            └───────┬───────┘  │
  │  │ ✅ done     │                                             │         │
  │  └─────────────┘            Round 2:                         ▼         │
  │                             ┌────┐ ┌────┐           ┌────┐┌────┐┌────┐ │
  │  total: 1 pod               │ 📦 │ │ 📦 │           │ 📦 ││ 📦 ││ 📦 │ │
  │                             └────┘ └────┘           └────┘└────┘└────┘ │
  │  Use:                       (2 more)                 pull from queue   │
  │  • DB migration                                      until empty       │
  │  • Single report            Round 3:                                   │
  │  • Data cleanup             ┌────┐                  Use:               │
  │                             │ 📦 │ (last 1)          • Process uploads │
  │                             └────┘                   • Handle emails   │
  │                                                      • Image processing│
  │                             total: 5                                   │
  │                                                                        │
  │                             Use:                                       │
  │                             • Process N items                          │
  │                             • Send N emails                            │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Single Job

The simplest pattern — one pod, one execution. Perfect for migrations or one-time operations.

Single Job Pattern 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: anihpj-migrate
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  # Default: completions=1, parallelism=1
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: migrate
          image: anihpj:v1.2.0
          command: ["python", "manage.py", "migrate"]
      restartPolicy: Never

Parallel Jobs with Fixed Completions

Run N tasks in parallel, continuing until all completions are done. Great for processing a known number of items.

Fixed Completions Pattern — Send 100 Emails 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: anihpj-send-digest-emails
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  completions: 100              # Send 100 digest emails total
  parallelism: 10               # 10 pods running at once
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: mailer
          image: anihpj:v1.2.0
          command: 
            - python
            - manage.py
            - send_single_digest
            - --offset
            - "$(JOB_COMPLETION_INDEX)"    # Each pod processes different user
          env:
            - name: JOB_COMPLETION_INDEX
              valueFrom:
                fieldRef:
                  fieldPath: metadata.annotations['batch.kubernetes.io/job-completion-index']
      restartPolicy: Never

Work Queue Pattern

Pods pull work from a shared queue (Redis, RabbitMQ) and exit when the queue is empty. The number of completions is unknown upfront.

Work Queue Pattern — Process Resume Uploads 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: anihpj-process-resumes
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  # No completions set = run until pods exit successfully
  parallelism: 5                # 5 workers pulling from queue
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: resume-processor
          image: anihpj:v1.2.0
          command:
            - python
            - manage.py
            - process_resume_queue
            - --exit-when-empty         # Exit when no more work
          env:
            - name: REDIS_URL
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: anihpj-redis
                  key: url
      restartPolicy: Never
📝 Work Queue Behavior

When completions is not set, the job is complete when any pod exits successfully (exit code 0). All workers should exit when the queue is empty. This requires your code to detect an empty queue and exit cleanly.

20.4 CronJobs — Scheduled Tasks

A CronJob creates Jobs on a time-based schedule, just like Unix cron. Each scheduled run creates a new Job, which creates a new Pod. Perfect for recurring maintenance tasks.

🕐 CronJob — Creates Jobs on Schedule

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  CRONJOB: anihpj-daily-cleanup                                      │
  │  Schedule: 0 2 * * * (daily at 2:00 AM)                              │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Time ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────▶    │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Day 1           Day 2           Day 3           Day 4               │
  │   2:00 AM         2:00 AM         2:00 AM         2:00 AM            │
  │    │               │               │               │                 │
  │    ▼               ▼               ▼               ▼                 │
  │  ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐           │
  │  │ Job 1   │    │ Job 2   │    │ Job 3   │    │ Job 4   │           │
  │  │ cleanup │    │ cleanup │    │ cleanup │    │ cleanup │           │
  │  │ ✅ done │    │ ✅ done │    │ ✅ done │    │ running │           │
  │  └─────────┘    └─────────┘    └─────────┘    └─────────┘           │
  │   (deleted)      (deleted)      (kept: 3)     (current)              │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │  CronJob keeps last N jobs (successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3)  │    │
  │  │  Older jobs are automatically deleted                        │    │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📅 Cron Syntax — Five-Field Schedule

  ┌───────────────── minute (0 - 59)
  │ ┌───────────────── hour (0 - 23)
  │ │ ┌───────────────── day of month (1 - 31)
  │ │ │ ┌───────────────── month (1 - 12)  
  │ │ │ │ ┌───────────────── day of week (0 - 6, Sunday = 0)
  │ │ │ │ │
  * * * * *
  
  Special characters:
  *    = every value (every minute, every hour, etc.)
  */5  = every 5 units (*/5 in minute = every 5 minutes)
  1,15 = specific values (1st and 15th)
  1-5  = range (Monday through Friday if in day-of-week)
Schedule Meaning Use Case
*/5 * * * * Every 5 minutes Health checks, quick syncs
0 * * * * Every hour (at :00) Hourly reports
0 2 * * * Daily at 2:00 AM Nightly cleanup/backups
0 9 * * 1 Monday at 9:00 AM Weekly email digest
0 0 1 * * First of month at midnight Monthly reports
0 */6 * * * Every 6 hours Database backups

20.5 CronJob Configuration

CronJobs have additional configuration options for handling missed schedules, concurrent runs, and job history.

CronJob with All Configuration Options 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: anihpj-daily-cleanup
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"                     # Daily at 2 AM
  timeZone: "America/New_York"              # Timezone (K8s 1.27+)
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid                 # Don't run if previous still running
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3             # Keep last 3 successful jobs
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1                 # Keep last 1 failed job
  startingDeadlineSeconds: 200              # If missed by >200s, skip this run
  suspend: false                            # Set true to pause the schedule
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: cleanup
              image: anihpj:v1.2.0
              command: ["python", "manage.py", "clearsessions"]
          restartPolicy: Never
      backoffLimit: 2
      activeDeadlineSeconds: 300            # Kill job if still running after 5 min
✅ Allow

concurrencyPolicy: Allow
Multiple jobs can run simultaneously. Use for independent tasks that don't conflict.

⏸️ Forbid

concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
Skip new job if previous is still running. Use for tasks that shouldn't overlap.

🔄 Replace

concurrencyPolicy: Replace
Cancel running job, start new one. Use when latest run is more important.

📝 startingDeadlineSeconds

If the CronJob controller misses a scheduled time (e.g., controller was down), it will still start the job if less than startingDeadlineSeconds has passed. If more time has passed, that run is skipped. Set this to prevent a flood of catch-up runs after downtime.

20.6 CronJobs for anihpj — Real Examples

Here are practical CronJob examples for the anihpj job board application, covering common maintenance and automation tasks.

Clean Expired Job Postings (Daily)

Remove job postings that have passed their expiration date — runs every night at midnight.

k8s/cronjob-cleanup.yaml 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: anihpj-cleanup-expired
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  schedule: "0 0 * * *"                     # Daily at midnight
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: cleanup
              image: anihpj:v1.2.0
              command:
                - python
                - manage.py
                - shell
                - -c
                - |
                  from django.utils import timezone
                  from jobpost.models import JobPost
                  expired = JobPost.objects.filter(
                      expires_at__lt=timezone.now(),
                      is_active=True
                  )
                  count = expired.update(is_active=False)
                  print(f'Deactivated {count} expired job postings')
              envFrom:
                - secretRef:
                    name: anihpj-db-secrets
          restartPolicy: Never
      ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 86400        # Delete after 24 hours

Email Weekly Summary (Monday 9 AM)

Send weekly digest emails to all Authors with their job posting statistics.

k8s/cronjob-weekly-digest.yaml 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: anihpj-weekly-digest
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  schedule: "0 9 * * 1"                     # Monday at 9:00 AM
  timeZone: "America/New_York"
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: mailer
              image: anihpj:v1.2.0
              command: ["python", "manage.py", "send_weekly_digest"]
              env:
                - name: EMAIL_HOST
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: anihpj-email
                      key: host
                - name: EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: anihpj-email
                      key: password
              envFrom:
                - secretRef:
                    name: anihpj-db-secrets
          restartPolicy: Never
      backoffLimit: 2                       # Retry twice on failure

Generate Salary Reports (Monthly)

Generate CSV reports of salary statistics across all job postings — saved to cloud storage.

k8s/cronjob-monthly-report.yaml 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: anihpj-monthly-report
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  schedule: "0 0 1 * *"                     # First of month at midnight
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: reporter
              image: anihpj:v1.2.0
              command: ["python", "manage.py", "generate_salary_report"]
              args:
                - "--output-bucket"
                - "s3://anihpj-reports/salary/"
                - "--format"
                - "csv"
              env:
                - name: AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: anihpj-aws
                      key: access_key
                - name: AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: anihpj-aws
                      key: secret_key
              envFrom:
                - secretRef:
                    name: anihpj-db-secrets
          restartPolicy: Never
      activeDeadlineSeconds: 1800           # 30 min timeout

Database Backup (Every 6 Hours)

Create PostgreSQL backups and upload to S3 — runs 4 times per day.

k8s/cronjob-db-backup.yaml 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: anihpj-db-backup
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  schedule: "0 */6 * * *"                   # Every 6 hours
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 4             # Keep last 4 backups' jobs
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 2
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: backup
              image: postgres:15-alpine     # Use official postgres image
              command:
                - /bin/sh
                - -c
                - |
                  TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)
                  BACKUP_FILE="/tmp/anihpj-${TIMESTAMP}.sql.gz"
                  
                  echo "Starting backup: ${BACKUP_FILE}"
                  pg_dump $DATABASE_URL | gzip > ${BACKUP_FILE}
                  
                  echo "Uploading to S3..."
                  aws s3 cp ${BACKUP_FILE} s3://anihpj-backups/db/
                  
                  echo "Backup complete: ${BACKUP_FILE}"
              env:
                - name: DATABASE_URL
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: anihpj-db-secrets
                      key: DATABASE_URL
                - name: AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: anihpj-aws
                      key: access_key
                - name: AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: anihpj-aws
                      key: secret_key
          restartPolicy: Never
      activeDeadlineSeconds: 600            # 10 min timeout
      backoffLimit: 2

20.7 Monitoring Jobs and CronJobs

Keeping track of your Jobs and CronJobs is essential for ensuring scheduled tasks run correctly and debugging failures.

Job and CronJob Monitoring Commands 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# VIEWING JOBS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════

# List all jobs
kubectl get jobs -n anihpj-app
# NAME                          COMPLETIONS   DURATION   AGE
# anihpj-migrate                1/1           8s         2h
# anihpj-db-backup-28456789     1/1           45s        6h
# anihpj-cleanup-expired-284..  1/1           12s        18h

# Get job details
kubectl describe job anihpj-migrate -n anihpj-app

# View job's pod logs
kubectl logs job/anihpj-migrate -n anihpj-app

# Watch job progress
kubectl get jobs -n anihpj-app -w

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# VIEWING CRONJOBS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════

# List all cronjobs
kubectl get cronjobs -n anihpj-app
# NAME                    SCHEDULE      SUSPEND   ACTIVE   LAST SCHEDULE
# anihpj-cleanup-expired  0 0 * * *     False     0        18h
# anihpj-weekly-digest    0 9 * * 1     False     0        3d
# anihpj-db-backup        0 */6 * * *   False     0        2h

# Get cronjob details (shows next scheduled run)
kubectl describe cronjob anihpj-db-backup -n anihpj-app

# View jobs created by a cronjob
kubectl get jobs -n anihpj-app -l job-name=anihpj-db-backup

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# DEBUGGING FAILED JOBS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════

# Find failed jobs
kubectl get jobs -n anihpj-app --field-selector=status.successful=0

# Get failed job's pod
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -l job-name=anihpj-migrate --field-selector=status.phase=Failed

# View failure logs
kubectl logs job/anihpj-migrate -n anihpj-app --previous

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# MANUAL OPERATIONS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════

# Manually trigger a cronjob NOW
kubectl create job --from=cronjob/anihpj-db-backup manual-backup -n anihpj-app

# Suspend a cronjob (pause scheduling)
kubectl patch cronjob anihpj-weekly-digest -p '{"spec":{"suspend":true}}' -n anihpj-app

# Resume a cronjob
kubectl patch cronjob anihpj-weekly-digest -p '{"spec":{"suspend":false}}' -n anihpj-app

# Delete a job manually
kubectl delete job anihpj-migrate -n anihpj-app

📊 Job/CronJob Status Reference

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  JOB STATUS                                                          │
  │                                                                       │
  │  COMPLETIONS column:                                                  │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ 0/1  = Job running, 0 completions so far (out of 1 needed)     │  │
  │  │ 1/1  = Job completed successfully ✅                            │  │
  │  │ 0/5  = Parallel job, 0 of 5 completions done                   │  │
  │  │ 3/5  = Parallel job, 3 of 5 completions done                   │  │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                                       │
  │  CRONJOB STATUS                                                       │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ACTIVE column:                                                       │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ 0 = No jobs currently running                                  │  │
  │  │ 1 = One job instance currently running                         │  │
  │  │ 2 = Two jobs running (concurrencyPolicy: Allow)                │  │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                                       │
  │  SUSPEND column:                                                      │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ False = CronJob is active, will create jobs on schedule        │  │
  │  │ True  = CronJob is paused, no new jobs will be created         │  │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
