✅ Quick Reference: Jobs
  • kubectl get jobs — list all
  • kubectl logs job/NAME — view output
  • kubectl delete job NAME — cleanup
🕐 Quick Reference: CronJobs
  • kubectl get cronjobs — list all
  • kubectl create job --from=cronjob/NAME — manual run
  • kubectl patch cronjob NAME -p '{"spec":{"suspend":true}}' — pause
💡 Best Practices for Jobs and CronJobs
  • Always set ttlSecondsAfterFinished to auto-cleanup completed jobs
  • Use activeDeadlineSeconds to prevent runaway jobs
  • Set concurrencyPolicy: Forbid for jobs that shouldn't overlap
  • Keep backoffLimit low (2-3) to fail fast on broken jobs
  • Monitor CronJob history regularly — missed runs indicate problems
  • Use kubectl create job --from=cronjob/NAME to test manually before scheduling

🔧 Job Troubleshooting Guide

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  COMMON JOB ISSUES AND FIXES                                         │
  │                                                                       │
  │  SYMPTOM                   CAUSE                   FIX                │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────       │
  │  Job stuck at 0/1         Pod can't schedule      Check node         │
  │                           (no resources)           capacity/events   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Pod: ImagePullBackOff    Wrong image name        Fix image tag      │
  │                           or missing auth          or add imagePull  │
  │                                                    Secret            │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Pod: CrashLoopBackOff    Command fails           Check logs:        │
  │                           immediately              kubectl logs      │
  │                                                    job/NAME          │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Job keeps retrying       restartPolicy: Always   Change to Never    │
  │  forever                  (invalid for Jobs)       or OnFailure      │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Job completed but pods   Normal! Jobs don't      Set ttlSeconds     │
  │  still visible            auto-delete pods        AfterFinished      │
  │                                                                       │
  │  CronJob never runs       Suspended or wrong      kubectl describe   │
  │                           schedule syntax          cronjob NAME      │
  │                                                                       │
  │  CronJob runs but fails   startingDeadline        Increase deadline  │
  │  silently                 too short               or check logs      │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Two jobs running at      concurrencyPolicy       Set to Forbid      │
  │  same time                is Allow (default)      or Replace         │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Check Command What to Look For
Job events kubectl describe job NAME FailedCreate, DeadlineExceeded
Pod logs kubectl logs job/NAME Application errors, stack traces
Pod status kubectl get pods -l job-name=NAME Running, Failed, Completed
CronJob schedule kubectl describe cronjob NAME Last Schedule, Active
Failed jobs kubectl get jobs --field-selector=status.successful=0 Any failed completions

📦 Part 7: Workload Types Chapters 21-22b

Chapter 21: StatefulSets — Stateful Applications

Not all applications are stateless. Databases, message queues, and caches need stable identities, persistent storage, and ordered operations. StatefulSets provide these guarantees — this chapter shows you how to run stateful workloads like PostgreSQL for your anihpj application.

🗄️ Databases 🔢 Ordered Pods 💾 Persistent Storage 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Stateless vs Stateful — fundamental differences
  • ✅ StatefulSet features — what makes them special
  • ✅ When to use StatefulSets (and when NOT to)
  • ✅ Running PostgreSQL for anihpj — complete example
  • ✅ Headless Services — stable network identity
  • ✅ Update strategies — RollingUpdate, OnDelete, partition
  • ✅ Backup and restore strategies

21.1 Stateless vs Stateful Applications

Before diving into StatefulSets, you must understand the fundamental difference between stateless and stateful applications. This determines which workload controller you should use.

🔄 Stateless vs Stateful — Visual Comparison

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  STATELESS APPLICATION (anihpj Django app)                                 │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐                        │
  │  │  anihpj-abc │  │  anihpj-xyz │  │  anihpj-123 │   ◀── Random names     │
  │  │  🔵 Pod     │  │  🔵 Pod     │  │  🔵 Pod     │                        │
  │  │  Any can    │  │  Any can    │  │  Any can    │   ◀── Interchangeable │
  │  │  handle     │  │  handle     │  │  handle     │                        │
  │  │  request    │  │  request    │  │  request    │                        │
  │  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘                        │
  │         │                │                │                               │
  │         └────────────────┴────────────────┘                               │
  │                          │                                                │
  │                    No local state                                         │
  │                    Data lives in DB                                       │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ✅ Use: Deployment + HPA                                                  │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  STATEFUL APPLICATION (PostgreSQL database)                                │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐                        │
  │  │  postgres-0 │  │  postgres-1 │  │  postgres-2 │   ◀── Ordered names   │
  │  │  🟢 PRIMARY │  │  🟡 REPLICA │  │  🟡 REPLICA │                        │
  │  │  Writes     │  │  Reads      │  │  Reads      │   ◀── Unique roles    │
  │  │  Only this  │  │  Syncs from │  │  Syncs from │                        │
  │  │  gets data  │  │  primary    │  │  primary    │                        │
  │  └──────┬──────┘  └──────┬──────┘  └──────┬──────┘                        │
  │         │                │                │                               │
  │    ┌────▼────┐      ┌────▼────┐      ┌────▼────┐                          │
  │    │ PVC-0   │      │ PVC-1   │      │ PVC-2   │   ◀── Own storage       │
  │    │ 💾 10Gi │      │ 💾 10Gi │      │ 💾 10Gi │                          │
  │    └─────────┘      └─────────┘      └─────────┘                          │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ✅ Use: StatefulSet                                                       │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📊 Complete Comparison Table

Aspect Stateless (Deployment) Stateful (StatefulSet)
Pod Identity Random names: anihpj-7f8b9c-abc Ordered names: postgres-0, -1, -2
Storage Shared or none (ephemeral) Each pod gets own PVC (persistent)
Network Identity Random IP, accessed via Service Stable DNS per pod: pod-0.svc.ns
Scaling Up Any order (parallel OK) Sequential: 0 → 1 → 2 (ordered)
Scaling Down Any order (terminate any) Reverse: 2 → 1 → 0 (ordered)
Pod Restart New random name, new IP Same name, reconnects to same PVC
Example Apps Web apps, APIs, workers Databases, queues, caches

⚠️ Key Misconception

"My app stores data, so it must be stateful" — Wrong! If your Django app stores data in a database, the app itself is still stateless. The database is stateful. Your Django Deployment connects to a PostgreSQL StatefulSet.

  anihpj pods (stateless)          PostgreSQL pods (stateful)
  ┌──────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────────┐
  │  ⚡ Deployment        │  ────▶ │  🗄️ StatefulSet       │
  │  3 interchangeable   │   SQL   │  1 primary + 2 replica│
  │  pods, no local data │         │  each has own storage │
  └──────────────────────┘         └──────────────────────┘
🌐

Stateless Examples

  • Django/Flask/React apps
  • REST APIs
  • Worker processes
  • Load balancers
  • Cron job containers

Use Deployment

🗄️

Stateful Examples

  • PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB
  • Kafka, RabbitMQ
  • Redis Cluster
  • Elasticsearch
  • Zookeeper, etcd

Use StatefulSet

21.2 StatefulSet Features

StatefulSets provide four key guarantees that Deployments don't. These are essential for running databases and other stateful applications.

🏗️ StatefulSet Architecture

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  StatefulSet: postgres   (requires Headless Service)                       │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐         │
  │  │   postgres-0     │  │   postgres-1     │  │   postgres-2     │         │
  │  │   ───────────    │  │   ───────────    │  │   ───────────    │         │
  │  │   🗄️ PRIMARY     │  │   🗄️ REPLICA     │  │   🗄️ REPLICA     │         │
  │  │                  │  │                  │  │                  │         │
  │  │  Stable DNS:     │  │  Stable DNS:     │  │  Stable DNS:     │         │
  │  │  postgres-0.     │  │  postgres-1.     │  │  postgres-2.     │         │
  │  │  postgres-svc.   │  │  postgres-svc.   │  │  postgres-svc.   │         │
  │  │  anihpj.svc.     │  │  anihpj.svc.     │  │  anihpj.svc.     │         │
  │  │  cluster.local   │  │  cluster.local   │  │  cluster.local   │         │
  │  │                  │  │                  │  │                  │         │
  │  └────────┬─────────┘  └────────┬─────────┘  └────────┬─────────┘         │
  │           │                     │                     │                   │
  │  ┌────────▼─────────┐  ┌────────▼─────────┐  ┌────────▼─────────┐         │
  │  │     PVC-0        │  │     PVC-1        │  │     PVC-2        │         │
  │  │     💾 10Gi      │  │     💾 10Gi      │  │     💾 10Gi      │         │
  │  │  data-postgres-0 │  │  data-postgres-1 │  │  data-postgres-2 │         │
  │  │                  │  │                  │  │                  │         │
  │  │    PERSISTS!     │  │    PERSISTS!     │  │    PERSISTS!     │         │
  │  │  Pod dies? PVC   │  │  Pod dies? PVC   │  │  Pod dies? PVC   │         │
  │  │  survives! ✅    │  │  survives! ✅    │  │  survives! ✅    │         │
  │  └──────────────────┘  └──────────────────┘  └──────────────────┘         │
  │                                                                            │
  │  CREATE ORDER: 0 ─────▶ 1 ─────▶ 2                                        │
  │                (wait ready) (wait ready)                                   │
  │                                                                            │
  │  DELETE ORDER: 2 ◀───── 1 ◀───── 0                                        │
  │                (terminate) (terminate)                                     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🔑 Four Key StatefulSet Guarantees

🏷️
1. Stable Pod Names

Pods are named with a predictable pattern: <statefulset>-0, -1, -2...

postgres-0
postgres-1
postgres-2

Names survive restarts!

🌐
2. Stable Network ID

Each pod gets a stable DNS record via the Headless Service

<pod>.<svc>.<ns>.svc.cluster.local

Clients can connect to specific pods

💾
3. Stable Storage

Each pod gets its own PVC via volumeClaimTemplates

data-postgres-0
data-postgres-1

PVC persists even if pod deleted!

📋
4. Ordered Operations

Pods are created/deleted in order, one at a time

Create: 0→1→2
Delete: 2→1→0

Essential for primary/replica setup

💡 Why Ordered Operations Matter

For databases like PostgreSQL with primary/replica architecture:

  • postgres-0 must be running and ready before postgres-1 starts (replica needs to connect to primary)
  • Scaling down removes postgres-2 first (replica), never the primary
  • This ensures data consistency and proper replication

📜 Headless Service — Required for StatefulSets

A headless service (clusterIP: None) gives each pod its own DNS record instead of a single cluster IP.

postgres-headless-svc.yaml 
# Headless Service for PostgreSQL StatefulSet
# This creates DNS entries for each pod
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres-svc
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app: postgres
spec:
  clusterIP: None  # ◀── This makes it a headless service
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
    - port: 5432
      targetPort: 5432
      name: postgres

# Result DNS records:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
# postgres-0.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local
# postgres-1.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local
# postgres-2.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────

21.3 When to Use StatefulSets

StatefulSets add complexity. Use them only when you need their guarantees.

✅ Use StatefulSet When

  • Databases: PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, Cassandra
  • Message Queues: Kafka, RabbitMQ, NATS
  • Distributed Caches: Redis Cluster, Memcached
  • Search Engines: Elasticsearch, Solr
  • Coordination Systems: Zookeeper, etcd, Consul
  • Any app that needs:
    • Stable network identity
    • Persistent per-pod storage
    • Ordered scaling/updates

❌ Don't Use StatefulSet When

  • Web Applications: Django, Flask, React, Node.js
  • REST APIs: Any stateless HTTP service
  • Background Workers: Celery, Sidekiq
  • Cron Jobs: Scheduled tasks
  • Any app where:
    • Pods are interchangeable
    • State lives in external database
    • Order doesn't matter
  • Use Deployment + HPA instead!

🚨 Common Mistakes

Mistake Why It's Wrong Correct Approach
"Django stores sessions, so StatefulSet" Sessions go in Redis/DB, not pods Use Deployment for Django
"I need persistent logs" Logs should go to central logging Use Deployment + Fluentd
"I want stable pod names" Name stability alone isn't enough reason Use Deployment, identify by labels
"Running PostgreSQL? Always StatefulSet" For dev/test, simple Deployment is OK StatefulSet for production DBs

🤔 Decision Flowchart: Deployment vs StatefulSet

                          ┌─────────────────────────┐
                          │   Your Application      │
                          └───────────┬─────────────┘
                                      │
                          ┌───────────▼─────────────┐
                          │ Does each pod need its  │
                          │ OWN persistent storage? │
                          └───────────┬─────────────┘
                                      │
                         ┌────────────┴────────────┐
                         │                         │
                        YES                        NO
                         │                         │
                ┌────────▼────────┐       ┌────────▼────────┐
                │ Does pod need   │       │                 │
                │ stable network  │       │   Use           │
                │ identity (DNS)? │       │   DEPLOYMENT    │
                └────────┬────────┘       │                 │
                         │                └─────────────────┘
                        YES
                         │
                ┌────────▼────────┐
                │ Does order of   │
                │ create/delete   │
                │ matter?         │
                └────────┬────────┘
                         │
                        YES
                         │
                ┌────────▼────────┐
                │                 │
                │   Use           │
                │   STATEFULSET   │
                │                 │
                └─────────────────┘

  Examples:
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  PostgreSQL, Kafka, Elasticsearch ───▶ StatefulSet ✅
  Django, React, API servers       ───▶ Deployment  ✅
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

21.4 Running PostgreSQL for anihpj

Let's put theory into practice. We'll set up a production-ready PostgreSQL StatefulSet for our anihpj Django application with headless service, persistent volumes, and proper configuration.

🏗️ anihpj + PostgreSQL Architecture

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  anihpj Production Architecture                                              │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │  EXTERNAL TRAFFIC                                                       │ │
  │  │                       ▼                                                 │ │
  │  │              ┌───────────────┐                                         │ │
  │  │              │   Ingress     │                                         │ │
  │  │              │  (anihpj.com) │                                         │ │
  │  │              └───────┬───────┘                                         │ │
  │  │                      │                                                 │ │
  │  │              ┌───────▼───────┐                                         │ │
  │  │              │   Service     │  ClusterIP (normal)                     │ │
  │  │              │ anihpj-svc    │                                         │ │
  │  │              └───────┬───────┘                                         │ │
  │  └───────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
  │                          │                                                   │
  │  ┌───────────────────────▼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │  DEPLOYMENT: anihpj (stateless Django)                                  │ │
  │  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐                  │ │
  │  │  │ anihpj-abc   │  │ anihpj-xyz   │  │ anihpj-123   │                  │ │
  │  │  │ 🐍 Django    │  │ 🐍 Django    │  │ 🐍 Django    │                  │ │
  │  │  │              │  │              │  │              │                  │ │
  │  │  │ Env:         │  │ Env:         │  │ Env:         │                  │ │
  │  │  │ DATABASE_URL │  │ DATABASE_URL │  │ DATABASE_URL │                  │ │
  │  │  │ =postgres-0. │  │ =postgres-0. │  │ =postgres-0. │                  │ │
  │  │  │  postgres-svc│  │  postgres-svc│  │  postgres-svc│                  │ │
  │  │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘                  │ │
  │  │              │              │              │                           │ │
  │  └──────────────┴──────────────┴──────────────┴───────────────────────────┘ │
  │                                │                                             │
  │                        SQL connection                                        │
  │                                │                                             │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────▼───────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │  HEADLESS SERVICE: postgres-svc (clusterIP: None)                       │ │
  │  │                                                                          │ │
  │  │  DNS: postgres-0.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local ◀── connect here│ │
  │  │  DNS: postgres-1.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local   (replica)      │ │
  │  └─────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
  │                                │                                             │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────▼───────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │  STATEFULSET: postgres                                                   │ │
  │  │  ┌──────────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────────┐                     │ │
  │  │  │     postgres-0       │  │     postgres-1       │                     │ │
  │  │  │  🗄️ PRIMARY (writes) │  │  🗄️ REPLICA (reads)  │                     │ │
  │  │  │                      │  │  syncs from -0       │                     │ │
  │  │  └──────────┬───────────┘  └──────────┬───────────┘                     │ │
  │  │             │                         │                                  │ │
  │  │        ┌────▼────┐               ┌────▼────┐                            │ │
  │  │        │ PVC-0   │               │ PVC-1   │                            │ │
  │  │        │ 💾 10Gi │               │ 💾 10Gi │                            │ │
  │  │        └─────────┘               └─────────┘                            │ │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

21.4a Headless Service for PostgreSQL

First, create the headless service that will provide stable DNS for each PostgreSQL pod.

k8s/postgres-headless-svc.yaml 
# Headless Service for PostgreSQL StatefulSet
# Creates DNS entries: postgres-0.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres-svc
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app: postgres
    component: database
spec:
  clusterIP: None    # ◀── HEADLESS: no single IP, individual pod DNS
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
    - name: postgres
      port: 5432
      targetPort: 5432
  # Important: publishNotReadyAddresses allows DNS even for not-ready pods
  publishNotReadyAddresses: true
🖥️ Apply the Headless Service
# Create namespace if not exists
kubectl create namespace anihpj --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

# Apply the headless service
kubectl apply -f k8s/postgres-headless-svc.yaml

# Verify it's headless (clusterIP should be "None")
kubectl get svc postgres-svc -n anihpj
# NAME          TYPE       CLUSTER-IP   PORT(S)    AGE
# postgres-svc  ClusterIP  None         5432/TCP   5s

21.4b StatefulSet for PostgreSQL

Now create the StatefulSet with persistent storage for each pod.

k8s/postgres-statefulset.yaml 
# PostgreSQL StatefulSet for anihpj
# Production-ready with persistent storage
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app: postgres
spec:
  serviceName: postgres-svc    # ◀── Must match headless service name
  replicas: 2                  # Primary + 1 replica
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: postgres
          image: postgres:15-alpine
          ports:
            - containerPort: 5432
              name: postgres
          
          # Environment variables from Secret
          env:
            - name: POSTGRES_DB
              value: anihpj_db
            - name: POSTGRES_USER
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: postgres-secret
                  key: username
            - name: POSTGRES_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: postgres-secret
                  key: password
            - name: PGDATA
              value: /var/lib/postgresql/data/pgdata
          
          # Resource limits (adjust for your cluster)
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "256Mi"
              cpu: "250m"
            limits:
              memory: "512Mi"
              cpu: "500m"
          
          # Mount the persistent volume
          volumeMounts:
            - name: data
              mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
          
          # Health checks
          readinessProbe:
            exec:
              command:
                - pg_isready
                - -U
                - $(POSTGRES_USER)
                - -d
                - $(POSTGRES_DB)
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 10
          
          livenessProbe:
            exec:
              command:
                - pg_isready
                - -U
                - $(POSTGRES_USER)
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 10

  # ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  # │  volumeClaimTemplates — each pod gets its own PVC               │
  # └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  volumeClaimTemplates:
    - metadata:
        name: data    # Creates: data-postgres-0, data-postgres-1, etc.
      spec:
        accessModes:
          - ReadWriteOnce
        storageClassName: standard  # Use your cluster's storage class
        resources:
          requests:
            storage: 10Gi

💡 volumeClaimTemplates Explained

Unlike Deployments that share volumes, StatefulSets use volumeClaimTemplates to create a unique PVC for each pod:

PodPVC CreatedData Location
postgres-0data-postgres-0Independent 10Gi volume
postgres-1data-postgres-1Independent 10Gi volume
postgres-2data-postgres-2Independent 10Gi volume

Critical: If you delete the pod, the PVC survives! When the pod restarts, it reconnects to the same PVC.

🔐 Create the Secret First
k8s/postgres-secret.yaml 
# PostgreSQL credentials (use sealed secrets in production!)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: postgres-secret
  namespace: anihpj
type: Opaque
stringData:    # Using stringData for plain text (auto base64 encoded)
  username: anihpj_user
  password: "super-secret-password-change-me"  # ◀── Change this!
  
# ⚠️ WARNING: Don't commit real passwords to git!
# Use: kubectl create secret generic postgres-secret \
#        --from-literal=username=anihpj_user \
#        --from-literal=password=$(openssl rand -base64 32)
🖥️ Deploy PostgreSQL StatefulSet
# Create the secret (use proper secrets management in prod!)
kubectl apply -f k8s/postgres-secret.yaml

# Deploy the StatefulSet
kubectl apply -f k8s/postgres-statefulset.yaml

# Watch pods being created IN ORDER
kubectl get pods -n anihpj -w
# NAME         READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# postgres-0   0/1     Pending   0          0s
# postgres-0   0/1     ContainerCreating   0          2s
# postgres-0   1/1     Running             0          10s   ◀── 0 ready first
# postgres-1   0/1     Pending             0          0s    ◀── THEN 1 starts
# postgres-1   1/1     Running             0          12s

# Check PVCs created
kubectl get pvc -n anihpj
# NAME              STATUS   VOLUME    CAPACITY   ACCESS MODES
# data-postgres-0   Bound    pv-xxx    10Gi       RWO
# data-postgres-1   Bound    pv-yyy    10Gi       RWO

21.4c Connecting anihpj to PostgreSQL

Configure your Django application to connect to the PostgreSQL StatefulSet using its stable DNS name.

k8s/anihpj-deployment.yaml (updated) 
# anihpj Django Deployment connecting to PostgreSQL StatefulSet
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: yourregistry/anihpj:v1.2.0
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          
          env:
            # ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
            # │  DATABASE_URL points to StatefulSet via headless DNS    │
            # └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
            - name: DATABASE_HOST
              # Connect to primary (postgres-0) for writes
              value: "postgres-0.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local"
            
            - name: DATABASE_PORT
              value: "5432"
            
            - name: DATABASE_NAME
              value: "anihpj_db"
            
            - name: DATABASE_USER
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: postgres-secret
                  key: username
            
            - name: DATABASE_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: postgres-secret
                  key: password
            
            # Or use a single DATABASE_URL environment variable
            # DATABASE_URL: postgres://user:pass@postgres-0.postgres-svc/db

💡 DNS Connection Patterns

Use CaseDNS to UseExample
Write to primary Specific pod DNS postgres-0.postgres-svc
Read from any replica Service DNS (round-robin) postgres-svc
Read from specific replica Specific pod DNS postgres-1.postgres-svc
🐍 Django settings.py Configuration
anihpj/settings.py 
import os

DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.postgresql',
        'NAME': os.environ.get('DATABASE_NAME', 'anihpj_db'),
        'USER': os.environ.get('DATABASE_USER', 'anihpj_user'),
        'PASSWORD': os.environ.get('DATABASE_PASSWORD', ''),
        'HOST': os.environ.get('DATABASE_HOST', 'localhost'),
        'PORT': os.environ.get('DATABASE_PORT', '5432'),
        'OPTIONS': {
            'connect_timeout': 10,
        },
    }
}

# For read replicas (optional):
# 'read_replica': {
#     'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.postgresql',
#     'HOST': 'postgres-1.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local',
#     ...
# }

21.4d Initializing the Database

After deploying PostgreSQL, you need to run Django migrations to set up the database schema.

🖥️ Database Initialization Commands
# 1. Verify PostgreSQL is running
kubectl get pods -n anihpj -l app=postgres
# NAME         READY   STATUS    AGE
# postgres-0   1/1     Running   5m
# postgres-1   1/1     Running   4m

# 2. Connect to PostgreSQL to verify
kubectl exec -it postgres-0 -n anihpj -- psql -U anihpj_user -d anihpj_db
# psql (15.2)
# anihpj_db=# \dt
# No relations found.  (empty database, ready for migrations!)
# anihpj_db=# \q

# 3. Run Django migrations (one-time Job or exec)
kubectl exec -it deploy/anihpj -n anihpj -- python manage.py migrate
# Operations to perform:
#   Apply all migrations: admin, auth, contenttypes, jobpost, sessions
# Running migrations:
#   Applying contenttypes.0001_initial... OK
#   Applying auth.0001_initial... OK
#   ...

# 4. Create Django superuser (optional)
kubectl exec -it deploy/anihpj -n anihpj -- python manage.py createsuperuser

# 5. Verify tables created
kubectl exec -it postgres-0 -n anihpj -- psql -U anihpj_user -d anihpj_db -c "\dt"
#               List of relations
#  Schema |            Name             | Type  |   Owner
# --------+-----------------------------+-------+-----------
#  public | auth_group                  | table | anihpj_user
#  public | auth_user                   | table | anihpj_user
#  public | jobpost_job                 | table | anihpj_user
#  ...

⚠️ Migration Best Practices

  • Use a Job: For production, use a Kubernetes Job to run migrations during deployment
  • Rolling migrations: Ensure migrations are backward compatible so old pods work during rollout
  • Never run concurrent migrations: Only one migration job should run at a time

21.5 StatefulSet Update Strategies

StatefulSets support two update strategies. Unlike Deployments, updates happen in reverse ordinal order (highest to lowest) to protect the primary.

📊 Update Strategies Comparison

Strategy Behavior Use When
RollingUpdate Updates pods automatically
Order: 2 → 1 → 0 (reverse)
One pod at a time 		Default choice for most apps
OnDelete Manual control
Pod updates only when deleted
You control the pace 		Database version upgrades
Critical systems
partition: N Canary updates
Only pods ≥ N get updated
Gradual rollout 		Testing new version on replicas
before updating primary

🔄 RollingUpdate (Default) — Reverse Order

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  StatefulSet RollingUpdate: postgres v14 → v15                             │
  │                                                                            │
  │  Step 1: Initial state (all v14)                                          │
  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐                     │
  │  │  postgres-0  │  │  postgres-1  │  │  postgres-2  │                     │
  │  │  🗄️ v14 🔵   │  │  🗄️ v14 🔵   │  │  🗄️ v14 🔵   │                     │
  │  │  PRIMARY     │  │  REPLICA     │  │  REPLICA     │                     │
  │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │  Step 2: Update highest ordinal FIRST (postgres-2)                        │
  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐                     │
  │  │  postgres-0  │  │  postgres-1  │  │  postgres-2  │                     │
  │  │  🗄️ v14 🔵   │  │  🗄️ v14 🔵   │  │  🗄️ v15 🟢   │  ◀── Updated      │
  │  │  PRIMARY     │  │  REPLICA     │  │  REPLICA     │  ◀── first!        │
  │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │  Step 3: Continue with postgres-1                                         │
  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐                     │
  │  │  postgres-0  │  │  postgres-1  │  │  postgres-2  │                     │
  │  │  🗄️ v14 🔵   │  │  🗄️ v15 🟢   │  │  🗄️ v15 🟢   │                     │
  │  │  PRIMARY     │  │  REPLICA     │  │  REPLICA     │                     │
  │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │  Step 4: Finally update primary (postgres-0) — LAST!                      │
  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐                     │
  │  │  postgres-0  │  │  postgres-1  │  │  postgres-2  │                     │
  │  │  🗄️ v15 🟢   │  │  🗄️ v15 🟢   │  │  🗄️ v15 🟢   │  ✅ Complete       │
  │  │  PRIMARY     │  │  REPLICA     │  │  REPLICA     │                     │
  │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │  💡 Primary updated LAST = safer! Replicas verified first.                │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📜 Partition Strategy — Canary for StatefulSets

postgres-statefulset.yaml (partition strategy) 
# Using partition for canary updates
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
spec:
  replicas: 3
  updateStrategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      partition: 2    # ◀── Only pods with ordinal ≥ 2 get updated
  # ... rest of spec

# How partition works:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# partition: 2 → Only postgres-2 gets the new spec
# partition: 1 → postgres-1 and postgres-2 get new spec
# partition: 0 → All pods get new spec (default behavior)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Workflow:
# 1. Set partition: 2, update image to v15
# 2. Only postgres-2 updates — verify it works
# 3. Set partition: 1 — postgres-1 updates
# 4. Set partition: 0 — postgres-0 (primary) finally updates
🖥️ Update Strategy Commands
# Check current update strategy
kubectl get sts postgres -n anihpj -o jsonpath='{.spec.updateStrategy}'

# Trigger a rolling update (change image version)
kubectl set image sts/postgres postgres=postgres:15.1-alpine -n anihpj

# Watch the rollout (note the reverse order!)
kubectl rollout status sts/postgres -n anihpj

# Use partition for canary update
kubectl patch sts postgres -n anihpj -p '{"spec":{"updateStrategy":{"rollingUpdate":{"partition":2}}}}'

# After verifying postgres-2, reduce partition
kubectl patch sts postgres -n anihpj -p '{"spec":{"updateStrategy":{"rollingUpdate":{"partition":0}}}}'

21.6 Backup and Restore Strategies

Data durability is critical for databases. Here are production-ready backup strategies for your PostgreSQL StatefulSet.

📋

pg_dump CronJob

Traditional logical backup using CronJob

  • SQL dump to cloud storage
  • Easy to restore anywhere
  • Good for small/medium DBs
📸

Volume Snapshots

CSI snapshot of the entire volume

  • Fast point-in-time recovery
  • Instant clone for dev/test
  • Requires CSI snapshot support
🏠

Velero

Full cluster backup solution

  • Backs up resources + volumes
  • Disaster recovery
  • Cluster migration

📜 pg_dump CronJob for Automated Backups

k8s/postgres-backup-cronjob.yaml 
# Automated PostgreSQL backup CronJob
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: postgres-backup
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"    # ◀── Every day at 2 AM
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1
  
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
            - name: backup
              image: postgres:15-alpine
              env:
                - name: PGPASSWORD
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: postgres-secret
                      key: password
                - name: PGUSER
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: postgres-secret
                      key: username
              
              command:
                - /bin/sh
                - -c
                - |
                  # Generate backup filename with timestamp
                  BACKUP_FILE="anihpj-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).sql.gz"
                  
                  # Run pg_dump and compress
                  pg_dump -h postgres-0.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local \
                          -U $PGUSER \
                          -d anihpj_db \
                          --format=plain | gzip > /backup/$BACKUP_FILE
                  
                  # Upload to S3 (requires aws-cli, configure with your bucket)
                  # aws s3 cp /backup/$BACKUP_FILE s3://your-bucket/postgres-backups/
                  
                  echo "Backup completed: $BACKUP_FILE"
              
              volumeMounts:
                - name: backup-storage
                  mountPath: /backup
          
          volumes:
            - name: backup-storage
              emptyDir: {}
              # For persistent backups, use a PVC:
              # persistentVolumeClaim:
              #   claimName: backup-pvc

📸 Volume Snapshot Architecture

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  CSI Volume Snapshots for PostgreSQL                                       │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────┐                                                  │
  │  │  postgres-0         │                                                  │
  │  │  🗄️ PRIMARY         │                                                  │
  │  └──────────┬──────────┘                                                  │
  │             │                                                             │
  │        ┌────▼────┐        VolumeSnapshot                                  │
  │        │ PVC-0   │  ──────────────────────┐                              │
  │        │ 💾 10Gi │                        │                              │
  │        └─────────┘                        ▼                              │
  │                              ┌─────────────────────────┐                  │
  │                              │  📸 Snapshot             │                  │
  │                              │  postgres-0-snap-001     │                  │
  │                              │  2024-01-15 02:00:00     │                  │
  │                              │                          │                  │
  │                              │  Can restore to:         │                  │
  │                              │  • Same PVC (rollback)   │                  │
  │                              │  • New PVC (clone)       │                  │
  │                              │  • Different namespace   │                  │
  │                              └─────────────────────────┘                  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  Commands:                                                                 │
  │  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────               │
  │  # Create snapshot                                                         │
  │  kubectl apply -f volume-snapshot.yaml                                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │  # List snapshots                                                          │
  │  kubectl get volumesnapshots -n anihpj                                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │  # Restore: create PVC from snapshot                                       │
  │  kubectl apply -f pvc-from-snapshot.yaml                                   │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

💡 Backup Best Practices

PracticeWhy
Backup from replica, not primary Reduces load on primary
Test restores regularly Untested backups aren't backups
Store backups off-cluster Survives cluster failure
Use 3-2-1 rule 3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite
Encrypt backups Protect sensitive data

Chapter 22: DaemonSets — One Pod Per Node

Some workloads need to run on every node in your cluster — log collectors, monitoring agents, network plugins. DaemonSets ensure exactly one pod per node, automatically adjusting as nodes join or leave. This chapter shows you when and how to use them.

📝 Log Collection 📊 Monitoring 🌐 Network Plugins 🟢 Beginner-Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ What DaemonSets are and how they work
  • ✅ Common use cases — logging, monitoring, networking
  • ✅ Writing DaemonSet YAML from scratch
  • ✅ Node selection — targeting specific nodes
  • ✅ DaemonSet vs Deployment — when to use which

22.1 What are DaemonSets?

A DaemonSet ensures that exactly one copy of a pod runs on every node (or a selected subset of nodes) in your cluster. It's like having one dedicated worker on every machine.

🔄 DaemonSet — One Pod Per Node

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  DaemonSet: fluentd-log-collector                                          │
  │  "Exactly 1 pod per node — ALWAYS"                                         │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐               │
  │  │    Node 1      │  │    Node 2      │  │    Node 3      │               │
  │  │    (worker)    │  │    (worker)    │  │    (worker)    │               │
  │  │                │  │                │  │                │               │
  │  │  ┌──────────┐  │  │  ┌──────────┐  │  │  ┌──────────┐  │               │
  │  │  │ 📦 app-1 │  │  │  │ 📦 app-x │  │  │  │ 📦 app-y │  │               │
  │  │  └──────────┘  │  │  │ 📦 app-z │  │  │  └──────────┘  │               │
  │  │  ┌──────────┐  │  │  └──────────┘  │  │  ┌──────────┐  │               │
  │  │  │ 📦 app-2 │  │  │                │  │  │ 📦 app-q │  │               │
  │  │  └──────────┘  │  │                │  │  └──────────┘  │               │
  │  │                │  │                │  │                │               │
  │  │  ╔══════════╗  │  │  ╔══════════╗  │  │  ╔══════════╗  │               │
  │  │  ║ 📝 LOG  ║  │  │  ║ 📝 LOG  ║  │  │  ║ 📝 LOG  ║  │               │
  │  │  ║ DAEMON  ║  │  │  ║ DAEMON  ║  │  │  ║ DAEMON  ║  │               │
  │  │  ║(fluentd)║  │  │  ║(fluentd)║  │  │  ║(fluentd)║  │               │
  │  │  ╚══════════╝  │  │  ╚══════════╝  │  │  ╚══════════╝  │               │
  │  │   1 per node   │  │   1 per node   │  │   1 per node   │               │
  │  └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘               │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ✨ NEW NODE JOINS THE CLUSTER                                             │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  ┌──────────┐ │
  │  │    Node 1      │  │    Node 2      │  │    Node 3      │  │  Node 4  │ │
  │  │    (worker)    │  │    (worker)    │  │    (worker)    │  │  (NEW!)  │ │
  │  │  ╔══════════╗  │  │  ╔══════════╗  │  │  ╔══════════╗  │  │          │ │
  │  │  ║ 📝 LOG  ║  │  │  ║ 📝 LOG  ║  │  │  ║ 📝 LOG  ║  │  │╔════════╗│ │
  │  │  ╚══════════╝  │  │  ╚══════════╝  │  │  ╚══════════╝  │  │║📝 LOG ║│ │
  │  │                │  │                │  │                │  │║AUTO!  ║│ │
  │  └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘  │╚════════╝│ │
  │                                                              └──────────┘ │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ✅ Node joins  → DaemonSet pod auto-created                              │
  │  🗑️ Node removed → DaemonSet pod auto-deleted                             │
  │  💡 No manual scaling needed — it's automatic!                            │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
✨
Auto Node Tracking

New node joins? Pod gets scheduled automatically.

🗑️
Auto Cleanup

Node removed? Pod gets garbage collected.

1️⃣
Exactly One

Guaranteed single pod per node — never more, never less.

22.2 Common DaemonSet Use Cases

DaemonSets are essential for infrastructure-level workloads that need to run everywhere in your cluster.

📝

a) Log Collection

Collect and ship logs from every node to a central location.

Popular Tools:
  • Fluentd / Fluent Bit — CNCF logging solution
  • Filebeat — Elastic Stack log shipper
  • Promtail — Loki/Grafana log agent
  • Vector — High-performance log pipeline
Node 1       Node 2       Node 3
┌─────┐     ┌─────┐     ┌─────┐
│📝 FD│     │📝 FD│     │📝 FD│   FD = Fluentd
│  │  │     │  │  │     │  │  │
│  ▼  │     │  ▼  │     │  ▼  │
└──┼──┘     └──┼──┘     └──┼──┘
   │           │           │
   └─────┬─────┴─────┬─────┘
         │           │
         ▼           ▼
    ┌─────────────────────┐
    │ 📊 Elasticsearch    │
    │    or Loki          │
    └─────────────────────┘
📊

b) Node Monitoring

Collect CPU, memory, disk, and network metrics from each node.

Popular Tools:
  • Prometheus Node Exporter — Exposes node metrics
  • Datadog Agent — Full-stack observability
  • New Relic Agent — APM + infrastructure
  • cAdvisor — Container resource metrics
Node 1       Node 2       Node 3
┌─────┐     ┌─────┐     ┌─────┐
│📊 NE│     │📊 NE│     │📊 NE│   NE = Node Exporter
│:9100│     │:9100│     │:9100│   (metrics port)
└──▲──┘     └──▲──┘     └──▲──┘
   │           │           │
   └─────┬─────┴─────┬─────┘
         │
    ┌────▼─────────────┐
    │ 📈 Prometheus    │
    │    (scrapes all) │
    └──────────────────┘
🌐

c) Network Plugins (CNI)

Container Network Interface agents that handle pod networking.

Popular CNIs:
  • Calico — Network policy + BGP
  • Cilium — eBPF-based networking
  • Weave Net — Simple mesh networking
  • Flannel — Basic overlay network

📍 These are usually pre-installed by your cluster provider.

💾

d) Storage Plugins (CSI)

Container Storage Interface node components for volume management.

Examples:
  • AWS EBS CSI — EBS volume driver
  • GCP PD CSI — Persistent Disk driver
  • Azure Disk CSI — Azure Disk driver
  • Longhorn — Distributed storage

📍 The node plugin runs as DaemonSet; controller is Deployment.

🔒

e) Security Agents

Runtime security monitoring and intrusion detection on every node.

Popular Tools:
  • Falco — Runtime threat detection
  • Sysdig — Security + monitoring
  • Aqua Security — Container security
  • Twistlock — Vulnerability scanning

22.3 DaemonSet YAML Example

Let's create two practical DaemonSet examples: a log collector and a node monitoring agent.

📜 Example 1: Fluentd Log Collector

k8s/fluentd-daemonset.yaml 
# Fluentd DaemonSet — collects logs from every node
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: fluentd
  namespace: kube-system
  labels:
    app: fluentd
    component: logging
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: fluentd
  
  # ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  # │  updateStrategy — how to update DaemonSet pods                  │
  # └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  updateStrategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 1    # ◀── Only 1 pod unavailable during update
  
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: fluentd
    spec:
      # Tolerate control plane taint (run on master nodes too)
      tolerations:
        - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
          effect: NoSchedule
        - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/master
          effect: NoSchedule
      
      containers:
        - name: fluentd
          image: fluent/fluentd-kubernetes-daemonset:v1.16-debian-elasticsearch8
          
          env:
            - name: FLUENT_ELASTICSEARCH_HOST
              value: "elasticsearch.logging.svc.cluster.local"
            - name: FLUENT_ELASTICSEARCH_PORT
              value: "9200"
          
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "200Mi"
              cpu: "100m"
            limits:
              memory: "500Mi"
              cpu: "500m"
          
          # ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
          # │  Mount host paths to collect logs from nodes           │
          # └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
          volumeMounts:
            # Container logs (Docker/containerd)
            - name: varlog
              mountPath: /var/log
              readOnly: true
            
            # Container data directory
            - name: containers
              mountPath: /var/lib/docker/containers
              readOnly: true
            
            # Fluentd config
            - name: config
              mountPath: /fluentd/etc/
      
      # Terminate quickly during node drain
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30
      
      volumes:
        - name: varlog
          hostPath:
            path: /var/log
        - name: containers
          hostPath:
            path: /var/lib/docker/containers
        - name: config
          configMap:
            name: fluentd-config

📜 Example 2: Prometheus Node Exporter

k8s/node-exporter-daemonset.yaml 
# Prometheus Node Exporter DaemonSet
# Exposes hardware and OS metrics from each node
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: node-exporter
  namespace: monitoring
  labels:
    app: node-exporter
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: node-exporter
  
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: node-exporter
      annotations:
        prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
        prometheus.io/port: "9100"
    spec:
      # Run on ALL nodes including control plane
      tolerations:
        - operator: Exists
      
      # Use host network for accurate network metrics
      hostNetwork: true
      hostPID: true
      
      containers:
        - name: node-exporter
          image: prom/node-exporter:v1.7.0
          
          args:
            - "--path.procfs=/host/proc"
            - "--path.sysfs=/host/sys"
            - "--path.rootfs=/host/root"
            - "--collector.filesystem.ignored-mount-points=^/(sys|proc|dev|host|etc)($$|/)"
          
          ports:
            - containerPort: 9100
              name: metrics
              hostPort: 9100    # ◀── Expose on node IP
          
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "30Mi"
              cpu: "50m"
            limits:
              memory: "100Mi"
              cpu: "200m"
          
          # Security: read-only, non-root
          securityContext:
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
            runAsNonRoot: true
            runAsUser: 65534   # nobody user
          
          volumeMounts:
            - name: proc
              mountPath: /host/proc
              readOnly: true
            - name: sys
              mountPath: /host/sys
              readOnly: true
            - name: root
              mountPath: /host/root
              mountPropagation: HostToContainer
              readOnly: true
      
      volumes:
        - name: proc
          hostPath:
            path: /proc
        - name: sys
          hostPath:
            path: /sys
        - name: root
          hostPath:
            path: /
🖥️ DaemonSet Management Commands
# Apply DaemonSet
kubectl apply -f k8s/fluentd-daemonset.yaml

# Check DaemonSet status (should match node count)
kubectl get ds -n kube-system
# NAME      DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   NODE SELECTOR   AGE
# fluentd   3         3         3       3            3                     1h

# List pods — should be one per node
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=fluentd -o wide
# NAME            READY   STATUS    NODE
# fluentd-abc12   1/1     Running   node-1
# fluentd-def34   1/1     Running   node-2
# fluentd-ghi56   1/1     Running   node-3

# View an individual pod's logs
kubectl logs -n kube-system daemonset/fluentd

# Describe DaemonSet for events
kubectl describe ds fluentd -n kube-system

22.4 Node Selection with DaemonSets

By default, DaemonSets run on every node. But you can target specific nodes using nodeSelector, nodeAffinity, or tolerations.

🎯 Node Selection Methods

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Node Selection Methods for DaemonSets                                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  1. nodeSelector — Simple label matching                            │   │
  │  │  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────    │   │
  │  │  nodeSelector:                                                      │   │
  │  │    node-type: worker     ◀── Only nodes with this label            │   │
  │  │                                                                     │   │
  │  │  Cluster:                                                           │   │
  │  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ✗ ┌─────────┐              │   │
  │  │  │ worker  │  │ worker  │  │ worker  │    │ control │              │   │
  │  │  │ ✓ RUNS │  │ ✓ RUNS │  │ ✓ RUNS │    │ ✗ SKIP │              │   │
  │  │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘    └─────────┘              │   │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  2. tolerations — Run on tainted nodes                              │   │
  │  │  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────    │   │
  │  │  tolerations:                                                       │   │
  │  │    - key: gpu                                                       │   │
  │  │      operator: Exists       ◀── Tolerates GPU taint                 │   │
  │  │      effect: NoSchedule                                             │   │
  │  │                                                                     │   │
  │  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐                             │   │
  │  │  │ worker  │  │ worker  │  │ GPU 🎮  │                             │   │
  │  │  │ ✓ RUNS │  │ ✓ RUNS │  │ ✓ RUNS │ ◀── With toleration          │   │
  │  │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  │(tainted)│                             │   │
  │  │                             └─────────┘                             │   │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  3. nodeAffinity — Advanced node selection                          │   │
  │  │  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────    │   │
  │  │  affinity:                                                          │   │
  │  │    nodeAffinity:                                                    │   │
  │  │      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:                │   │
  │  │        nodeSelectorTerms:                                           │   │
  │  │          - matchExpressions:                                        │   │
  │  │              - key: kubernetes.io/os                                │   │
  │  │                operator: In                                         │   │
  │  │                values: [linux]                                      │   │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📜 DaemonSet with Node Selection

k8s/selective-daemonset.yaml 
# DaemonSet that runs only on worker nodes (not control plane)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: app-monitor
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: app-monitor
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: app-monitor
    spec:
      # ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
      # │  nodeSelector — only nodes with these labels                    │
      # └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
      nodeSelector:
        node-type: worker              # Only worker nodes
        kubernetes.io/os: linux        # Only Linux nodes
      
      # ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
      # │  tolerations — needed for special nodes                         │
      # └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
      tolerations:
        - key: nvidia.com/gpu
          operator: Exists
          effect: NoSchedule
        - key: node.kubernetes.io/not-ready
          operator: Exists
          effect: NoSchedule
      
      # ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
      # │  affinity — advanced selection                                  │
      # └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
      affinity:
        nodeAffinity:
          requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
            nodeSelectorTerms:
              - matchExpressions:
                  - key: kubernetes.io/os
                    operator: In
                    values: [linux]
                  - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
                    operator: DoesNotExist
      
      containers:
        - name: monitor
          image: prom/node-exporter:latest

📊 Node Selection Quick Reference

GoalMethodExample
Workers only nodeAffinity DoesNotExist node-role.../control-plane
Linux nodes nodeSelector kubernetes.io/os: linux
GPU nodes nodeSelector + toleration gpu: "true"
Include control plane tolerations control-plane NoSchedule
🖥️ Node Label Commands
# View node labels
kubectl get nodes --show-labels

# Add label to a node
kubectl label nodes node-1 node-type=worker

# Remove label
kubectl label nodes node-1 node-type-

# View taints
kubectl describe node node-1 | grep Taints

22.5 DaemonSet vs Deployment

Both create pods, but they serve different purposes. Here's how to decide.

🔄 DaemonSet vs Deployment — Visual Comparison

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  DEPLOYMENT (replicas: 3)                                                  │
  │  "I need 3 pods somewhere in the cluster"                                 │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐                       │
  │  │ Node 1  │  │ Node 2  │  │ Node 3  │  │ Node 4  │                       │
  │  │ ┌─────┐ │  │ ┌─────┐ │  │         │  │ ┌─────┐ │                       │
  │  │ │Pod 1│ │  │ │Pod 2│ │  │  empty  │  │ │Pod 3│ │                       │
  │  │ └─────┘ │  │ └─────┘ │  │         │  │ └─────┘ │                       │
  │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘                       │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ✅ Scheduler decides where    ✅ Can have multiple pods/node             │
  │  ✅ Scales with HPA            ❌ No guarantee of node coverage           │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  DAEMONSET                                                                 │
  │  "I need exactly 1 pod on EVERY node"                                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐                       │
  │  │ Node 1  │  │ Node 2  │  │ Node 3  │  │ Node 4  │                       │
  │  │ ╔═════╗ │  │ ╔═════╗ │  │ ╔═════╗ │  │ ╔═════╗ │                       │
  │  │ ║ DS  ║ │  │ ║ DS  ║ │  │ ║ DS  ║ │  │ ║ DS  ║ │                       │
  │  │ ╚═════╝ │  │ ╚═════╝ │  │ ╚═════╝ │  │ ╚═════╝ │                       │
  │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘                       │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ✅ Exactly 1 pod per node     ✅ Auto adds when node joins               │
  │  ✅ Auto removes when gone     ❌ Cannot have multiple pods/node          │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📊 Detailed Comparison

FeatureDaemonSetDeployment
Pods per node Exactly 1 (or 0) 0 to many
Replicas field None (auto) replicas: N
Scaling Auto (node count) Manual or HPA
Use case Infrastructure Applications
Example Fluentd, Node Exporter Django, React

💡 Quick Decision Rule

Ask: "Does this pod need access to the node, or just needs to exist?"

  • Needs node access (logs, metrics) → DaemonSet
  • Just needs to run (web app) → Deployment
  • Needs storage + identity → StatefulSet
  • Runs once → Job

Chapter 22b: ReplicaSets, ReplicationControllers, and Beyond

You've learned about Deployments, StatefulSets, DaemonSets, and Jobs. But there's one more workload type you should know — ReplicaSets. While you rarely create them directly, understanding them helps you debug and comprehend what Deployments do behind the scenes.

📦 ReplicaSets 🏛️ Legacy Controllers 🟢 Beginner

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ What ReplicaSets are and how they differ from Deployments
  • ✅ Why you rarely create ReplicaSets directly
  • ✅ ReplicationController — the deprecated predecessor
  • ✅ When you might encounter ReplicaSets

22b.1 ReplicaSets Revisited

A ReplicaSet ensures that a specified number of pod replicas are running at any given time. Sound familiar? That's because Deployments use ReplicaSets internally.

🏗️ Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pods Hierarchy

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  The Workload Object Hierarchy                                             │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │                    DEPLOYMENT                                     │    │
  │  │  name: anihpj                                                     │    │
  │  │  replicas: 3                                                      │    │
  │  │  strategy: RollingUpdate                                          │    │
  │  │                                                                   │    │
  │  │  ✅ Rolling updates    ✅ Rollback    ✅ Revision history          │    │
  │  └──────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                 │                                         │
  │               Deployment creates & manages                                │
  │                                 │                                         │
  │                                 ▼                                         │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │                    REPLICASET                                     │    │
  │  │  name: anihpj-6f7b8c9d0e                                          │    │
  │  │  replicas: 3                                                      │    │
  │  │  selector: app=anihpj                                             │    │
  │  │                                                                   │    │
  │  │  ⚠️ No rolling updates    ⚠️ No rollback    ⚠️ No revision history │    │
  │  └──────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                 │                                         │
  │                ReplicaSet creates & manages                               │
  │                                 │                                         │
  │           ┌─────────────────────┼─────────────────────┐                   │
  │           ▼                     ▼                     ▼                   │
  │  ┌──────────────┐      ┌──────────────┐      ┌──────────────┐            │
  │  │    POD       │      │    POD       │      │    POD       │            │
  │  │ anihpj-xxx-1 │      │ anihpj-xxx-2 │      │ anihpj-xxx-3 │            │
  │  │   🐍         │      │   🐍         │      │   🐍         │            │
  │  └──────────────┘      └──────────────┘      └──────────────┘            │
  │                                                                            │
  │  💡 When you do "kubectl get rs", you see the ReplicaSets                 │
  │     created by your Deployments!                                           │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📊 Deployment vs ReplicaSet

FeatureDeploymentReplicaSet
Rolling Updates ✅ Yes — built-in ❌ No — must delete & recreate
Rollback ✅ Yes — kubectl rollout undo ❌ No — manual recreation
Revision History ✅ Yes — tracks versions ❌ No — single version
Selectors matchLabels + matchExpressions matchLabels + matchExpressions
Create directly? ✅ Yes — recommended ⚠️ Rarely — use Deployment instead

⚠️ Why You Rarely Create ReplicaSets Directly

Deployments do everything ReplicaSets do, plus more:

  • Rolling updates with zero downtime
  • Rollback to any previous revision
  • Pause/resume deployments
  • Declarative updates

Bottom line: Use Deployment for 99% of stateless workloads. Only use bare ReplicaSet if you have a specific reason (e.g., custom controller).

📜 Standalone ReplicaSet YAML (For Understanding Only)

k8s/replicaset-standalone.yaml (⚠️ use Deployment instead) 
# Standalone ReplicaSet — rarely used directly
# ⚠️ Prefer Deployment for real workloads
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: ReplicaSet   # ◀── Not Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-rs
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
    # Advanced: matchExpressions
    matchExpressions:
      - key: environment
        operator: In
        values: [production, staging]
  
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        environment: production
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: yourregistry/anihpj:v1.0.0
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000

# ⚠️ If you change the image:
#    - ReplicaSet: All pods keep running old image!
#                  Must delete RS or pods manually!
#    - Deployment: Rolling update automatically! ✅
🖥️ View ReplicaSets Created by Deployments
# See all ReplicaSets
kubectl get rs -n anihpj
# NAME                  DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   AGE
# anihpj-6f7b8c9d0e     3         3         3       2h    ◀── Current
# anihpj-5a4b3c2d1f     0         0         0       1d    ◀── Old (rollback)
# anihpj-9x8y7z6w5v     0         0         0       2d    ◀── Older

# View ReplicaSet details (owned by Deployment)
kubectl describe rs anihpj-6f7b8c9d0e -n anihpj
# Controlled By:  Deployment/anihpj  ◀── Shows parent

# Orphaned pods — when you delete a RS without deleting pods
kubectl delete rs anihpj-rs --cascade=orphan

22b.2 ReplicationController (Legacy)

The ReplicationController is the original method for ensuring a specified number of pods run. It's now deprecated — use Deployments or ReplicaSets instead.

🚨 Deprecated — Don't Use in New Projects

ReplicationController is superseded by ReplicaSet. The key differences:

AspectReplicationControllerReplicaSet
API Version v1 (core) apps/v1
Selectors Equality only: app=anihpj Set-based: app in (anihpj, test)
Status ⚠️ Deprecated ✅ Current (but use Deployment)

📜 Why You Might See ReplicationController

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Evolution of Pod Replication in Kubernetes                                │
  │                                                                            │
  │  2014 │  2015  │  2016  │  2017  │  2018  │  2019  │  Now                 │
  │       │        │        │        │        │        │                       │
  │  ┌────────────────┐                                                       │
  │  │ Replication    │                                                       │
  │  │ Controller     │────────────────▶ ⚠️ DEPRECATED                        │
  │  │ (v1)           │                                                       │
  │  └────────────────┘                                                       │
  │                                                                            │
  │        ┌────────────────┐                                                 │
  │        │  ReplicaSet    │────────────▶ ✅ Still works                     │
  │        │  (apps/v1)     │              (but don't use directly)           │
  │        └────────────────┘                                                 │
  │                                                                            │
  │              ┌─────────────────────────┐                                  │
  │              │      Deployment         │──────▶ ✅ USE THIS!              │
  │              │      (apps/v1)          │                                  │
  │              │  [manages ReplicaSets]  │                                  │
  │              └─────────────────────────┘                                  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  WHERE YOU MIGHT SEE ReplicationController:                                │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────                        │
  │  • Old tutorials (pre-2016)                                               │
  │  • Legacy systems not yet migrated                                        │
  │  • Old exam questions (CKA/CKAD)                                          │
  │  • kubectl run --generator=run/v1 (removed in 1.18)                       │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ACTION: If you see RC in production, migrate to Deployment! 🔄            │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📜

ReplicationController YAML

(For reference only — don't use!)

apiVersion: v1  # core API
kind: ReplicationController
metadata:
  name: anihpj-rc
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    app: anihpj  # equality only!
  template:
    # pod spec...
✅

Deployment YAML

(What you should use!)

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    # pod spec...

💡 Workload Controller Summary

Workload TypeUse ForCreate Directly?
Deployment Stateless apps (web, API) ✅ Yes
StatefulSet Databases, stateful apps ✅ Yes
DaemonSet One pod per node ✅ Yes
Job / CronJob Batch / scheduled tasks ✅ Yes
ReplicaSet Pod replication ⚠️ Rarely (use Deployment)
ReplicationController Legacy replication ❌ Never (deprecated)

📋 Part 7 Quick Reference

A comprehensive cheat sheet for StatefulSets, DaemonSets, and ReplicaSets.

🗺️ Workload Controller Decision Tree

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Which Workload Controller Should I Use?                                   │
  │                                                                            │
  │                    ┌──────────────────────────┐                            │
  │                    │   What kind of workload? │                            │
  │                    └─────────────┬────────────┘                            │
  │                                  │                                         │
  │       ┌──────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┐            │
  │       │                          │                           │            │
  │       ▼                          ▼                           ▼            │
  │  ┌─────────────┐         ┌──────────────┐          ┌────────────────┐    │
  │  │ Run once?   │         │ Run forever? │          │ Run on every   │    │
  │  │ Batch task? │         │ Long-running │          │ node?          │    │
  │  │             │         │ service?     │          │                │    │
  │  └──────┬──────┘         └──────┬───────┘          └───────┬────────┘    │
  │         │                       │                          │             │
  │         ▼                       │                          ▼             │
  │  ┌─────────────┐                │               ┌─────────────────────┐  │
  │  │    JOB      │                │               │     DAEMONSET       │  │
  │  │  or CRONJOB │                │               │  One per node       │  │
  │  └─────────────┘                │               │  (logs, monitoring) │  │
  │                                 │               └─────────────────────┘  │
  │                    ┌────────────┴────────────┐                           │
  │                    │ Needs stable identity,  │                           │
  │                    │ persistent storage,     │                           │
  │                    │ or ordered operations?  │                           │
  │                    └────────────┬────────────┘                           │
  │                                 │                                         │
  │              ┌──────────────────┴──────────────────┐                     │
  │              │                                     │                     │
  │             YES                                    NO                    │
  │              │                                     │                     │
  │              ▼                                     ▼                     │
  │    ┌─────────────────────┐             ┌─────────────────────┐          │
  │    │    STATEFULSET      │             │     DEPLOYMENT      │          │
  │    │  Databases, Kafka,  │             │  Web apps, APIs,    │          │
  │    │  Redis Cluster      │             │  workers, etc.      │          │
  │    └─────────────────────┘             └─────────────────────┘          │
  │                                                                          │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📊 Complete Workload Comparison Matrix

Feature Deployment StatefulSet DaemonSet ReplicaSet
Pod Naming Random hash Ordered (0,1,2) Random hash Random hash
Scaling Set replicas Ordered scale Auto (=nodes) Set replicas
Storage Shared/none Per-pod PVC Host path Shared/none
Network Via Service Stable DNS per pod Host network OK Via Service
Rolling Update ✅ Built-in ✅ Reverse order ✅ Built-in ❌ None
Rollback ✅ Built-in ✅ partition ❌ Manual ❌ Manual
Use Case Web, API DB, stateful Logs, metrics Rarely direct
⚡
StatefulSet Commands
# List StatefulSets
kubectl get sts -n anihpj

# Scale StatefulSet
kubectl scale sts postgres --replicas=3

# View pod status (note ordering)
kubectl get pods -l app=postgres

# Partition update (canary)
kubectl patch sts postgres -p \
  '{"spec":{"updateStrategy":{"rollingUpdate":{"partition":2}}}}'

# Check PVCs
kubectl get pvc -l app=postgres
🔧
DaemonSet Commands
# List DaemonSets
kubectl get ds --all-namespaces

# Check pod distribution
kubectl get pods -l app=fluentd -o wide

# View DaemonSet details
kubectl describe ds fluentd

# Update DaemonSet image
kubectl set image ds/fluentd \
  fluentd=fluent/fluentd:v1.17

# Check nodes vs pods
kubectl get nodes | wc -l
kubectl get pods -l app=fluentd | wc -l
📦
ReplicaSet Commands
# List ReplicaSets
kubectl get rs -n anihpj

# View owner (Deployment)
kubectl describe rs anihpj-abc123 \
  | grep "Controlled By"

# List old ReplicaSets (0 replicas)
kubectl get rs --sort-by='.metadata.creationTimestamp'

# Delete orphaned RS
kubectl delete rs old-rs --cascade=orphan

# Check revision history
kubectl rollout history deploy/anihpj

🔧 Part 7 Troubleshooting Guide

ProblemCheckSolution
StatefulSet pods stuck Pending kubectl describe pod postgres-0 Check PVC binding, StorageClass exists, capacity available
StatefulSet pod won't start kubectl logs postgres-0 Check secrets mounted, env vars correct
DaemonSet missing on node kubectl describe node NODE Check taints — add tolerations to DS
DaemonSet has multiple pods on node kubectl get pods -l app=ds -o wide Shouldn't happen — check node affinity or selector
ReplicaSet not updating kubectl get rs Use Deployment instead — RS doesn't have rolling updates
PVC persists after StatefulSet deleted kubectl get pvc Expected! Delete PVCs manually or use persistentVolumeClaimRetentionPolicy
DNS not working for StatefulSet nslookup postgres-0.postgres-svc Ensure headless service exists (clusterIP: None)

💡 Part 7 Best Practices Summary

✅ DO
  • Use Deployment for stateless apps
  • Use StatefulSet for databases
  • Use DaemonSet for node-level tools
  • Test backup restores regularly
  • Set resource limits on DaemonSets
  • Use headless services with StatefulSets
  • Use partition for canary updates
❌ DON'T
  • Create ReplicaSets directly (use Deployment)
  • Use ReplicationController (deprecated)
  • Run databases without persistent storage
  • Forget tolerations for DaemonSets on masters
  • Assume PVCs are deleted with StatefulSet
  • Skip health checks on StatefulSets
  • Use StatefulSet for stateless apps

🔐 Part 8: Security Chapters 23-25

Chapter 23: RBAC — Role-Based Access Control

In a multi-tenant Kubernetes cluster, who can do what matters. RBAC is the gatekeeper — it controls which users or services can access which resources. Without RBAC, anyone with cluster access could delete production databases. This chapter shows you how to implement the principle of least privilege in Kubernetes.

🔐 Authentication 🛡️ Authorization 👤 Roles 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why security matters in Kubernetes
