  • Use privileged: true
  • Use hostNetwork/PID/IPC
  • Mount hostPath volumes
  • Leave namespaces without PSS

🔒 Security Layers for anihpj

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Defense in Depth — Multiple Security Layers                              │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  LAYER 1: RBAC                                                       │  │
  │  │  Who can deploy to anihpj namespace?                                │  │
  │  │  ✅ Only dev-team can create pods                                   │  │
  │  └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                             │                                             │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  LAYER 2: Pod Security Standards                                    │  │
  │  │  What kind of pods can run?                                         │  │
  │  │  ✅ namespace: enforce=baseline, warn=restricted                    │  │
  │  └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                             │                                             │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  LAYER 3: SecurityContext                                           │  │
  │  │  How secure are pod configurations?                                 │  │
  │  │  ✅ runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, drop ALL                 │  │
  │  └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                             │                                             │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  LAYER 4: Network Policies                                          │  │
  │  │  What traffic is allowed?                                           │  │
  │  │  ✅ Default deny + explicit allow frontend→backend→database        │  │
  │  └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                             │                                             │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  LAYER 5: Secrets Management (Chapter 25)                           │  │
  │  │  How are credentials handled?                                       │  │
  │  │  ✅ External secrets, encrypted at rest, RBAC on secrets           │  │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🏷️ PSS Namespace Labels Quick Reference

🛡️
Development Namespace
pod-security.kubernetes.io/
  enforce: baseline
  warn: restricted
  audit: restricted

Flexible for devs, warns about production requirements

🔒
Production Namespace
pod-security.kubernetes.io/
  enforce: restricted
  enforce-version: v1.28

Maximum security, blocks all violations

⚙️
System Namespace
pod-security.kubernetes.io/
  enforce: privileged

For system components that need host access

🌐 Network Policy Patterns

PatternUse CaseKey Configuration
Default Deny All Start with zero trust podSelector: {} + empty ingress/egress
Allow Same Namespace Pods in NS can talk from: [podSelector: {}]
Allow Specific Pods Frontend→Backend only from: [{podSelector: {app: frontend}}]
Allow Cross-Namespace From monitoring NS from: [{namespaceSelector: {name: monitoring}}]
Allow External CIDR External API access to: [{ipBlock: {cidr: 0.0.0.0/0}}]
Allow DNS (Critical!) Name resolution egress: [{port: 53, protocol: UDP}] to kube-dns
🖥️ Pod Security Cheat Sheet
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  PSS NAMESPACE LABELS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Apply baseline enforcement
kubectl label namespace anihpj pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=baseline

# Check if current pods would pass restricted
kubectl label --dry-run=server namespace anihpj \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  SECURITYCONTEXT VALIDATION
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Check who's running as root
kubectl get pods -A -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.namespace}/{.metadata.name}: runAsUser={.spec.containers[0].securityContext.runAsUser}{"\n"}{end}'

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  NETWORK POLICY TESTING
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# List all network policies
kubectl get networkpolicies -A

# Test connectivity from a debug pod
kubectl run nettest --image=busybox --rm -it -- wget -qO- --timeout=2 http://backend:8000

# Check which pods are affected by a policy
kubectl describe networkpolicy backend-allow-frontend -n anihpj

📋 Chapter 24 Summary: Pod Security

  • ✅ SecurityContext — Pod/container security settings (runAsNonRoot, capabilities)
  • ✅ Pod Security Standards — 3 levels: Privileged, Baseline, Restricted
  • ✅ PSS Modes — enforce (block), warn (notify), audit (log)
  • ✅ Network Policies — Firewall rules for pod traffic
  • ✅ Default Deny — Start blocked, allow explicitly
  • ✅ Defense in Depth — Multiple layers of security

Chapter 25: Secrets Management

PART 8 INTERMEDIATE 🔐 SECURITY

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • ✅ Create and use Kubernetes Secrets safely
  • ✅ Understand Secret types and encoding
  • ✅ Configure encryption at rest for Secrets
  • ✅ Integrate with external secret managers (Vault, AWS, Azure)
  • ✅ Implement secret rotation strategies

25.1 Kubernetes Secrets — Basics

Secrets store sensitive data like passwords, tokens, and keys. They're base64-encoded (NOT encrypted!) by default.

⚠️ Secret Misconceptions

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  COMMON MISCONCEPTION: "Secrets are encrypted"                            │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ❌ WRONG: Secrets are base64-ENCODED, not encrypted!                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  apiVersion: v1                                                      │  │
  │  │  kind: Secret                                                        │  │
  │  │  data:                                                               │  │
  │  │    password: cGFzc3dvcmQxMjM=  ◀── This is just base64!           │  │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  $ echo "cGFzc3dvcmQxMjM=" | base64 -d                                    │
  │  password123    ◀── Anyone can decode this!                               │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════    │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ✅ WHAT SECRETS PROVIDE:                                                 │
  │  • Separate sensitive data from pod specs                                 │
  │  • RBAC-controlled access (who can read secrets)                          │
  │  • Encryption at rest (optional, must configure)                          │
  │  • Mounted as files or env vars (not in image)                            │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
k8s/secrets/secret-basic.yaml 
# Secret with base64-encoded values (encoded, NOT encrypted!)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-credentials
  namespace: anihpj
type: Opaque          # Generic secret (most common)
data:
  # base64-encoded values
  username: YW5paHBqX3VzZXI=      # echo -n "anihpj_user" | base64
  password: c3VwZXJTZWNyZXQxMjM=  # echo -n "superSecret123" | base64

---
# Alternative: stringData for plain text (K8s encodes for you)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-credentials-v2
  namespace: anihpj
type: Opaque
stringData:           # ◀── Plain text, K8s converts to base64
  username: anihpj_user
  password: superSecret123
  
# Both methods produce identical secrets!
# stringData is easier but shows password in YAML (gitignore!)

---
# Connection string as a single secret value
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: database-url
  namespace: anihpj
type: Opaque
stringData:
  DATABASE_URL: postgresql://anihpj_user:superSecret123@db:5432/anihpj

📊 Secret Types

TypeUse CaseRequired Keys
Opaque Generic (passwords, API keys) Any
kubernetes.io/tls TLS certificates tls.crt, tls.key
kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson Docker registry auth .dockerconfigjson
kubernetes.io/basic-auth Basic authentication username, password
kubernetes.io/ssh-auth SSH private keys ssh-privatekey

25.2 Using Secrets in Pods

Secrets can be consumed as environment variables or mounted files.

k8s/secrets/pod-with-secrets.yaml 
# Method 1: Environment Variables (convenient but visible in logs)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: backend-env
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: anihpj/backend:v1
      env:
        # Single secret key
        - name: DB_USERNAME
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: db-credentials
              key: username
        
        - name: DB_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: db-credentials
              key: password
              optional: false    # Pod fails if secret missing
      
      # All keys from secret as env vars
      envFrom:
        - secretRef:
            name: db-credentials
            # Creates DB_USERNAME and DB_PASSWORD env vars

---
# Method 2: Volume Mount (more secure, file-based)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: backend-mount
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: anihpj/backend:v1
      volumeMounts:
        - name: db-secrets
          mountPath: /etc/secrets/db
          readOnly: true        # ◀── Secrets should be read-only!
      
      # Application reads: /etc/secrets/db/username
      #                    /etc/secrets/db/password
  
  volumes:
    - name: db-secrets
      secret:
        secretName: db-credentials
        defaultMode: 0400       # ◀── Restrictive permissions
        items:                  # Optional: select specific keys
          - key: username
            path: db_username   # Mounts as /etc/secrets/db/db_username
          - key: password
            path: db_password

# ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
# │  BEST PRACTICE: Volume mounts over env vars                       │
# │  ✅ Secrets auto-update when secret changes                      │
# │  ✅ Not visible in `kubectl describe pod`                        │
# │  ✅ Not in container environment (env vars can leak to logs)     │
# └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📊 Env Vars vs Volume Mounts

⚠️
Environment Variables
  • Requires pod restart to update
  • Visible in kubectl describe
  • May leak to logs, crash dumps
  • Simple to use
✅
Volume Mounts
  • Auto-updates (kubelet syncs)
  • Not in pod describe output
  • File permissions control
  • App must read from file

25.3 Securing Secrets

Native K8s Secrets need additional hardening. Here's the complete security checklist.

🔐 Secret Security Maturity Levels

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Secrets Security Maturity Model                                          │
  │                                                                            │
  │  LEVEL 1: Basic K8s Secrets (DEFAULT — INSECURE)                          │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────                        │
  │  ❌ base64-encoded (readable)                                             │
  │  ❌ Stored unencrypted in etcd                                            │
  │  ❌ Secrets in Git (if not careful)                                       │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  + RBAC                                                              │  │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  LEVEL 2: RBAC-Protected Secrets                                          │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────                                    │
  │  ✅ Only specific ServiceAccounts can read secrets                       │
  │  ❌ Still unencrypted in etcd                                            │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  + Encryption at Rest                                                │  │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  LEVEL 3: Encrypted at Rest                                               │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────                                        │
  │  ✅ RBAC + etcd encryption enabled                                       │
  │  ⚠️ Still managed in-cluster                                             │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  + External Secret Manager                                           │  │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  LEVEL 4: External Secret Management (RECOMMENDED)                        │
  │  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────                     │
  │  ✅ Secrets in HashiCorp Vault / AWS Secrets Manager / Azure Key Vault   │
  │  ✅ Automatic rotation                                                    │
  │  ✅ Audit logging                                                         │
  │  ✅ Not stored in K8s (injected at runtime)                               │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

25.3a RBAC for Secrets

Restrict who can read secrets. Most pods should NOT have access to all secrets.

k8s/secrets/rbac-secrets.yaml 
# Role: Only allow reading specific secret
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: backend-secrets-reader
  namespace: anihpj
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["secrets"]
    resourceNames: ["db-credentials"]  # ◀── Only this secret!
    verbs: ["get"]

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: backend-read-db-secret
  namespace: anihpj
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: backend-sa
    namespace: anihpj
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: backend-secrets-reader
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

# Result: backend pods can only read db-credentials
# Cannot read other secrets in the namespace

25.3b Encryption at Rest

Configure the API server to encrypt Secrets in etcd.

/etc/kubernetes/encryption-config.yaml 
# Encryption configuration for kube-apiserver
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: EncryptionConfiguration
resources:
  - resources:
      - secrets      # ◀── Encrypt Secrets
    providers:
      # Most secure: Use external KMS
      - kms:
          name: aws-kms
          endpoint: unix:///var/run/kmsplugin/socket.sock
      
      # Alternative: Local AES encryption
      - aescbc:
          keys:
            - name: key1
              secret: 
      
      # Fallback: identity means no encryption (for reading old secrets)
      - identity: {}

# ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
# │  API SERVER FLAG:                                                   │
# │  --encryption-provider-config=/etc/kubernetes/encryption-config.yaml│
# │                                                                     │
# │  After enabling, re-encrypt existing secrets:                      │
# │  kubectl get secrets -A -o json | kubectl replace -f -             │
# └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🖥️ Verify Encryption
# Check if secrets are encrypted (on control plane node)
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl get /registry/secrets/anihpj/db-credentials \
  --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

# If encrypted: Shows binary/encrypted data
# If NOT encrypted: Shows readable base64 YAML

25.3c Sealed Secrets (GitOps-Safe)

Sealed Secrets allow encrypted secrets in Git. Only the cluster can decrypt them.

🔐 Sealed Secrets Workflow

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Sealed Secrets (Bitnami)                                                  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐             │
  │  │ Developer   │     │   kubeseal    │     │    Git Repo   │             │
  │  │ creates:    │────▶│  (CLI tool)   │────▶│               │             │
  │  │ Secret.yaml │     │  encrypts     │     │ SealedSecret  │             │
  │  │ (plain)     │     │  with cluster │     │ .yaml         │             │
  │  └─────────────┘     │  public key   │     │ (encrypted)   │             │
  │                      └───────────────┘     └───────┬───────┘             │
  │                                                     │                     │
  │                                                     │ GitOps sync        │
  │                                                     ▼                     │
  │  ┌─────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐             │
  │  │   Pod uses  │◀────│    Secret     │◀────│ SealedSecret  │             │
  │  │   Secret    │     │  (decrypted)  │     │ Controller    │             │
  │  │             │     │               │     │ (in-cluster)  │             │
  │  └─────────────┘     └───────────────┘     └───────────────┘             │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ✅ Secrets safe in Git (only cluster can decrypt)                        │
  │  ✅ GitOps compatible (ArgoCD, Flux)                                       │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🖥️ Sealed Secrets Setup
# Install Sealed Secrets controller
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/bitnami-labs/sealed-secrets/releases/download/v0.24.0/controller.yaml

# Install kubeseal CLI
brew install kubeseal  # macOS
# or download from GitHub releases

# Seal a secret (encrypt)
kubectl create secret generic db-credentials \
  --from-literal=password=secret123 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | \
  kubeseal --format=yaml > sealed-secret.yaml

# Apply sealed secret (controller decrypts → creates Secret)
kubectl apply -f sealed-secret.yaml

25.3d External Secret Managers

Use dedicated secret management tools for production workloads.

🔑 External Secret Options

🔐
HashiCorp Vault

Industry standard, self-hosted or cloud

Dynamic secrets, leases, audit

☁️
AWS Secrets Manager

Native AWS integration

Auto-rotation, IAM-based

🔷
Azure Key Vault

Native Azure integration

HSM-backed, RBAC

🔴
GCP Secret Manager

Native GCP integration

Versioning, IAM

k8s/secrets/external-secret.yaml 
# External Secrets Operator example (syncs from Vault/AWS/Azure)
# Install: helm install external-secrets external-secrets/external-secrets

apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ExternalSecret
metadata:
  name: db-credentials
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  refreshInterval: 1h          # ◀── Sync every hour
  
  secretStoreRef:
    name: vault-backend        # Reference to SecretStore
    kind: SecretStore
  
  target:
    name: db-credentials       # K8s Secret name to create
    creationPolicy: Owner
  
  data:
    - secretKey: username
      remoteRef:
        key: secret/anihpj/database
        property: username
    
    - secretKey: password
      remoteRef:
        key: secret/anihpj/database
        property: password

---
# SecretStore: How to connect to Vault
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: SecretStore
metadata:
  name: vault-backend
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  provider:
    vault:
      server: "https://vault.example.com"
      path: "secret"
      auth:
        kubernetes:
          mountPath: "kubernetes"
          role: "anihpj-role"
          serviceAccountRef:
            name: external-secrets-sa

25.3e Secret Best Practices Summary

✅
DO
  • Use volume mounts over env vars
  • Enable encryption at rest
  • RBAC restrict secret access
  • Use external secret managers
  • Rotate secrets regularly
  • Use Sealed Secrets for GitOps
❌
DON'T
  • Commit plain secrets to Git
  • Log secrets in applications
  • Give pods access to all secrets
  • Trust base64 as "encryption"
  • Use default ServiceAccount for apps
  • Share secrets across namespaces

25.4 Secret Rotation

Secrets should be rotated periodically to limit exposure from potential leaks.

🔄 Secret Rotation Strategies

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Secret Rotation Methods                                                   │
  │                                                                            │
  │  1. MANUAL ROTATION                                                        │
  │  ───────────────────                                                       │
  │  Human: kubectl → update Secret → restart pods                            │
  │  ⚠️ Prone to errors, not scalable                                         │
  │                                                                            │
  │  2. AUTOMATED + POD RESTART                                                │
  │  ────────────────────────────                                              │
  │  External tool: rotates secret → triggers pod rollout                     │
  │  ⚠️ Brief downtime during rollout                                         │
  │                                                                            │
  │  3. VOLUME MOUNT + HOT RELOAD                                              │
  │  ──────────────────────────────                                            │
  │  Secret updated → kubelet syncs file → app detects change                 │
  │  ✅ Zero downtime (if app supports)                                        │
  │                                                                            │
  │  4. DYNAMIC SECRETS (VAULT)                                                │
  │  ────────────────────────────                                              │
  │  Vault: generates short-lived credentials on-demand                       │
  │  ✅ Secrets auto-expire, never stored permanently                         │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
scripts/rotate-secret.sh 
#!/bin/bash
# Secret rotation script for anihpj database credentials

NAMESPACE="anihpj"
SECRET_NAME="db-credentials"

# Generate new password
NEW_PASSWORD=$(openssl rand -base64 24)

# Update secret
kubectl create secret generic $SECRET_NAME \
  --from-literal=username=anihpj_user \
  --from-literal=password="$NEW_PASSWORD" \
  --namespace=$NAMESPACE \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

# Trigger rolling restart (if using env vars)
kubectl rollout restart deployment/backend -n $NAMESPACE

# For volume mounts: kubelet auto-syncs, app should detect
echo "Secret rotated. New password set."

# Also update the database password!
# psql -h db -U postgres -c "ALTER USER anihpj_user PASSWORD '$NEW_PASSWORD';"

25.5 Chapter Summary

🔑 Kubernetes Secret Types

📦
Opaque (default)
type: Opaque
data:
  key: base64value

Generic key-value pairs, most common

🐳
Docker Registry
type: docker-registry
--docker-server
--docker-username

Pull images from private registries

🔐
TLS Certificate
type: tls
tls.crt: <cert>
tls.key: <key>

TLS/SSL for Ingress and apps

🎯 Secrets Security Maturity Levels

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    SECRETS SECURITY MATURITY MODEL                      │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  ┌─ LEVEL 1 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[ 🔴 ]┐
  │  CONFIG FILES / HARDCODED                                               │
  │  • Secrets in code, .env files committed to Git                        │
  │  • ❌ Never do this! Exposed in version history forever                │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                    ⬇️
  ┌─ LEVEL 2 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[ 🟠 ]┐
  │  KUBERNETES SECRETS (Base64)                                            │
  │  • kubectl create secret, base64-encoded                               │
  │  • ⚠️ Better, but not encrypted in etcd by default                    │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                    ⬇️
  ┌─ LEVEL 3 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[ 🟡 ]┐
  │  ENCRYPTED AT REST + RBAC                                               │
  │  • EncryptionConfiguration on API server                               │
  │  • ✅ Secrets encrypted in etcd, RBAC controls access                  │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                    ⬇️
  ┌─ LEVEL 4 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[ 🟢 ]┐
  │  SEALED SECRETS / GITOPS-SAFE                                           │
  │  • Bitnami Sealed Secrets, Mozilla SOPS                                │
  │  • ✅ Encrypted secrets safe to commit to Git                          │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                    ⬇️
  ┌─ LEVEL 5 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[ 🏆 ]┐
  │  EXTERNAL SECRETS MANAGER                                               │
  │  • HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault               │
  │  • ✅ Centralized, audited, dynamic secrets, auto-rotation            │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🔄 External Secrets Managers Comparison

ManagerBest ForK8s IntegrationKey Feature
HashiCorp Vault Multi-cloud, on-prem CSI Driver, Agent Sidecar Dynamic secrets, leases
AWS Secrets Manager AWS-native workloads External Secrets Operator RDS integration, rotation
Azure Key Vault Azure/AKS workloads CSI Driver (native) Managed identity auth
GCP Secret Manager GKE workloads External Secrets Operator IAM-based access
Sealed Secrets GitOps, simple setup Native controller Git-safe encryption

📋 Chapter 25 Summary: Secrets Management

  • ✅ K8s Secrets — Base64-encoded, NOT encrypted by default
  • ✅ Volume mounts — Preferred over environment variables
  • ✅ RBAC — Restrict secret access per ServiceAccount
  • ✅ Encryption at rest — Configure API server for etcd encryption
  • ✅ Sealed Secrets — Encrypt secrets for Git (GitOps-safe)
  • ✅ External managers — Vault, AWS, Azure for production
  • ✅ Rotation — Regular rotation with zero-downtime strategies
🖥️ Quick Secret Commands Cheat Sheet
# Create secret from literal
kubectl create secret generic my-secret \
  --from-literal=key1=value1 \
  --from-literal=key2=value2 -n anihpj

# Create secret from file
kubectl create secret generic tls-secret \
  --from-file=tls.crt \
  --from-file=tls.key -n anihpj

# Create docker registry secret
kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred \
  --docker-server=docker.io \
  --docker-username=user \
  --docker-password=pass -n anihpj

# View secret (base64 decoded)
kubectl get secret db-credentials -n anihpj -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d

# Edit secret
kubectl edit secret db-credentials -n anihpj

# Delete secret
kubectl delete secret db-credentials -n anihpj

👁️ Part 9: Observability Chapters 26-28

📊
📝
🔍
📈

PART 9 Observability — Seeing What's Happening

Master the three pillars of observability: Logs, Metrics, and Traces. Learn to monitor, debug, and troubleshoot your Kubernetes applications like a pro.

📝 Logging 📊 Monitoring 🔍 Debugging ⏱️ ~3 hours

🎯 The Three Pillars of Observability

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                     THE THREE PILLARS OF OBSERVABILITY                           │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

       ┌──────────────────┐   ┌──────────────────┐   ┌──────────────────┐
       │     📝 LOGS       │   │   📊 METRICS      │   │   🔗 TRACES       │
       │                  │   │                  │   │                  │
       │  "What happened" │   │ "How many/much"  │   │ "Request journey"│
       │                  │   │                  │   │                  │
       │  ┌────────────┐  │   │  ┌────────────┐  │   │  A ──▶ B ──▶ C  │
       │  │ ERROR: DB   │  │   │  │ CPU: 45%   │  │   │  │     │     │  │
       │  │ connection  │  │   │  │ Req/s: 200 │  │   │  10ms  5ms  3ms │
       │  │ failed at   │  │   │  │ Error: 2%  │  │   │                  │
       │  │ 14:32:05    │  │   │  │ Latency:50ms│ │   │  Spans show time │
       │  └────────────┘  │   │  └────────────┘  │   │  per service     │
       │                  │   │                  │   │                  │
       │  ✅ Debugging     │   │  ✅ Alerting      │   │  ✅ Bottlenecks   │
       │  ✅ Audit trail   │   │  ✅ Dashboards    │   │  ✅ Dependencies  │
       │  ✅ Compliance    │   │  ✅ Trends        │   │  ✅ Performance   │
       └──────────────────┘   └──────────────────┘   └──────────────────┘
               ▲                      ▲                      ▲
               │                      │                      │
               └──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┘
                                      │
                             ┌────────┴────────┐
                             │  OBSERVABILITY   │
                             │  "Understanding  │
                             │   system state   │
                             │   from outputs"  │
                             └─────────────────┘

       Chapter 26: 📝 Logging        Chapter 27: 📊 Monitoring        Chapter 28: 🔍 Debug
       └─ kubectl logs              └─ Prometheus + Grafana           └─ Troubleshooting
       └─ EFK/Loki stacks           └─ Metrics Server                 └─ kubectl debug
       └─ Django logging            └─ Alerting                       └─ Pod states

Chapter 26: Logging

PART 9 INTERMEDIATE 👁️ OBSERVABILITY

Logs are your first line of defense when something goes wrong. In Kubernetes, understanding how to access, aggregate, and analyze logs is essential for maintaining healthy applications. This chapter covers everything from basic kubectl logs to centralized logging stacks.

26.1 Why Logging Matters

📝 The Difference Logs Make

Without logs, debugging is guesswork. With logs, you have evidence.

🔍 Debugging: With vs Without Logs

❌
Without Proper Logging
  • User: "The app is down"
  • You: shrug "Let me restart it"
  • Hours of guessing what happened
  • Same issue returns next week
  • No evidence for post-mortems
✅
With Proper Logging
  • User: "The app is down"
  • You: "DB password changed at 3:42 PM"
  • Root cause found in minutes
  • Fix deployed, documented
  • Prevent recurrence

🏛️ The Three Pillars of Observability

📝
LOGS

What: Discrete events with timestamps

When: Debug specific issues

Example: "ERROR: DB connection failed at 14:32:05"

📊
METRICS

What: Numeric measurements over time

When: Dashboards, alerting, trends

Example: "CPU: 45%, Requests/sec: 200"

🔗
TRACES

What: Request path across services

When: Distributed system debugging

Example: "Request took 10ms→API, 50ms→DB"

💡 Focus for This Chapter

This chapter focuses on Logs. Chapter 27 covers Metrics (Prometheus/Grafana). Traces (Jaeger, OpenTelemetry) are typically added once you have microservices — we'll touch on them in advanced patterns.

26.2 kubectl Logs — The Basics

The kubectl logs command is your primary tool for viewing container output in Kubernetes. Every container writes to stdout/stderr, and Kubernetes captures this automatically.

📦 How Kubernetes Captures Logs

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    KUBERNETES LOG FLOW                               │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

    Your App (anihpj)          Container Runtime           Node Filesystem
   ┌─────────────────┐       ┌─────────────────┐       ┌─────────────────┐
   │   Django app    │       │   containerd    │       │ /var/log/pods/  │
   │                 │       │                 │       │                 │
   │  print("...")   │──────▶│  Captures       │──────▶│  pod-xyz.log    │
   │  logger.info()  │ stdout│  stdout/stderr  │ writes│                 │
   │                 │ stderr│                 │       │  (rotated)      │
   └─────────────────┘       └─────────────────┘       └────────┬────────┘
                                                                │
                                                                │ kubectl logs
                                                                ▼
                                                       ┌─────────────────┐
                                                       │   Your Terminal │
                                                       │                 │
                                                       │   $ kubectl logs│
                                                       │   anihpj-xyz    │
                                                       └─────────────────┘

View Current Logs

🖥️ Basic Log Commands
# View logs from a specific pod
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-xyz

# View logs from a deployment (picks one pod)
kubectl logs deployment/anihpj-deployment

# View logs from all pods with a label
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj

# Shorthand: k logs (if alias set)
k logs anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-xyz
📤 Example Output
[2024-01-15 14:32:05] INFO: Starting Gunicorn 21.2.0
[2024-01-15 14:32:05] INFO: Listening at: http://0.0.0.0:8000
[2024-01-15 14:32:06] INFO: Booting worker with pid: 1
[2024-01-15 14:32:10] INFO: GET /jobs/ 200 OK 45ms
[2024-01-15 14:32:12] INFO: GET /api/jobs/ 200 OK 23ms

Follow Live Logs

🖥️ Stream Logs in Real-Time
# Follow logs (like tail -f) — streams new logs as they arrive
kubectl logs -f anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-xyz

# Follow logs from deployment
kubectl logs -f deployment/anihpj-deployment

# Follow logs from all pods matching label
kubectl logs -f -l app=anihpj --all-containers

# Press Ctrl+C to stop following
💡 Pro Tip

Combine -f (follow) with --tail=50 to see the last 50 lines, then stream new ones. Perfect for watching deployments in action.

View Previous Container Logs

🔴 Critical for Debugging Crashes!

When a container crashes and restarts, the logs from the crashed instance are gone — unless you use --previous.

🖥️ Retrieve Logs from Crashed Container
# View logs from the PREVIOUS container instance
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-xyz --previous

# Short form
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-xyz -p

# This is ESSENTIAL for CrashLoopBackOff debugging!
📤 Example: Finding the Crash Cause
[2024-01-15 14:32:05] INFO: Starting Gunicorn 21.2.0
[2024-01-15 14:32:06] ERROR: ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'psycopg2'
[2024-01-15 14:32:06] ERROR: Worker failed to boot
[2024-01-15 14:32:06] CRITICAL: Application startup failed
# ← Container crashed here — this log would be lost without --previous!

View Specific Container Logs

Multi-container pods have multiple log streams. Use -c to specify which container.

🖥️ Select Container in Multi-Container Pod
# List containers in a pod first
kubectl get pod anihpj-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[*].name}'
# Output: anihpj nginx sidecar

# View logs from specific container
kubectl logs anihpj-pod -c nginx

# View logs from init container
kubectl logs anihpj-pod -c init-migrations --previous

# View ALL containers at once
kubectl logs anihpj-pod --all-containers
  Multi-Container Pod: anihpj-pod
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                     │
  │  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐ │
  │  │  anihpj     │  │   nginx     │  │  sidecar    │ │
  │  │  (Django)   │  │  (proxy)    │  │  (logging)  │ │
  │  │             │  │             │  │             │ │
  │  │  -c anihpj  │  │  -c nginx   │  │  -c sidecar │ │
  │  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘ │
  │                                                     │
  │  kubectl logs anihpj-pod -c nginx   ← specific     │
  │  kubectl logs anihpj-pod --all-containers ← all    │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

View Last N Lines

🖥️ Limit Log Output
# View last 50 lines only
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-xyz --tail=50

# View last 100 lines and follow
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-xyz --tail=100 -f

# View last 10 lines from all pods with label
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj --tail=10 --all-containers

View Logs Since a Time

🖥️ Time-Based Log Filtering
# Logs from the last hour
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-xyz --since=1h

# Logs from the last 30 minutes
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-xyz --since=30m

# Logs from the last 2 hours, following
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-xyz --since=2h -f

# Logs since specific timestamp (RFC 3339)
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-xyz --since-time=2024-01-15T14:00:00Z

⏱️ Time Duration Formats

FormatMeaningExample
--since=30sLast 30 secondsQuick check after deploy
--since=5mLast 5 minutesRecent errors
--since=1hLast hourCurrent session
--since=24hLast 24 hoursDaily review
--since-time=...Since exact timestampIncident investigation

View Logs by Label

🖥️ Aggregate Logs from Multiple Pods
# All pods with app=anihpj label
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj

# All containers in all matching pods
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj --all-containers

# Follow with tail, all pods
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj --all-containers --tail=20 -f

# Multiple labels (AND)
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj,tier=backend --all-containers

# With prefix showing pod name (great for debugging)
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj --prefix
📤 Output with --prefix
[pod/anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-abc/anihpj] GET /jobs/ 200 OK
[pod/anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-xyz/anihpj] GET /api/jobs/ 200 OK
[pod/anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-123/anihpj] POST /jobs/create/ 201 Created

📋 kubectl logs Quick Reference

FlagPurposeExample
-fFollow/stream logskubectl logs -f pod-name
-p / --previousPrevious (crashed) containerkubectl logs -p pod-name
-cSpecific containerkubectl logs pod -c nginx
--all-containersAll containers in podkubectl logs pod --all-containers
--tail=NLast N lineskubectl logs pod --tail=100
--since=Since durationkubectl logs pod --since=1h
--since-time=Since timestampkubectl logs pod --since-time=2024-01-15T14:00:00Z
-lBy label selectorkubectl logs -l app=anihpj
--prefixShow pod name prefixkubectl logs -l app=anihpj --prefix
--timestampsAdd timestampskubectl logs pod --timestamps
🔥 Pro Tool: Stern — Multi-Pod Log Tailing Made Easy

stern is a popular open-source tool that makes tailing logs from multiple pods effortless. Unlike kubectl logs, it auto-follows new pods and color-codes output.

🖥️ Stern: Install and Usage
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  INSTALL STERN
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# macOS
brew install stern

# Windows (with Chocolatey)
choco install stern

# Linux (download binary)
curl -Lo stern https://github.com/stern/stern/releases/download/v1.28.0/stern_1.28.0_linux_amd64.tar.gz
tar -xvf stern_1.28.0_linux_amd64.tar.gz
sudo mv stern /usr/local/bin/

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  USE STERN FOR MULTI-POD LOGGING
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Tail all pods matching "anihpj" (auto-follows new pods!)
stern anihpj

# In specific namespace
stern anihpj -n anihpj

# Only specific container
stern anihpj -c django

# With timestamps
stern anihpj -t

# Last 10 lines per pod, then follow
stern anihpj --tail 10

# Exclude certain pods
stern anihpj --exclude-pod postgres

# Output with JSON parsing (if logs are JSON)
stern anihpj -o json | jq '.message'

⚖️ kubectl logs vs Stern

Featurekubectl logsStern
Multiple podsManual with -l label✅ Auto with regex
Color coding❌ None✅ Per pod colors
Auto-follow new pods❌ No✅ Yes
Regex filtering❌ No✅ --include/exclude
Built-in✅ Yes❌ Install required

26.3 Application-Level Logging in Django

While kubectl logs captures stdout, your Django application should use Python's logging module for structured, useful output.

📊 Python Log Levels

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                     LOG LEVELS (ascending severity)              │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

    LEVEL       │ VALUE │ USE CASE                      │ PRODUCTION?
  ──────────────┼───────┼───────────────────────────────┼───────────
    DEBUG       │  10   │ Detailed diagnostic info      │ ❌ NO
    INFO        │  20   │ Normal operations, requests   │ ✅ YES
    WARNING     │  30   │ Something unexpected          │ ✅ YES
    ERROR       │  40   │ Error occurred, handled       │ ✅ YES  
    CRITICAL    │  50   │ System failure, shutting down │ ✅ YES

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  💡 Set level to INFO in production, DEBUG only in dev         │
  │     Higher level = less noise, but might miss context          │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
anihpj/settings.py 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  LOGGING CONFIGURATION FOR KUBERNETES
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
import os

LOGGING = {
    'version': 1,
    'disable_existing_loggers': False,
    
    # ─── FORMATTERS ───
    'formatters': {
        # JSON format: machine-parseable, perfect for log aggregation
        'json': {
            'format': '{"time": "%(asctime)s", "level": "%(levelname)s", '
                      '"logger": "%(name)s", "message": "%(message)s", '
                      '"module": "%(module)s", "line": %(lineno)d}'
        },
        # Simple format: human-readable for development
        'simple': {
            'format': '[%(asctime)s] %(levelname)s %(name)s: %(message)s'
        },
    },
    
    # ─── HANDLERS ───
    'handlers': {
        # Console handler: outputs to stdout (captured by K8s)
        'console': {
            'class': 'logging.StreamHandler',
            'formatter': 'json' if os.getenv('K8S_ENV') else 'simple',
            'stream': 'ext://sys.stdout',  # stdout, not stderr
        },
    },
    
    # ─── LOGGERS ───
    'loggers': {
        # Django framework logs
        'django': {
            'handlers': ['console'],
            'level': os.getenv('DJANGO_LOG_LEVEL', 'INFO'),
            'propagate': False,
        },
        # Django request logs (HTTP requests)
        'django.request': {
            'handlers': ['console'],
            'level': 'INFO',
            'propagate': False,
        },
        # Your application logs
        'jobpost': {
            'handlers': ['console'],
            'level': 'DEBUG' if os.getenv('DEBUG') else 'INFO',
            'propagate': False,
        },
    },
    
    # Root logger (catch-all)
    'root': {
        'handlers': ['console'],
        'level': 'WARNING',
    },
}
jobpost/views.py 
import logging
from django.shortcuts import render
from django.http import JsonResponse
from .models import JobPost

# Get logger for this module
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)  # 'jobpost.views'

def job_list(request):
    """List all job postings."""
    logger.info(f"Job list requested by {request.META.get('REMOTE_ADDR')}")
    
    try:
        jobs = JobPost.objects.filter(is_active=True)
        logger.debug(f"Found {jobs.count()} active jobs")
        
        return render(request, 'jobpost/list.html', {'jobs': jobs})
        
    except Exception as e:
        logger.error(f"Failed to fetch jobs: {e}", exc_info=True)
        return JsonResponse({'error': 'Database error'}, status=500)


def job_create(request):
    """Create a new job posting."""
    if request.method == 'POST':
        logger.info(f"Job creation attempt by user {request.user}")
        
        # ❌ NEVER log sensitive data!
        # logger.info(f"API key: {request.headers.get('Authorization')}")  # BAD!
        
        try:
            job = JobPost.objects.create(
                title=request.POST['title'],
                description=request.POST['description'],
            )
            logger.info(f"Job created: id={job.id}, title={job.title}")
            return JsonResponse({'id': job.id}, status=201)
            
        except Exception as e:
            logger.exception("Job creation failed")  # Includes stack trace
            return JsonResponse({'error': str(e)}, status=400)

✅❌ What to Log vs What NOT to Log

✅
DO Log
  • Request method, path, status code
  • User ID (not username/email)
  • Operation start/end times
  • Error messages with context
  • Database query counts
  • External API call results
  • Authentication events (login/logout)
❌
NEVER Log
  • Passwords or credentials
  • API keys or tokens
  • Credit card numbers
  • Personal data (PII)
  • Session tokens
  • Full request bodies with sensitive data
  • Database connection strings

26.4 Centralized Logging Architecture

kubectl logs works for quick checks, but production clusters need centralized logging. Why?

🤔 Why Centralize Logs?

😰
Problems Without Centralization
  • Pods die → logs disappear forever
  • 50 pods = 50 separate log streams
  • Can't search across pods/time
  • No correlation with metrics
  • No long-term retention
🎯
Benefits of Centralization
  • Logs persist beyond pod lifecycle
  • Single pane of glass for all logs
  • Full-text search across cluster
  • Dashboards and visualizations
  • Alerting on log patterns

🏗️ Centralized Logging Architecture

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    CENTRALIZED LOGGING IN KUBERNETES                          │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

    Node 1                    Node 2                    Node 3
  ┌────────────────┐       ┌────────────────┐       ┌────────────────┐
  │ ┌────┐ ┌────┐ │       │ ┌────┐ ┌────┐ │       │ ┌────┐ ┌────┐ │
  │ │Pod │ │Pod │ │       │ │Pod │ │Pod │ │       │ │Pod │ │Pod │ │
  │ │📦  │ │📦  │ │       │ │📦  │ │📦  │ │       │ │📦  │ │📦  │ │
  │ └──┬─┘ └──┬─┘ │       │ └──┬─┘ └──┬─┘ │       │ └──┬─┘ └──┬─┘ │
  │    │ stdout│   │       │    │      │   │       │    │      │   │
  │    ▼      ▼   │       │    ▼      ▼   │       │    ▼      ▼   │
  │  /var/log/pods│       │  /var/log/pods│       │  /var/log/pods│
  │       │       │       │       │       │       │       │       │
  │   ┌───┴───┐   │       │   ┌───┴───┐   │       │   ┌───┴───┐   │
  │   │Fluentd│   │       │   │Fluentd│   │       │   │Fluentd│   │
  │   │(DaemonSet)│       │   │(DaemonSet)│       │   │(DaemonSet)│
  │   └───┬───┘   │       │   └───┬───┘   │       │   └───┬───┘   │
  └───────┼───────┘       └───────┼───────┘       └───────┼───────┘
          │                       │                       │
          └───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┘
                                  │
                                  ▼
                    ┌─────────────────────────┐
                    │    Elasticsearch         │ ← Store & Index
                    │    (or Loki)             │
                    └────────────┬────────────┘
                                 │
                                 ▼
                    ┌─────────────────────────┐
                    │    Kibana / Grafana      │ ← Search & Visualize
                    │    🔍 Dashboard           │
                    └─────────────────────────┘

EFK Stack (Elasticsearch + Fluentd + Kibana)

📝
Fluentd / Fluent Bit

Role: Log collector

Runs as: DaemonSet (1 per node)

Does: Reads /var/log/pods, parses, ships to Elasticsearch

🗄️
Elasticsearch

Role: Log storage + indexing

Runs as: StatefulSet

Does: Full-text search, aggregations

📊
Kibana

Role: Visualization UI

Runs as: Deployment

Does: Search, dashboards, discover

🖥️ Deploy EFK with Helm
# Add Elastic Helm repo
helm repo add elastic https://helm.elastic.co
helm repo update

# Install Elasticsearch
helm install elasticsearch elastic/elasticsearch \
  --namespace logging --create-namespace \
  --set replicas=1 \
  --set resources.requests.memory=1Gi

# Install Kibana
helm install kibana elastic/kibana \
  --namespace logging

# Install Fluent Bit (lightweight Fluentd)
helm repo add fluent https://fluent.github.io/helm-charts
helm install fluent-bit fluent/fluent-bit \
  --namespace logging \
  --set backend.type=es \
  --set backend.es.host=elasticsearch-master
⚠️ Resource Warning

Elasticsearch is memory-hungry (requires 2GB+ heap). For learning/small clusters, consider Loki (next section) which is much lighter.

Loki + Grafana (Lightweight Alternative)

🚀 Loki: "Prometheus, but for Logs"

Loki is designed by Grafana Labs. It doesn't index log content (unlike Elasticsearch), only labels — making it 10x cheaper to operate.

📝
Promtail

Role: Log collector (like Fluentd)

Runs as: DaemonSet

📦
Loki

Role: Log aggregator

Runs as: StatefulSet

📊
Grafana

Role: Visualization

Bonus: Also shows metrics!

🖥️ Deploy Loki Stack with Helm
# Add Grafana Helm repo
helm repo add grafana https://grafana.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update

# Install Loki Stack (includes Loki, Promtail, Grafana)
helm install loki grafana/loki-stack \
  --namespace logging --create-namespace \
  --set grafana.enabled=true \
  --set prometheus.enabled=false

# Get Grafana password
kubectl get secret loki-grafana -n logging \
  -o jsonpath="{.data.admin-password}" | base64 -d

# Port-forward to access Grafana
kubectl port-forward svc/loki-grafana 3000:80 -n logging
# Open http://localhost:3000, login: admin / (password from above)

⚖️ EFK vs Loki: Which to Choose?

AspectEFK StackLoki + Grafana
Memory UsageHigh (2GB+ for ES)Low (256MB+)
Search SpeedFast (indexed)Slower (grep-like)
Full-text Search✅ Excellent⚠️ Label-based only
Cost at ScaleExpensive10x cheaper
Metrics IntegrationSeparateSame dashboard
Best ForLarge enterpriseMost K8s clusters
RecommendationComplex log analysis✅ Start here

26.5 Log Aggregation Best Practices

✅ Logging Best Practices Checklist

✅
DO These
  • ✅ Use structured JSON logging
  • ✅ Include request IDs for tracing
  • ✅ Set retention policies (30/90 days)
  • ✅ Alert on ERROR frequency spikes
  • ✅ Log to stdout (not files)
  • ✅ Use consistent timestamp format
  • ✅ Add K8s metadata (pod, namespace)
❌
AVOID These
  • ❌ Logging sensitive data (PII, secrets)
  • ❌ DEBUG level in production
  • ❌ Logging inside tight loops
  • ❌ Unstructured log messages
  • ❌ Logging request/response bodies
  • ❌ No rotation (disk fills up)
  • ❌ Ignoring log storage costs

📋 Chapter 26 Summary: Logging

  • ✅ kubectl logs — Basic log access, use -f, --previous, -c, --since
  • ✅ Structured logging — JSON format in Django for parsing
  • ✅ Log levels — DEBUG dev only, INFO+ in production
  • ✅ EFK Stack — Elasticsearch + Fluentd + Kibana (powerful, heavy)
  • ✅ Loki Stack — Promtail + Loki + Grafana (lightweight, recommended)
  • ✅ Best practices — Structured, retention policies, no sensitive data
🖥️ Chapter 26 Quick Reference
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  KUBECTL LOGS ESSENTIALS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl logs deployment/anihpj-deployment          # Basic logs
kubectl logs -f pod-name                            # Follow live
kubectl logs pod-name --previous                    # Crashed container
kubectl logs pod-name -c container-name             # Specific container
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj --all-containers         # All matching pods
kubectl logs pod-name --tail=100 --since=1h         # Last 100 lines, 1 hour

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  INSTALL LOKI STACK
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
helm repo add grafana https://grafana.github.io/helm-charts
helm install loki grafana/loki-stack -n logging --create-namespace \
  --set grafana.enabled=true

Chapter 27: Monitoring

PART 9 INTERMEDIATE 📊 METRICS

Logs tell you what happened. Metrics tell you how your system is performing right now — and how it's trending. In this chapter, you'll learn to monitor Kubernetes clusters with the Metrics Server, Prometheus, and Grafana.

27.1 What to Monitor in Kubernetes

🏔️ The Three Layers of K8s Monitoring

                    ┌────────────────────────────────────┐
                    │     LAYER 3: APPLICATION            │
                    │                                     │
                    │  📊 HTTP latency (p50, p99)        │
                    │  📈 Request rate (req/sec)         │
                    │  ❌ Error rate (4xx, 5xx %)        │
                    │  🎯 Business metrics (jobs/min)    │
                    │                                     │
                    │  "Is my Django app healthy?"       │
                    └───────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                                    │
              ┌─────────────────────┴─────────────────────┐
              │        LAYER 2: KUBERNETES OBJECTS          │
              │                                              │
              │  🔄 Pod restarts (CrashLoopBackOff?)       │
              │  📦 Deployment replicas (desired vs ready) │
              │  💾 PVC usage (storage full?)              │
              │  🔌 Service endpoints (healthy?)           │
              │                                              │
              │  "Are my K8s resources in good state?"     │
              └───────────────────┬───────────────────────┘
                                  │
    ┌─────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────┐
    │              LAYER 1: INFRASTRUCTURE                        │
    │                                                              │
    │  🖥️ Node CPU utilization (%)      │  💾 Memory usage (%)   │
    │  💿 Disk I/O and space            │  🌐 Network bandwidth   │
    │  🔋 Node conditions (Ready?)      │  📊 kubelet health      │
    │                                                              │
    │  "Are my physical/virtual machines healthy?"                │
    └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

    ⚡ Golden Rule: Monitor ALL THREE layers for complete visibility!

📊 Key Metrics for anihpj

🎯
Application Layer
  • Response time: /jobs/ endpoint
  • Requests per second
  • Error rate (5xx responses)
  • Database query time
  • Jobs created per hour
☸️
Kubernetes Layer
  • Pod restarts count
  • Deployment replica status
  • HPA scaling events
  • PVC usage percentage
  • Service endpoint health
🖥️
Infrastructure Layer
  • Node CPU & memory
  • Disk space / I/O
  • Network throughput
  • Node conditions
  • kubelet health

27.2 Metrics Server

📊 Metrics Server: Lightweight Built-in Monitoring

Metrics Server is a cluster-wide aggregator of resource usage data. It enables kubectl top and is required for Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA).

🔄 How Metrics Server Works

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    METRICS SERVER FLOW                         │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

    Node 1              Node 2              Node 3
  ┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐
  │  kubelet   │     │  kubelet   │     │  kubelet   │
  │    │       │     │    │       │     │    │       │
  │ cAdvisor   │     │ cAdvisor   │     │ cAdvisor   │
  │ (container │     │ (container │     │ (container │
  │  metrics)  │     │  metrics)  │     │  metrics)  │
  └─────┬──────┘     └─────┬──────┘     └─────┬──────┘
        │                  │                  │
        └──────────────────┼──────────────────┘
                           │ scrape every 15s
                           ▼
                  ┌─────────────────┐
                  │  Metrics Server │
                  │  (aggregates)   │
                  └────────┬────────┘
                           │
          ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐
          ▼                ▼                ▼
    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐
    │ kubectl  │    │   HPA    │    │   VPA    │
    │   top    │    │ (scaler) │    │ (scaler) │
    └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘
🖥️ Enable and Use Metrics Server
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  ENABLE METRICS SERVER (Minikube)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
minikube addons enable metrics-server

# Wait for it to be ready (~1-2 minutes)
kubectl get deployment metrics-server -n kube-system

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  VIEW RESOURCE USAGE
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Node-level metrics
kubectl top nodes

# Pod-level metrics
kubectl top pods

# Sorted by CPU usage
kubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu

# Sorted by memory usage
kubectl top pods --sort-by=memory

# Specific namespace
kubectl top pods -n anihpj

# With containers breakdown
kubectl top pods --containers
📤 Example kubectl top Output
$ kubectl top nodes
NAME       CPU(cores)   CPU%   MEMORY(bytes)   MEMORY%
minikube   245m         12%    1847Mi          47%

$ kubectl top pods -n anihpj
NAME                                  CPU(cores)   MEMORY(bytes)
anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-abc       25m          128Mi
anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-xyz       31m          135Mi
postgres-0                            15m          256Mi
💡 CPU Units

245m = 245 millicores = 0.245 CPU cores. 1000m = 1 full CPU core.

27.3 Prometheus

Prometheus is the industry standard for Kubernetes monitoring. It's a CNCF graduated project (like Kubernetes itself).

What is Prometheus?

🔥
Prometheus Features
  • Pull-based: Scrapes /metrics endpoints
  • Time-series DB: Stores metrics with timestamps
  • PromQL: Powerful query language
  • Alerting: Built-in AlertManager
  • Service discovery: Auto-finds K8s pods
✅
Why Prometheus for K8s?
  • Native K8s integration
  • Labels match K8s metadata
  • Auto-discovers pods/services
  • Pre-built K8s metrics
  • De facto standard (all tools support it)

How it Works in Kubernetes

🔄 Prometheus Architecture in K8s

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                      PROMETHEUS IN KUBERNETES                          │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

         Application Pods             System Components
  ┌────────────────────────┐    ┌────────────────────────┐
  │  anihpj  │  postgres   │    │  kubelet │ kube-state  │
  │  :8000   │  :9187      │    │  :10255  │  -metrics   │
  │ /metrics │ /metrics    │    │ /metrics │  /metrics   │
  └─────┬────┴──────┬──────┘    └─────┬────┴──────┬──────┘
        │           │                 │           │
        └───────────┴─────────────────┴───────────┘
                              │
                        scrapes /metrics
                         every 15-30s
                              │
                              ▼
                    ┌─────────────────────┐
                    │     PROMETHEUS       │
                    │                     │
                    │  ┌───────────────┐  │
                    │  │ Time-series   │  │
                    │  │   Database    │  │
                    │  └───────────────┘  │
                    │                     │
                    │  ┌───────────────┐  │
                    │  │  Alert Rules  │──┼──▶ AlertManager ──▶ Slack/PagerDuty
                    │  └───────────────┘  │
                    └──────────┬──────────┘
                               │
                    PromQL queries
                               │
                               ▼
                    ┌─────────────────────┐
                    │       GRAFANA        │
                    │    📊 Dashboards     │
                    │    📈 Graphs         │
                    │    🔔 Alerts         │
                    └─────────────────────┘

Installing with Prometheus Operator

🖥️ Install kube-prometheus-stack (Recommended)
# Add Prometheus Community Helm repo
helm repo add prometheus-community \
  https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update

# Install the full stack (Prometheus + Grafana + AlertManager + exporters)
helm install prometheus prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack \
  --namespace monitoring --create-namespace

# Check what got installed
kubectl get pods -n monitoring

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  OUTPUT: Multiple components deployed
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# prometheus-kube-prometheus-operator-xxx   (manages Prometheus)
# prometheus-prometheus-xxx                  (Prometheus server)
# prometheus-grafana-xxx                     (Grafana UI)
# prometheus-alertmanager-xxx                (AlertManager)
# prometheus-kube-state-metrics-xxx          (K8s object metrics)
# prometheus-prometheus-node-exporter-xxx    (Node metrics - DaemonSet)
🖥️ Access the Dashboards
# Port-forward Prometheus UI
kubectl port-forward svc/prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus 9090:9090 -n monitoring
# Open http://localhost:9090

# Port-forward Grafana
kubectl port-forward svc/prometheus-grafana 3000:80 -n monitoring
# Open http://localhost:3000
# Default login: admin / prom-operator

# Port-forward AlertManager
kubectl port-forward svc/prometheus-kube-prometheus-alertmanager 9093:9093 -n monitoring
# Open http://localhost:9093

Instrumenting anihpj

To expose Django metrics to Prometheus, use the django-prometheus library:

requirements.txt 
Django==4.2
gunicorn==21.2.0
psycopg2-binary==2.9.9
django-prometheus==2.3.1  # Add this line
anihpj/settings.py 
INSTALLED_APPS = [
    # ... other apps
    'django_prometheus',  # Add this
]

MIDDLEWARE = [
    'django_prometheus.middleware.PrometheusBeforeMiddleware',  # First!
    # ... other middleware
    'django_prometheus.middleware.PrometheusAfterMiddleware',   # Last!
]
anihpj/urls.py 
from django.urls import path, include

urlpatterns = [
    # ... your other URLs
    path('', include('django_prometheus.urls')),  # Exposes /metrics
]
📤 What /metrics Exposes
# HELP django_http_requests_total Total HTTP requests
# TYPE django_http_requests_total counter
django_http_requests_total{method="GET",path="/jobs/",status="200"} 1523

# HELP django_http_request_duration_seconds HTTP request duration
# TYPE django_http_request_duration_seconds histogram
django_http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="0.05"} 1200
django_http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="0.1"} 1400

# HELP django_db_execute_total Total DB queries
# TYPE django_db_execute_total counter
django_db_execute_total{alias="default"} 4521

📊 Essential PromQL Queries for anihpj

What to MeasurePromQL Query
Request rate (req/sec) sum(rate(django_http_requests_total[5m]))
Error rate (%) sum(rate(django_http_responses_total{status=~"5.."}[5m])) / sum(rate(django_http_responses_total[5m])) * 100
p99 latency (seconds) histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(django_http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le))
Pod CPU usage sum(rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="anihpj"}[5m])) by (pod)
Pod memory (MB) sum(container_memory_usage_bytes{namespace="anihpj"}) by (pod) / 1024 / 1024
Pod restarts sum(increase(kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="anihpj"}[1h])) by (pod)

🚦 The RED Method: 3 Key Metrics

📈
Rate

Requests per second

rate(requests_total[5m])
❌
Errors

Failed request percentage

rate(errors[5m]) / rate(total[5m])
⏱️
Duration

Response time (p50, p99)

histogram_quantile(0.99, ...)

💡 Tip: Dashboard these 3 metrics first — they cover 80% of what you need to know about service health.

27.4 Grafana

📊 Grafana: Visualization Powerhouse

Grafana turns Prometheus metrics into beautiful, actionable dashboards. It comes pre-installed with kube-prometheus-stack.

🎨 Popular Pre-built Dashboards

Dashboard IDNameShows
3119Kubernetes ClusterCluster overview, nodes, pods
6417Kubernetes PodsPer-pod CPU, memory, network
6663Kubernetes DeploymentsDeployment health, replicas
1860Node Exporter FullDetailed node metrics
9628PostgreSQLDatabase metrics (with exporter)
🖥️ Import a Dashboard
1. Go to Grafana (http://localhost:3000)
2. Click + → Import
3. Enter Dashboard ID: 3119
4. Select Prometheus data source
5. Click Import

Done! 📊 Instant Kubernetes visibility

📊 Grafana Dashboard Layout (Conceptual)

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  📊 ANIHPJ MONITORING DASHBOARD                          🔄 Last 1h  ▾     │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                                                                             │
  │  ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐      │
  │  │ 🟢 PODS      │ │ 📈 REQ/SEC   │ │ ⏱️ LATENCY   │ │ ❌ ERROR %   │      │
  │  │     3/3      │ │    156       │ │   45ms       │ │   0.2%       │      │
  │  └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘      │
  │                                                                             │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  HTTP Request Rate                                                  │   │
  │  │                                                                     │   │
  │  │  200 ┤                    ╭──────────╮                              │   │
  │  │      │               ╭───╯          ╰───╮                           │   │
  │  │  100 ┤          ╭───╯                    ╰──╮                       │   │
  │  │      │     ╭───╯                             ╰──────                │   │
  │  │    0 ┼────╯                                                         │   │
  │  │      └────────────────────────────────────────────────              │   │
  │  │       10:00   10:15   10:30   10:45   11:00   11:15                 │   │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                                                             │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────────────┐         │
  │  │ CPU Usage by Pod            │  │ Memory Usage by Pod          │         │
  │  │   ████████░░░░ anihpj-abc  │  │   ██████████░░ anihpj-abc    │         │
  │  │   ███████░░░░░ anihpj-xyz  │  │   █████████░░░ anihpj-xyz    │         │
  │  │   ███░░░░░░░░░ postgres    │  │   ████████████ postgres      │         │
  │  └─────────────────────────────┘  └─────────────────────────────┘         │
  │                                                                             │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

27.5 Alerting

Metrics without alerts mean staring at dashboards 24/7. Configure alerts to notify you when thresholds are breached.

🚨 What to Alert On (anihpj)

🔴
Critical (Page immediately)
  • Pod restart count > 5 in 10m
  • All replicas down
  • Error rate > 10%
  • Response time > 5s (p99)
🟡
Warning (Investigate soon)
  • Error rate > 5%
  • PVC usage > 80%
  • CPU > 90% for 5m
  • Response time > 2s (p95)
🔵
Info (Log/ticket)
  • HPA scaled up
  • Deployment updated
  • Certificate expires < 30d
anihpj-alerts.yaml 
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: PrometheusRule
metadata:
  name: anihpj-alerts
  namespace: monitoring
  labels:
    release: prometheus  # Must match Prometheus Operator selector
spec:
  groups:
    - name: anihpj.rules
      rules:
        # ─── Critical: High error rate ───
        - alert: AnihpjHighErrorRate
          expr: |
            sum(rate(django_http_responses_total{status=~"5.."}[5m]))
            /
            sum(rate(django_http_responses_total[5m])) > 0.10
          for: 5m
          labels:
            severity: critical
          annotations:
            summary: "High error rate on anihpj"
            description: "Error rate is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}"

        # ─── Warning: Pod restarts ───
        - alert: AnihpjPodRestarts
          expr: |
            sum(increase(kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total
              {namespace="anihpj"}[1h])) > 5
          for: 0m
          labels:
            severity: warning
          annotations:
            summary: "Pod restarts detected"
            description: "{{ $value }} restarts in the last hour"

        # ─── Warning: High latency ───
        - alert: AnihpjHighLatency
          expr: |
            histogram_quantile(0.95,
              sum(rate(django_http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le)
            ) > 2
          for: 5m
          labels:
            severity: warning
          annotations:
            summary: "High response latency"
            description: "p95 latency is {{ $value }}s"

27.6 Kubernetes Dashboard

The Kubernetes Dashboard is a built-in web UI for cluster management. It's simpler than Grafana but useful for quick overviews.

🖥️ Access Kubernetes Dashboard
# Minikube: Built-in dashboard
minikube dashboard

# Or manually install for other clusters
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/dashboard/v2.7.0/aio/deploy/recommended.yaml

# Create admin user for login
kubectl create serviceaccount dashboard-admin -n kubernetes-dashboard
kubectl create clusterrolebinding dashboard-admin \
  --clusterrole=cluster-admin \
  --serviceaccount=kubernetes-dashboard:dashboard-admin

# Get login token
kubectl create token dashboard-admin -n kubernetes-dashboard

# Port-forward
kubectl port-forward svc/kubernetes-dashboard 8443:443 -n kubernetes-dashboard
# Open https://localhost:8443

📋 Chapter 27 Summary: Monitoring

  • ✅ 3 Layers — Monitor application, K8s objects, AND infrastructure
  • ✅ Metrics Server — Enables kubectl top, required for HPA
  • ✅ Prometheus — Pull-based metrics collection, time-series DB
  • ✅ Grafana — Visualization dashboards, import pre-built with IDs
  • ✅ Alerting — PrometheusRule for automated notifications
  • ✅ django-prometheus — Expose Django metrics at /metrics

Chapter 28: Debugging and Troubleshooting

PART 9 INTERMEDIATE 🔍 DEBUGGING

Things will break. Pods will crash. Services will be unreachable. This chapter teaches you the systematic approach to debugging Kubernetes issues — from quick checks to deep investigation.

28.1 The Debugging Mindset

🧘 Rule #1: Don't Panic

Kubernetes errors look scary but are usually fixable. Follow a systematic approach: Status → Events → Describe → Logs → Exec

🔍 Debugging Decision Tree

                              🚨 Something's wrong!
                                      │
                                      ▼
                        ┌─────────────────────────┐
                        │   kubectl get pods       │
                        └────────────┬────────────┘
                                     │
         ┌───────────┬───────────┬───┴───┬───────────┬───────────┐
         ▼           ▼           ▼       ▼           ▼           ▼
    ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐
    │ Pending │ │CrashLoop│ │ImagePull│ │ Running │ │  No     │ │Terminat-│
    │         │ │BackOff  │ │BackOff  │ │ but bad │ │  pods   │ │  ing    │
    └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘
         │           │           │           │           │           │
         ▼           ▼           ▼           ▼           ▼           ▼
    ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐
    │describe │ │  logs   │ │describe │ │  logs   │ │  check  │ │  wait   │
    │  pod    │ │--previous│ │  pod    │ │ + exec │ │ deploy  │ │ or force│
    │         │ │         │ │         │ │         │ │         │ │ delete  │
    └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘
         │           │           │           │           │           │
         ▼           ▼           ▼           ▼           ▼           ▼
    ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐
    │ Check   │ │ Fix app │ │ Fix     │ │ Check   │ │ Check   │ │--force  │
    │ node    │ │ crash   │ │ image   │ │ service │ │ events  │ │--grace- │
    │resources│ │ issue   │ │ name    │ │selector │ │         │ │period=0 │
    └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘

🔢 The 5-Step Debugging Flow

1️⃣
STATUS
kubectl get pods
2️⃣
EVENTS
kubectl get events
3️⃣
DESCRIBE
kubectl describe
4️⃣
LOGS
kubectl logs
5️⃣
EXEC
kubectl exec

28.2 Essential Debugging Commands

🛠️ Debugging Command Reference

CommandWhen to UseWhat it Shows
kubectl get pods First check Pod status, restarts, age
kubectl get pods -o wide Node issues IP, node assignment
kubectl describe pod <name> Scheduling/startup issues Events, conditions, volumes
kubectl logs <pod> Application errors Container stdout/stderr
kubectl logs <pod> -p CrashLoopBackOff Previous container logs
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- sh Runtime investigation Shell access to container
kubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp Recent cluster activity Scheduling, errors, warnings
kubectl top pods Resource issues CPU/memory usage
kubectl port-forward <pod> 8000:8000 Connection testing Direct access to pod
kubectl debug <pod> -it --image=busybox Distroless containers Debug sidecar shell
🖥️ Complete Debugging Sequence
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  STEP 1: CHECK POD STATUS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl get pods -n anihpj
kubectl get pods -n anihpj -o wide  # See node, IP

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  STEP 2: CHECK RECENT EVENTS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl get events -n anihpj --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | tail -20

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  STEP 3: DESCRIBE THE PROBLEM POD
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n anihpj
# Look for: Events section, Conditions, State/Reason

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  STEP 4: CHECK LOGS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n anihpj
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n anihpj --previous  # If crashed

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  STEP 5: EXEC INTO CONTAINER (if running)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -n anihpj -- /bin/sh
# Check: env vars, file paths, network connectivity

28.3 Pod States — What They Mean

📊 Pod Status Quick Reference

StateMeaningCommon CausesFix
✅ Running All containers running — None needed
⏳ Pending Waiting to be scheduled No node capacity, nodeSelector mismatch, PVC pending Check resources, node labels, PVC
🔄 ContainerCreating Image pulling or volume mounting Large image, slow registry, volume issues Wait, or check describe events
💀 CrashLoopBackOff Container keeps crashing App error, missing config, wrong command kubectl logs --previous
❌ ImagePullBackOff Can't pull image Wrong name, no auth, registry down Check image name, imagePullSecret
⚠️ ErrImagePull Image pull failed Network issue, image doesn't exist Verify image exists, check network
🔒 Init:0/1 Init container running Init container taking long/stuck kubectl logs -c init-container
🏁 Completed Pod finished (normal for Jobs) Job completed successfully Check Job status
⏸️ Terminating Pod shutting down Being deleted, stuck on finalizers Wait, or force delete
❓ Unknown Node lost contact Node crashed, network partition Check node health

🔄 Pod Lifecycle States

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                        POD LIFECYCLE                                  │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

        Create                                                Delete
          │                                                      │
          ▼                                                      │
     ┌─────────┐      ┌──────────────┐      ┌─────────┐         │
     │ Pending │─────▶│ContainerCreate│─────▶│ Running │─────────┤
     └────┬────┘      └──────────────┘      └────┬────┘         │
          │                                      │               │
          │ (scheduling fails)                   │ (crash)       │
          ▼                                      ▼               ▼
    ┌───────────┐                         ┌───────────────┐ ┌───────────┐
    │ Unschedul-│                         │ CrashLoopBack │ │Terminating│
    │   able    │                         │     Off       │ └─────┬─────┘
    └───────────┘                         └───────┬───────┘       │
                                                  │               │
                                            (restart)             ▼
                                                  │          ┌─────────┐
                                                  └─────────▶│ Deleted │
                                                             └─────────┘

28.4 Scenario-Based Debugging for anihpj

Real-world debugging scenarios you'll encounter with anihpj:

🎯 Common Debugging Scenarios

💀 Scenario 1: CrashLoopBackOff

Problem: anihpj pod keeps restarting

# Investigate
kubectl get pods -n anihpj
# NAME                        READY   STATUS             RESTARTS
# anihpj-xyz                  0/1     CrashLoopBackOff   5

kubectl logs anihpj-xyz --previous
# ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'psycopg2'

# Fix: Add missing dependency to requirements.txt, rebuild image
🖼️ Scenario 2: ImagePullBackOff

Problem: Pod can't pull container image

# Investigate
kubectl describe pod anihpj-xyz
# Events:
#   Failed to pull image "myregistry/anihpj:latest": 
#   Error: repository not found

# Fix options:
# 1. Correct image name in deployment
# 2. Add imagePullSecret for private registry
# 3. Push image to registry
⏳ Scenario 3: Pod Stuck in Pending

Problem: Pod won't start

# Investigate
kubectl describe pod anihpj-xyz
# Events:
#   0/1 nodes are available: 1 Insufficient memory.
